qa_results.bill_summary: The Maryland Paint Stewardship bill requires architectural paint producers to establish and participate in a statewide Paint Stewardship Program to manage the collection, transportation, and processing of postconsumer (leftover) paint. The program is funded by a uniform 'paint stewardship assessment' added to the purchase price of architectural paint sold in the state. The bill mandates that producers or a representative organization submit a program plan to the Maryland Department of the Environment for approval, prohibits the sale of paint by non-participating producers, and requires annual reporting on program performance.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment (Office of Recycling)
qa_results.stakeholders: Architectural paint producers, retailers, distributors, consumers, local governments, and state agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in a state special fund revenue and expenditure increase of approximately 70,212 dollars in fiscal 2026 and 64,307 dollars in fiscal 2027 to cover the Maryland Department of the Environment's costs for plan review and program oversight. These costs are offset by fees paid by paint producers. State and local governments may experience a minimal increase in expenditures for paint purchases due to the program assessment, but local governments may see a decrease in waste management costs as the program diverts paint from local landfills and hazardous waste collection systems.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) is the primary implementing and oversight agency for the Paint Stewardship Program. Its Office of Recycling is specifically tasked with reviewing and approving program plans and annual reports, setting fees, and ensuring producer and retailer compliance.

The bill explicitly requires MDE's Office of Recycling to coordinate with the Maryland Environmental Service to study and report on the sufficiency of recycling centers and the economics of waste disposal and recycling systems.

The State Treasurer's Office is responsible for holding the State Recycling Trust Fund separately and investing the money in the fund, which will receive new fees and penalties established under this bill.
The Comptroller of Maryland is responsible for accounting for the State Recycling Trust Fund, which will collect paint stewardship plan review fees, annual report fees, and associated penalties.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to MDE and would be involved in enforcing the civil and administrative penalties authorized for violations of the Paint Stewardship Program.

DBM is impacted by the bill's fiscal requirements, including the management of new special fund revenues and the potential need for additional contractual personnel at MDE as identified in the fiscal note.

As the agency responsible for the maintenance and renovation of state-owned facilities, DGS is a significant consumer of architectural paint and will be impacted by the paint stewardship assessment added to the purchase price.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and MDE; the bill requires that reports regarding the program and state recycling goals be submitted to the Governor.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the governing bodies of Baltimore City and all Maryland counties to create a specific property tax subclass for vacant lots and abandoned buildings that have been cited as unfit for habitation. Local governments are empowered to set a special tax rate for these properties and must provide an annual report on the rate, the number of properties affected, the revenue generated, and conversion plans for those properties.
qa_results.responsible_party: The Mayor and City Council of Baltimore City, county governing bodies, and the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners of vacant or abandoned real property, Baltimore City government, and Maryland county governments
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances. Local property tax revenues for Baltimore City and individual counties may be affected depending on whether they choose to establish a special tax rate for the new subclass of vacant and abandoned property. While the State Department of Assessments and Taxation identifies approximately 242,361 vacant real property accounts with a total assessed value of 14100000000 dollars, the specific fiscal impact is indeterminate as it depends on future local legislative actions.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates that Baltimore City and counties enacting a special tax rate must report annually to the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) regarding property counts, revenue changes, and adaptive reuse plans, aligning with the department's mission of community revitalization.

As the board responsible for second-level administrative review of property tax assessments and classifications, PTAAB will likely handle appeals from property owners disputing their property's inclusion in the new vacant and abandoned subclass.

The Maryland Tax Court is the highest administrative body for tax disputes and will adjudicate further appeals from local Property Tax Assessment Appeals Boards regarding the valuation and classification of properties under the newly authorized special tax rate.

The Department of Planning provides technical assistance to local governments on land use and sustainable growth; this bill provides a new planning and revitalization tool for managing vacant urban and rural land.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies like DHCD and will be responsible for advising on the legal implementation and enforcement of the new reporting requirements.
The Governor's Office oversees executive branch policy and coordinated the initiative to provide local governments with more tools to address blight and community revitalization.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller monitors tax policy and the fiscal health of local jurisdictions, including changes to property tax structures and local revenue generation.

This initiative coordinates multi-agency efforts for community revitalization; authorizing a special tax rate for vacant properties is a direct tool for achieving the initiative's sustainable growth and revitalization goals.

The team pilots initiatives to address economic mobility and child poverty; reducing neighborhood blight through property tax policy is a component of enhancing economic stability for families in historically underinvested communities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits Maryland institutions of higher education that receive state funding from using legacy preference or donor preference as criteria for admissions. Legacy preference refers to favoritism based on a familial relationship with an alum, while donor preference refers to favoritism based on a familial relationship with a financial supporter. Institutions are still permitted to collect information about familial relationships for data collection purposes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland institutions of higher education that receive State funds
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants to Maryland colleges and universities, alumni, financial donors, and admissions departments of public and private state-funded higher education institutions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, public four-year institutions of higher education can implement the prohibition with no material fiscal impact. Baltimore City Community College and local community colleges do not use these preferences and are unaffected. While private nonprofit institutions receiving state funds may see indeterminate impacts on tuition revenue or alumni giving, the Department of Legislative Services assumes any overall impact is minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's central regulatory and coordinating board for postsecondary education, the Commission is responsible for establishing and overseeing statewide policies that affect public and private colleges and universities, including the admissions standards addressed in this bill.

The bill directly prohibits the University System's 12 institutions from using legacy or donor preferences in their admissions processes, requiring a change in policy for any member institutions currently utilizing such criteria.
As an independent state university, Morgan State is directly subject to the bill's prohibition on considering legacy or donor preferences in its admissions standards.
As an independent public honors college, Saint Mary's is directly impacted by the requirement to exclude legacy and donor preferences from its admissions criteria.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and institutions of higher education and would be responsible for advising on the interpretation and enforcement of this new legal prohibition.

The bill explicitly allows institutions to continue collecting familial relationship data for reporting and data purposes; the MLDS Center is the state's central repository for linking and analyzing such student data.

As the principal fiscal agency overseeing the state budget and funding for public higher education institutions, DBM monitors the fiscal impact and compliance of policies related to institutions receiving state funds.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill codifies the common law crime of indecent exposure and establishes a new misdemeanor offense for committing indecent exposure with prurient intent when the perpetrator knows or should know a minor is present. To qualify for the new offense, the minor must be at least two years old and more than four years younger than the perpetrator. The bill sets the penalty for this new offense at up to five years of imprisonment and/or a fine of up to $10,000.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary, Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, and the Office of the Public Defender
qa_results.stakeholders: Minors in Maryland, individuals charged with indecent exposure, law enforcement, the state court system, and correctional facilities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State and local general fund revenues and expenditures are expected to increase minimally due to the bill's penalty provisions. The new misdemeanor carries a maximum fine of $10,000 and a maximum prison sentence of five years. Although the Office of the Public Defender estimated a fiscal 2025 cost of $89,686 to hire an additional attorney, the Department of Legislative Services determined that additional staffing is unlikely to be required as the bill applies to individuals who would already face charges under existing statutes.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill codifies a common law crime and establishes a new misdemeanor offense, impacting court filings and processing as noted by the Administrative Office of the Courts in the fiscal note.
The agency is responsible for collecting state revenues, which would include the minimal increase in general fund revenues from monetary penalties established by the bill.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for supervising budget execution and long-term fiscal planning, which includes the minimal increase in State expenditures identified in the fiscal note.
The bill creates a specific offense for indecent exposure in the presence of a minor, which relates to the agency's responsibility for protecting children from abuse and neglect.
The agency is responsible for managing youth involved in the juvenile justice system; if a minor is charged with this new offense, DJS would be responsible for their supervision or treatment.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill increases criminal penalties and establishes a new misdemeanor with up to five years of imprisonment, directly impacting the department's oversight of correctional facilities and parole/probation intakes.
The Governor oversees the executive branch and enforces state laws; this office is also responsible for the annual state budget, which is minimally impacted by this bill's penalty provisions.
The office is dedicated to the well-being of Maryland's children; the bill's provisions specifically target crimes where children are present to enhance their protection.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The office serves as the state's coordinating body for public safety and advises the Governor on criminal justice strategies, including the creation of new criminal offenses.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new criminal offense and increases penalties, which the agency advises will increase caseloads for public defenders representing indigent defendants.
As the primary law enforcement agency for the state, the Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing the criminal laws codified or established by this bill.
As the chief legal officer, the OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and represents the state in criminal appeals, which may arise from prosecutions under this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill establishes the Housing Innovation Pilot Program and the Housing Innovation Fund within the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development to provide loans to local housing authorities and county governments for mixed-income, cross-subsidized housing projects.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Local housing authorities, county governments, private housing developers, and low-to-moderate income households.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general obligation bond expenditures increase by 10,000,000 in FY 2025 to capitalize the Housing Innovation Fund, with an additional 5,000,000 in annual funding authorized for FY 2026 through FY 2029. General fund expenditures for program administration are estimated at 170,500 in FY 2025, rising to 175,200 by FY 2029. Local jurisdictions will experience increased revenues and expenditures to the extent they receive and implement program loans.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Housing Innovation Pilot Program and Housing Innovation Fund within this department, making it the primary agency responsible for program administration, loan management, and project selection.
The bill explicitly requires the State Treasurer to hold the Housing Innovation Fund separately and invest its money in accordance with state guidelines.
The bill mandates that the Comptroller of Maryland account for the newly established Housing Innovation Fund.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is involved in the budget execution and planning for the General Obligation bond expenditures and potential future $5 million annual appropriations mentioned in the fiscal note.
The bill includes a priority for projects that commit to prevailing wage requirements, which are typically enforced and monitored by this department.
The board oversees the issuance of General Obligation bonds used to capitalize the fund and approves significant state contracts and property acquisitions related to housing projects.
The Governor oversees the executive branch departments implementing the program and has the authority to include appropriations for the fund in the annual budget.
The OLA will be responsible for conducting fiscal compliance audits of the new Housing Innovation Pilot Program and its associated fund to ensure financial accountability and legal compliance.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes an affordable housing land trust to establish a condominium regime on a leasehold estate it owns, a privilege previously restricted to the State, certain counties, municipal corporations, and the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Secretary of State and local land record offices
qa_results.stakeholders: Affordable housing land trusts, low-income and moderate-income families, and developers of affordable housing units.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances or operations. The financial impact on small businesses is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Secretary of State is the primary agency responsible for the registration and oversight of condominiums in Maryland. This bill directly amends the authority to establish condominium regimes.

DHCD is the state's lead agency for affordable housing and community revitalization. Since the bill enables affordable housing land trusts to establish condominium regimes, it directly impacts their mission and the projects they finance or support.

The bill requires the recording of declarations, bylaws, and plats among land records in the county where the property is located. The AOC provides operational support to the state's courts, which manage these land records.

MDP promotes sustainable growth and provides land use planning guidance. The expansion of condominium regime authority to affordable housing land trusts is a significant land use and housing policy development.

As the chief legal officer for the state, the OAG provides legal counsel on the interpretation and enforcement of real property laws and may be involved in consumer protection issues related to condominiums.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives, including those related to affordable housing and state-recognized land trusts.
As the principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors legislation for fiscal impacts on state agencies, although the impact of this specific bill is expected to be minimal.
The State Archives is the central depository for government records of permanent value, including land records which will be generated by the establishment of these new condominium regimes.
The Innovation Team focuses on enhancing economic mobility and reducing child poverty; affordable housing expansion is a key tool in addressing these priorities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) to maintain a website for voluntary donations to the Homeowner Protection Program and requires local property tax collectors to include an insert in property tax bills that describes the program and encourages donations to the Homeowner Protection Fund.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation and local tax collectors
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income, elderly, and disabled homeowners; Maryland taxpayers; and local government tax collection agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues will increase starting in FY 2025 due to voluntary donations to the Homeowner Protection Fund, though the specific amount is indeterminate. Local government expenditures will increase beginning in FY 2025 to produce and include the mandated informational inserts in property tax bills, with reported costs ranging from $3,200 to $70,000 annually per jurisdiction.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The department's mission involves ensuring state residents have access to safe, affordable, and secure housing. The Homeowner Protection Program (HPP) aligns with this core objective by preventing tax sale foreclosures. The department was also identified as a relevant information source for the fiscal analysis of the bill.
The bill mandates that the Governor include a specific annual appropriation of $750,000 for the Homeowner Protection Fund in the state budget for fiscal years 2023 through 2025.

As the state's chief financial officer and tax collector, the Comptroller is responsible for oversight of state revenues and financial integrity. This bill introduces a new revenue source for a state fund via voluntary donations on property tax bills.
The Treasurer is the principal custodian of state funds. This bill affects the Homeowner Protection Fund, which is a state fund established to finance the program.

The Homeowner Protection Program specifically provides assistance to elderly homeowners to prevent the loss of their homes to tax sale foreclosure, aligning with the department's mission to protect the rights and quality of life for older adults.

The department is responsible for developing the state's budget and overseeing budget execution. The bill includes mandated appropriations that must be managed and estimated within the state's fiscal framework.

The Homeowner Protection Program specifically assists disabled homeowners in avoiding tax sale foreclosure, which falls within the department's mandate to improve service delivery for residents with disabilities.
The department assists residents in economic need. The Homeowner Protection Program's focus on helping low-income homeowners avoid foreclosure overlaps with the department's social service mission.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for state agencies and provides counsel on the implementation of state laws, including the regulations governing the Homeowner Protection Program and tax sale processes.

The bill requires the State Department of Assessments and Taxation to maintain a website for electronic donations, a function that falls under the state's broader information technology and telecommunications strategy overseen by DoIT.
The judicial branch oversees the legal proceedings related to the foreclosure of the right of redemption in tax sales. The agency was identified as an information source in the fiscal note.
The Maryland Tax Court adjudicates disputes regarding state and local taxes, including assessments foundational to the tax bills and sale processes mentioned in the bill.
This agency provides administrative review for property tax assessment disputes, which directly impact the tax bills that will now be required to include HPP donation inserts.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland charter counties and certain code counties to include an assessment of fair housing in the housing element of their comprehensive plans. The goal is to ensure these counties are affirmatively furthering fair housing by taking meaningful actions to overcome patterns of segregation and address disparities in housing needs and access to opportunity.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Planning and the Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Maryland charter and code counties, local planning commissions, and populations seeking low-income or workforce housing.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill's requirements can be implemented using existing State resources, and there is no expected direct or material impact on local government finances. Specific dollar estimates for expenditures or revenues were not provided.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for land use planning in Maryland, the Department of Planning (MDP) requested this departmental bill. MDP is explicitly tasked by the bill to provide technical assistance to charter counties to help them develop the fair housing assessment within their comprehensive plans.

The bill defines 'Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing' and other terms by reference to the Housing and Community Development Article. Furthermore, DHCD is required to consult with the Department of Planning to provide technical assistance to counties regarding housing elements.

The Maryland Commission on Civil Rights (MCCR) enforces anti-discrimination laws in housing. The bill's core requirement—to affirmatively further fair housing and overcome patterns of segregation—is central to the agency's mission and policy objectives regarding fair housing.

This multi-agency initiative, managed by the Department of Planning, coordinates land use, revitalization, and planning visions. The bill's changes to the Land Use Article regarding comprehensive county plans fall under the scope of this initiative's coordination efforts.

The requirement to affirmatively further fair housing involves addressing barriers for protected classes, including people with disabilities. The agency's focus on advancing accessible housing makes the bill's planning requirements relevant to their advocacy and coordination work.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Department of Planning and the Department of Housing and Community Development. The bill was a departmental request from the executive branch and aligns with the Governor's policy priorities regarding housing and urban development.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies like MDP and DHCD. They would be responsible for advising these departments on the legal standards and definitions of 'affirmatively furthering fair housing' when providing technical assistance to counties.

DBM oversees the fiscal and personnel resources of state agencies. While the fiscal note indicates the bill can be implemented with existing resources, DBM manages the budget for the technical assistance functions provided by MDP and DHCD.

OLA conducts fiscal and performance audits of executive agencies. They would be responsible for auditing MDP and DHCD to ensure they are fulfilling their statutory duties, such as providing requested technical assistance to counties and meeting planning standards.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames the Northern Snakehead fish as the "Chesapeake Channa" for official state purposes, including commercial licensing. It requires the Department of Natural Resources to adopt regulations by January 1, 2025, to establish this name and update all departmental publications and materials to use the new name.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Commercial fishers, the seafood industry, and recreational anglers targeting the Northern Snakehead.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is expected to have no state or local fiscal impact, as the Department of Natural Resources can implement the name changes and regulatory updates using existing resources. There is a potential meaningful impact on small businesses in the commercial fishing and seafood industries if the renaming leads to improved marketing and increased harvesting of the fish.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary implementing agency, DNR is specifically required by the bill to adopt regulations establishing the new common name and to update all applicable published materials and licenses.

MDA supports the marketing of Maryland seafood products; renaming the northern snakehead to a more marketable common name like 'Chesapeake Channa' directly impacts their seafood promotion and quality assurance efforts.

The bill's intended effect of increasing the marketing and harvesting of this species impacts the state's seafood industry and economic development, which falls under the purview of the Department of Commerce.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the Department of Natural Resources, and the Governor signed the bill into law.
Changes to the common names of local species used in culinary and commercial contexts can impact the state's tourism branding and promotion of Maryland's 'Visit Maryland' seafood identity.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland State Highway Administration (SHA) to update its pollinator habitat plan to include specific policies for using pollinator-friendly native plants, developing habitat areas along State highways, and conducting public outreach and education. The SHA must also monitor and apply for federal funding related to pollinator habitats and invasive species control, and provide annual updates to its online habitat plan, including habitat locations and sizes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Highway Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland drivers, bicyclists, pedestrians, community associations, and the pollinator population.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) expenditures are estimated to increase by less than 50,000 annually, with approximately 35,000 projected for fiscal 2025 for informational signage and public events. There is a potential increase in federal fund revenues if the SHA receives federal grants for pollinator habitats and invasive species control.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill is primarily focused on the State Highway Administration (SHA), requiring it to update its pollinator habitat plan, establish new policies for native plants and public outreach, and track and apply for federal funding related to pollinator habitats.

SHA is a modal administration within the Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT). MDOT oversees SHA operations and budget, and is identified as a primary information source for the fiscal analysis of this bill.

The bill amends the Agriculture Article of the Maryland Code. SHA is required to consult with the Department of Agriculture on its pollinator habitat plan, which must be consistent with the Department's managed pollinator protection plan.

DNR is explicitly named in the statute being amended (Section 2-1801) as one of the agencies required to establish and implement a pollinator habitat plan in consultation with the Department of Agriculture.
The Maryland Environmental Service (MES) is explicitly named in the statute being amended as one of the agencies required to establish and implement a pollinator habitat plan.

The statute specifies that the Secretary of Health has the authority to determine if certain pesticides are necessary to respond to public health threats, which would exempt them from the restrictions in pollinator habitat areas.

The bill mandates that SHA track, evaluate, and apply for federal funding for pollinator habitats, such as those under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021. The Governor's Grants Office assists state agencies in identifying and acquiring federal grant funding.

This multi-agency initiative coordinates sustainable development and environmental protection efforts across departments like SHA, DNR, and MDA, aligning with the bill's goals for habitat restoration and native plant use.

DBM oversees the state budget and personnel resources. The fiscal note indicates that SHA will incur minimal annual expenditures from the Transportation Trust Fund to implement the bill's signage and outreach requirements.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments responsible for implementing this bill and coordinates policy initiatives related to the environment and transportation.

As the primary agency for environmental protection and restoration, MDE is involved in the broader context of habitat conservation and was listed as an information source during the fiscal review process.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like SHA, MDA, and DNR, and would assist in ensuring the updated pollinator habitat plan complies with state and federal legal standards.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires all qualified health plans offered through the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange to include pediatric dental benefits as an essential health benefit. It repeals a previous exception that allowed health plans to exclude these benefits if stand-alone dental plans were available, and it removes the Exchange's authority to separately mandate pediatric dental coverage for children since it will now be included in all qualified health plans by default.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Health Benefit Exchange and Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Children and families enrolling in health insurance through the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange, and health insurance carriers offering plans on the Exchange.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note states that the bill does not substantively change State activities or operations and has no local fiscal impact. While the bill mandates pediatric dental coverage, the Maryland Insurance Administration notes that carriers currently include these benefits voluntarily, making the bill a consumer protection measure to prevent future exclusion rather than a source of new costs or revenue changes.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the requirements for Qualified Health Plans (QHPs) certified by the Exchange, specifically mandating the inclusion of pediatric dental benefits and altering the Exchange's authority regarding dental plan enrollment.

MIA is the primary regulator of the insurance market in Maryland and requested this departmental bill to ensure consumer protection and consistency in the coverage provided by health insurance carriers.

MDH oversees state health policy and access to care; as the parent agency for several health entities and an information source for the fiscal note, it has an interest in changes to essential health benefits.

As an independent regulatory agency within MDH, the MHCC is responsible for health system planning and improving access to quality care, which is impacted by changes to essential health benefit requirements.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange and the Maryland Insurance Administration and would be responsible for advising on the implementation of these statutory changes.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinated the departmental request for this legislation, which was signed into law by the Governor.
DBM is the principal fiscal agency for the state and was consulted as an information source to determine the fiscal impact of the legislation on state operations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the types of financial assistance available through the Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account (SMWOBA) by permitting eligible fund managers to provide grants of up to $10,000 to businesses, provided the grant is issued in conjunction with a loan. It also clarifies the authority for fund managers to provide grants or convert existing loans into grants for businesses in areas under declared federal or state disasters or emergencies, with certain limitations on award amounts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Commerce
qa_results.stakeholders: Small, minority, women-owned, and veteran-owned businesses; clean energy industry businesses; and eligible fund managers including county development corporations and private firms.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Commerce can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not affect State revenues into or expenditures from the Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account (SMWOBA) because it only changes the allowable uses of existing funds derived from video lottery terminal proceeds. Local economic development entities serving as fund managers may see increased activity and administrative costs, and potentially lower future revenue from loan repayments since grants do not require repayment. The bill maintains an aggregate limit of $10,000,000 per fiscal year for financial assistance provided in disaster or emergency areas.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Commerce is the primary agency responsible for the Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account (SMWOBA). This bill directly alters the types of financial assistance the department's fund managers can provide and the reporting/administrative procedures they must follow.

The bill explicitly mandates that the Office of Legislative Audits must audit the fund managers receiving grants from the account and the utilization of funds allocated to businesses in jurisdictions surrounding video lottery facilities.

The Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account receives funding from the Strategic Energy Investment Fund (SEIF), which is managed by the MEA. The bill specifies how these SEIF funds must be used for businesses in the clean energy industry.

The bill explicitly requires the Comptroller to account for the Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account and issue warrants to pay out money from the account based on Department of Commerce transmittals.

The bill explicitly tasks the State Treasurer with investing and reinvesting the money in the Small, Minority, and Women-Owned Businesses Account and ensuring interest and earnings accrue to the account.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the agency responsible for monitoring and implementing socioeconomic programs for small, minority, and women-owned businesses, this bill's alterations to financial assistance for these groups align with their primary mission and oversight areas.

The Governor is responsible for declaring the states of emergency mentioned in the bill, which trigger the eligibility for certain grant conversions and increased financial assistance limits ($50,000) for impacted businesses.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill aligns Maryland health insurance law and regulations with existing federal requirements, including consumer protections under the federal No Surprises Act. It updates definitions for emergency medical conditions and services, clarifies prior authorization prohibitions, and permits the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange to establish expanded open enrollment periods and alternate coverage effective dates. Additionally, it adjusts the criteria for special enrollment periods in the Small Business Health Options Program (SHOP) Exchange and updates the definitions of grandfathered health plans to conform with federal standards.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration and the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange
qa_results.stakeholders: Health insurance carriers, health care providers (specifically emergency and behavioral health providers), insured individuals, and small businesses enrolled in the SHOP Exchange.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, any change in State activities resulting from the bill does not materially affect State finances. There is no impact on local government finances, and the Maryland Insurance Administration determined the bill has minimal or no economic impact on small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's primary health insurance regulator, the MIA is the requesting agency for this bill. It is responsible for adopting regulations that conform to federal law (NSA and ACA) and ensuring insurance carriers comply with the updated consumer protection and coverage standards.

The bill directly impacts MHBE by authorizing it to adopt expanded open enrollment periods and altering the material error triggers for special enrollment periods within the Small Business Health Options Program (SHOP) Exchange.

The bill amends the Health - General Article, which falls under the Department's purview. Specifically, it updates definitions of emergency medical conditions to include mental health and substance use disorders, aligning with the Department's oversight of behavioral health services and HMO regulations.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to the MIA, MHBE, and MDH, and is responsible for enforcing consumer protection laws, which are directly relevant to the health insurance and billing protections (such as the No Surprises Act) addressed in the bill.

The HSCRC regulates hospital rates and revenues in Maryland. The bill's alignment with the federal No Surprises Act and changes to emergency service definitions impact hospital billing practices and reimbursement, which intersect with the commission's management of the all-payer system.

The MHCC is responsible for health system planning and improving access. Updates to state health insurance law to conform with federal standards directly influence the healthcare landscape and access to emergency services that the commission monitors.

The bill's explicit inclusion of mental health and substance use disorders in the definition of an 'emergency medical condition' is relevant to the Department's mission to ensure inclusive health policy for residents with disabilities.

As a departmental bill requested by the MIA and approved by the Governor, the Executive Department oversees the coordination of these policy changes across the state agencies involved in health and insurance regulation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires local building permit offices to include an estimated cost of construction when submitting copies of issued building permits to the county supervisor of assessments. The information is intended to help the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT) more accurately value property improvements for tax purposes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Local building permit offices and the State Department of Assessments and Taxation (SDAT)
qa_results.stakeholders: Local government permitting offices, the State Department of Assessments and Taxation, and Maryland property owners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no state fiscal impact as it does not significantly affect the operations or finances of the State Department of Assessments and Taxation. The local fiscal impact is expected to be minimal, as many jurisdictions already provide construction cost data or can do so within existing resources; however, some counties like Montgomery indicate the impact on workload is currently unknown.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Property Tax Assessment Appeals Board provides the second level of administrative review for property valuations performed by county supervisors of assessments. Since this bill changes the data required for those assessments (estimated construction costs), it directly affects the valuation decisions the board is tasked with reviewing.

The Maryland Tax Court is the highest level of administrative review for property tax disputes. Changes in the statutory data used to establish property assessments (building permit cost estimates) impact the factual evidence and valuations adjudicated by the court.

This is a departmental bill from the State Department of Assessments and Taxation, an executive agency. The Governor's Office oversees policy initiatives for the executive branch and is responsible for signing the legislation into law.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions, including the Property Tax Assessment Appeals Board and the agencies responsible for tax assessment policy, to ensure compliance with the Tax - Property Article.

The Department of Budget and Management estimates state revenues and conducts fiscal planning. Property tax assessments affect the calculation of state property tax revenue and are a factor in wealth-based formulas for state education aid.

The Department of Planning provides data analysis and land use guidance to local governments. More uniform and accurate data regarding building permits and estimated construction costs improves the agency's ability to track development trends and infrastructure needs.

The Office of Legislative Audits conducts fiscal compliance audits of state agencies. This bill adds a specific statutory requirement for the submission of estimated construction costs, which would be subject to audit verification.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes consumer protections for property owners following loss or damage. It prohibits public adjusters from soliciting clients within 24 hours of an insurance loss or between the hours of 8:00 p.m. and 8:00 a.m. The bill extends the contract cancellation period from 3 business days to 10 business days (or 7 business days for individuals aged 65 and older) and requires specific disclosure statements in public adjuster contracts. It also mandates that adjusters notify the Maryland Insurance Commissioner when entering a contract within 72 hours of a loss and expands laws regarding fraudulent insurance acts by contractors to cover all loss-producing events rather than only weather-related damages.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Public adjusters, insured property owners, and contractors providing home repair or remodeling services.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Insurance Administration can implement the bill using existing budgeted resources. There is no fiscal impact on local governments. Small business impacts are expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Insurance Administration is the primary agency responsible for enforcing the bill. The bill regulates public adjusters, alters contract requirements, and expands definitions of fraudulent insurance acts, which are duties directly within the MIA's regulatory and oversight jurisdiction. As a departmental bill initiated by MIA, they are the primary implementers.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to state agencies like the Maryland Insurance Administration and enforces state laws regarding consumer protection. Since the bill involves consumer protections against insurance fraud and regulating the practices of public adjusters, it falls within the OAG's functional scope and legal oversight.

The bill expands the definition of fraudulent insurance acts for contractors providing home repairs and remodeling. As the Department of Labor regulates various professions and industries—including home improvement contractors—it is relevant to their oversight of licensee conduct and professional standards.

The bill includes specific provisions regarding the rescission period for public adjuster contracts for individuals based on age, specifically those at least 65 years old. This age-based regulatory distinction directly aligns with the Department's mission to protect the rights and interests of Maryland's older adults.

The OAH provides a centralized panel for contested cases involving state agencies, including the Maryland Insurance Administration. Regulatory enforcement actions or licensing disputes involving public adjusters or contractors as a result of this bill would likely be adjudicated through OAH.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives. As a departmental bill that regulates the insurance industry and enhances consumer protections, the office provides high-level administrative oversight for the implementing agency and the policy's execution.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits the Mayor and City Council of Baltimore City from requiring off-street parking for developments that include residential use and are located within a 0.25-mile radius of specified mass transit stations, including MARC, Baltimore MTA Light Rail, Red Line, or Metro stations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Mayor and City Council of Baltimore City
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential developers, small businesses, and residents of Baltimore City living or working near mass transit stations.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not directly affect State or local government finances. It may, however, provide a meaningful financial benefit to developers and small businesses by reducing construction costs through the elimination of off-street parking requirements near transit hubs.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MDOT is the parent agency for the transit systems (MARC, MTA, and the Red Line) defined in the bill. The legislation is specifically tied to development within proximity to MDOT-managed transportation hubs, and the agency was a primary information source for the fiscal analysis.

The MTA is the specific division within MDOT that operates the Baltimore Light RailLink, Metro SubwayLink, and MARC Train service. The bill's parking prohibitions are directly triggered by proximity to MTA stations and the planned Red Line project managed by this agency.

The Department of Planning oversees sustainable growth and land use planning. This bill alters local land use authority in Baltimore City to align with transit-oriented development (TOD) goals, and the department was consulted for the fiscal note.

DHCD is responsible for community revitalization and supporting residential development. Reducing off-street parking requirements can lower construction costs for housing developments in transit-rich areas, which supports the agency's mission to enhance the economic vitality of Maryland's communities.

This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and reducing the state's carbon footprint. The bill promotes transit-oriented development and reduced car reliance, aligning with the initiative's 12 state planning visions for land use and transportation.

The Governor's Office oversees executive branch policy and coordinates initiatives related to the state's growth. As the bill addresses a significant policy change in urban planning and transit infrastructure (specifically the Red Line), it falls under the Governor's strategic administrative and policy oversight.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill restricts the Maryland Department of Health from providing Medicaid reimbursements to residential service agencies for personal assistance services unless the service provider is classified as an employee. This measure aims to prevent worker misclassification and ensure homecare workers receive legal protections such as workers' compensation and overtime pay.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential service agencies (RSAs), personal care aides (PCAs), and Medicaid participants receiving community long-term services and supports.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) anticipates no direct impact on state Medicaid expenditures or revenues, assuming residential service agencies transition to an all-employee model by the January 1, 2026, effective date. If agencies fail to comply, MDH may incur indeterminate administrative costs to disenroll and revalidate providers. There is no expected local fiscal impact. Small business agencies will face meaningful costs to transition independent contractors to employee status. For context, MDH reimbursed agencies $394,400,000 for these services in fiscal 2022.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) by restricting its ability to reimburse Residential Service Agencies (RSAs) for personal assistance services under Medicaid unless the services are provided by employees. MDH's Office of Health Care Quality (OHCQ) also licenses these agencies.

The bill centers on worker classification (employee vs. independent contractor). The Department of Labor oversees workforce development, labor laws, and employee protections, and was previously consulted on guidance documents regarding worker classification for RSAs.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and was specifically required by previous related legislation to produce guidance documents regarding the application of employee protection laws to homecare workers.

As the agency responsible for developing and supervising the state budget, DBM is relevant because the bill affects Medicaid reimbursement criteria, which involves significant state and federal funding allocations managed within the MDH budget.

The reclassification of workers from independent contractors to employees has significant tax implications, including payroll tax collection and withholding, which falls under the Comptroller's jurisdiction as the state's chief tax collector.

The bill may lead to administrative disputes or appeals if RSAs are disenrolled from Medicaid or face licensure issues due to non-compliance with the employee classification requirement, and the OAH adjudicates such contested cases.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill aligns Maryland law with federal law by repealing tobacco sales exceptions for active-duty military personnel under age 21. It also authorizes the Maryland Department of Health to reallocate unspent cancer grant funds between counties, transfers the responsibility for paying emergency medical treatment costs in child abuse cases to the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy, and consolidates child abuse research and reporting duties under the State Council on Child Abuse and Neglect.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Governor’s Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
qa_results.stakeholders: Tobacco retailers, active-duty military personnel aged 18 to 20, local health departments, and children and families affected by abuse or neglect.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State operations or finances. It shifts existing responsibility for emergency medical treatment payments for child abuse cases from the Maryland Department of Health to the Governor’s Office of Crime Prevention and Policy, which is already the current practice. At the local level, grant funding for cancer prevention programs may be reallocated between counties to address unmet needs using projected unspent funds.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency affected by this bill. It is responsible for the Tobacco Use Prevention and Cessation Program, the reallocation of local public health cancer grants, and currently pays for emergency medical treatment for abused children—a responsibility this bill transfers to another office.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill explicitly transfers the responsibility for paying emergency medical treatment charges for children in cases of abuse and neglect from MDH to the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy (GOCPP).

The bill involves child abuse and neglect procedures. Local departments of social services, which operate under the Department of Human Services, are tasked with determining medical assistance eligibility for treated children and coordinating with the State Council on Child Abuse and Neglect.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy. Additionally, the State Council on Child Abuse and Neglect reports and makes recommendations annually to the Governor.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This entry includes the State Council on Child Abuse and Neglect (SCCAN), whose duties and coordination requirements are expanded by this bill to include information from the Maryland CHAMP Initiative.
The Office of the Comptroller is specifically named as a collaborating partner with MDH and other agencies to develop ongoing enforcement strategies for tobacco distribution laws.

The bill repeals an exception that previously allowed active-duty military members under 21 to purchase tobacco products. As the senior military advisor and head of state military forces, the Adjutant General is relevant to policy changes affecting active-duty personnel.
This department assists active-duty service members and their families. The repeal of age-based tobacco exceptions for military personnel directly impacts the demographic they serve.

The bill alters criminal and local government laws regarding tobacco sales and child abuse reporting/payment protocols, which may impact the judicial branch's caseload and administrative processing of related infractions.

The bill requires the development of enforcement strategies for tobacco distribution laws in collaboration with local law enforcement, and the Maryland State Police is the primary state law enforcement agency.
DBM oversees the state budget and personnel. The bill's transfer of payment responsibilities between agencies and the reallocation of grant funds are significant fiscal management actions.
As the chief legal officer for the state, the OAG provides legal counsel to MDH, GOCPP, and the child welfare agencies implementing these statutory revisions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes counties and municipalities in Maryland to designate specific local highways where golf carts can be operated without motor vehicle registration. To operate on these designated roads, the speed limit must generally not exceed 30 miles per hour (or 35 miles per hour in Golden Beach Patuxent Knolls), the operator must have a valid driver’s license, and the golf cart must be equipped with approved lighting. This statewide authorization replaces several previous local laws that applied only to specific areas like Smith Island and Crisfield.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governing bodies of Maryland counties and municipalities
qa_results.stakeholders: Golf cart owners and operators, local governments, and residents of designated communities
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect State or local finances or operations. The impact on small businesses is estimated to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the parent department overseeing the state's transportation network and the Maryland Vehicle Law, MDOT is the primary agency responsible for the laws governing motor vehicle registration and highway use being amended by this bill.

The MVA is specifically responsible for motor vehicle registration, driver licensing, and vehicle equipment standards. The bill establishes registration exemptions for golf carts and mandates that they meet lighting standards and driver's license requirements overseen by the Administration.

As the state's primary law enforcement agency for motor vehicle laws and traffic safety, the Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing the rules of the road, including the new conditions and restrictions for operating golf carts on public highways.

The Judicial Branch, supported by the AOC, handles the adjudication of traffic violations and motor vehicle citations. Changes to highway operation laws and registration requirements directly impact the volume and type of cases processed by the state's courts.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates state policy initiatives. The Governor signed this legislation into law and oversees the agencies (MDOT, MSP) responsible for its implementation.

The bill re-enacts Section 13-402 of the Transportation Article, which contains the specific registration exemption for vehicles operated within terminals owned or leased by the Maryland Port Administration.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill creates an exception to the general prohibition on selling wild game specimens, allowing the sale, barter, or exchange of legally acquired mounted game birds (excluding migratory game birds) and game mammals if the original owner is deceased and the specimen is part of an estate being liquidated or distributed.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Personal representatives of decedent estates, heirs and legatees, and auctioneers who may handle estate liquidations.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not expected to materially affect State finances or local government finances. The Department of Natural Resources and the Department of Legislative Services both determined that the bill will have minimal or no economic impact on small businesses, although auctioneers may see a slight benefit from the ability to sell taxidermy mounts.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for managing wildlife and game birds/mammals, DNR requested this departmental bill and is responsible for enforcing prohibitions and exceptions related to the sale or barter of mounted wild specimens.

The bill creates a legal exception specifically for the liquidation and distribution of personal property within a decedent's estate, a process managed and recorded by the Register of Wills in each jurisdiction.
The AOC provides support to the Maryland Judicial Branch, which oversees probate and the estates of decedents laws referenced in the bill's application to liquidating personal property.
The Comptroller acts as the state's chief tax collector and was consulted regarding the bill, potentially concerning any tax implications of selling or bartering assets from an estate.
The Governor's Office oversees the Department of Natural Resources, which proposed this departmental legislation, and the Governor is responsible for signing the bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the Maryland Pediatric Cancer Fund to focus exclusively on pediatric cancer research and related administrative costs, removing the previous authorization for prevention and treatment grants. It establishes the Pediatric Cancer Research Commission to oversee the competitive grant process, develop application criteria, and conduct progress reviews. Additionally, the bill allows the Secretary of Health to use up to 5% of the fund for administration and promotional activities aimed at educating the scientific community and soliciting donations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Physicians, hospitals, laboratories, educational institutions, pediatric cancer researchers, pediatric cancer patients, and survivors in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State special fund expenditures for the Maryland Department of Health increase by $52,900 in fiscal 2025 to hire a part-time physician program manager to support the new commission. These costs are projected to rise to $69,000 by fiscal 2029. Administrative and promotional expenses are limited to 5% of the fund. While the bill does not mandate a new appropriation, the fund currently maintains a $5,000,000 balance from a prior fiscal 2023 appropriation. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Health (MDH) is the primary implementing agency. The Secretary of Health is responsible for administering the Maryland Pediatric Cancer Fund, adopting regulations for the grant program, providing staff for the new Commission, and serving as an ex officio member of the Pediatric Cancer Research Commission.
The Governor has the primary authority to appoint the members of the newly established Pediatric Cancer Research Commission, including scientists, physicians, survivors, and caregivers.

The bill specifically requires the appointment of multiple scientists and physicians from the University of Maryland (a USM institution) to the Pediatric Cancer Research Commission. Furthermore, USM institutions are eligible educational institutions that may apply for and receive grants from the Maryland Pediatric Cancer Fund.

The Office of Appointments will be responsible for recruiting, vetting, and recommending candidates to the Governor for the numerous professional and citizen positions on the Pediatric Cancer Research Commission.

DBM is responsible for supervising the budget execution of the Maryland Pediatric Cancer Fund and managing the personnel resources required to staff the new Commission, as noted in the fiscal impact assessment.
The OAG will provide legal counsel to the Department of Health and the Commission regarding the development of grant criteria, the adoption of regulations, and the administration of the fund.

The bill explicitly requires each member of the Pediatric Cancer Research Commission to disclose potential financial interests in grant applicants to the State Ethics Commission to ensure compliance with the Maryland Public Ethics Law.

The Treasurer's Office acts as the custodian for state funds, including the Maryland Pediatric Cancer Fund, and is responsible for the management and distribution of these monies as specified in the budget.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill provides paid parental bereavement leave to State employees in the Executive, Legislative, and Judicial branches, including temporary employees and staff at public institutions of higher education. It allows for up to 10 days of paid leave following the death of a child between 6 months and 27 years old, and up to 60 days of paid leave following a stillbirth or the death of an infant under 6 months old.
qa_results.responsible_party: Secretary of Budget and Management
qa_results.stakeholders: State employees in the Executive, Legislative, and Judicial branches, temporary State employees, and employees of public institutions of higher education.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State operations or finances. In limited circumstances, agency expenditures may increase minimally to provide overtime coverage for employees on leave. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives, including the implementation of new state employee benefits across the agencies under its jurisdiction.
As the agency responsible for paying state employees, the Comptroller's office must update its payroll and financial systems to accommodate the new paid parental bereavement leave category.
The State Treasurer's Office manages the distribution of state funds for employee benefits and provides fiscal oversight for significant state personnel expenditures.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies regarding the implementation and enforcement of the new parental bereavement leave regulations.
The Secretary of the Department of Budget and Management is explicitly mandated by the bill to adopt regulations governing the administration of parental bereavement leave for state employees.
As one of the state's largest employers, the Maryland Department of Health is directly impacted by the bill's requirements and was a primary source of information for the fiscal analysis.
The Department of Transportation operates an independent personnel system and is a major employer that must implement these specific leave provisions for its large, multi-modal workforce.
The Administrative Office of the Courts is the central personnel agency for the Maryland Judicial Branch, whose employees are specifically covered by the bill's requirements.
The Commission serves as the state's regulatory and coordinating board for the public institutions of higher education that are mandated to provide this leave to their employees.
As an independent public institution of higher education specifically covered by the bill, Morgan State University must implement and manage the new paid leave requirements for its staff.
The Office of Legislative Audits is a legislative branch agency that ensures financial accountability and compliance with state laws, including the new personnel regulations established by this bill.
The Board oversees labor relations and collective bargaining for state and higher education employees, making it responsible for adjudicating potential disputes related to the new leave category.
As a public honors college and independent unit of state government, Saint Mary's College is a covered employer required to provide the mandated parental bereavement leave.
The University System of Maryland is the primary coordinating and personnel-setting body for its constituent public institutions of higher education, all of which are covered by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows residential property to be transferred from a decedent's estate to an heir or legatee without the immediate full payment of taxes, provided the recipient enters into a payment plan. It mandates the State Tax Sale Ombudsman to contract with a vendor for an installment payment program that allows homeowners to pay taxes in advance, currently, or in arrears. Additionally, it authorizes local governments to establish their own installment payment programs for residential property taxes in arrears.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Tax Sale Ombudsman (within the State Department of Assessments and Taxation)
qa_results.stakeholders: Homeowners, heirs and legatees of decedents, grantees of land with tax payment plans, local tax collectors, and the State Department of Assessments and Taxation.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by at least $67,300 in FY 2025, reaching $89,900 by FY 2029, primarily for a program manager to oversee the new installment program. While the vendor contract is intended to be funded by participant fees, additional state general funds may be required. Local governments' total property tax revenue is not expected to be materially affected, though they may incur administrative costs or see changes in interest and penalty revenue if they choose to implement local installment programs.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the administrative probate process by facilitating the transfer of residential property from a decedent's estate to heirs or legatees through installment payment plans for taxes.

The Clerks of the Circuit Court, supported by the AOC, are responsible for recording deeds and must now implement new procedures to accept affidavits of payment plans instead of requiring full prepayment of taxes.
The Board must approve the significant state contract and expenditure required for the State Tax Sale Ombudsman to hire a vendor to operate the new installment payment program.
The bill has a notable fiscal impact requiring a new personnel position and ongoing general fund expenditures, which falls under DBM's budget execution and personnel management oversight.
As the central procurement authority, DGS is likely to oversee or assist in the required contracting process for the third-party vendor that will operate the installment payment program.

The bill assists family members and heirs, often within aging populations, to maintain home ownership and prevent tax sales of inherited property, aligning with the department's housing advocacy mission.
The legislation acts as a housing stability measure to prevent displacement due to tax arrears, directly supporting the department's goal of ensuring residents have secure housing.
By providing a pathway to pay delinquent taxes and avoid tax sales, the bill helps prevent homelessness for vulnerable residents, a primary concern for DHS.

The Governor's Office provides high-level oversight of the executive branch agencies implementing the new program and coordinates the policy initiative to reduce child poverty and enhance economic mobility.
The bill supports the office's core mission of reducing child poverty by helping families maintain economic stability and retain their homes through flexible tax payment options.
The bill aligns with the team's priority to address child poverty and enhance economic mobility, potentially serving as one of the evidence-based strategies they pilot or support.
The OAG will provide legal counsel to the implementing agencies regarding the new transfer regulations and the review of the vendor contract for the installment program.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Maryland Commission on Veterans and Military Families to study and develop a statewide strategic plan to make Maryland the best state for veterans and military families, including identifying performance indicators and making legislative and budgetary recommendations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland veterans, military families, and military spouses
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by an estimated $74,237 in fiscal 2025 for the Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs to hire one contractual administrative officer to staff the commission and produce the required report. Expense reimbursements for commission members are assumed to be minimal and absorbable within existing resources. There is no impact on local government finances or small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for veterans and military families, the department is responsible for providing staff for the Commission, and its Secretary serves as the Chair. The department is also expected to incur fiscal costs for contractual support to implement the bill.
The Adjutant General is a statutory member of the Commission, providing senior military expertise to the study and recommendations.
The Commission is required by law to consult with the Office of Military and Federal Affairs and the Maryland Military Installation Council, both of which are units within the Department of Commerce.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes a new temporary body, the Commission on Veterans and Military Families, to address specific policy and quality-of-life issues.
The Governor appoints several members to the Commission and will receive the final report on findings and recommendations.
The Commission is tasked with making budgetary recommendations, and the bill involves general fund expenditures that DBM oversees.

The Commission includes a member representing the Maryland College Collaboration for Student Veterans Commission, which relates to the Higher Education Commission's oversight of postsecondary services for veterans.
This office assists the Governor in fulfilling the statutory responsibility of appointing diverse representatives to this new state commission.
The Commission is charged with identifying key performance indicators (KPIs) to assess quality of life, aligning with GOPI's mission of data-driven performance management.

The Commission's recommendations for making Maryland a top state for veterans and military families involves workforce development, and the bill requires a member with specific expertise in this area.
As the chief legal officer for the state, the OAG provides mandatory legal counsel to state agencies and commissions, including this new body.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the definition of 'disability' used for state rehabilitation services to align with federal standards. It replaces outdated terminology with a definition focused on physical or mental impairments that result in a substantial impediment to employment or independent living.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education (Division of Rehabilitation Services)
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals with disabilities seeking vocational or independent living rehabilitation services and the Maryland State Department of Education.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state or local fiscal impact. According to the fiscal note, altering the definition to conform with federal law is not anticipated to affect governmental operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Education Article regarding the definition of 'disability' for rehabilitation services. The Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS), which is responsible for these programs, is housed within this department.

As the principal agency for coordinating services for Marylanders with disabilities, this department is directly impacted by changes to the legal definition of disability used for rehabilitation and independent living services.

The updated definition relates to impairments that result in impediments to employment. The Department of Labor oversees workforce development and adult learning, which often intersects with vocational rehabilitation services.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office promotes the welfare of deaf and hard of hearing residents and coordinates policy. Changes to the definition of disability for rehabilitation services directly affect the population they represent and the services they advocate for.
The school provides transition services for students entering the workforce or postsecondary activities, often in coordination with the Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS).

The Department of Health manages developmental disabilities support and health policy. While rehabilitation services are under Education, there is significant overlap in the populations served and interagency coordination.
The department serves older adults with disabilities. Changes to the legal definition of disability for independent living services may affect the clients they support through Area Agencies on Aging.
DHS assists residents with disabilities who are in economic need. The definition of disability for vocational and independent living services is relevant to their client population.

The MLDS Center links student and workforce data from MSDE and the Department of Labor; a change in the statutory definition of disability may affect how data related to student outcomes and rehabilitation is categorized or analyzed.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including MSDE, and ensures that state laws correctly align with federal regulations such as the federal rehabilitation acts mentioned in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the timeframe for an investigation to be conducted and a written decision issued regarding complaints of discrimination or harassment filed by Maryland State Executive Branch employees or applicants from 30 days to 60 days. It also permits a further 30-day extension if the complainant provides written authorization.
qa_results.responsible_party: Heads of principal units within the Executive Branch and Equal Employment Officers
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State Executive Branch employees and applicants for employment
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no State or local fiscal impact as the changes are procedural. The Department of Budget and Management notes that many equal employment officers already request extensions for investigations, so the extended timeframe aligns with current practice.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the principal personnel agency for the State of Maryland, DBM oversees the state's employee resources and policies. The Secretary of Budget and Management is responsible for hearing appeals of the discrimination and harassment complaints affected by this bill, and the agency provided primary guidance on the procedural impact of the timeline changes.

MCCR is the state agency responsible for enforcing anti-discrimination laws. Because this bill directly alters the investigation procedures and timelines for harassment and discrimination complaints within state government, it aligns with MCCR's core mission and regulatory expertise.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, whose employees and applicants are the primary subjects of the bill. The legislation modifies the administrative duties of heads of principal units within the executive branch regarding personnel investigations.
