agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MARBIDCO is the primary agency responsible for implementing and administering both the Maryland Watermen’s Microloan Program and the Maryland Oyster Shucking House Loan Fund. The bill directly modifies their loan eligibility requirements, job definitions, and reporting protocols.

DNR manages Maryland's tidal fish licenses, which are used to determine eligibility for the programs. Additionally, DNR is responsible for receiving oyster shells returned for loan forgiveness and verifying the planting of spat-on-shell as part of the seafood processing loan terms.

This entry includes the Potomac River Fisheries Commission (PRFC). The bill specifically expands microloan eligibility to Maryland residents who hold commercial licenses issued by the PRFC, rather than only those holding licenses from the Maryland Department of Natural Resources.

MARBIDCO is established to support the agricultural and resource-based industries which are central to the Department of Agriculture's mission. MDA also serves as a technical and policy resource for issues regarding seafood quality and marketing related to these loans.

DBM is responsible for the state's budget execution, including managing the mandated annual general fund appropriations of $500,000 for the microloan program and the prior $1 million appropriation for the oyster shucking fund.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive agencies involved and is responsible for proposing the annual state budget, which must include the mandated funding for these specific loan programs through fiscal year 2026.

The bill requires certain loan applicants (generational watermen) to demonstrate eligibility through federal tax returns. The Comptroller is the state's principal tax authority and oversees financial reporting related to business entities in the state.

As the primary economic development agency for the state, Commerce has a broad interest in legislation that provides financial assistance and job creation incentives to Maryland's seafood processing and maritime industries.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows individuals under the supervision of the Maryland Division of Parole and Probation to earn compliance credits, which reduce their active supervision period, even if they have not paid their monthly supervision fees. Previously, being current on these fees was a requirement for earning the credits. Supervised individuals must still remain current on court-ordered restitution, fines, and other fees to be eligible for these credits.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, Division of Parole and Probation
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals on probation, parole, or mandatory release supervision (excluding those convicted of violent crimes, sex offenses, or certain drug offenses), the Division of Parole and Probation, and the Maryland Parole Commission.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's fiscal impact includes a likely reduction in the Division of Parole and Probation's (DPP) caseload and a potential, though speculative, decrease in State general fund revenue from supervision fees. By shortening supervision periods for eligible individuals, the bill may also decrease the volume of outstanding fees transferred to the Central Collection Unit for collection, thereby reducing its workload. Specific dollar estimates for revenue loss are unavailable as many affected individuals currently fail to pay the fees in full. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Department's Division of Parole and Probation (DPP), which is the primary entity responsible for supervising individuals and calculating earned compliance credits. The bill was introduced as a departmental request from DPSCS.

The fiscal note indicates that the Central Collection Unit (CCU) within DBM will see a reduction in the volume of outstanding supervision fees transferred to it for collection as a result of shortened supervisory periods.

The bill references fees imposed by courts under the Criminal Procedure Article. Changes to the duration of probation and the impact of fee payment on supervision status directly affect court caseloads and judicial oversight of supervised individuals.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill benefits indigent defendants represented by the Office of the Public Defender by ensuring that the inability to pay monthly supervision fees does not prolong their period of active state supervision.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, this office is relevant to the implementation of policy changes affecting the parole and probation system.
The OAG provides legal counsel to DPSCS and the Division of Parole and Probation and would be responsible for interpreting the statutory changes regarding credit accrual.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments involved (DPSCS and DBM) and signed the bill into law as part of broader criminal justice and poverty-reduction policy goals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes circuit court clerks to maintain military discharge records in electronic formats and modifies the conditions under which these records can be inspected or copied. It removes the requirement to show 'good cause' and the requirement that the requester be a relative to inspect records filed at least 70 years ago, provided Social Security numbers are redacted. It also removes the 'good cause' requirement for relatives seeking certified copies of records filed at least 70 years ago.
qa_results.responsible_party: Clerks of the Circuit Court
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the uniformed services, their families, and individuals seeking access to historical military discharge records.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Judiciary can handle the bill's requirements with existing resources. State revenues are not affected, and there is no material impact on local government operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the operations of the clerks of the circuit courts, who are supported and overseen by the Administrative Office of the Courts. It authorizes the use of electronic records for military discharge papers and mandates redaction of Social Security numbers.

This agency is responsible for assisting veterans in securing benefits, which requires access to military discharge records (DD-214s). Changes to how these records are indexed, recorded, and inspected directly affect the documentation needed for veteran advocacy and support.

The Archives preserves permanent judicial and government records. The bill's provisions regarding the inspection of records after 70 years and the requirement to redact sensitive information (SSNs) align with archival standards and the long-term management of these historically significant records.

As the head of the Maryland Military Department, the Adjutant General oversees the state's military forces. The bill expands the types of service recognized (changing 'Armed Forces' to 'Uniformed Services') and regulates the handling of discharge papers for these personnel.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and the Judiciary regarding the interpretation of privacy laws and the Public Information Act, which are central to the bill's requirements for redaction and inspection of personal identifiers.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Nonoccupational Postexposure Prophylaxis (nPEP) Standing Order Program in Maryland, authorizing registered pharmacists to dispense nPEP medication without an individual prescription to patients who may have been exposed to HIV within the preceding 72 hours. The Maryland Department of Health is responsible for administering the program, establishing clinical and training guidelines, and adopting regulations to ensure access for specific populations, including victims of sexual assault and pediatric patients.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Pharmacists, healthcare providers, individuals potentially exposed to HIV through nonoccupational means, victims of sexual assault, pediatric patients, and Medicaid enrollees.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Health can implement the program with existing budgeted resources. General fund revenues may increase by an indeterminate amount if the Department collects administrative fees. While Medicaid expenditures (and federal matching funds) may increase due to higher utilization of nPEP drugs, these costs are expected to be offset by savings from the prevention of HIV infections. There is no reported impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MDH is the primary agency responsible for establishing and administering the nPEP Standing Order Program, adopting necessary regulations, establishing training guidelines, and collecting program data.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOCPP administers the Program for Preventing HIV Infection for Rape Victims; the bill specifically coordinates with this program regarding fee exemptions and regulatory protections for victims of sexual assault.
The OAG will provide legal counsel to the Department of Health regarding the implementation of the standing order program and the drafting of the required regulations.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments (MDH and GOCPP) and coordinates the policy initiatives related to public health and crime victim support addressed in the bill.
The Comptroller oversees the collection of state revenues, including the administrative fees MDH is authorized to collect for the program which will increase general fund revenues.
DBM oversees the fiscal execution and personnel resources required for MDH to implement the new program within its existing budget.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland local jurisdictions with a population of at least 150,000 to submit an annual report to the Department of Housing and Community Development and the Maryland Department of Planning regarding building and development permit applications that include a residential housing component. The report must include data such as the number of applications received and approved, mean and median processing times, and information on any expedited permit processes used to accelerate housing development.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development and the Maryland Department of Planning
qa_results.stakeholders: Local governments with at least 150,000 residents, residential housing developers, and state housing and planning officials.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State agencies can implement the bill using existing resources. However, local government expenditures may increase for jurisdictions required to track and report this data; for example, Charles County estimates a one-time programming cost of $10,000 to update its software for these reporting requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires local jurisdictions with at least 150,000 residents to submit an annual report containing detailed building and development permit data to the Maryland Department of Planning (MDP).

The bill explicitly requires local jurisdictions with at least 150,000 residents to submit an annual report regarding housing development permit applications and residential unit approvals to the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD).

The bill requires jurisdictions to report performance metrics such as mean and median processing times and standard deviations for permit applications, data that aligns with this office's mission to enhance government efficiency and responsiveness using data-driven strategies.

This multi-agency initiative coordinates land use and sustainable development efforts; the data collected under this bill regarding development patterns and planning visions directly supports the initiative's oversight of state planning goals.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (MDP and DHCD) responsible for receiving these reports and coordinates the policy initiatives related to housing and community development.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like MDP and DHCD and may be required to provide guidance on the implementation of these new reporting requirements and the interpretation of the Land Use Article.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits employers from taking or threatening to take adverse actions, such as discharge or demotion, against employees who inquire about their rights, file complaints, or participate in investigations related to state employment standards, including wage and hour, equal pay, and living wage laws. It authorizes the Commissioner of Labor and Industry to investigate violations, mediate disputes, issue orders for the recovery of lost wages and economic damages, and assess civil penalties of up to $1,000 per noncompliant employee.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor, Commissioner of Labor and Industry
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland employees, private and public sector employers, and the Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $225,000 in FY 2025 for the Maryland Department of Labor to hire enforcement staff (one civil rights officer, one investigator, and one part-time assistant Attorney General) and update tracking databases. Ongoing annual expenditures are estimated between $209,300 and $237,000 through FY 2029. State general fund revenues may increase minimally from civil penalties of up to $1,000 for employer violations or administrative penalties for groundless complaints.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Commissioner of Labor and Industry, located within this department, is the primary official responsible for investigating complaints, conducting mediation, issuing orders, and assessing civil penalties for violations of the bill's anti-retaliation provisions.

The office is authorized to bring civil actions in court on behalf of employees when an employer fails to comply with an order issued by the Commissioner of Labor and Industry, and it provides legal staffing to support enforcement.

The agency is responsible for conducting administrative hearings when an employer contests an order or penalty issued by the Commissioner of Labor and Industry under the contested case provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act.

The judiciary will handle civil actions brought by the Commissioner, the Attorney General, or employees to enforce compliance with orders, with the authority to award triple wages, punitive damages, and attorney's fees.

As the central agency for state personnel and fiscal planning, it is impacted by the budget allocations for new enforcement positions and oversees employment policies for state employees who are covered by these labor protections.

The board oversees the approval of state procurement contracts that must comply with the prevailing wage and living wage laws, which are subject to the bill's new enforcement and anti-retaliation standards.

As the state's central procurement authority, it manages contracts and public works projects that are subject to the wage and labor standards enforcement mechanisms established or modified by the bill.

The department manages extensive multi-modal infrastructure projects and contracts that are subject to prevailing wage requirements and the bill's associated anti-retaliation and enforcement provisions.
The agency is responsible for the collection of state revenues, including the civil and administrative penalties assessed against employers and individuals who violate the bill's provisions.
The office oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for implementing the bill and coordinates the statewide policy initiatives related to employment standards and workplace protection.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill provides civil immunity to administrative, educational, and support staff members of public, private, and parochial schools who intervene in student altercations or disturbances. The immunity applies to personal injury or property damage resulting from the intervention, provided the staff member acted in a reasonably prudent manner and their conduct was not grossly negligent, willful, wanton, or intentionally tortious.
qa_results.responsible_party: Public, private, and parochial school systems in Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: School administrative, educational, and support staff; students; and local boards of education.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not materially affect State or local finances or operations. There are no specific revenue or expenditure estimates provided as the bill is expected to have a negligible fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Department of Education oversees the state's public education system and local school systems. This bill directly impacts the legal protections and civil immunity for the administrative, educational, and support staff employed within these schools.

As an independent unit within the Department of Education, the Center is responsible for coordinating school safety approaches and training. The bill's provisions regarding intervention in student altercations and disturbances are central to school safety protocols and staff training.

The bill creates a specific civil immunity in the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, which directly affects how the judiciary handles personal injury and property damage claims involving school staff intervention.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Education. They would be responsible for interpreting the scope of this immunity and representing state-employed school staff in relevant civil litigation.

As a state agency providing public education, the staff at the Maryland School for the Deaf are directly covered by the immunity provisions for interventions in student disturbances acting in their official capacity.

The Treasurer's Office manages the state's insurance protection and risk management. Changes to civil liability for school staff, particularly those at state-operated schools, affect the state's potential fiscal exposure and insurance claims processing.

The Department operates secure residential facilities and educational programs for youth; staff in these settings who perform educational or administrative functions may be affected by the bill's immunity provisions when intervening in student disturbances.

qa_results.bill_summary: This legislation requires Maryland landlords who handle utility billing for their tenants to provide written notice in the lease and documentation of utility costs. It expands existing requirements for water and sewer services to include gas and electric services, with specific documentation rules based on whether a building has six or more units or five or fewer units.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland residential landlords
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential landlords and tenants in Maryland properties where utility bills are paid directly to the landlord instead of the utility service provider.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. Small business impacts for landlords are expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division enforces state laws regarding landlord-tenant relations. This bill adds specific requirements for utility bill disclosures in residential leases, which would be subject to OAG oversight and consumer complaint resolution.

The bill originally included mandates for the Office of Home Energy Programs (OHEP), which is housed within DHS. While specific administrative penalties and OHEP-specific mandates were amended out, the bill's transparency requirements for utility bills directly impact the population served by DHS energy assistance programs.

The Department of Housing and Community Development is the state's principal agency for housing policy. This legislation amends the Real Property Article to regulate how landlords interact with tenants regarding utility services, a core component of residential housing policy.

Changes to landlord-tenant law and the legal requirements for residential leases are adjudicated in the state's District Court system. The AOC must ensure the judiciary is prepared for potential litigation arising from these new statutory lease requirements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires that contracts for the initial sale or resale of condominium units include specific disclosures regarding the presence of asbestos. For a contract to be enforceable, it must now contain a statement of the seller's or unit owner's actual knowledge of asbestos on the site or in the unit, including its location and details regarding any past abatement. Additionally, the council of unit owners must disclose any health or building code violations related to asbestos in the resale certificate.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Secretary of State
qa_results.stakeholders: Condominium developers (vendors), current condominium unit owners, prospective condominium purchasers, and condominium councils of unit owners
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect State or local government operations or finances. The financial impact on small businesses is estimated to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Secretary of State is explicitly named in the bill as the agency responsible for the registration of public offering statements for condominiums. The bill requires that these statements be amended to include asbestos disclosures.

The Office of the Attorney General, particularly the Consumer Protection Division, is involved in enforcing disclosure laws in real estate transactions. The fiscal note also identifies the OAG as a source of information regarding the bill's impact.

The bill establishes the legal unenforceability of sales contracts that lack the required disclosures. This affects civil litigation and the adjudication of contract disputes within the Maryland court system.

The Maryland Department of the Environment is the state's primary agency for regulating hazardous materials, including asbestos. While the bill focuses on real estate disclosure, the underlying subject matter falls within MDE's regulatory expertise.

The Department of Housing and Community Development is the principal state agency for housing policy. This bill impacts the regulatory environment for condominium sales, a significant portion of the state's residential housing market.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the Public Employee Relations Act by defining 'interested employee organization,' adjusting the timeline for public employers to notify unions about new employees based on mandatory check-in events, and clarifying the investigative duties of deputy directors regarding unfair labor practice charges.
qa_results.responsible_party: Public Employee Relations Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Public employees (State, community college, and school board employees), public employers, and employee organizations (labor unions).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no state or local fiscal impact, as the changes are primarily procedural. The bill may have an operational impact on state agencies due to revised notice requirements for new employee check-ins, but no direct financial expenditure or revenue change is anticipated.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Employee Relations Board (PERB) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the Public Employee Relations Act (PERA). The bill directly alters its internal structure regarding deputy directors and modifies its procedures for investigating unfair labor practices and managing union elections.

The bill explicitly names the Office of Administrative Hearings (OAH) as a venue for hearings regarding unfair labor practice complaints. The fiscal note indicates that OAH will need to train its Administrative Law Judges on the substantive changes introduced by the bill.

As the state's principal personnel and fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for managing state employee resources. The fiscal note highlights that the bill's altered notice requirements for new employees may place a human resources burden on state agencies, which DBM oversees.

The bill clarifies the role of the deputy director primarily responsible for public school labor relations and affects the collective bargaining processes for local boards of education, which MSDE oversees and leads at the state level.

The bill affects the collective bargaining and labor relations framework for higher education employees. USM, as the state's primary public university system, is directly impacted by changes to how exclusive representatives access new employees and how labor disputes are investigated.

As an independent public university and state agency, Morgan State's labor relations and collective bargaining for its employees are governed by PERA. The bill's alterations to employer notice requirements and investigation processes directly affect its administration.

As an independent public honors college and state agency, its labor relations with faculty and staff fall under the jurisdiction of the higher education provisions of PERA, which are modified by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is a major state employer whose workforce (including correctional officers) is subject to collective bargaining under PERA. The procedural changes regarding employee notices and unfair labor practice investigations will impact its departmental operations.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Public Employee Relations Board and all other state agencies. It would be responsible for advising PERB on the legal interpretation of the new definition of 'interested employee organization' and the clarified investigative procedures.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments and appoints the leadership of agencies like PERB. The bill's requirement that deputy director salaries be provided in the state budget directly involves the Governor's role in proposing the annual budget.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes health occupations boards that do not already have statutory authority to adopt regulations for reciprocal licensure and certification. It applies to individuals licensed or certified in other states that offer similar reciprocity to Maryland licensees, provided the applicants meet equivalent requirements, remain in good standing, and pay required fees.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and various Health Occupations Boards
qa_results.stakeholders: Out-of-state health care professionals seeking Maryland licensure, Maryland health occupations boards, and Maryland residents requiring health care services.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill may lead to a minimal increase in State special fund expenditures for processing new applications. State special fund revenues are indeterminate; while reciprocity fees might be lower than standard out-of-state fees, the potential for a higher volume of applicants could offset any revenue loss. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency impacted, as the vast majority of health occupations boards are housed within this department. The bill was a departmental request by MDH to address health care workforce challenges through reciprocal licensure.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS is an independent state agency responsible for the licensing and regulation of emergency medical services (EMS) providers, which are health occupations. The bill's broad authorization for health occupations boards to adopt reciprocity regulations applies to the EMS Board managed by MIEMSS.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Governor and all state agencies, including the health occupations boards. It will be responsible for reviewing the legality of new reciprocity regulations adopted under this bill.

The Department of Budget and Management oversees state fiscal planning and budget execution. The bill has a potential fiscal impact on the special fund expenditures and revenues of the health occupations boards, which DBM monitors.

The Governor's Office coordinates state policy initiatives and proposed this departmental bill. The Governor also signed the bill into law and oversees the executive branch departments implementing the new reciprocity standards.

The Office of Administrative Hearings adjudicates contested cases from state agencies. If an individual is denied reciprocal licensure by a health occupations board, they may contest the decision through a hearing at OAH.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes individuals or entities who purchase tobacco tax stamps to receive a refund for stamps lost due to theft, provided they submit specific documentation including a police report and a notarized affidavit. It also establishes penalties for false claims and requires claimants to return refunds if the stolen property is recovered.
qa_results.responsible_party: Comptroller of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed tobacco wholesalers and the Comptroller of Maryland
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues will decrease by an indeterminate amount starting in fiscal 2024 due to the issuance of refunds for stolen tobacco tax stamps. The Comptroller’s Office can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the primary implementing agency for this bill. The agency is responsible for collecting tobacco taxes, administering tax stamps, processing refund claims, verifying the required documentation (police reports and affidavits), and potentially adopting new regulations to manage refunds for stamps lost due to theft.

As the highest level of administrative review for tax disputes, the Maryland Tax Court would be responsible for hearing appeals if the Comptroller denies a refund claim for stolen tobacco tax stamps or if disputes arise regarding the validity of the documentation or the assessment of fines for false claims.

The bill requires claimants to provide a police report with a detailed inventory of stolen property to receive a refund. As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police may be involved in generating these reports, particularly in cases of large-scale theft from transit trucks or wholesalers.

DBM is responsible for estimating state revenues and long-term fiscal planning. This bill is projected to decrease General Fund revenues by an indeterminate amount through new tax refunds, which DBM must account for in revenue forecasting and budget development.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Comptroller and would likely assist in drafting regulations authorized by the bill. Furthermore, the agency would be involved in prosecuting or providing legal support regarding the penalty provisions for false claims and perjury.

As the agency responsible for the management and protection of state funds, the Treasurer's Office is impacted by changes in the collection and distribution of tax revenues, including those resulting from tobacco tax stamp refunds.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, including the Comptroller's regulatory functions, and signed this bill into law. The office maintains a general interest in policy changes affecting state tax revenues and criminal penalties.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the required period for which a health care provider must retain medical records and laboratory or X-ray reports from 5 years to 7 years. For minor patients, records must be kept for 7 years after the patient reaches the age of majority. The bill also requires that notices of intent to destroy records be sent via email in addition to first-class mail and extends the window for patients or guardians to retrieve records to 60 days before the proposed destruction date.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Health care providers, patients, and parents or guardians of minor patients.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Health can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. Local health departments and small business health care providers may experience minimal expenditure increases related to the longer retention period of medical records if they do not utilize the destruction notification process. State revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly amends the Health - General Article, which is under the jurisdiction of the Maryland Department of Health (MDH). The Secretary of Health is specifically tasked with adopting regulations regarding the destruction of medical records. Additionally, MDH operates numerous hospitals and freestanding health facilities that must comply with the extended 7-year record retention and updated notice requirements.

As an independent regulatory agency within MDH responsible for health system planning and improving access to health services, the Commission is involved in policies concerning health data and hospital accountability, both of which are affected by the bill's changes to medical record retention.

The Maryland State Archives is responsible for overseeing the record retention and disposal schedules of all state agencies. State-operated healthcare entities (such as those under MDH, DJS, and DPSCS) will need to update their formal retention schedules to comply with the new 7-year statutory minimum established by this bill.

The Department of Juvenile Services operates secure residential facilities that provide health care to youth. The department must ensure its medical record-keeping practices comply with the bill's new retention periods, particularly the specific provisions for minor patients.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS provides medical and mental health services to individuals in correctional facilities. As an operator of these services, the department is a 'health care provider' as defined by the bill and must adhere to the new 7-year retention and electronic notice mandates.

The department oversees the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home, which is a 'related institution' and healthcare facility. This facility will be subject to the bill's requirements for retaining and notifying patients about the destruction of medical records.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Secretary of Health and the various health occupation boards (e.g., Board of Physicians, Board of Nursing) that are responsible for enforcing the regulatory requirements and administrative fines established in this bill.

Member institutions of the University System of Maryland operate student health centers and clinical programs that fall under the bill's definition of health care providers, requiring them to update their record retention and notification protocols.

Morgan State University operates its own student health center. As a state-run healthcare provider, it must comply with the extended retention periods and new email notification requirements for patient medical records.
St. Mary's College provides healthcare services to its students; therefore, its health center staff must follow the new state standards for the destruction and retrieval of medical records.

The school provides health and support services to its students. Because its student body is composed of minors, the school is directly impacted by the bill's extended retention requirements for the medical records of minor patients.

The Department of Aging advocates for the rights of older adults and oversees long-term care programs. The bill's impact on 'related institutions' (such as nursing homes) regarding the preservation and notification of health records is relevant to the department's oversight and advocacy mission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Charles County Board of Education to fill a board vacancy within 90 days, unless the vacancy occurs during an election year for that seat. It also mandates that the board livestream and maintain unedited archived video recordings of public interviews with vacancy applicants on its website.
qa_results.responsible_party: Charles County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Charles County Board of Education members, applicants for vacancies on the board, and residents of Charles County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no state fiscal impact. Locally, the Charles County Board of Education can implement the requirements for livestreaming and archiving video recordings of public meetings using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) oversees the state's 24 local school systems, including the Charles County Board of Education. This bill amends the Education Article of the Maryland Code to change the governance and vacancy procedures for this specific local board.

The Office of Legislative Audits (OLA) is responsible for conducting performance audits and evaluations of local school systems. This bill establishes new statutory requirements for vacancy filling and public meeting transparency that would be subject to OLA oversight during audits.

The Maryland State Archives oversees the management and preservation of records from state and local government entities. The bill mandates the creation and archiving of video recordings of public meetings for the Charles County Board of Education.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits health care providers from charging fees for providing copies of medical records to an attorney who is representing a patient for the purpose of filing a claim for or appealing a denial of Social Security disability income or Social Security benefits.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Attorneys representing patients, patients applying for Social Security disability benefits, health care providers, and local health departments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to have a material impact on State operations or finances. Local government revenues may decrease to the extent that local health departments currently charge fees for providing medical records for these purposes. Small business health care providers will experience a loss of revenue as they will be prohibited from charging fees for record retrieval, preparation, and per-page copying in these specific instances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for the administration of the Health - General Article and the regulation of health care providers. The bill directly impacts the fees these providers (including local health departments overseen by the agency) can charge for medical records, and the agency was a cited information source in the fiscal note.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and other state boards, and is responsible for interpreting and enforcing laws related to consumer protection and health record access.

The bill facilitates the filing of Social Security disability claims by prohibiting fees for medical records requested by a patient's attorney. This directly aligns with the department's mission to improve services and remove barriers for Maryland residents with disabilities.

The bill addresses records needed for Social Security benefit claims, which are critical for the older adults and caregivers supported by the Department of Aging. Easing access to these records supports the department's advocacy and quality of life goals.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Office of the Public Defender was identified as a primary stakeholder and information source in the fiscal note. The agency represents indigent clients who frequently need medical records for disability-related benefit claims.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office signed the bill into law and oversees the Department of Health, which is responsible for the implementation of the statute.

The Office of Administrative Hearings may be tasked with adjudicating administrative disputes arising from health care providers who fail to comply with the revised fee prohibitions for medical records.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill extends the application filing deadline for the homeowners' property tax credit to three years for individuals enrolled in the Homeowner Protection Program. It also allows homeowners aged 70 or older to qualify for the homestead property tax credit even if they failed to file an application in the prior year, provided they were eligible, and includes a temporary provision for retroactive credit calculations for seniors who apply by May 31, 2025.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Department of Assessments and Taxation
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland homeowners aged 70 and older, homeowners enrolled in the Homeowner Protection Program, the State Department of Assessments and Taxation, and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures will increase starting in fiscal 2025 to cover additional homeowners' property tax credits; for instance, the fiscal note estimates that 25 homeowners receiving a three-year retroactive credit would cost approximately 109500. Additionally, there will be a decrease in State revenues for the Annuity Bond Fund and a decrease in local property tax revenues due to the expanded eligibility and retroactive applications for the homestead property tax credit.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Comptroller pay homeowners the homeowners' property tax credit upon certification by the State Department of Assessments and Taxation.
The fiscal note indicates a decrease in revenues for the Annuity Bond Fund (ABF), which the Treasurer's Office manages to pay debt service on the state's general obligation bonds.
The bill is projected to increase general fund expenditures for tax credits and impact state revenues, requiring budget oversight and planning by DBM.
The bill specifically targets homeowners aged 70 and older by extending application deadlines and providing more favorable assessment calculations for property tax credits.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like SDAT, which will likely require guidance on implementing and interpreting the bill's requirement that certain provisions be 'interpreted broadly.'
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor oversees the implementing department and manages the overall state budget, which is impacted by the bill's fiscal changes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes requirements and procedures for the installation and use of electric vehicle (EV) recharging equipment within common ownership communities, including condominiums, homeowners associations, and cooperative housing corporations. It expands the definition of such equipment to include electric bicycles and requires the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) to update its website with related resources, contractor information, and a point of contact for assistance.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD)
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and governing bodies of common ownership communities (condominiums, cooperative housing corporations, and homeowners associations) and electrical contractors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $41,700 in fiscal 2025, rising to $52,500 by fiscal 2029. This funding supports a part-time development officer at the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) to handle website updates and inquiries. Local government finances are not significantly affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) maintain a common ownership community website with summaries of EV recharging equipment requirements, information on contractors and insurers, and a designated point of contact. The fiscal note identifies a direct fiscal impact on DHCD for hiring staff to fulfill these requirements.

The Office of the Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division is cited as a source of information for the bill's impact. The agency provides legal counsel on the Maryland Condominium Act and the Maryland Homeowners Association Act, both of which are amended by this bill.

The Maryland Energy Administration is a key information source for the legislation, as it oversees state energy policy and electric vehicle infrastructure programs. The bill's focus on expanding EV and electric bicycle recharging equipment installation in residential communities aligns with the agency's mission.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill expands the list of individuals exempt from identification card issuance fees to include those recently released from correctional facilities, youth in the care of the Department of Juvenile Services or Department of Human Services, homeless individuals, and those under the care of the Maryland Department of Health. Additionally, it extends the duration of certain learner's instructional permits from 180 days to one year and ensures that driver's licenses for federal contract employees and their dependents remain valid while they are working outside the state.
qa_results.responsible_party: Motor Vehicle Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Formerly incarcerated individuals, homeless individuals, youth in state care, federal contract employees working abroad, and learner's permit holders.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note states that the bill is not anticipated to materially affect Transportation Trust Fund finances or operations. While it waives a $1 fee for several categories of identification card applicants, the Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA) notes that the cost of collecting and accounting for this fee currently exceeds the revenue generated. System reprogramming can be managed with existing resources, and there is no fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency within MDOT responsible for issuing driver's licenses, instructional permits, and identification cards, the MVA must implement the new fee waivers, term extensions, and validity rules mandated by the bill.

The bill was a departmental request from MDOT. The agency oversees the MVA and is responsible for the overall administration of the state's transportation and vehicle laws modified by this legislation.
The bill specifically creates an identification card fee exemption for youth under the care of DJS, requiring the department to provide verification documentation to the MVA.
DHS is explicitly named as the agency responsible for approving programs and providing documentation for youth participants (ages 15-20) to qualify for identification card fee waivers.

The bill creates a fee waiver for individuals under the care of the Maryland Department of Health, necessitating MDH to provide the required documentation for these applicants to obtain identification cards.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill provides fee exemptions for individuals recently released from any correctional facility. DPSCS, as the manager of state correctional facilities, is directly involved in providing the necessary release documentation.

The bill establishes a fee waiver for homeless individuals. As the department managing community revitalization and homeless solutions, DHCD is a key stakeholder and was identified as a source of information for the fiscal impact on this population.

The bill extends the validity of driver's licenses for federal agency and armed forces contract employees and their dependents serving abroad, which directly impacts a major portion of the military family constituency supported by DVMF.

The Governor's Office oversees the various executive agencies tasked with implementing these fee waivers and regulatory changes, and coordinates the broader policy initiative to improve access to identification for vulnerable populations.

The Adjutant General oversees Maryland's military forces; the bill's expanded provisions for license validity during active service or federal contract work abroad are relevant to personnel under the Adjutant General's purview.

The OAG provides legal counsel to all state agencies, including MVA and MDOT, and will likely be involved in drafting the regulations required to establish the conditions for the documentation of fee waiver eligibility.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Comptroller to discontinue 20% of State aid for police, highway user revenues, and disparity grants for any county, municipality, or special taxing district that fails to submit its required annual audit report within two years of the statutory deadline. If the local jurisdiction does not submit the report within the same fiscal year the funds were discontinued, the withheld money reverts to the State's General Fund or the Transportation Trust Fund.
qa_results.responsible_party: Comptroller of Maryland and the Department of Legislative Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland counties, municipalities, and special taxing districts
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in a potential decrease in State General Fund and Transportation Trust Fund expenditures and a corresponding reduction in local government revenues if jurisdictions fail to submit audit reports. While the exact impact depends on local compliance, the Fiscal Note identifies over $1,500,000 in potential annual penalties based on currently delinquent jurisdictions. No fiscal impact is expected until FY 2027 due to the bill's two-year grace period and effective date.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Comptroller, upon notice from the Department of Legislative Services, must order the discontinuance of 20% of State aid (including police aid, highway user revenues, and disparity grants) for local jurisdictions that fail to file required audit reports.

The bill requires local governments to report audit results directly to the Legislative Auditor and tasks the Executive Director of the Department of Legislative Services (the parent agency of OLA) with notifying the Comptroller of non-compliance to trigger financial penalties.

The bill impacts the distribution of Highway User Revenues. If a local government fails to comply with audit requirements, discontinued highway user revenues revert to the Transportation Trust Fund (TTF), which is managed by MDOT.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for budget execution and oversight of the General Fund. The bill specifies that discontinued disparity grants (a form of state aid to local governments) revert to the General Fund.
The bill specifically targets 'State aid for police aid' as one of the categories of funding to be discontinued for non-compliant local jurisdictions.
The bill identifies the clerks of the court as one of the entities that distribute State aid subject to discontinuance if a local government fails to meet audit reporting deadlines.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the implementation of state laws. The Governor is also responsible for the annual state budget, which would be affected by the reversion of local aid to the General Fund.
The State Treasurer is responsible for the management and protection of state funds, including the distribution and investment of the funds impacted by the bill's discontinuance provisions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the application and payment processes for various professional licenses and certifications regulated by the Department of State Police, including private detectives, security guards, security systems technicians, special police officers, and railroad police officers. It transitions these processes from paper-based forms to electronic formats and manners designated by the Secretary of State Police to facilitate automated processing and online payments.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of State Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants and licensed agencies for private detectives, security guards, security systems technicians, railroad police officers, and special police officers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues are expected to decrease minimally, by approximately $9,500 annually, beginning in fiscal 2025 due to third-party vendor processing fees for online payments. The Department of State Police can implement the administrative changes with existing budgeted resources. There is no local government fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly relates to the Secretary of State Police and the Department of State Police's (DSP) Licensing Division. It authorizes the Secretary to determine the manner and format of applications for private detectives, security guards, security systems technicians, and railroad police, facilitating the department's transition to electronic and automated processing.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill references the Central Repository, which is housed within DPSCS. The bill updates requirements for fingerprint submissions (two sets in approved formats) and State and national criminal history records checks, which are processed through the Central Repository.

The bill's primary purpose is to allow the Department of State Police to move from paper-based forms to electronic 'manner and formats' for professional licensing and payments. DoIT oversees state IT strategy and infrastructure, which supports these digital transformation initiatives.

The bill mentions minors in apprenticeship programs approved by the Maryland Department of Labor regarding exemptions from criminal background check and fingerprint requirements for security systems technician registrations.

The fiscal note indicates a minimal decrease in general fund revenues (estimated at $9,500 annually) due to the department absorbing 3% vendor processing fees for online payments. DBM oversees the state's budget and revenue estimations.

The OAG serves as the legal counsel for the Maryland State Police. They would be involved in interpreting these statutory changes and ensuring the new application formats meet legal requirements for professional licensing.

The Governor is responsible for the final appointment or commissioning of Special Police and Railroad Police officers. While the bill impacts the preliminary application process handled by the State Police, it ultimately relates to the Governor's executive appointment authority.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, GOCPP has an interest in the efficiency of the background check and licensing process for security personnel and police officers.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows individuals or businesses that tow or remove vehicles from parking lots to provide the required notice to the vehicle's insurer of record through electronic means, rather than by certified and first-class mail, if both the towing entity and the insurer agree to electronic notification in writing or via electronic communication.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation
qa_results.stakeholders: Vehicle towing companies, insurance companies, vehicle owners, and secured parties.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not materially affect State or local government finances or operations. A formal fiscal note indicates that there are no material revenue or expenditure changes for state or local entities, with only a potential minimal impact on small business towing operations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the parent agency of the Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA), MDOT oversees the regulatory framework for vehicle services and transportation laws. The MVA is specifically charged with maintaining the database of insurer addresses used by towers for these notifications.

The MVA is responsible for maintaining a database of addresses for insurers of record and making it available to towing companies. This bill modifies how towers use that information to provide notice, specifically allowing for electronic delivery.

The MIA regulates the 'insurers of record' who are the recipients of these notifications. Any change in the statutory method of notification between towers and insurers falls under the regulatory purview of insurance industry practices and standards.

MAIF serves as the insurer of last resort for many Maryland drivers. As an insurer of record, they will be directly impacted by the transition to electronic notifications if they choose to agree to such methods with towing companies.

MSP is the primary law enforcement agency that oversees towing regulations and receives immediate reports of vehicle removals. While the bill focuses on insurer notification, MSP is involved in the broader regulatory oversight of the towing industry.

The OAG provides legal counsel to MDOT, MVA, and MIA. They would be responsible for advising these agencies on the legal requirements and validity of the electronic agreements and communications authorized by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Safe Sleep Act of 2024 requires Maryland hospitals to provide oral and written educational resources to parents or legal guardians of newborns before discharge. These resources must cover safe sleep environments, the prevention of shaken baby syndrome, and strategies for coping with the stress of caring for an infant. Hospitals must also inquire about the infant's planned sleep environment at home and provide a list of resources to families who cannot afford or access safe sleep items such as cribs or bassinets. The Maryland Department of Health is required to develop and maintain a county-by-county list of these resources.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and Maryland hospitals
qa_results.stakeholders: Newborn infants, parents or legal guardians of newborns, Maryland hospitals, and local health departments
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures will increase by $19,400 in fiscal 2025 to hire a part-time contractual health educator at the Maryland Department of Health to develop the required resource list. Local health departments and hospitals are expected to implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no estimated impact on State or local revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill. It must develop the county-by-county list of resources for safe sleep environments, consult with local health officers, and oversee hospital compliance with the new educational requirements. The fiscal note also specifically identifies MDH as the entity requiring general fund expenditures for contractual staff.

The Governor's Office for Children is dedicated to promoting the well-being of Maryland's children. This bill directly addresses infant health and safety (preventing SIDS and sleep-related deaths), aligning with the office's mission to improve access to health services and support historically underinvested communities.

The University System of Maryland includes large academic medical centers and hospitals that provide birthing services. As clinical providers, these institutions will be directly responsible for establishing the mandated educational processes, documentation, and resource distribution required by the bill.

The Department of Human Services provides aid to vulnerable families and individuals in economic need. Since the bill requires hospitals to provide resources for families who cannot afford cribs or bassinets, DHS may be involved in coordinating the delivery of these social services or managing programs that provide such essential items.

As an independent regulatory agency within MDH, the MHCC is responsible for health system planning and improving access to health care. The bill's requirements for hospital discharge protocols and public health education fall under the broad planning and quality improvement goals of the Commission.

The HSCRC regulates hospital rates and revenues. While the fiscal impact on hospitals is not estimated to be massive, the new mandate requires hospitals to implement administrative processes and maintain records, which falls under the regulatory purview of hospital operations and quality-of-care incentives overseen by the HSCRC.

The Innovation Team is charged with reducing child poverty and enhancing economic mobility. Reducing infant mortality through safe sleep environments is a critical component of improving long-term outcomes for families in underinvested communities, fitting the team's priority areas.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Maryland Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA) to issue special disabled veteran registration plates to veterans with a service-connected disability rating between 50% and 99%. It also specifies that while these veterans must pay standard registration fees and surcharges, they are exempt from certain replacement plate fees when transferring vehicle ownership to a spouse or joint owner who does not qualify for the special plates.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation's Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland veterans with a service-connected disability rating between 50% and 99% and their spouses or joint vehicle owners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is expected to have no material impact on State Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) revenues or expenditures. Newly eligible veterans are still required to pay standard registration fees and surcharges, and the MVA currently has a supply of plates available. System reprogramming costs can be managed with existing resources, and local government finances are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Motor Vehicle Administration is the primary implementing agency responsible for issuing the special disabled veteran registration plates, verifying disability eligibility through the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, and adopting necessary regulations.

As the parent agency of the Motor Vehicle Administration, MDOT oversees the implementation of vehicle laws and the management of the Transportation Trust Fund, which is the repository for the registration fees and surcharges collected under this bill.

The department is responsible for assisting veterans in securing state benefits and services. This bill creates a specific administrative benefit and form of recognition for veterans with a 50% to 99% service-connected disability rating.

The Department of Disabilities coordinates policies for residents with disabilities. This bill establishes criteria for specialized disability-related vehicle identification that distinguishes between service-connected status and the use of the international symbol of access.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS receives a significant portion of its funding through a $17.00 surcharge on vehicle registrations. The bill and accompanying fiscal note explicitly state that these special plates remain subject to this surcharge.

As the primary law enforcement agency for motor vehicle laws on state highways, the Maryland State Police must be trained to recognize and verify the validity of these new specialized registration plates during routine traffic operations.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Motor Vehicle Administration and will be responsible for reviewing the legality of any new regulations the MVA adopts to govern the issuance and transfer of these special plates.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the administration of the Summer Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) for children and the Pilot Program for Human Services Careers Scholarship. It allows the Department of Human Services (DHS) to use state funding to offset administrative costs of the federal Summer Electronic Benefit Transfer (EBT) program and alters scholarship requirements, specifically establishing a service obligation of 15 months for each academic year a scholarship is awarded.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Human Services and the Maryland Higher Education Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Children in low-income households receiving food supplement benefits, county governments, and students pursuing degrees to work in human services positions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill allows DHS to use state funds to cover administrative costs of the federal Summer EBT program. For fiscal 2025, the state budget includes $5.8 million in general funds and $5.8 million in federal funds for these administrative expenses. There is no material fiscal impact on local governments, as participation in the state's supplemental food program remains optional and requires local matching funds.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary state agency for food and cash assistance (SNAP) and the department where the 'targeted positions' for the Human Services Careers Scholarship are located, DHS is the lead implementing agency for the bill's changes to the food supplement program and the scholarship program.

MHEC is specifically tasked with administering the Pilot Program for Human Services Careers Scholarship, including entering into written agreements with recipients, determining repayment amounts for failure to fulfill service obligations, and managing scholarship reductions.

DBM oversees the state budget execution and fiscal planning. The bill alters the use of existing funds for administrative costs and removes specific mandatory funding language, which impacts the fiscal management of these human services and scholarship programs.
The bill's focus on improving food supplement benefits for children aligns directly with this agency's mission to reduce child poverty and address food insecurity in Maryland.

The food supplement programs addressed in the bill target children eligible for free and reduced-price meals, a program overseen by MSDE. Additionally, the scholarship program involves transitions from high school to higher education pathways.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments (DHS, MHEC) and is responsible for proposing the state budget that funds the Summer SNAP and scholarship programs.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including DHS and MHEC, and would be responsible for advising on the legal requirements of the new scholarship written agreements and administrative rule changes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the maximum hotel rental tax rate that Eastern Shore code counties are authorized to impose from 5% to 6%, provided there is unanimous consent from the county commissioners.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Eastern Shore code counties (Caroline, Kent, Queen Anne's, and Worcester)
qa_results.stakeholders: Hotel and lodging establishment owners, tourists and guests staying in Eastern Shore code counties, and local county and municipal governments in Caroline, Kent, Queen Anne's, and Worcester counties.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no impact on state finances. Local county revenues are estimated to increase by approximately $501,000 annually across the four affected counties (Caroline, Kent, Queen Anne’s, and Worcester). Additionally, municipal revenues are estimated to increase by approximately $5,400,000 annually, largely driven by Ocean City in Worcester County. These estimates assume each jurisdiction implements the maximum authorized 6% rate.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency is responsible for stimulating the state's tourism economy. The bill alters hotel rental tax rates in Eastern Shore counties, which directly impacts the costs for travelers and often affects the local tax revenues dedicated to tourism marketing and promotion.

As the parent agency of the Office of Tourism Development, the Department of Commerce oversees the state's economic strategy regarding tourism and enhances Maryland's competitive position, which is influenced by local hospitality tax policies.

While hotel rental taxes are generally administered at the local level, the Comptroller is the state's chief tax officer and provided information and data for the fiscal analysis of this tax-related legislation.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the Governor signed this bill into law; the office also coordinates policy initiatives related to economic development and local government relations.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Governor and the General Assembly regarding the legality and implementation of changes to the Local Government Article of the Annotated Code of Maryland.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires residential service agencies that provide personal care services under certain Medicaid home- and community-based programs to submit annual reports to the Maryland Department of Labor regarding the wage rates of personal care aides. It also mandates that the Maryland Department of Health report to the General Assembly on its plans to implement the federal Ensuring Access to Medicaid Services rule, specifically regarding the review of wage reports and Medicaid reimbursement rates.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor and Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential service agencies, personal care aides, and the Maryland Department of Health.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the Maryland Department of Labor and the Maryland Department of Health can implement the bill's reporting and administrative requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no projected impact on state revenues or local government finances. Small business residential service agencies may face a meaningful administrative impact due to the annual wage reporting mandate.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the administrator of the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid), the Department of Health is mandated to report on the federal Ensuring Access to Medicaid Services rule and develop plans to operationalize it, which directly affects program reimbursements and service delivery.

The bill explicitly requires residential service agencies to submit annual reports documenting wage rates for personal care aides to the Department of Labor, making it the primary agency responsible for collecting and managing this data.

The bill impacts home- and community-based services such as the Community Options waiver, which are primary programs utilized by the older adults that the Department of Aging is tasked with protecting and supporting.
The legislation addresses personal care services and Medicaid programs used by residents with disabilities; the department coordinates services and policy for this population across state agencies.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (Health and Labor) tasked with implementing the bill and is responsible for broader policy initiatives related to Medicaid and workforce wages.
DBM oversees the personnel and fiscal resources for the implementing agencies and monitors the long-term fiscal planning related to Medicaid reimbursement rates and administrative costs.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and the Department of Labor regarding the interpretation of the new state reporting requirements and federal Medicaid regulations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Anne Arundel County and its municipalities to decrease the maximum speed limit to no less than 15 miles per hour on highways after performing an engineering and traffic investigation. It also prohibits the implementation of new speed monitoring systems to enforce speed limits on highway segments where the limit has been reduced under this authority.
qa_results.responsible_party: Anne Arundel County and municipalities within Anne Arundel County
qa_results.stakeholders: Motorists, residents, and local government authorities in Anne Arundel County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues may increase minimally from additional speeding citations. Local expenditures in Anne Arundel County and its municipalities will increase to conduct necessary engineering and traffic investigations. Local revenues may increase from existing speed cameras on roads where limits are reduced, though new speed monitoring systems are prohibited on those segments. Specific dollar estimates are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: SHA is the primary state entity responsible for approving any alterations to speed limits on state highways initiated by local authorities in Anne Arundel County, as required by existing Maryland law cited in the fiscal note.

As the parent department of the State Highway Administration and the Motor Vehicle Administration, MDOT oversees the state's transportation network and highway safety initiatives impacted by changes to speed limit authority.
The bill is expected to increase the volume of traffic citations, which directly impacts the caseload and administrative support requirements for the District Courts managed by the AOC.

As the primary agency for enforcing motor vehicle and criminal laws on state highways, the Maryland State Police will be responsible for enforcing the newly established speed limits in the affected jurisdictions.

The Comptroller is the recipient of excess revenue generated by local speed monitoring systems; the bill's prohibition on new systems on modified highway segments impacts potential state revenue flows.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Purple Alert Program to facilitate the rapid dissemination of information to assist in locating missing persons with cognitive impairments, mental disorders, intellectual or developmental disabilities, or brain injuries, as well as individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing, whose disappearance poses a threat to their safety and who do not qualify for a Silver Alert.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of State Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals with cognitive impairments, mental disorders, intellectual or developmental disabilities, or brain injuries; individuals who are deaf, deafblind, hard of hearing, or late-deafened; caregivers; and state and local law enforcement agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the requirements of the bill can be handled within the existing budgeted resources of the Maryland Department of State Police, the State Highway Administration, the Maryland State Department of Education, and local law enforcement agencies. There is no projected impact on state or local revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of State Police is the primary agency responsible for establishing and administering the Purple Alert Program, including adopting guidelines, developing procedures, providing training to local law enforcement, and coordinating with other state entities.

The State Highway Administration, a modal administration of MDOT, is explicitly required to consult with the Department of State Police to establish a plan for providing Purple Alert information via the dynamic message sign system.

MSDE is explicitly required to consult with the Department of State Police to develop a program allowing high school students to assist in searches for missing persons as part of student service-learning guidelines.

The bill establishes an alert system specifically for individuals with intellectual or developmental disabilities and brain injuries; as the principal agency for disability services and policy, this program directly impacts their constituent population and mandate.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill expands the definition of 'missing person' to include individuals who are deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing, making this agency a key stakeholder for policy development and community outreach for the program.

The bill utilizes definitions of 'developmental disability,' 'intellectual disability,' and 'mental disorder' from the Health - General Article, areas overseen by MDH's Behavioral Health and Developmental Disabilities Administrations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies, this agency oversees policy development and fund allocation that may intersect with statewide missing person alert programs.

The bill requires the Department of State Police to recruit public and commercial television and radio broadcasters to assist in implementing Purple Alerts; as the state's public broadcaster, MPT would be a primary partner in this effort.
The bill specifically includes deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals in the alert criteria; students and families served by MSD are directly impacted by the creation of this safety resource.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Secretary of Labor to exempt furloughed federal civilian employees from work search requirements for unemployment insurance benefits during a federal government shutdown, provided they are able and available for work and remain work-attached.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
