As a principal department, it is mandated to submit a catalog of its permits and certificates to the Governor and publish processing timelines.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a principal department, DPSCS is required to comply with the cataloging and reporting requirements for any licenses or certificates it issues.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a principal department, this agency must fulfill the cataloging and website reporting mandates established by the bill.
As a principal department, it must catalog any certificates or benefits-related permits it issues and provide the required processing time transparency.

As a principal department, the OAG must catalog its relevant certifications; it also serves as the primary legal counsel advising all other agencies on compliance with these new statutory requirements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill, known as the Pava LaPere Legacy of Innovation Act of 2024, establishes the Pava LaPere Innovation Acceleration Grant Program and the Baltimore Innovation Initiative Pilot Program within the Maryland Technology Development Corporation (TEDCO). These programs provide financial support and technical assistance to technology start-up companies founded by students or faculty at postsecondary institutions in the Baltimore-Columbia-Towson metropolitan area. The bill mandates $1.5 million for the pilot program in fiscal years 2025 and 2026 and repeals a $1 million annual mandate for the Business Telework Assistance Grant Program.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Technology Development Corporation (TEDCO)
qa_results.stakeholders: Technology-based start-up companies, university students and faculty in the Baltimore-Columbia-Towson MSA, and participating public and private higher education institutions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, net State general fund expenditures will increase by $1,000,000 annually from FY 2025 through FY 2027, but decrease by $500,000 annually starting in FY 2028. This impact includes a mandated $1,500,000 appropriation for the Baltimore Innovation Initiative Pilot Program in FY 2025 and FY 2026, an estimated $500,000 annual appropriation for the Pava LaPere Innovation Acceleration Grant Program, and a $1,000,000 annual expenditure reduction from the repeal of the Business Telework Assistance Grant Program mandate. Participating universities must also contribute up to $50,000 annually to the pilot program.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes two new grant programs—the Pava LaPere Innovation Acceleration Grant Program and the Baltimore Innovation Initiative Pilot Program—directly within TEDCO and its Maryland Innovation Initiative (MII), charging the agency with their administration and regulation.
The bill repeals the $1,000,000 mandated annual appropriation for the Business Telework Assistance Grant Program, which is currently administered by the Department of Commerce.

Morgan State University is specifically listed as an 'eligible university' for the Baltimore Innovation Initiative Pilot Program and is required to pay an annual contribution of up to $50,000 to participate.

Several constituent institutions of the University System of Maryland (including Coppin State, Towson, University of Baltimore, UMB, and UMBC) are specifically named as eligible universities that must provide annual financial contributions to participate in the pilot program.
The bill requires specific mandated appropriations for the new programs and repeals an existing mandate, directly impacting the state budget and fiscal planning managed by DBM.
The Governor is required by the bill to include specific appropriations for these entrepreneurial programs in the annual state budget bill.

The bill establishes innovation programs centered on postsecondary institutions and their students/faculty; as the state's coordinating board for higher education, the Commission oversees the policies and standards of the institutions involved.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Center for Firearm Violence Prevention and Intervention within the Maryland Department of Health. The Center is tasked with reducing firearm violence and misuse through a public health approach, collaborating with federal, state, and local agencies, and developing a comprehensive State Strategic Plan for firearm violence reduction.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents, communities disproportionately impacted by firearm violence, local health departments, and community-based organizations.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $2,000,000 in FY 2025, with an additional $171,387 in federal fund expenditures. Costs are estimated to grow to $8,000,000 in FY 2026 and $10,000,000 annually starting in FY 2027. Local health departments may receive grant funding starting in FY 2026.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Center for Firearm Violence Prevention and Intervention within the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) and tasks the Secretary of Health with appointing its Executive Director.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Center work in consultation with this office (referred to in the bill by its previous name, the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention, Youth, and Victim Services) for firearm violence reduction strategies and data sharing.
The bill explicitly mandates that the Center work in consultation with the Maryland State Police to ensure a whole-of-government approach to reducing firearm violence.
The Center must submit its preliminary and strategic state plans to the Governor, and the Governor is authorized to include funding for the Center in the annual budget bill.

The fiscal note indicates a significant increase in general fund expenditures ($2M-$10M annually) and the creation of three new personnel positions, which fall under DBM's oversight of state budget and personnel management.

The bill authorizes the Center to share data and collaborate with higher education and research institutions; the fiscal note further anticipates awarding grants to academic partners for research and evaluation.
As a public research university, Morgan State is a potential academic partner for data sharing and research grants as authorized by the bill and mentioned in the fiscal note.
As a state-independent public college, it is identified as a potential partner for research collaboration and data sharing related to firearm violence prevention.
The OAG serves as the primary legal representative for state agencies and will likely provide legal counsel regarding the establishment and regulatory activities of the new Center within MDH.
Given that firearm violence is identified as a leading cause of death for children in Maryland, the Center's public health approach directly aligns with GOC's mission to promote child well-being.
The bill requires the Center to offer training and technical assistance to community and faith-based organizations, which are core constituencies managed and engaged by GOCI.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As an agency involved in public safety and the supervision of justice-involved individuals, DPSCS is a relevant partner in the bill's 'whole-of-government' approach to reducing firearm violence.
The fiscal note anticipates approximately $8.7 million in annual contractual services for the Center, which may require approval by the Board of Public Works as significant state expenditures.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS oversees the emergency medical network that responds to firearm-related trauma, making it a relevant stakeholder in the Center's mission to reduce harm from firearm violence.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Caring for Public Employees in the Safety Professions (CAPES) Act expands workers' compensation laws to include thyroid, colon, and ovarian cancer under the occupational disease presumptions for certain first responders. This presumption applies to paid and volunteer firefighters, fire marshals, and rescue squad members who have at least 10 years of service and whose cancer is caused by contact with toxic substances encountered in the line of duty.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Workers' Compensation Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Paid and volunteer firefighters, firefighting instructors, rescue squad members, advanced life support unit members, and fire marshals employed by airport authorities, counties, fire control districts, municipalities, or the State.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures (all funds) are expected to increase minimally starting in fiscal 2025. Local government and Chesapeake Employers’ Insurance Company expenditures are projected to increase, potentially significantly, due to the expansion of occupational disease presumptions. While a total fiscal estimate for the new presumptions is not provided, historical data for similar cases indicates an average cost of $48,351 per case.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for administering Maryland's workers' compensation law and adjudicating claims, the Commission will directly apply the new occupational disease presumptions for thyroid, colon, and ovarian cancer when evaluating claims from first responders.

Acting as the third-party administrator for State of Maryland employee workers' compensation claims, this agency (operating as Chesapeake Employers' Insurance Company) will manage the increased volume of claims and pay out benefits resulting from the expanded presumptions.
The Maryland State Police includes the Office of the State Fire Marshal, whose sworn members are explicitly named in the bill as being eligible for the new cancer presumptions.

The MAA employs the BWI Marshall Airport Fire and Rescue Department. These state-employed firefighters are directly affected by the bill's expansion of cancer-related workers' compensation presumptions.
As the parent department of the Maryland Aviation Administration, MDOT oversees the airport fire and rescue personnel covered by the bill's expanded protections.

DBM is the principal fiscal and personnel agency for the state. It will be responsible for managing the fiscal impact of increased workers' compensation expenditures and coordinating state personnel benefits related to these claims.

The bill includes provisions regarding the adjustment of benefits so that the combined weekly total of workers' compensation and retirement benefits does not exceed the individual's weekly salary, necessitating coordination with the State Retirement and Pension System.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the state agencies whose employees are covered (such as MSP and MDOT) and represents the state in contested workers' compensation hearings before the Commission.

This is an Administration bill (CAPES Act) introduced at the request of the Governor to expand protections for first responders, and the office oversees the executive agencies responsible for its implementation.

The SIF investigates workers' compensation claims involving pre-existing conditions that increase disability. The establishment of new cancer presumptions may result in claims involving pre-existing health factors that require SIF review.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits individuals from knowingly and willfully making threats to harm an election official or their immediate family members because of the official's role in administering the election process. The prohibition covers oral, written, and electronic communications, and establishes that a violation is a misdemeanor punishable by up to three years of imprisonment and/or a fine of up to $2,500.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Elections and local boards of elections
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland election officials, including the State Administrator, board members, employees, counsel, county election directors, election judges, and pages, as well as their parents, spouses, and children.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not expected to materially affect State or local government finances. While it establishes a criminal fine of up to $2,500, any resulting revenue from fines or expenditures for incarceration is expected to be minimal. The Department of Legislative Services notes that the bill does not require additional State or local funding for implementation.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly protects the State Administrator, board members, and employees of the Maryland State Board of Elections, as well as the personnel of the local boards it oversees, from threats of harm.

The Office of the State Prosecutor is responsible for investigating and prosecuting criminal offenses related to the integrity of the electoral process, which includes the new criminal violations regarding threats against election officials.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender is tasked with providing legal representation to indigent individuals charged with the new misdemeanor offenses established by this legislation.
The Administrative Office of the Courts will oversee the judicial proceedings and adjudication of criminal cases resulting from the enforcement of this new law.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Board of Elections and represents the state in civil and criminal appeals, which may involve the interpretation or enforcement of this act.

As the primary statewide law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police would be responsible for investigating criminal threats, including those made via electronic communication, against election officials.
This legislation was proposed as an Administration bill by the Governor and involves the protection of officials within an executive branch department (the State Board of Elections).

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency coordinates statewide public safety and criminal justice strategies, which encompass new laws designed to protect public servants and preserve the integrity of state processes.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Individuals convicted of the misdemeanor offense established by the bill could be subject to imprisonment or community supervision managed by this department.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill renames the Class C (Weinberg Center) beer, wine, and liquor license in Frederick County to the Class C (Weinberg Center/New Spires Arts Stages) license and expands its scope to include the New Spires Arts Stages. It modifies the application process by allowing a manager or a designated municipal official to sign the application and authorizes the license holder to sell alcoholic beverages during performances, fundraisers, or events hosted by outside groups renting either facility.
qa_results.responsible_party: Frederick County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Weinberg Center for the Arts, New Spires Arts Stages, the City of Frederick, and outside groups or organizations renting these facilities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, Frederick County revenues are expected to decrease by approximately $375 annually starting in fiscal 2025. This decrease results from replacing multiple per diem licenses, which currently generate about $700 annually, with a single permanent annual license fee of $325. Frederick County can manage enforcement using existing resources, and there is no impact on state finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the state agency responsible for regulating the alcohol, tobacco, and motor fuel industries and enforcing related state laws, including those within the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article being amended by this bill.

The Maryland Department of Commerce oversees the state's efforts in promoting the arts and culture. The bill directly impacts the Weinberg Center for the Arts and New Spires Arts Stages, which are significant cultural institutions.

The Maryland Office of Tourism Development is tasked with promoting cultural and recreational attractions; the expansion of alcohol service capabilities at these arts venues supports their attractiveness as tourist and cultural destinations.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, and the Governor is responsible for signing the legislation into law and appointing members to local boards of license commissioners.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Governor and state agencies regarding the implementation and legality of state laws, including changes to the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a manufacturer's limited beer, wine, and liquor permit in Frederick County, authorizing the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners to issue it to Class 1 distillery and Class 5 brewery license holders. The permit allows these manufacturers to host private events where a licensed caterer or contracted party provides alcoholic beverages not manufactured by the permit holder for on-premises consumption, provided they have no pecuniary interest in the caterer and follow specific event approval and public separation guidelines.
qa_results.responsible_party: Frederick County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Class 1 distillery license holders, Class 5 brewery license holders, licensed caterers, and private event organizers in Frederick County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Frederick County local revenues are expected to increase by approximately $4,500 annually beginning in fiscal 2025, assuming nine eligible license holders apply for the permit at a projected annual fee of $500. There is no impact on State revenues or expenditures, and local enforcement activities can be handled using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland regulates the alcohol industry and oversees the state-issued manufacturer's licenses (Class 1 distillery and Class 5 brewery licenses) that are necessary prerequisites for obtaining the local Frederick County permit created by this bill.
As the chief executive, the Governor oversaw the approval and signing of this legislation into law and maintains ultimate responsibility for the execution of the state's alcoholic beverages laws.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies and local licensing boards regarding the interpretation and enforcement of the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article.

The bill has a potential economic impact on small businesses in the manufacturing sector (breweries and distilleries) in Frederick County by allowing them to expand their revenue opportunities through private events.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners to issue a Class M-G beer, wine, and liquor license for use at a municipal golf course to an official of the City of Frederick designated by the Mayor. It also allows the Board to transfer the license to a different city official designated by the Mayor and clarifies that the license allows for on-premises consumption on golf course land and facilities.
qa_results.responsible_party: Frederick County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Officials of the City of Frederick, municipal golf course management, and patrons of municipal golf courses in Frederick County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no State fiscal impact and does not materially affect Frederick County finances or operations. The Class M-G license carries an annual fee of $600.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office is responsible for the final signing and oversight of legislation passed by the General Assembly, including this local Frederick County bill.

The Comptroller functions as the state's principal tax collector and regulator of the alcohol industry. This bill specifically identifies the agent as the vendor responsible for collecting and remitting sales and use tax, which falls under the Comptroller's jurisdiction.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill exempts applicants for employment as paramedics, emergency medical technicians, and 9-1-1 dispatcher specialists within the Dorchester County Department of Emergency Services from the Maryland law that generally prohibits employers from requiring polygraph examinations as a condition of prospective employment.
qa_results.responsible_party: Dorchester County Department of Emergency Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants for paramedic, emergency medical technician, and 9-1-1 dispatcher specialist positions in Dorchester County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no State fiscal impact as the bill pertains only to Dorchester County. The bill is estimated to have no material effect on Dorchester County operations or finances, and county revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Labor and Employment Article regarding polygraph testing. The Commissioner of Labor and Industry, a position within the Department of Labor, is responsible for receiving and investigating complaints regarding violations of the polygraph prohibition and attempting to resolve disputes through mediation.

The bill explicitly authorizes the Attorney General to bring civil actions for injunctive relief, damages, or other relief on behalf of applicants or employees if the Commissioner of Labor and Industry determines that the polygraph prohibition has been violated.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates polygraph exemptions for paramedics and emergency medical technicians in Dorchester County. As the state agency responsible for overseeing and regulating the emergency medical services network and personnel, this impacts the hiring standards for the professionals within its regulatory purview.

The bill establishes polygraph exemptions for 9-1-1 dispatcher specialists in Dorchester County. The Department of Emergency Management oversees the Maryland 9-1-1 Board and maintains a primary interest in the personnel and infrastructure of the state's emergency communication systems.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill exempts correctional officers at the Dorchester County Detention Center from the polygraph prohibition. As the principal department for correctional services and standards in Maryland, DPSCS has a regulatory interest in the qualifications and hiring processes for correctional personnel across the state.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the number of resident judges in the Circuit Court for Prince George's County from 24 to 25.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary
qa_results.stakeholders: Prince George's County residents and the Prince George's County government
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $361,500 in FY 2025 for one judge and two staff positions (courtroom clerk and law clerk). These expenditures increase annually, reaching an estimated $493,400 by FY 2029. Additionally, Prince George's County is responsible for local expenditures related to funding an administrative assistant and other operating costs for the new judgeship.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the central support agency for the Maryland Judicial Branch, the Administrative Office of the Courts is responsible for implementing the bill's requirement to add a new judgeship, including managing the operational and financial needs for the judge and associated staff.

The Office of Appointments is directly responsible for recruiting, vetting, and recommending candidates to the Governor for judicial vacancies, including the newly created judgeship in Prince George's County.
The Governor has the constitutional and statutory authority to appoint the individual to the new judicial seat established by this bill.

The Department of Budget and Management oversees the state's personnel and fiscal management; the fiscal note indicates that general fund expenditures for the additional judgeship and staff were incorporated into the FY 2025 budget.
The agency manages retirement and pension benefits for judges and their associated staff (law clerks and courtroom clerks), all of whom are members of the State Retirement and Pension System.
The Comptroller is the state's chief financial officer and is responsible for paying state employees, including the newly created judicial position and support staff.
The Treasurer's Office manages the state's funds and expenditures, and the Treasurer sits on the Board of Public Works which may oversee related procurement or facility needs.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill updates the definition of 'newspaper' or 'newspaper in general circulation' for legal notices to include publications in digital format. Specifically in Dorchester County, any required legal advertisement or notice must be published in both print and digital formats, with the publication period starting upon the first appearance in either format. If a qualifying combined print and digital publication is unavailable, the county may use a digital publication that regularly contains local news for the county.
qa_results.responsible_party: Dorchester County government
qa_results.stakeholders: Dorchester County residents, local government agencies in Dorchester County, and print or digital news publishers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect Dorchester County operations or finances, and the impact on small businesses is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically references the publication of legal notices required by court orders, judgments, or decrees. As the central support agency for the Maryland Judicial Branch, the AOC is responsible for operational and legal support related to court procedures, which are directly affected by the modified definition of a valid newspaper for legal notices.

As the chief legal officer providing counsel to state agencies and the General Assembly, the OAG is relevant because the bill alters the statewide definition of 'newspaper' and 'newspaper in general circulation' within the General Provisions article, affecting how all state laws requiring public notice are interpreted and enforced.

The Comptroller's Office is responsible for publishing various legal notices, including those related to unclaimed property and tax compliance. The bill's expansion of the definition of 'newspaper' to include digital formats directly impacts how this agency meets its statutory notice requirements.

DGS serves as the state's central procurement authority. Many state procurement laws require the publication of legal advertisements for bids and contracts. The change in the general definition of a newspaper for legal notices affects DGS's compliance with these procurement advertising standards.

The Board of Elections is responsible for supervising elections, which includes the statutory requirement to publish various public notices. The bill's modification of what constitutes a valid publication for such notices, especially the specific provisions for Dorchester County, affects their administrative duties.

The PSC frequently holds public hearings regarding utility rates and infrastructure, which require the publication of legal notices. The bill's inclusion of digital formats in the general definition of a newspaper for these notices impacts the commission's regulatory processes.

MDE is required by state and federal law to publish numerous public notices regarding environmental permits, hearings, and hazardous waste site cleanups. The updated definition of a 'newspaper' for legal notices affects how MDE satisfies these public participation requirements.

MDOT and its modal administrations (such as the State Highway Administration) must publish legal notices for construction projects and public hearings. The expansion of the definition of newspaper to include digital formats impacts their project communication and legal compliance.

The MIA regulates the insurance market and may be required to publish legal notices regarding rate filings, public hearings, or enforcement actions. The bill's change to the General Provisions regarding legal notices applies to these administrative functions.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Growing Apprenticeships and the Public Safety Workforce (GAPS) Act renames the Law Enforcement Cadet Apprenticeship Program to the Public Safety Apprenticeship Program and expands its scope to include correctional units and other emergency service agencies. The bill establishes start-up grants of up to $200,000 and stabilization grants of up to $5,000 to support registered apprenticeship programs in public safety. Additionally, it requires the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission to develop mental health wellness policies for law enforcement and correctional facilities and establishes a temporary workgroup to study and address workforce shortages in these fields.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor and Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Young individuals seeking careers in public safety, state and local law enforcement agencies, correctional units, and emergency medical service providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is funded through an existing statutory mandate requiring at least $750,000 in the State budget annually for the apprenticeship program. The Maryland Department of Labor may award competitive start-up grants of up to $200,000 and stabilization grants of up to $5,000 per apprentice to eligible public safety agencies. Implementation is expected to be handled within existing budgeted resources by the Maryland Department of Labor and the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill renames and expands the Law Enforcement Cadet Apprenticeship Program to the Public Safety Apprenticeship Program, which is housed within and administered by the Department of Labor. The agency is responsible for awarding start-up and stabilization grants and adopting related regulations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission (usually associated with DPSCS), which is tasked with developing mental health wellness policies and staffing the new Workgroup. Furthermore, correctional units under DPSCS are primary beneficiaries of the expanded apprenticeship program.

The Maryland State Police is a primary public safety agency that will be impacted by the apprenticeship program and mental health policies. The Secretary of State Police is also a designated member of the Workgroup on Growing Engagement in the Law Enforcement and Corrections Workforce.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Workgroup on Growing Engagement in the Law Enforcement and Corrections Workforce, which falls under this category of temporary bodies created to address specific government issues.

This is an administration bill proposed by the Governor. The Governor is responsible for designating the Chair of the newly established Workgroup and receives reports from both the Workgroup and the Police Training and Standards Commission.

The Secretary of Higher Education is a member of the newly established Workgroup. Additionally, the fiscal note discusses the relationship between this bill and other recruitment programs like the Maryland Police Officers Scholarship Program managed by the Commission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill expands the apprenticeship program to include 'public safety agencies,' which includes providers of medical or other emergency services. MIEMSS, which oversees the state's EMS network, is listed as an information source and represents a sector directly affected by the program's expansion.

The bill stipulates that grants may not be awarded to agencies that fail to bargain with exclusive representatives regarding adjustments to wages and hours for the program. The PERB oversees public sector collective bargaining and would be relevant in disputes or compliance regarding these requirements.

The State Superintendent of Schools is a member of the Workgroup. The program targets 'young individuals,' often involving high school or early post-secondary pathways that coordinate with MSDE's oversight of vocational and educational standards.

MDEM coordinates the state's response to emergencies and works with public safety agencies. The bill's expanded definition of public safety agencies includes divisions that provide emergency services coordinated by or involving MDEM.
As an agency that employs public safety and law enforcement-aligned personnel, DJS is a potential beneficiary of the expanded apprenticeship program and was consulted as an information source.
The DNR's Natural Resources Police are considered a law enforcement agency eligible for the program's grants and impacted by the workforce development strategies in the bill.

MDOT oversees several modal administrations with law enforcement components (e.g., Maryland Transit Administration Police) that are eligible beneficiaries of the apprenticeship program and mental health policies.

As the principal personnel agency for the state, DBM is involved in the broader workforce shortage studies and fiscal planning related to the mandated appropriations and personnel costs for state-level public safety apprentices.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The GOCPP manages related initiatives like the Police Recruitment & Retention (PRAR) initiative. The bill's objectives align with GOCPP's mission to support law enforcement recruitment and public safety policy.

The definition of 'law enforcement agency' in the bill includes university police departments. USM institutions with such departments are eligible for the grants and impacted by the training standards.
As an independent state university with its own police department, Morgan State is included in the program's scope for law enforcement apprenticeship grants.
As an independent state college with its own security or law enforcement force, it is a potential beneficiary and was identified as an information source for the fiscal note.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits discrimination based on military status in the areas of housing and employment. It defines military status to include active-duty members of the uniformed services, members of the reserve components of the armed forces, veterans, and their dependents, adding them as a protected class under Maryland's anti-discrimination laws.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Commission on Civil Rights
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the uniformed services, members of the military reserve, veterans, military dependents, employers, and housing providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures are expected to increase, at least minimally, for the Maryland Commission on Civil Rights (MCCR) to handle potential increases in caseloads for housing and employment discrimination complaints. The fiscal note provides an illustrative estimate that each additional investigator required would cost at least $80,000 annually. The bill is not anticipated to materially affect local government finances, though it may have a meaningful impact on small businesses subject to penalties for violations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Commission on Civil Rights (MCCR) is the primary agency responsible for enforcing anti-discrimination laws in the state. The bill directly increases their workload by adding 'military status' as a protected class in housing and employment, requiring them to investigate new complaints and conduct conciliation efforts.

As the principal agency for Maryland's veterans, service members, and their families, the DVMF is directly impacted by legislation that creates legal protections for 'military status.' This bill aligns with their mission to advocate for and support the military community.

The bill explicitly states it does not limit the authority of the Attorney General to investigate, prosecute, and remediate civil rights violations. The OAG provides legal counsel to the state and enforces consumer and civil rights laws relevant to housing and employment.

The Maryland Department of Labor oversees workforce development and enforces labor laws. The bill's prohibition on employment discrimination based on military status directly impacts the regulatory environment for workers and employers managed by this agency.

The OAH is responsible for conducting administrative hearings for contested cases involving the Commission on Civil Rights. An increase in discrimination complaints related to military status will likely lead to an increase in hearings adjudicated by this agency.

As the state's central personnel agency, DBM is responsible for managing state employee policies. The bill's prohibition on employment discrimination extends to state government personnel actions, requiring DBM to ensure compliance within the executive branch.

The Adjutant General oversees the Maryland National Guard and military department. The bill's definition of 'military status' specifically includes members of the uniformed services and reserve components, directly affecting the personnel managed by this agency.

The bill's prohibition on housing discrimination based on military status is relevant to the DHCD's mission of ensuring safe and secure housing for residents, although enforcement is primarily handled by the Commission on Civil Rights.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Maryland Community Investment Corporation (MCIC) as an independent State instrumentality. The corporation's primary purpose is to qualify as a Community Development Entity under federal law, apply for federal New Markets Tax Credits, and provide financial assistance and equity investments to projects in low-income communities. Additionally, the bill expands the eligible uses of the Strategic Demolition and Smart Growth Impact Fund to include debt payments and credit enhancement for revitalization projects.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Community Investment Corporation (MCIC)
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and businesses in low-income communities, nonprofit entities, and local government agencies involved in community revitalization.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $800,000 in FY 2025 for the establishment and administrative costs of the corporation, with similar levels of funding assumed through FY 2029. Nonbudgeted revenues and expenditures are expected to increase significantly (potentially by an average of $50,000,000 per award) starting as early as FY 2026 as the corporation receives and allocates federal New Markets Tax Credits. Local governments benefit from increased flexibility in using funds from the Strategic Demolition and Smart Growth Impact Fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for appointing members to the Board of Directors of the newly established Maryland Community Investment Corporation (MCIC) and receives annual and quarterly reports on the corporation's activities, financial statements, and performance metrics.

The Comptroller or their designee serves as an ex officio member of the MCIC Board of Directors. Additionally, the Comptroller is responsible for accounting for the Strategic Demolition and Smart Growth Impact Fund, which is modified by this bill.

The State Treasurer's Office is responsible for holding the Strategic Demolition and Smart Growth Impact Fund, which this bill expands to include credit enhancement and debt payments. The Treasurer also manages the investment of the fund's money.

The Office of the Attorney General is the designated legal advisor to the MCIC and is required to assign Assistant Attorneys General, including a General Counsel, to support the corporation's complex financial and tax credit activities.

The Department of Budget and Management is responsible for the fiscal oversight and general fund allocation ($0.8 million in FY 2025) required to establish and support the administrative costs of the new corporation.

The Secretary of Commerce serves on the MCIC Board of Directors. The department is authorized to provide staff to the corporation and is directly involved in the economic development initiatives and 'follow-on financing' pathways the bill aims to create.
The Secretary of General Services or their designee serves as an ex officio member of the Board of Directors for the newly established Maryland Community Investment Corporation.

The Secretary of Housing and Community Development serves on the MCIC Board. The agency administers the Strategic Demolition and Smart Growth Impact Fund, whose eligible uses are expanded by the bill, and the department is authorized to provide staffing support to the new corporation.
The Secretary of Planning or their designee serves as an ex officio member of the Board of Directors of the Maryland Community Investment Corporation.

The bill authorizes the MCIC to make investments under an agreement with the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, potentially involving the management of certain excess pension contributions.
The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting the non-profit and community representatives for the Board of Directors of the MCIC, who require Senate advice and consent.

The board members, officers, and employees of the MCIC, as well as its investment and advisory committee members, are subject to the Public Ethics Law and must file financial disclosures with the State Ethics Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits exhibition driving on any highway or private property used by the general public in Maryland and increases penalties for motor vehicle violations related to racing or speed contests. It defines exhibition driving as operating a vehicle near a crowd in a manner that intentionally results in behaviors like excessive acceleration, skidding, or unreasonable noise. The bill also expands the authority to designate special event zones with enhanced penalties and lower speed limits—previously limited to Worcester County—to all local jurisdictions across the state.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation (Motor Vehicle Administration and State Highway Administration), the Maryland Judiciary, and local law enforcement agencies
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland motorists, law enforcement agencies, the state court system, and the general public
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill will result in a minimal increase in State general fund revenues from higher fines and a potential minimal increase in State and local expenditures due to new incarceration penalties beginning in FY 2025. The Motor Vehicle Administration and the Judiciary are expected to handle implementation with existing resources, and Transportation Trust Fund revenues are not significantly affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary oversight agency for Maryland's transportation network, MDOT is responsible for the overall implementation of the bill's provisions through its sub-agencies, including the MVA and SHA.
The MVA is directly responsible for assessing the increased driver's license points (8 or 12 points) mandated by the bill and maintaining the driving records of those convicted.
The SHA is specifically authorized by the bill to designate 'special event zones' on state highways and to reduce speed limits within those zones to ensure public safety.
The Maryland State Police is the primary law enforcement agency tasked with enforcing the bill's prohibitions against street racing and the newly defined offense of exhibition driving on highways.
The AOC must support the judicial system in handling mandatory court appearances for these offenses, as the bill removes the option for prepayment and introduces possible imprisonment.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OPD will be required to represent indigent defendants facing the new incarceration penalties (up to 60 days or 1 year) established for racing and exhibition driving violations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The DPSCS is impacted by the potential increase in the correctional population due to the bill's new and increased imprisonment penalties, particularly for detentions in Baltimore City.
The OAH will likely see an increase in hearings related to motor vehicle license suspensions resulting from the higher points assessments required under this bill.
The MDTA operates its own police force which will enforce the bill's racing and exhibition driving prohibitions on the state's toll bridges, tunnels, and roads.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The GOCPP coordinates statewide public safety strategies and manages grant funding for the law enforcement agencies responsible for enforcing these traffic safety laws.
The MAIF, as the insurer of last resort, may see an influx of drivers who can no longer obtain private insurance due to the significant points and criminal penalties associated with these offenses.
The MIA regulates the insurance market and handles consumer issues; the bill's changes to points and motor vehicle violations will impact insurance risk profiles and rates.
The OAG provides necessary legal counsel to the primary implementing agencies, including MDOT and the Maryland State Police, regarding the enforcement of the new regulations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS coordinates the emergency medical response system that handles serious bodily injuries resulting from the high-speed racing and exhibition driving prohibited by the bill.
DBM oversees state fiscal planning and the personnel costs associated with the increased workload for enforcement and judicial agencies mentioned in the fiscal note.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands Maryland's Equal Pay for Equal Work law to prohibit employment discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation. It prohibits employers from paying employees of one sexual orientation less than employees of another sexual orientation for comparable work and forbids providing less favorable employment opportunities based on sexual orientation.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor (Commissioner of Labor and Industry)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland employees and employers, including small businesses with fewer than 15 employees who were not previously covered by certain sexual orientation discrimination provisions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, enforcement of the bill can be handled with existing resources and will not affect State or local revenues. The bill has no impact on the State as an employer because State agencies are already prohibited from such discrimination. Small businesses with fewer than 15 employees are newly subject to these provisions, which may have a meaningful but unquantified impact; specific dollar estimates for fiscal implications are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Labor, through the Commissioner of Labor and Industry, is the primary agency responsible for implementing and enforcing the state's Equal Pay for Equal Work law, which this bill directly amends.

The Commission on Civil Rights is the state's lead agency for enforcing anti-discrimination laws; this bill aligns labor-specific pay equity statutes with broader civil rights protections for sexual orientation.

The Attorney General is authorized by law to bring civil actions on behalf of the Commissioner of Labor and Industry or impacted employees for violations of the Equal Pay for Equal Work provisions amended by this bill.

As the central personnel agency for the state, DBM oversees employment policies for state agencies and was consulted to ensure the state's executive branch already adheres to the discrimination prohibitions expanded by this bill.
The bill expands the grounds for legal action under the Labor and Employment Article, which may be adjudicated in the state's circuit court system supported by the AOC.
The OAH provides the hearing process for contested administrative cases involving state agencies, including those related to labor and personnel disputes that may arise from this legislation.
As a major state employer, the USM was identified as a stakeholder in the bill's fiscal note to confirm compliance with updated employment and pay equity standards.
As a large multi-modal state employer, MDOT was consulted regarding the fiscal impact and compliance requirements of incorporating sexual orientation into the Equal Pay for Equal Work law.
The Governor oversees the executive branch and signed this legislation; the bill impacts state personnel policies and broad social policy related to civil rights and labor.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Maryland Age-Appropriate Design Code Act, or Maryland Kids Code, requires businesses that provide online products likely to be accessed by children to conduct data protection impact assessments and implement privacy-by-design features. It prohibits profiling children by default, using dark patterns to collect excess data, and tracking precise geolocation without notice, while establishing civil penalties for violations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Office of the Attorney General, Consumer Protection Division
qa_results.stakeholders: Children under the age of 18, their parents or guardians, and for-profit businesses that meet specific revenue or data processing thresholds and offer online products in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures are estimated to increase by at least 355700 dollars in fiscal 2025 for the Office of the Attorney General to implement enforcement, potentially reaching 469000 dollars by fiscal 2029. Revenue may increase from civil penalties of up to 2500 dollars for negligent violations or 7500 dollars for intentional violations per affected child. There is no significant impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Attorney General is the primary agency responsible for enforcing the Maryland Age-Appropriate Design Code Act. The bill specifically tasks the OAG's Division of Consumer Protection with investigating violations, requesting data protection impact assessments, and seeking civil penalties for unfair, abusive, or deceptive trade practices.

The bill focuses on the well-being and online safety of children, utilizing a 'best interests of children' standard to regulate online products. This aligns with the Governor's Office for Children's mission to lead statewide efforts in promoting the well-being of Maryland's children and families.

According to the fiscal note, the bill is expected to increase general fund expenditures for the Office of the Attorney General, potentially requiring new personnel positions. DBM is the principal agency for state personnel and budget execution, overseeing these changes.

The bill establishes civil penalties and classifies violations as unfair or deceptive trade practices, which may result in increased litigation and judicial proceedings within the Maryland court system.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives. This bill represents a significant policy shift in child digital safety and was a high-priority legislative action signed into law by the Governor.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for the recreational vehicle (RV) industry in Maryland. It requires RV manufacturers, distributors, and factory branches to be licensed by the Motor Vehicle Administration and mandates signed, written dealer agreements. The bill also defines the terms for warranty services, vehicle deliveries, ownership transfers, and the termination or nonrenewal of dealer agreements, while providing mechanisms for dispute resolution and civil penalties.
qa_results.responsible_party: Motor Vehicle Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Recreational vehicle manufacturers, distributors, factory branches, and dealers, as well as RV consumers in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local finances. The Motor Vehicle Administration can handle the new regulatory activities with existing budgeted resources. Revenue from license fees is expected to be maintained at current levels (between $90 and $900 annually per licensee), and any new revenue from fines (up to $1,000 per violation) is not expected to be significant.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA), which is a modal administration of MDOT, is the primary agency responsible for the licensing and regulation of recreational vehicle manufacturers, distributors, and factory branches, as well as the enforcement of dealer agreements as established by this bill.

The bill specifies that dealers, manufacturers, distributors, and factory branches are entitled to a hearing to contest license suspensions, revocations, or fines imposed by the MVA; these hearings are adjudicated by the Office of Administrative Hearings.

The bill authorizes individuals who suffer financial injury from violations of the new subtitle to bring civil actions for damages, attorney's fees, and injunctive relief in a court of competent jurisdiction.

The OAG serves as the primary legal representative for MDOT and MVA, providing counsel on the implementation of the new regulatory framework and defending the state in any civil litigation or appeals.
As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors the implementation of new licensing fees and fines that impact the Transportation Trust Fund.

The Governor's Office provides executive oversight for the Department of Transportation and signs the legislation into law, coordinating high-level policy initiatives related to state business regulations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands eligibility for the Maryland Community College Promise Scholarship by exempting students enrolled in noncredit vocational certificate programs, noncredit sequences leading to licensure or certification, or registered apprenticeship programs from minimum grade point average (GPA) requirements and the requirement to file a Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA), except when the program qualifies for federal Pell Grants.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland community colleges and the Maryland Higher Education Commission (MHEC)
qa_results.stakeholders: Community college students in vocational, noncredit, or apprenticeship programs; Maryland community colleges; and the Maryland Higher Education Commission.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not mandate additional State spending because the program's annual appropriation is already fixed at a minimum of $15,000,000. Expanding eligibility may lead to more individuals being placed on waiting lists or fewer scholarships being awarded if funding remains at the minimum required level. Both State-funded and local community colleges can update their eligibility criteria and application processes using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees the state budget, including the $15 million annual mandated appropriation for the Maryland Community College Promise Scholarship program mentioned in the fiscal note.

MSDE oversees public education and GED certification; the scholarship's eligibility is based on high school graduation or GED completion, and the agency provides student data used in administrative processes.

The bill specifically expands scholarship eligibility for individuals participating in registered apprenticeship programs and vocational training, which are overseen and regulated by the Department of Labor.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives related to workforce development and education, which this scholarship program directly supports.

The board is responsible for setting the statewide vision for workforce development and coordinating partnerships for apprenticeships and vocational training, which are prioritized by this bill's expanded eligibility.

MHEC is the primary regulatory and coordinating board for postsecondary education in Maryland and oversees the administration and reporting of state financial aid programs like the Community College Promise Scholarship.

MHEC is the primary regulatory and coordinating board for postsecondary education in Maryland and oversees the administration and reporting of state financial aid programs like the Community College Promise Scholarship.

MLDS links student data from MHEC with workforce data from the Department of Labor; changes in scholarship eligibility for vocational and apprenticeship pathways directly impact their data sets and outcomes analysis.

The team focuses on enhancing economic mobility and reducing child poverty; expanding access to vocational and apprenticeship scholarships is a primary strategy for improving economic mobility for Maryland families.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Higher Education Commission and the Department of Labor regarding the implementation of statutory changes to state scholarship requirements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a collective bargaining process for full-time and part-time non-management employees of public library systems in Maryland. It authorizes employees to form or join employee organizations, negotiate on wages, hours, and working conditions, and establishes procedures for certification, mediation of impasses, and grievance resolution, while prohibiting strikes and work stoppages.
qa_results.responsible_party: Public Library Systems and their Boards of Trustees
qa_results.stakeholders: Non-management full-time and part-time employees of Maryland public library systems (excluding Baltimore, Montgomery, and Prince George's counties), library administrators, and county governing bodies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's fiscal impact is indeterminate but potentially significant. State general fund expenditures for retirement costs may increase starting in FY 2029 if collective bargaining leads to higher salaries for eligible library employees. Local library systems may face increased costs for hiring outside mediators and fact-finders, as well as potential increases in employee salaries and benefits resulting from negotiations. Specific dollar estimates are not provided in the Fiscal Note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the official state agency providing strategic leadership and funding coordination for Maryland's public library systems, the Maryland State Library Agency is the primary body overseeing the entities directly impacted by the establishment of collective bargaining for library employees.

The Public Employee Relations Board is the state's central agency for public sector labor relations. Although the bill utilizes federal mediators for certification and impasse resolution, PERB is responsible for standardizing the administration of collective bargaining across the state's public workforce and was a key information source for the bill's fiscal analysis.

The bill has a notable fiscal impact on this agency because the state pays retirement benefits for eligible library employees. Increases in salaries resulting from collective bargaining will lead to higher state pension contributions and administrative obligations for the retirement system starting in FY 2029.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM must oversee the budget execution and long-term fiscal planning related to the projected increase in state general fund expenditures for library employee retirement costs.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for implementation and provided the final approval for the bill. It also coordinates the broad policy initiatives regarding labor relations and state funding for education and libraries.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the state agencies involved, such as the Maryland State Library Agency, in interpreting the new collective bargaining requirements and representing the state in any legal disputes arising from the law.

The Treasurer's Office manages the state's general funds and retirement disbursements. It will be responsible for the protection and distribution of the additional state funds required for retirement contributions identified in the fiscal note.

The bill specifies that certain outcomes of the collective bargaining and grievance process, specifically arbitration, are subject to judicial review under the Maryland Uniform Arbitration Act, which involves the operations of the state court system.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the Electronic Transactions Protection Act, which originally established the Electronic Transaction Education, Advocacy, and Mediation Unit within the Office of the Attorney General to handle consumer complaints and education related to electronic commerce and privacy.
qa_results.responsible_party: Office of the Attorney General
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland consumers and the Office of the Attorney General
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no substantive fiscal impact on State or local operations or finances. The Office of the Attorney General reported that the specific unit established by the Act was never funded and that its duties are already performed by the Consumer Protection Division.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals the Electronic Transactions Protection Act, which established the Electronic Transaction Education, Advocacy, and Mediation Unit within the Office of the Attorney General. The OAG is the primary agency affected by the removal of this statutory mandate, and its Consumer Protection Division continues to handle the related functions.

The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies affected by this repeal and is responsible for signing the legislation into law. The act's repeal represents a technical change in the executive branch's statutory structure.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency for the state, DBM is responsible for the organizational structure and budget execution of executive branch units. While the unit being repealed was never funded, its formal removal from the state's organizational code is an administrative and fiscal planning matter.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a recreational oyster license for Maryland residents, requiring a $10 application fee for individuals aged 16 and older. It mandates that the Department of Natural Resources (DNR) use a portion of the collected fees to replenish public oyster bars with shell and juvenile oysters. The bill also provides for complimentary or discounted licenses for specific groups, including veterans and blind residents, and allows waterfront property owners to harvest oysters without a license provided they register with the DNR.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents interested in recreational oyster and clam harvesting, military veterans, blind individuals, waterfront property owners, and the commercial seafood industry.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues and expenditures will increase annually starting in fiscal 2025. According to the Fiscal Note, revenues are expected to increase by approximately $100,000 for every 10,000 licenses issued. Expenditures will increase because the DNR is required to use a portion of the license fees to replenish public fishery oyster bars. There is no fiscal impact on local governments, and small businesses in the seafood industry may benefit from future increases in oyster harvests.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Natural Resources (DNR) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill. It must create the recreational oyster license, manage the application process, collect fees, and oversee the program to replenish public fishery oyster bars using a portion of the fee revenue.

The bill creates new state-level benefits for veterans, specifically requiring DNR to provide complimentary recreational oyster licenses to former prisoners of war and disabled veterans, and discounted licenses to Purple Heart recipients.
The bill mandates that DNR provide complimentary recreational oyster licenses to residents who are blind, which directly impacts the community and services the department helps coordinate.

DNR will need to update its electronic licensing system (COMPASS) to incorporate the new recreational oyster license, application requirements, and the registry for individuals exempt from the license, which falls under the state's IT infrastructure management.
The bill establishes new special fund revenue and expenditure streams related to the Fisheries Management and Protection Fund, requiring fiscal oversight and budget execution supervision by DBM.

As the agency responsible for auditing fiscal compliance and performance of executive agencies, OLA will be responsible for auditing the new revenue collection and the specific expenditure of funds for oyster replenishment.
OAH may be required to adjudicate contested cases related to license denials, revocations, or enforcement actions taken by DNR under the new regulations established by the bill.

Oyster and clam harvesting are integral to the economic and cultural life of Maryland's rural coastal counties. Changes to recreational access and restoration funding directly affect the quality of life in these regions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Council on the Chesapeake Bay
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill's focus on replenishing oyster bars is a core component of Chesapeake Bay restoration, which the Council is tasked with advising the Governor on.
This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and Bay protection; the bill's provisions for oyster replenishment directly support these statewide environmental goals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill reduces the annual fee for a stadium beer, wine, and liquor license in Harford County from $10,000 to $7,000.
qa_results.responsible_party: Harford County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners, lessees, or concession managers of professional baseball stadiums in Harford County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are not affected. Harford County revenues are estimated to decrease by $3,000 annually beginning in fiscal 2025 due to the reduction of the license fee from $10,000 to $7,000. Currently, there is only one such license in effect in the county. The Board of License Commissioners, which is funded by these fees, indicates the reduction may impact their operational and educational capacity.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is responsible for regulating the alcohol industry and enforcing state tax laws related to it. As this bill amends the 'Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis' Article of the Maryland Code regarding license fees, it falls within the Comptroller's general regulatory and oversight scope for alcoholic beverages.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, coordinates policy initiatives, and the Governor signed this bill into law as Chapter 1008.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal officer for the state, providing counsel to the General Assembly and state agencies on the interpretation and enforcement of state laws, including the 'Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis' Article being amended.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits consumer reporting agencies from including records of certain criminal proceedings—such as those where a consumer was falsely accused, acquitted, exonerated, or where charges resulted in a nolle prosequi—in a consumer report. It also forbids reporting expunged criminal records and prohibits agencies from using any of this information to determine a consumer's creditworthiness.
qa_results.responsible_party: Office of Financial Regulation (OFR)
qa_results.stakeholders: Consumers with non-conviction or expunged criminal records, and consumer reporting agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that the Office of Financial Regulation can enforce the bill with existing resources. State revenues are not materially affected, and there is no material impact on local government operations or finances. While the Commissioner of Financial Regulation may impose civil penalties for violations (up to $10,000 for a first violation and $25,000 for subsequent violations), the overall fiscal impact is expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill is primarily enforced by the Office of Financial Regulation (OFR), which is a division of the Maryland Department of Labor. OFR oversees the regulation of financial institutions and consumer reporting agencies in the state.

The OAG's Consumer Protection Division is responsible for enforcing laws related to consumer rights and would provide legal counsel to the state regarding the enforcement of these new restrictions on consumer reporting agencies.

The bill restricts the use of records from criminal proceedings (such as acquittals, exonerations, and non-guilty verdicts) which are generated and maintained within the state's judicial system supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency manages the state's criminal records repository and processes expungements; the bill specifically prohibits consumer reporting agencies from including information about expunged records in their reports.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Department of Labor and the enforcement of state laws, including the new consumer protection regulations established by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill transfers the primary responsibility for administering the Next Generation Scholars of Maryland Program from the Maryland State Department of Education to the Maryland Higher Education Commission. It requires the Department to assist the Commission with reviewing grant applications and publicizing the program, while clarifying the types of financial aid literacy services provided to students.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Higher Education Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income students eligible for Guaranteed Access Grants or the National School Lunch Program, participating Maryland-based nonprofit organizations, and local school systems.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The mandated annual 5,000,000 dollar special fund appropriation for the program transfers from the Maryland State Department of Education to the Maryland Higher Education Commission beginning in fiscal 2026. The Maryland Higher Education Commission will also incur an estimated 223,700 dollars in general fund expenditures in fiscal 2026 for administrative staffing and system upgrades, with ongoing annual costs between 157,600 dollars and 171,800 dollars. No local fiscal impact is expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically mandates that the Comptroller shall account for the Next Generation Scholars of Maryland Program Fund.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is responsible for the budget execution related to the $5 million annual appropriation and the new staff positions required by the Maryland Higher Education Commission to administer the program.

The bill transfers the primary administration of the Next Generation Scholars of Maryland Program from the Department of Education (MSDE) to the Commission. However, MSDE is required to assist with application reviews, collaborate on publicity, identify eligible school systems, and co-author annual reports.
The Governor is responsible for including the mandated $5 million appropriation in the annual budget and receives annual implementation reports from the program administrators.

The bill designates the Maryland Higher Education Commission (MHEC) as the new primary administrator for the Next Generation Scholars of Maryland Program and its associated Fund, requiring new staff and the adoption of regulations.

The bill requires the annual implementation report to include findings regarding the potential for allocating funds for longitudinal data collection on student matriculation and retention, which falls under the purview of the MLDS Center.
The bill mandates that the State Treasurer shall hold the Program Fund separately and invest its money in the same manner as other State funds.

The program requires collaboration with institutions of higher education and provides services to students matriculating at such institutions. As a state-governed university, Morgan State University is a direct stakeholder in this educational pipeline.
As a public institution of higher education, the college is a mandated collaborator for program administration and a potential destination for participating students receiving program services.

The University System of Maryland, as the state's public higher education system, is a mandated collaborator in the administration of the program and oversees institutions where program participants matriculate.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the educational training requirements for the Board of Trustees of the State Retirement and Pension System to include topics such as retirement administration and operations. It also allows the Board to provide annual benefit statements and financial summaries to members and retirees through a secure online portal and the agency website rather than through traditional physical delivery.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Members and retirees of the Maryland State Retirement and Pension System and the members of its Board of Trustees.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note states that the bill does not directly affect State or local finances, but may lead to administrative efficiencies for the State Retirement and Pension System. No specific revenue or expenditure changes are identified.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The agency is the primary administrative body for the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS). The bill directly modifies the responsibilities and duties of its Board of Trustees, including training requirements and the method for delivering benefit statements to members and retirees.

As the state's central personnel agency, DBM is responsible for managing employee benefits and policies. The administration of the pension system is a core component of the state's compensation and benefits structure for the workforce DBM oversees.

The OAG provides legal counsel to all state boards and agencies. It would be responsible for advising the SRPS Board of Trustees on compliance with the new training mandates and the legal requirements for secure electronic delivery of benefit statements.

The bill mandates the use of an online portal and secure access participant portal to provide sensitive financial and benefit information. This falls under DoIT's purview regarding statewide IT strategy, cybersecurity standards, and data governance.

The State Treasurer serves as a key financial officer and is an ex-officio member of the SRPS Board of Trustees. The bill's updated training requirements and reporting mandates directly affect the Treasurer's role and duties as a trustee.

The Comptroller is an ex-officio member of the SRPS Board of Trustees. The bill changes the training requirements and the method of financial reporting/statement delivery for the board on which the Comptroller serves.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for appointing various members to the SRPS Board of Trustees. The bill alters the training requirements for those appointed officials.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the requirements for reciprocal licensure of clinical marriage and family therapists in Maryland. It mandates that the State Board of Professional Counselors and Therapists waive specific education, experience, and national examination requirements for applicants who are already licensed in another state or jurisdiction, provided they have no history of disciplinary action and meet administrative application criteria. The Board is prohibited from requiring a national certification exam for these applicants but may require an exam on Maryland law.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Professional Counselors and Therapists
qa_results.stakeholders: Clinical marriage and family therapists licensed in other states, territories, or jurisdictions who are seeking licensure in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no state or local fiscal impact. The changes are considered procedural in nature and do not directly affect governmental finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency responsible for regulating health care professionals and oversees the State Board of Professional Counselors and Therapists, which is directly tasked with implementing the new reciprocal licensure requirements for marriage and family therapists.
