The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to all state agencies, including the State Board of Professional Counselors and Therapists within MDH. They would be involved in drafting regulations and providing legal guidance on the enforcement of the new reciprocity standards.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the Department of Health. The Governor also signed this bill into law and is responsible for appointing members to various boards and commissions, including those that regulate health occupations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the definition of the Consumer Price Index (CPI) used by the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS) by replacing the obsolete 1967 base year index with the 1982–1984 base year index. This index is used to calculate cost-of-living adjustments for most system retirees.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Retirement and Pension System Board of Trustees
qa_results.stakeholders: Retirees of the State Retirement and Pension System (excluding those in the Judges’ Retirement System, Legislative Pension Plan, or former Governors) and the State Retirement Agency.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is technical in nature and has no material effect on State or local governmental operations or finances. The fiscal note indicates no material fiscal impact because the change simply corrects an obsolete reference used for cost-of-living adjustment calculations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Retirement Agency is the primary entity responsible for managing the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS). This bill directly updates the statutory definition of the Consumer Price Index that the agency must use to calculate cost-of-living adjustments (COLAs) for almost all of its retirees.

As the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM manages state employee benefits and engages in long-term fiscal planning. Changes to the pension system's cost-of-living calculation index are relevant to its oversight of personnel resources and budgetary forecasting.

The Maryland State Treasurer acts as a key trustee of the State Retirement and Pension System and is responsible for the management and protection of state funds, including those used to pay out retirement benefits affected by this technical change.

The Comptroller serves as the state's chief financial officer and principal tax collector, and is typically a member of boards overseeing significant state expenditures and the fiscal health of the state's retirement funds.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Retirement and Pension System and is responsible for ensuring that statutory updates, even technical ones like replacing an obsolete index, are correctly interpreted and legally sound.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and proposes the state budget, including the funding for the retirement system. The Governor signed this legislation into law as part of his role in administering state personnel policy.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the provision that limits pension benefits for members who retire within five years of transferring service credit between systems. It also corrects the member contribution rate reference for the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System and allows specific Department of Legislative Services employees with more than five years of contractual service to receive up to 10 years of service credit, provided they pay the required contributions or accept an actuarial reduction in benefits.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees of the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the State Retirement and Pension System, specifically those transferring service credit, members of the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System, and certain employees of the Department of Legislative Services.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no discernible impact on State pension liabilities or contribution rates. The provision for purchasing service credit applies to only two individuals and requires member contributions, which mitigates fiscal effects. There is no local fiscal impact, and the bill conforms certain statutes to current administrative practice.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Retirement Agency (SRA) is the primary agency responsible for the administration and management of the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS). The bill directly amends rules concerning service credit transfers within SRPS, corrects member contribution rates for LEOPS, and provides for specific service credit eligibility, all of which fall under SRA's operational mandate. SRA also requested the legislative changes to align statute with current administrative practices.

The Office of Legislative Audits is a unit within the Department of Legislative Services (DLS). The bill includes a specific, uncodified provision (Section 2) that grants eligibility and service credit in the Employees' Pension System to certain contractual employees of DLS, directly impacting personnel within its parent organization.

As the state's central personnel agency, the Department of Budget and Management manages state employee resources, benefits, and policies. Changes to service credit transfer rules and retirement eligibility for state employees intersect with its role in overseeing personnel management and the fiscal implications of employee benefits.

The Comptroller functions as the state's chief financial officer and serves as a key member of the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, providing oversight on financial matters and state expenditures related to employee benefits.

The Maryland State Treasurer serves as a principal member of the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System and is responsible for the management and protection of state funds, including those involved in the pension system's operations.

The bill corrects a reference to the member contribution rate for transfers of service into the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System (LEOPS). Maryland State Police officers are primary members of LEOPS and are affected by the technical accuracy of these pension rules.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill corrects a reference to the contribution rate for service credit transfers into the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System (LEOPS), which includes various law enforcement and correctional personnel employed by this department.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Harford County Board of License Commissioners to issue a Class GSF (golf simulator facility) alcoholic beverages license to facilities that are open to the public, have at least five golf simulator bays, and meet specific space dedication requirements. The license permits the sale of beer, wine, and liquor for on-premises consumption, provided that food is available at all times and the licensee meets specific annual gross sales targets: alcoholic beverage sales must not exceed 35%, simulator rentals must be at least 50%, and food sales must be at least 15% of total gross sales.
qa_results.responsible_party: Harford County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Golf simulator facility owners and operators in Harford County, patrons of golf simulator facilities, and the Harford County Board of License Commissioners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are not affected. Harford County revenues will increase by $1,500 for each Class GSF license issued starting in fiscal 2025 and by $1,500 annually for each renewal. The county can manage any additional enforcement activity using existing resources. Small business golf simulator facilities may see a meaningful increase in revenue from alcoholic beverage sales or be incentivized to open new locations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly authorizes and requires actions from the Board of License Commissioners for Harford County, which is a state-authorized administrative body tasked with regulating alcoholic beverages within the county.

The Comptroller is the state's principal tax collector and regulator of the alcohol industry. While the license is issued locally, the agency maintains oversight over the industry and tax revenue related to alcoholic beverages.

The fiscal note indicates a potential meaningful impact on small businesses, specifically golf simulator facilities, which falls under the Department of Commerce's mission to stimulate economic growth and support business retention and expansion.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the Governor signed the bill into law; additionally, the Governor's Office of Appointments typically handles the appointment of members to local boards of license commissioners.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Governing Board of the Maryland Longitudinal Data System (MLDS) Center to allow the Center to send student information, including Social Security numbers, programs of study, enrollment data, and the names of educational institutions, to the United States Census Bureau to support research projects such as the Post-Secondary Employment Outcomes project.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Longitudinal Data System (MLDS) Center Governing Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland students, educational institutions, the Maryland Longitudinal Data System (MLDS) Center, and the United States Census Bureau
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no effect on State revenues or local government finances. The MLDS Center can implement the data sharing requirements using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly concerns the governing board and operations of the MLDS Center, authorizing it to share specific student information with the U.S. Census Bureau for research and employment outcome tracking.
The bill modifies Section 24-703.2 of the Education Article, which dictates joint protocols between the MLDS Center and the Comptroller for data matching, handling, and security standards.
The MLDS Center is administratively housed within the Maryland State Department of Education, and the data managed by the Center includes information on students within MSDE's oversight.

The MLDS Center links student data from postsecondary institutions regulated by the Commission. The bill's intent (supporting projects like Post-Secondary Employment Outcomes) directly impacts higher education data reporting.
The MLDS Center links student data with workforce data provided by the Department of Labor to track employment outcomes, which is a key purpose of the data sharing authorized by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a performing arts nonprofit organization beer, wine, and liquor license in Harford County. The license allows qualifying nonprofit organizations that produce plays, dramas, concerts, or films to sell and serve alcoholic beverages for on-premises consumption at up to three designated locations during and around the time of performances. It also establishes record-keeping requirements, an annual license fee of $750, and specific penalty provisions for violations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Harford County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Performing arts nonprofit organizations in Harford County, theater and concert patrons, and licensed alcoholic beverage wholesalers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no impact on State finances. Harford County local revenues are estimated to increase by at least $750 annually beginning in fiscal year 2025, based on the projected issuance of at least one license. Additional local revenue may be collected from fines ranging between $250 and $2,000 for non-compliance. Local enforcement activities related to the new license can be managed with existing county resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly grants the Comptroller the authority to require reports and information from the new license holders, set the form and content requirements for their records, and audit or inspect those records. Additionally, the Comptroller is authorized to immediately suspend a license for up to 30 days for non-compliance with record-keeping requirements.

The Department of Commerce oversees the state's efforts regarding the arts. This bill establishes a new alcoholic beverages license specifically for performing arts nonprofit organizations, which impacts the economic and operational environment of the organizations the Department is tasked with supporting and promoting.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of state laws and the agencies involved (such as the Comptroller). The Governor also signed this bill into law.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Comptroller. The OAG would be responsible for providing legal advice regarding the Comptroller's new regulatory and suspension powers established by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the governing body of Allegany County or a municipal corporation within the county to grant a property tax credit for property owned by the Evergreen Heritage Center Foundation, Inc., or Evergreen Heritage Center Holdings, LLC.
qa_results.responsible_party: The governing body of Allegany County or a municipal corporation in Allegany County
qa_results.stakeholders: Evergreen Heritage Center Foundation, Inc., Evergreen Heritage Center Holdings, LLC, and Allegany County government
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Allegany County property tax revenues may decrease by approximately $5,300 annually beginning in fiscal 2025 to the extent the property tax credit is granted. State revenues and county expenditures are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for signing bills into law and oversaw the approval of this legislation which grants tax credit authority to Allegany County.

The Evergreen Heritage Center is a historic site listed on the Maryland Historical Trust (MHT) inventory. The Department of Planning oversees the MHT and programs related to historical and cultural preservation, which are relevant to the center's operations and viability.
The Maryland Tax Court is the highest level of administrative review for tax-related disputes, including those involving real property tax assessments and credits authorized by law.

This agency is responsible for hearing appeals regarding property tax assessments and various property tax credits. As this bill establishes a new optional property tax credit, the board would have jurisdiction over any disputes related to its application.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the Maryland income tax subtraction modification for qualifying police auxiliary and reserve volunteers from $5,000 to $7,000 and consolidates the program with existing modifications for other volunteer emergency personnel under the term "public safety volunteer." It also outlines reporting and record-keeping requirements for police agencies to certify eligible volunteers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Comptroller of Maryland, Maryland Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, and Maryland police agencies
qa_results.stakeholders: Volunteer police auxiliary and reserve members and Maryland police agencies
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues decrease by an estimated $21,000 annually starting in FY 2025, while local income tax revenues decrease by approximately $14,000 annually. The Comptroller’s Office can implement the changes with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the state's chief tax collector and is responsible for administering the Maryland income tax subtraction modification. The bill requires the Comptroller to receive annual reports from police agencies and other public safety organizations to verify eligibility for the subtraction.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires every Maryland police agency and the Maryland State Firemen's Association to submit annual reports to DPSCS listing the individuals who qualified for the tax subtraction. Additionally, the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission, which is associated with the department, must approve the volunteer programs.

The Adjutant General oversees the Maryland Defense Force. The bill includes the Maryland Defense Force in the definition of a 'public safety organization' and requires it to maintain records and report on members qualifying for the subtraction modification.

As a primary law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police must maintain records for its own auxiliary or reserve members, provide them with individual reports, and certify lists of qualified members to DPSCS and the Comptroller.

DBM is responsible for state revenue estimation and fiscal planning. The bill is projected to decrease general fund revenues by $21,000 annually starting in FY 2025, which impacts the state budget managed by this agency.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency promotes service and volunteerism. The bill incentivizes participation in volunteer public safety roles by increasing the value of the tax subtraction for police auxiliaries and reserve volunteers.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary coordinating body for public safety policy, the expansion of benefits for volunteer law enforcement personnel aligns with their mission to support law enforcement and develop criminal justice strategies.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a regulatory framework for pet insurance in Maryland, requiring specific disclosures for policy exclusions like preexisting conditions and chronic illnesses. It limits waiting periods for coverage, regulates the marketing of pet wellness programs to ensure they are distinct from insurance, and mandates that the Maryland Insurance Administration provide educational materials for veterinary offices.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Pet owners, pet insurance companies, insurance producers, and veterinary practitioners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Insurance Administration will experience a minimal increase in special fund revenues in fiscal 2025 due to rate and form filing fees. Enforcement and the development of informational materials are expected to be managed using existing resources. There is no estimated fiscal impact on local governments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Insurance Administration is the primary agency responsible for establishing the regulatory framework for pet insurance, enforcing disclosure requirements, and developing informational materials for veterinary practitioners.

The bill cross-references the Agriculture Article for the definition of 'veterinary practitioner' and concerns services provided by professionals within the department's broader agricultural and animal health scope.

The Office of Administrative Hearings is responsible for conducting the hearings required by the bill before the Insurance Commissioner can impose civil penalties or suspend an insurer's certificate of authority.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the primary legal representative and counsel for the Maryland Insurance Administration, which will be necessary for implementing regulations and conducting enforcement actions.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of new state laws and policy initiatives, including this new regulatory framework for the insurance industry.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill requires Maryland employers to disclose wage ranges and benefit descriptions in all public and internal job postings. It updates retaliation protections for employees seeking promotions or transfers and mandates that employers maintain compliance records for at least three years.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor, Commissioner of Labor and Industry
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland employers including private businesses and State and local government units, current employees, and job applicants.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund expenditures for the Maryland Department of Labor increase by $98,800 in fiscal 2025 for staffing and administration, with annual costs projected between $70,600 and $78,400 in subsequent years. Local governments and small businesses may face minimal operational impacts to comply with disclosure and recordkeeping requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Labor is the primary implementing and enforcement agency for this bill. The Commissioner of Labor and Industry, housed within this department, is responsible for developing compliance forms, investigating complaints, issuing compliance orders, and assessing civil penalties for violations.

As the central personnel agency for the State, DBM is responsible for managing state employee policies and pay scales. This bill requires all state units to disclose wage ranges and benefits in job postings, which will require coordination and policy updates by DBM for the executive branch workforce.

The OAG serves as the legal counsel for the Department of Labor and may be tasked with bringing enforcement actions against non-compliant employers. The fiscal note specifically identifies the need for a part-time contractual assistant Attorney General to assist with the implementation and interpretation of the bill.
The OAH is responsible for adjudicating contested cases and appeals related to civil penalties or compliance orders issued by the Commissioner of Labor and Industry under the State's labor laws.

The AOC provides operational and personnel support for the Judicial Branch. As the bill applies to the State and its units as employers, the AOC must ensure that all judicial job postings and recruitment processes comply with the new wage transparency requirements.

MDOT is one of the state's largest employers and operates with diverse modal administrations. The fiscal note explicitly identifies MDOT as an agency that must adjust its recruitment and recordkeeping procedures to comply with the bill.

USM represents 12 state institutions of higher education and is a major employer in the state. The fiscal note highlights that USM and its institutions must update their recruitment policies to meet the proactive disclosure requirements.

MSDE oversees the state's public school systems, which are explicitly mentioned in the fiscal note as entities that will face operational impacts and minimal expenditure increases to comply with the bill's disclosure and recordkeeping mandates.

The Comptroller's Office is identified as a source of information regarding the bill's impact on state operations. As a major state employer, the agency must also comply with the transparency and recordkeeping mandates for its own personnel.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and its units, all of which are defined as employers subject to the requirements of the bill. The office coordinates policy initiatives and will oversee the broad implementation of these labor standards across the state government.
As an independent state agency and employer, Morgan State University must update its internal and external job posting procedures and maintain compliance records as required by the bill.
As an independent unit of state government and an employer, the college is required to comply with the bill's mandates for wage range transparency in all employment opportunities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Labor to submit a report to the General Assembly by October 1, 2024, analyzing apprenticeship mentoring ratios for nontraditional occupations. The report must include participation data, an identification of occupations where higher ratios would not compromise worker safety, a five-year history of ratio waiver requests, and strategies to expand apprenticeship ratios to meet the goals of the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Apprentices and employers in nontraditional trade occupations, and the Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Labor can complete the required report using existing resources, resulting in no state or local fiscal impact. State and local revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency overseeing the Division of Workforce Development and Adult Learning and the Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council, the Department of Labor is directly responsible for conducting the study and submitting the mandated report on apprenticeship mentoring ratios.

The board serves as the Governor's chief policy-making body for the workforce system; the report's findings and recommended strategies to expand apprenticeship ratios directly inform the board's strategic vision for Maryland's talent pipeline.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This collective entity includes the Maryland Apprenticeship and Training Council, which receives the ratio waiver requests analyzed in the report, and the Apprenticeship 2030 Commission, whose goals the report aims to support.

The bill's goals are tied to the Blueprint for Maryland's Future, which aims for 45% of high school graduates to complete a registered apprenticeship. Additionally, the bill covers nontraditional occupations like Child Care Development Specialist, which intersect with educational oversight.
The MLDS Center links student and workforce data; it may be involved in providing or analyzing the data regarding the total number of apprentices and employer participation required for the report.

As the agency responsible for attracting businesses and enhancing Maryland's competitive position, the expansion of apprenticeship ratios in nontraditional trades (e.g., additive manufacturing, cybersecurity) is vital for the state's economic growth and business retention.

The bill specifically lists several IT and cybersecurity roles (e.g., Cybersecurity Specialist, System Security Analyst) as nontraditional trade occupations subject to the apprenticeship ratio analysis.

The Governor's Office coordinates high-level policy initiatives, including the Apprenticeship 2030 initiative, and oversees the executive agencies tasked with implementing these workforce expansion strategies.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Labor regarding the implementation of labor laws and the interpretation of regulations concerning apprenticeship standards.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a beer, wine, and liquor tasting (BWLT) permit in St. Mary’s County for Class A license holders. It allows for on-premises samples of beer, wine, and liquor for tasting purposes with specific daily volume limits per individual and sets permit fees based on the duration of the permit (26-day, 52-day, or 365-day licensing periods).
qa_results.responsible_party: St. Mary’s County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Class A alcoholic beverages license holders in St. Mary's County and consumers participating in tastings.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state fiscal impact. St. Mary's County local revenues will increase based on the number of BWLT permits issued, with fees set at $200 for a 26-day permit, $250 for a 52-day permit, and $300 for a 365-day permit. Any additional enforcement activity can be handled with existing county resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the principal state agency responsible for regulating the alcohol industry and enforcing state laws related to it. While this permit is issued locally by the St. Mary's County Board of License Commissioners, the bill amends the statewide Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article of the Maryland Code, which falls under the Comptroller's broad regulatory and revenue-oversight purview.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal representative for the state and provides counsel to state agencies and boards regarding the interpretation and enforcement of Maryland law. It would be responsible for advising on the legal parameters of the newly established permit within the Maryland Code and representing the state's regulatory interests.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill creates a specific beer and wine license for nonprofit performing arts theaters in St. Mary's County. It allows theaters operated by nonprofit organizations to sell or serve beer and wine for on-premises consumption from one hour before until one hour after a regular or fundraiser performance.
qa_results.responsible_party: St. Mary’s County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonprofit performing arts theaters in St. Mary's County and the St. Mary's County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: St. Mary’s County revenues are estimated to decrease by $350 annually beginning in fiscal 2025. This reduction occurs because the new $300 annual license is expected to replace the collection of approximately 65 per diem licenses which currently generate $650 annually. The county expects the revenue decrease to be offset by a reduction in administrative work from processing fewer licenses. There is no impact on state finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's chief executive, the Governor signed this bill into law and oversees the executive branch departments involved in regulatory enforcement and arts promotion.

The Comptroller is responsible for regulating the alcohol industry and enforcing tax laws related to alcoholic beverages. While this license is issued locally, the agency oversees the broader regulatory and taxation framework for beer and wine.

The Department of Commerce oversees tourism and the arts. This bill creates a specific license for nonprofit performing arts theaters, which directly impacts the economic and operational viability of cultural institutions within their purview.
The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state, providing legal counsel on the interpretation and enforcement of the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article of the Maryland Code.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires that regulations for large family child care homes and child care centers set the minimum age for child care teachers at 18 years old, prohibiting the exclusion of individuals from these positions based solely on being 18.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Large family child care homes, child care centers, and 18-year-old applicants for child care teacher positions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland State Department of Education can implement the bill with existing resources, resulting in no direct state or local fiscal impact. There is a potential meaningful positive impact on small businesses (child care providers) by expanding the pool of eligible applicants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) as it requires the State Board of Education to adopt regulations setting the minimum age for child care teachers in licensed centers and registered large family child care homes. MSDE is the primary agency responsible for child care licensing and early childhood development programs.

The bill modifies workforce entry requirements by lowering the minimum age for child care teachers from 19 to 18. This falls under the department's interests in workforce development and the regulation of professions and labor laws.

The Governor's Office for Children coordinates efforts to improve access to education and support services for children. Regulations affecting the staffing and operation of child care facilities are relevant to their mission of improving the well-being of Maryland's children.

This board is responsible for Maryland's talent pipeline. Lowering the minimum age requirement for child care teachers impacts the available workforce for the early childhood education sector, specifically providing job opportunities for individuals age 18.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (such as MSDE) that will implement these changes and signed the bill into law.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note identifies a potentially meaningful impact on small businesses, as child care centers and large family homes are often small businesses that will now have an expanded pool of eligible teacher applicants.

qa_results.bill_summary: Known as the Judge Andrew F. Wilkinson Judicial Security Act, this bill establishes protections for the personal information of Maryland and federal judges, magistrates, commissioners, and their families. It creates the Office of Information Privacy and the Judicial Address Confidentiality Program to handle requests for the removal of personal data from the internet and to shield residential addresses from public records. The bill also establishes a Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities and creates criminal penalties for knowingly publishing a protected individual's information with malicious intent.
qa_results.responsible_party: Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC)
qa_results.stakeholders: Current and retired Maryland and federal judges, magistrates, and District Court commissioners, as well as their spouses, children, and resident dependents.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the Judiciary can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. The legislation is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances, including expenditures or revenues from criminal fines and incarceration. Small business impacts are expected to be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates the Office of Information Privacy (OIP) within the Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC) and tasks the agency with establishing and administering the Judicial Address Confidentiality Program and providing staff for the Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities.

The bill amends the Real Property Article to integrate the new Judicial Address Confidentiality Program with the existing Address Confidentiality Program administered by the Secretary of State, including the coordination of program identification numbers.

As the chief legal officer, the OAG provides guidance on Public Information Act (PIA) compliance and would defend state agencies in civil actions brought under the bill for failure to remove or protect personal information.

The Superintendent of the Maryland State Police is a mandated member of the Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities, which is responsible for developing security standards for courthouses.
The Secretary of General Services serves on the task force and the agency manages state-owned facilities and security, directly impacting the physical security requirements the bill aims to improve.
The Secretary of Budget and Management is a member of the task force, and the agency oversees state personnel data and the fiscal implications of implementing new security standards.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Secretary is a member of the task force, as the agency manages security protocols and personnel relevant to the protection of judicial officers and court facilities.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Defender is designated as a member of the Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities to represent the legal stakeholder perspective in safety planning.

The Governor is responsible for appointing multiple members to the Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities, including representatives for county leadership, sheriffs, police chiefs, and the Maryland Association of Counties.

The Comptroller's office manages records that include protected personal information like Tax Identification Numbers and Social Security Numbers, making them a 'governmental entity' subject to privacy and removal requests under the bill.

The bill specifically protects vehicle license plate numbers and driver's license numbers, which are managed by the Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA) within MDOT, making the agency a critical stakeholder for privacy requests.
The State Archives is involved in the notification and shielding process for participants of the Address Confidentiality Program when it concerns land and real property records.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new executive task force, the Task Force to Ensure the Safety of Judicial Facilities, to study courthouse security and develop legislative proposals.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill lowers the minimum age requirement for a household member to qualify for a State Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefit from 62 to 60 and increases the minimum total monthly benefit amount from $40 to $50.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Human Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Low-income households in Maryland with members aged 60 and older receiving SNAP benefits.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund expenditures increase by 4200000 in fiscal 2025 to cover expanded eligibility and the increased minimum benefit; the fiscal 2025 budget includes this amount contingent on the bill's enactment. Starting in fiscal 2026, general fund expenditures increase by an estimated 3900000 annually. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Human Services is the primary agency responsible for administering the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) in Maryland. This bill directly amends the Human Services Article to modify the state-funded supplemental benefits that DHS is tasked with implementing and distributing.

The bill has a significant fiscal impact, requiring an increase of $4.2 million in general fund expenditures for FY 2025 and approximately $3.9 million annually thereafter. DBM is responsible for state budget development, supervision, and long-term fiscal planning related to these expenditures.

The bill specifically lowers the eligibility age for the state SNAP supplement to 60, directly impacting the quality of life and nutrition for the older adult population that the Department of Aging is mandated to serve and protect.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (such as DHS) implementing this change and is responsible for proposing the annual state budget that includes the funds required to support this expanded benefit.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Human Services and would be involved in reviewing any regulatory or policy updates necessitated by the changes to SNAP eligibility and benefit amounts.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill changes the salary of the Sheriff of Anne Arundel County. Starting with the next term of office, the Sheriff's annual salary will be set to match the salary of a captain in the Anne Arundel County Police Department at step 20 of the pay scale.
qa_results.responsible_party: Anne Arundel County Government
qa_results.stakeholders: The Sheriff of Anne Arundel County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on state revenue or expenditures. Anne Arundel County expenditures are projected to increase by a minimum of $20,100 in fiscal 2027 and by at least $34,400 annually thereafter, based on current salary scales and excluding fringe benefits.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office is responsible for signing or vetoing legislation passed by the General Assembly, including this bill which was approved and signed into law (Chapter 979).

The Sheriff of Anne Arundel County is a constitutional officer and typically participates in the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS). The agency is responsible for managing retirement and pension benefits, and alterations to the Sheriff's salary will affect the agency's records and future benefit calculations.

Sheriffs are part of the Maryland Judicial Branch as constitutional officers under the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides operational and legal support to the judicial system, which encompasses the functions and administrative framework of the Sheriff's office.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the General Assembly and executive branch. This office typically reviews legislation for legal and constitutional sufficiency, particularly regarding Article III, Section 35 of the Maryland Constitution, which governs salary changes for public officers during their term.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office serves as the primary coordinating body for public safety and advises the Governor on criminal justice strategies. As the Sheriff is a key member of the state's law enforcement community, the office monitors policy and administrative changes affecting law enforcement leadership.

The Secretary of State is the principal custodian of the state's official records, including laws passed by the General Assembly and signed by the Governor. This agency is responsible for processing the official record of this enacted bill.

The Maryland State Archives is the central depository for government records of permanent value, including legislative acts. This agency will be responsible for preserving the official record of the bill and updating the Maryland Manual On-Line.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the standards for calculating workers' compensation benefits for occupational hearing loss by adding the 4,000 hertz frequency to the required measurements and altering the formula used to deduct age-related (nonoccupational) hearing loss. It also specifies that tinnitus shall be considered part of a covered employee's hearing loss and may be compensable even in the absence of other hearing loss.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Workers' Compensation Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Covered employees with occupational hearing loss or tinnitus, employers, and workers' compensation insurers including the State and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State, local, and Chesapeake Employers’ Insurance Company expenditures are expected to increase minimally starting in fiscal 2025 due to updated hearing loss calculations. Based on a preliminary analysis of historical claims, the modification to nonoccupational deafness calculations would increase the cost of each individual claim by an average of $12,620. Chesapeake revenues are expected to increase as premiums are raised to cover these additional liabilities.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Commission is the primary agency responsible for administering the state's Workers' Compensation Law and adjudicating claims. This bill directly alters the criteria and calculation methods the Commission must use to determine disability awards for hearing loss.

The IWIF (Chesapeake Employers' Insurance Company) is the third-party administrator for workers' compensation claims for State of Maryland employees and the state's largest insurer of such claims. The bill's changes to benefit calculations will directly impact the claims it processes and its financial liabilities.

The Subsequent Injury Fund handles workers' compensation claims involving pre-existing health conditions. Since hearing loss calculations often involve pre-existing nonoccupational loss versus current occupational exposure, the altered calculation methods will impact the fund's investigations and liability assessments.

The Department of Labor is responsible for protecting workers and enforcing labor laws in Maryland. Changes to workers' compensation benefits fall under the broader umbrella of labor protections and workplace safety regulations overseen by this department.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for the state budget. The fiscal note indicates a minimal increase in state expenditures (all funds) due to higher workers' compensation costs for state employees under the new calculation criteria.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Workers' Compensation Commission and represents state agencies in legal matters. They may be required to interpret or defend the new statutory requirements for hearing loss calculations in contested cases.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and sign bills into law. This bill modifies state personnel liability and agency administrative procedures within the executive department.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill amends the Maryland Right to Try Act to expand patient eligibility to those with life-threatening or severely debilitating illnesses, replacing the previous requirement for a terminal illness. It transitions the scope of the Act from general investigational drugs to 'individualized investigational treatments' tailored to a patient's genetic profile, such as gene therapy. The bill also removes the prohibition on manufacturers profiting from these treatments and allows, but does not require, insurance carriers and government agencies to cover associated costs.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health, Office of the Attorney General, and State health occupations boards
qa_results.stakeholders: Patients with life-threatening or severely debilitating illnesses, medical researchers and manufacturers, healthcare providers, and health insurance carriers
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local operations or finances. While government agencies and insurance carriers are authorized to provide coverage for individualized investigational treatments, they are not mandated to do so. Additionally, the bill repeals the previous prohibition on manufacturers profiting from the provision of these treatments and specifies that hospitals or health care facilities are not required to provide additional services unless they approve them.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically mandates the Office of the Attorney General to develop the informed consent form that must be used by physicians and patients seeking individualized investigational treatments.

The Department is prohibited from taking action against a provider's Medicare certification and oversees the various health occupations boards which are restricted from disciplining providers for recommending these treatments.

The agency regulates health insurance carriers and third-party administrators, who are authorized under the bill to provide coverage for individualized investigational treatments and related services.

As the agency responsible for managing the State Employee Health Insurance Plan, DBM is impacted by the bill's provision allowing government agencies and carriers to provide coverage for these treatments.
The bill explicitly states that it does not create a private cause of action against manufacturers or medical providers, which impacts the state's judicial branch and potential litigation.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the various agencies (MDH, OAG) responsible for implementing the policy changes and legal requirements established by this Act.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland's public four-year higher education institutions, excluding the University of Maryland Global Campus, to develop and update annual policies for targeted recruitment and outreach to 'communities of interest.' These communities include groups, demographics, or geographic areas historically underrepresented in higher education enrollment. Institutions must conduct specific outreach activities, such as establishing community partnerships and holding recruitment events, and submit an annual report to the Governor and General Assembly regarding their efforts and admission data for these communities.
qa_results.responsible_party: Public senior higher education institutions (excluding University of Maryland Global Campus), including constituent institutions of the University System of Maryland, Morgan State University, and St. Mary's College of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Historically underrepresented student populations, geographic communities underrepresented in higher education enrollment, and Maryland public four-year higher education institutions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, state higher education expenditures will increase for at least some institutions to hire additional staff for recruitment and reporting. While impacts vary by institution, Towson University estimates an annual cost of $500,000 for additional staff and programming. Other institutions, such as Morgan State University, expect to implement the requirements using existing resources, while the University of Maryland, Baltimore County may require a half-time position. The total statewide fiscal impact is indeterminate and depends on the specific policies adopted by each institution.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a primary subject of the bill, the University System of Maryland's constituent institutions (excluding UMGC) are required to adopt outreach policies, conduct targeted recruitment, and submit annual reports on these efforts.
Morgan State University is explicitly mandated by the bill to implement targeted recruitment and outreach efforts for communities of interest and to report annually on these activities.
As a public senior higher education institution, St. Mary's College of Maryland is directly required to comply with the bill's provisions regarding recruitment policies and annual reporting.
As the state's coordinating board for higher education, the commission oversees the policies and principles of public higher education institutions, which are being amended by this bill.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and public universities; it will likely review the new written policies for compliance with state and federal law, particularly regarding diversity and inclusion.
The Governor's Office is a recipient of the annual reports required by the bill and is responsible for coordinating the policy initiatives that the bill addresses.
The fiscal note indicates that some institutions may require additional staff to implement the bill's requirements, which involves DBM in its role as the state's fiscal and personnel agency.
The bill encourages higher education institutions to establish recruitment partnerships with local school systems and specific high schools, which fall under the oversight of MSDE.

The bill requires the collection and reporting of application and admission data for specific communities of interest, which intersects with the center's mission to link and analyze student performance data.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Radiation Emergency Response Program within the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE), designating it as the lead state agency for off-site response to nuclear incidents. It requires owners of nuclear power plants to pay annual fees to fund state emergency preparedness and program operations, and creates the Radiation Emergency Response Fund as a dedicated special fund for these activities.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners of nuclear power plants (such as Constellation Energy) and the general public, particularly residents located within nuclear emergency planning zones.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, special fund revenues and expenditures for the Maryland Department of the Environment increase by $600,000 annually beginning in fiscal 2025, driven by a $300,000 annual fee per nuclear power plant. Additionally, $120,000 in fees will be collected annually for the Maryland Department of Emergency Management; however, this results in no net fiscal impact for that agency as it replaces existing voluntary payments from industry stakeholders.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Radiation Emergency Response Program within the Department of the Environment, designating it as the lead State agency for protective actions during nuclear incidents and tasking it with plan development, equipment procurement, and fee collection.

The agency receives transferred fees from the Department of the Environment for radiological emergency response preparedness and is responsible for incorporating technical radiation response plans into the State Emergency Management Program.
The bill mandates the State Treasurer to hold the Radiation Emergency Response Fund separately and invest the money, with interest earnings being credited back to the fund.
The bill requires the Comptroller to account for the newly created Radiation Emergency Response Fund, which holds the fees collected from nuclear power plant owners.
As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, it will supervise the budget execution and personnel requirements for the new special fund and the Radiation Emergency Response Program.

The office provides legal counsel to the implementing agencies (MDE and MDEM) and represents the state in matters concerning the new technical emergency radiation response plans and regulatory fee requirements.

The Board oversees and approves significant State expenditures and procurement contracts; the bill specifically tasks MDE with procuring specialized supplies and equipment for nuclear incident response.

qa_results.bill_summary: Establishes the Maryland Aerospace and Technology Commission within the Department of Commerce to promote innovation in space exploration and commercial aerospace. The commission is tasked with identifying research and funding opportunities, creating a strategic plan, designating aerospace and technology zones, and fostering educational partnerships.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Commerce
qa_results.stakeholders: Aerospace and technology industry representatives, institutions of higher education, Maryland workforce, and local communities in proximity to aerospace facilities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $161,400 in FY 2025 for staffing and consultant costs to develop a strategic plan. Annual expenditures for staff are estimated to range between approximately $103,600 and $117,900 in subsequent years. There is no direct impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Maryland Aerospace and Technology Commission within the Department of Commerce and requires the Department to provide staff support and administrative oversight for the Commission.
The Commission's board includes the Presidents (or their designees) of the University of Maryland Baltimore County and the University of Maryland, College Park, both of which are USM institutions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new permanent commission (the Maryland Aerospace and Technology Commission) tasked with conducting studies, identifying funding, and providing recommendations to the Governor and General Assembly.
The Governor is responsible for appointing several members of the Commission and the Commission must submit an annual report of its activities and strategic plan to the Governor.
This office will be responsible for the recruitment, vetting, and processing of the industry and educational representatives appointed by the Governor to the new Commission.

The bill has a direct fiscal impact requiring a general fund expenditure increase for the Department of Commerce to hire a new program manager and fund consultant services, which requires DBM oversight.
The bill explicitly states that the Commission and its members are subject to the Maryland Public Ethics Law, which is administered and enforced by the State Ethics Commission.

The Commission is mandated to identify research and funding opportunities to capitalize, promote, and assist in the development of workforce training for space exploration technologies, aligning with the Department of Labor's mission.

The bill's definition of an 'Institution of Higher Education' requires meeting standards prescribed by the Maryland Higher Education Commission, and the board includes a representative of institutions of higher education.
As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG would provide legal counsel to the newly formed Maryland Aerospace and Technology Commission.

The Commission's mission to promote innovation and identify research funding in aerospace and emerging technologies overlaps with TEDCO's role in fostering technology-based businesses and commercialization.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Governor to include an annual appropriation of $5,000,000 for the Historical and Cultural Museum Assistance Program within the Maryland Historical Trust and repeals the restriction that prevented museums receiving operating support from the Maryland State Arts Council from also receiving these grants.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Historical Trust
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonprofit museums, political subdivisions (local governments), and small business vendors providing services such as exhibit design and strategic planning.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in an estimated $5,000,000 annual increase in general fund expenditures and special fund revenues/expenditures beginning in fiscal 2026. Approximately $4,811,029 will be available for grants annually after accounting for $188,971 in administrative costs to fund two full-time staff positions. Local government revenues will increase through the receipt of grant awards.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Historical Trust (MHT), which administers the Historical and Cultural Museum Assistance Program, is a unit within the Department of Planning. The agency is directly responsible for managing the program, reviewing applications, and distributing the authorized grant funding.

The bill authorizes a $5 million annual appropriation and, according to the fiscal note, will require the creation of two new full-time administrative positions. DBM is responsible for budget development and managing state personnel resources.
The bill explicitly authorizes the Governor to include an appropriation of $5,000,000 in the annual budget bill for the Historical and Cultural Museum Assistance Program.

The bill requires the Museum Assistance Review Panel to seek the advice and recommendations of the State Archivist when reviewing grant applications related to the care, preservation, or access of archival material.

The bill repeals a prohibition that prevented museums receiving support from the Maryland State Arts Council (housed within the Department of Commerce) from also receiving grants from the Maryland Historical Trust.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill outlines the duties and membership requirements of the Museum Assistance Review Panel, which serves as an advisory body for the grant program.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the membership requirements for the Adult Protective Services Review Board in Charles County by allowing a physician's assistant or nurse practitioner specializing in psychiatry to serve on the board instead of a psychiatrist.
qa_results.responsible_party: Charles County Board of County Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Vulnerable adults in Charles County, the Charles County Adult Protective Services Review Board, and psychiatric medical professionals.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill does not materially affect Maryland State or Charles County government operations or finances. While board members may be reimbursed for expenses, they are not entitled to compensation, and the change in membership qualifications is not expected to create a financial impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Human Services (DHS) is the primary state agency for Adult Protective Services. The bill alters the membership of Adult Protective Services Review Boards, which are responsible for reviewing guardianships held by public agencies (often the local director of social services, which falls under DHS oversight).

The bill impacts Adult Protective Services Review Boards, which must include a representative of a local commission on aging. These boards oversee guardianships for vulnerable adults, many of whom are older adults served by the Department of Aging's programs.

The review boards affected by this bill are required to include a professional in the field of disabilities and a person with a physical disability. The board's work in reviewing guardianships for vulnerable adults directly intersects with the Department of Disabilities' mission to support individuals with disabilities in integrated settings.

The bill specifically alters requirements for psychiatric health professionals (psychiatrists, nurse practitioners, and physician assistants in psychiatry) serving on a state-mandated board. MDH regulates health care professionals and provides behavioral health services relevant to the populations served by these boards.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Adult Protective Services Review Boards are state-mandated local bodies authorized by the General Assembly to provide expertise and oversight for public guardianships.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies like the Department of Human Services on the implementation of the Family Law Article, which governs these review boards.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Renters’ Rights and Stabilization Act of 2024 establishes the Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs within the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) to develop a Maryland Tenants’ Bill of Rights. The bill limits residential security deposits to a maximum of one month's rent in most cases, increases surcharges for eviction filings and other civil cases to fund rental assistance and legal services, and generally prohibits landlords from passing these surcharge costs to tenants. Additionally, it grants tenants of properties with three or fewer units a right of first refusal and an exclusive negotiation period to purchase the property before it is sold to a third party.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential tenants, landlords, the Maryland Judiciary, and the Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, state special fund revenues are estimated to increase by up to 13300000 in fiscal 2025 and 17700000 annually thereafter due to increased court surcharges. General fund expenditures increase by a total of 379300 in fiscal 2025 to cover Judiciary programming and the establishment of the Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs within DHCD. Local revenues, particularly in Baltimore City, may decrease minimally if the increased surcharges lead to a reduction in the volume of eviction filings.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, requiring the Judiciary to assess and collect increased surcharges in civil and landlord-tenant cases, implement stays of execution for warrants of restitution during extreme weather, and collect and share comprehensive eviction data with the Department of Housing and Community Development.

The bill establishes an Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs authorized to conduct fair housing testing and notify authorities regarding housing discrimination. These activities directly impact the Commission's mandate to enforce anti-discrimination laws in housing.

As the principal fiscal agency, the Department is impacted by the bill's requirement for general fund expenditures and the creation of three new staff positions within the Department of Housing and Community Development to support the new Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs.

The bill mandates that 10% of certain court surcharge revenues be deposited into the Rental Assistance for Community Schools Families Fund, a program established under the Education Article to assist student households at community schools.

This is the primary implementing agency. The bill establishes the Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs within the Department, requires it to publish the Maryland Tenants’ Bill of Rights, and tasks the Department with managing rental vouchers and monitoring property transfer and eviction data.
The bill links the maximum allowable security deposit for residential leases to a tenant's eligibility for utility assistance programs administered by the Department of Human Services.

As a departmental bill introduced by request of the Administration, this legislation aligns with the Governor's policy priorities regarding child poverty and housing stability. The Governor also appoints the Secretary of the implementing department.

The bill's primary goal of reducing child poverty through housing stability and prioritizing rental assistance for families with young children is directly relevant to the core mission of the Governor's Office for Children.

The bill's focus on reducing child poverty and enhancing economic mobility through evidence-based housing interventions directly aligns with the Innovation Team's specific charge to address the Governor's top priorities in these areas.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Housing and Community Development and will receive referrals from the new Office of Tenant and Landlord Affairs regarding unfair or illegal housing practices and discrimination.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes that buyers in commercial real estate transactions have the right to select their own title insurance company, settlement company, escrow company, or title lawyer. It requires real estate contracts for commercial properties to include a bold-faced statement notifying buyers of these rights and specifying that sellers are not prohibited from offering owner financing. Additionally, the bill prohibits real estate brokers and salespersons from requiring buyers to use specific service providers as a condition of settlement.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Real Estate Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Buyers and sellers of commercial real estate (including properties with five or more single-family units or those zoned for commercial, industrial, or multifamily use), real estate brokers, salespersons, title lawyers, and title, settlement, and escrow companies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not materially affect the finances or operations of the Maryland Real Estate Commission or local governments. The fiscal impact on small businesses is expected to be minimal, and no specific revenue changes or expenditure mandates are identified in the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill regulates real estate brokers, associate brokers, and salespersons by establishing new requirements for commercial real estate contracts and prohibiting certain mandatory service selections. These professionals are licensed and regulated by the Maryland Real Estate Commission, which is a division of the Maryland Department of Labor.

The OAG serves as the legal counsel for the Department of Labor and the Maryland Real Estate Commission. They would be responsible for providing legal advice on the implementation and enforcement of these new requirements in the Business Occupations and Professions Article.

The bill grants buyers the right to select their own title insurance company in commercial transactions. While the bill's requirements are directed at real estate brokers, the Maryland Insurance Administration regulates the title insurance companies and producers mentioned in the bill.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for enforcing these laws (Department of Labor) and the Governor signed this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the State Board of Elections (SBE) to develop and implement a marketing campaign for recruiting election judges and to standardize forms and documentation for their service. It mandates that county employees who serve as election judges during work hours receive administrative leave and compensation. Additionally, it defines 'returning election judge' to narrow eligibility for additional compensation and allows public school students to earn both service-learning hours and compensation for serving as election judges.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Elections
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State and county employees, public school students, current and prospective election judges, the State Board of Elections, and local boards of elections.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures are estimated to decrease by approximately $190,100 annually beginning in FY 2026. Local government expenditures are projected to increase by $25,000 in FY 2025 for programming costs, followed by an annual decrease of approximately $190,100 starting in FY 2026. These savings result from a narrower definition of 'returning election judge,' which reduces the number of judges eligible for the mandated $100 daily pay increase.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill primary impacts the State Board of Elections by requiring it to implement a recruitment marketing campaign, adopt regulations to standardize and minimize election judge forms, and manage the reimbursement program for local boards paying returning election judges.

The bill explicitly allows public school students to earn service-learning hours toward graduation requirements while also receiving compensation for serving as election judges, affecting MSDE's oversight of educational policies and graduation standards.

The bill modifies personnel policy by mandating that State employees receive administrative leave for serving as election judges, changing the previous permissive 'may use' language to a mandatory 'shall receive' requirement.

As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG provides counsel to the State Board of Elections on election law revisions and will be responsible for reviewing the legality of the new standardized form regulations required by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill exempts minor league baseball players from the Maryland Wage and Hour Law, provided they are compensated under a collective bargaining agreement that explicitly states their wages and working conditions.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Minor league baseball players and minor league baseball club employers in Maryland
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not substantively change State or local operations or finances. The Maryland Department of Labor reported no history of receiving Wage and Hour Law complaints from minor league baseball players.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Labor is the primary agency responsible for enforcing the state's Wage and Hour Law. This bill directly amends the Labor and Employment Article to create a new exemption for minor league baseball players, which the department's Division of Labor and Industry must account for in its regulatory and enforcement duties.

The Maryland Stadium Authority plans, builds, and manages professional sports facilities in the state. As the primary agency supporting the professional sports industry in Maryland, changes to labor laws specifically targeting professional athletes like minor league baseball players are relevant to their mission of maintaining the economic viability of sports franchises in the state.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal counsel for all state agencies, including the Department of Labor. They would be responsible for providing legal interpretations of this new exemption and representing the state in any civil challenges related to the Maryland Wage and Hour Law.

The Maryland Wage and Hour Law allows employees to bring civil actions against employers for unpaid wages. By creating a specific exemption for minor league baseball players under collective bargaining agreements, this bill defines the scope of potential litigation within the state court system overseen by the Administrative Office of the Courts.

As the state's primary economic development agency, the Department of Commerce is concerned with the business environment and the retention of industries such as professional sports. This labor law exemption affects the operating conditions of minor league baseball teams as business entities in Maryland.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows a licensed real estate broker to designate himself or herself as the manager of a branch office maintained by the broker. Previously, the manager was required to be a licensed associate broker or a qualified real estate salesperson; this change enables brokers to supervise their own branch offices directly.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Real estate Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed real estate brokers, real estate associate brokers, real estate salespersons, and small real estate businesses.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Real Estate Commission can implement the bill's changes using existing resources, and State revenues are not affected. There is no local fiscal impact. Small businesses may experience a meaningful positive impact as the bill may reduce costs associated with opening or maintaining a branch office by eliminating the requirement to hire a separate manager.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Labor oversees the Maryland Real Estate Commission, which is the primary body responsible for licensing real estate brokers, issuing branch office certificates, and regulating the management of those offices as specified in the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Labor and its boards and commissions, including advice on the implementation and enforcement of new licensing and regulatory requirements.

The Office of Administrative Hearings adjudicates contested cases and regulatory disputes for state agencies, including those involving professional licensing decisions made by the Department of Labor and the Real Estate Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Household Goods Movers Registration Fund as a special, non-lapsing fund within the Maryland Department of Labor. It mandates that the department set registration fees for household goods movers based on the annual actual costs of regulating the industry, with annual fee increases limited to 12.5%. The fund will be used to cover the direct and indirect administrative costs of fulfilling the department's regulatory duties regarding household goods moving services.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Household goods movers and moving services companies
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no net effect on State finances but shifts revenues and expenditures from the General Fund to a new special fund. The Department of Legislative Services previously estimated average annual expenditures of $265,600 for the regulation of household goods movers. The Maryland Department of Labor anticipates registering approximately 870 movers with an annual fee of about $325 to cover these costs.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Labor is the primary agency responsible for the registration of household goods movers and will house and administer the newly created Household Goods Movers Registration Fund.
The bill explicitly requires the Department of Labor to pay all collected registration fees to the Comptroller, who is then responsible for distributing those fees into the special fund.
The legislation mandates that the Legislative Auditor audit the accounts and transactions of the newly established Household Goods Movers Registration Fund.
As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM is impacted by the transition of the household goods movers regulatory program from general fund support to a special fund structure.
The Treasurer's Office manages the state's funds and investments; the bill specifies that investment earnings from the new fund must be credited to the General Fund.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Labor regarding the implementation of the new fund and the establishment of regulatory fees.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments involved and the broader administrative transition of regulatory activities to the new special fund.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Maryland Department of Natural Resources to issue lifetime complimentary hunting and fishing licenses, and exemptions from trout stamps, to veterans whom the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs has determined to be unemployable. This privilege applies to Maryland residents and out-of-state residents whose states offer similar reciprocal benefits.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Veterans determined to be unemployable by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Maryland residents, and eligible out-of-state veterans
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues are expected to decrease annually starting in FY 2025 because qualifying veterans will no longer pay fees for hunting and fishing licenses.
