MEDCO is a quasi-governmental organization that supports economic development, financing, and real estate development. The Authority's goals in Allegany County mirror MEDCO's mission to assist in business attraction and economic prosperity.
The bill allows the Authority to manage funds appropriated from the State and grants members reimbursement for expenses under standard state travel regulations, which are overseen by DBM.

The Authority is involved in land-use planning and infrastructure development for recreational facilities, which intersects with the Department of Planning's role in promoting sustainable growth and providing technical assistance to local governments.
The Authority includes the presidents of Frostburg State University and Allegany College. MHEC coordinates policy and provides oversight for these postsecondary institutions.

Canal Place is located in Cumberland (Allegany County) and is focused on heritage tourism and recreation. The development of new regional recreational facilities in the county may impact or complement Canal Place's operations.
As a state entity empowered to manage state funds and staff, the Authority would be subject to the periodic fiscal compliance audits conducted by the Office of Legislative Audits.

Members of the Authority, as public officials of a state-created entity, would likely be subject to the administration and enforcement of the Maryland Public Ethics Law by the State Ethics Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the County Commissioners of Carroll County to borrow up to $29,800,000 through the issuance of general obligation bonds to finance various public facility projects. These projects include construction and improvements for public schools, roads, bridges, water and sewer infrastructure, and Carroll Community College. The funds may also be used to provide loans to volunteer fire departments for equipment and facilities, and to acquire agricultural land and woodland preservation easements.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Carroll County
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Carroll County, volunteer fire departments, public school students and staff, Carroll Community College, and owners of agricultural land or woodland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Carroll County will receive up to $29,800,000 in bond proceeds. Debt service expenditures are estimated to increase by approximately $2,100,000 annually over a 20-year period, based on a 3.37% interest rate. The bill has no impact on State finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a commission county, Carroll County requires General Assembly authorization to issue bonds; the Governor must sign this enabling legislation into law.
The bill exempts the bond interest from state taxation, which falls under the Comptroller's jurisdiction as the state's chief tax collector.
A significant portion of the bond proceeds ($20.9 million) is dedicated to public school construction and systemic renovations, which are overseen by this commission.
The department oversees the public school system and facilities that will benefit from over $20 million in funding for HVAC, roofing, and additions.
The bill authorizes funding for roads, bridges, and sidewalk upgrades, which are part of the state's broader transportation infrastructure overseen by MDOT.
The bill funds water and sewer projects as well as stormwater and watershed assessment projects (NPDES) regulated by this department.
The bill provides for the acquisition of agricultural land preservation easements through the Maryland Agriculture Land Preservation Program (MALPF), managed by this department.
The department coordinates land use policy and state planning visions related to the agricultural preservation and sustainable development projects funded by the bonds.
The bill explicitly authorizes the use of bond proceeds for Carroll Community College buildings and facilities, which fall under the commission's coordination.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the legal procedures for individuals committed to the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) after being found not criminally responsible. It applies Maryland Rules for civil discovery to administrative release proceedings, requires courts and the MDH to notify a committed person's counsel if a violation of release conditions is reported, and mandates a court hearing upon request when an application is made to change the terms of a conditional release. It also clarifies that courts may shorten or terminate conditional releases and establishes the burden of proof for applicants.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals committed to state custody after being found not criminally responsible, the Maryland Department of Health, the Office of the Public Defender, State's Attorneys, and the Maryland Judiciary.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures for the Maryland Department of Health increase by up to $377,041 in fiscal 2025. This funding supports six new positions, including paralegals and social workers, to handle increased discovery workloads and additional court hearings. Costs are projected to grow to approximately $500,600 by fiscal 2029. The bill is not expected to have a significant fiscal impact on local governments, though it may improve operational efficiency for State's Attorneys' offices.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) as it governs the procedures for individuals committed to the department after being found not criminally responsible (NCR). The bill requires MDH to notify courts and counsel of violations, provide various clinical reports and evaluations upon request, and participate in modified release and discovery proceedings. The fiscal note highlights a significant expenditure increase for MDH to hire staff to manage these new procedural requirements.

The bill mandates changes to judicial proceedings within the court system, including new notification duties for the court, the application of civil discovery rules to release hearings, and requirements to hold hearings upon request. The AOC is responsible for the operational and technological support of the courts implementing these legislative changes.

OAH is responsible for conducting the administrative hearings for committed persons to determine eligibility for release. The bill's requirement that Maryland Rules governing civil discovery apply to these proceedings directly alters the adjudication process managed by OAH administrative law judges.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender represents most indigent defendants committed to MDH. The bill's provisions regarding the right to receive reports and assessments, and the change in discovery rules, significantly impact the legal strategy and workload of OPD attorneys representing committed persons in release and revocation proceedings.

As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health. The bill's expansion of discovery requirements in administrative and judicial settings will likely require OAG to represent MDH in legal disputes regarding the disclosure of clinical records and compliance with the new rules.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill limits the authority of local governments to impose zoning restrictions on cannabis dispensaries and outdoor growers, prohibiting requirements more restrictive than those for retail alcoholic beverage dealers or hemp farms. It modifies distance requirements for dispensaries, adding places of worship to the list of protected locations and allowing local jurisdictions to increase the minimum distance between dispensaries to up to one-half mile. Additionally, it establishes a formal process for at least 10 residents or property owners within 1,000 feet of a licensee to protest a license renewal, requiring the Maryland Cannabis Administration to hold a hearing unless the protest is found to be groundless.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Cannabis Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed cannabis dispensaries and outdoor growers, local government planning and zoning departments, and residents or commercial tenants near cannabis establishments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund expenditures for the Maryland Cannabis Administration are expected to increase minimally, with estimated costs of $44,100 in FY 2028 and $78,700 in FY 2029 to manage the license renewal protest process. These expenditures are to be covered by cannabis sales and use tax revenues, potentially resulting in a minimal reduction in tax distributions to the State General Fund and local jurisdictions. Local governments are expected to handle zoning changes within existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Cannabis Administration (MCA) is the primary agency responsible for the regulation and oversight of the cannabis industry. The bill directly impacts their operations by establishing new procedures for license renewal protests, requiring the administration to conduct hearings, and modifying distance and zoning restrictions that MCA must enforce.

The fiscal note indicates that the Maryland Cannabis Administration currently delegates hearings to the Office of Administrative Hearings (OAH). The bill's requirement for hearings on license renewal protests will likely result in an increased workload for OAH.

The Comptroller is responsible for the distribution of cannabis sales and use tax revenues. Since the bill may increase MCA's administrative costs, it could affect the amount of tax revenue remaining for distribution to the general fund and other special funds managed by the Comptroller.

The Department of Health receives 5% of adult-use cannabis tax revenues for the Cannabis Public Health Fund. Any changes to the distribution of these revenues caused by increased administrative costs for MCA would impact MDH's funding.

The Department of Commerce receives 5% of adult-use cannabis tax revenues for the Cannabis Business Assistance Fund. The bill's fiscal impact on the distribution of tax revenues would directly affect the funds available to Commerce for this program.

The bill explicitly references zoning requirements for outdoor cannabis cultivation in comparison to hemp farms registered under Title 14 of the Agriculture Article, which is overseen by the Department of Agriculture.

The bill places significant restrictions on the zoning authority of political subdivisions regarding cannabis dispensaries and growers. The Department of Planning provides data analysis and technical assistance to local governments on land use and zoning policies affected by this legislation.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Maryland Cannabis Administration and would likely be involved in advising on the implementation of the new protest and hearing regulations.
The bill modifies distance requirements for dispensaries relative to primary and secondary schools and licensed child care centers, which fall under the oversight of the Department of Education.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for monitoring the impact on general fund revenues, which the fiscal note indicates could be minimally affected by the redistribution of cannabis tax revenue.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Maryland Cannabis Administration, and the Governor signed this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Physician Assistant Modernization Act of 2024 transitions Maryland's regulation of physician assistants (PAs) from a supervision-based model to a collaboration-based model. It replaces delegation agreements with collaboration agreements, allowing PAs to perform medical acts authorized by their license and agreement as part of a patient care team. The bill also updates education and licensure standards, expands the PA scope of practice, and increases the maximum ratio of PAs to collaborating physicians from four to eight.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Board of Physicians
qa_results.stakeholders: Physician assistants, patient care team physicians, hospitals, ambulatory surgical facilities, federally qualified health centers, and healthcare consumers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The State Board of Physicians can implement the bill using existing budgeted resources. According to the Fiscal Note, State special fund revenues are not expected to be materially affected; while the board will lose revenue from the current $200 delegation agreement fee, it retains the authority to set new fees for collaboration agreements and advanced duty approvals. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill primarily concerns the licensure and regulation of physician assistants, which is overseen by the State Board of Physicians, a unit within the Department of Health. The department is responsible for managing the transition from delegation agreements to collaboration agreements and updating the list of advanced duties.

The bill explicitly authorizes the Office of Controlled Substances Administration to enter and inspect the offices where physician assistants practice to ensure compliance with drug dispensing and labeling requirements.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Physician Assistant Advisory Committee is specifically tasked with reviewing collaboration agreements, recommending regulations to the Board of Physicians, and assisting in updating the list of advanced duties.

The bill amends the definition of 'certifying provider' within the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article to include physician assistants with active collaboration agreements, impacting who can certify patients for medical cannabis.

The bill amends the Transportation Article to update the definition of a licensed physician assistant, which affects their authority to certify applications for disability plates and placards managed by the MVA.

The bill amends the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article to include physician assistants in the definition of 'health care provider' for the purposes of health care malpractice claims and arbitration.

The bill alters requirements in the Education Article regarding who may complete physical examinations for students, transitioning the requirement for physician assistants from delegation agreements to collaboration agreements.

The bill amends definitions in the Education Article related to physician assistants (Section 18-802), which impacts the administration of state financial aid and scholarship programs for health professionals managed by MHEC.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifies practice settings and ratios for physician assistants within correctional facilities, allowing for higher ratios of assistants to physicians in these settings compared to standard medical offices.
The bill includes provisions for physician assistants to perform acts and functions during a disaster as defined by the Governor, which falls under the state's emergency coordination managed by MDEM.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Board of Physicians and the Department of Health regarding the implementation of the new collaboration agreement framework and handling related disciplinary or legal challenges.
As the parent department for the Motor Vehicle Administration, MDOT is affected by the statutory changes to the Transportation Article regarding physician assistant definitions for vehicle services.

The bill authorizes physician assistants who are employees of the federal government (such as those serving in military or veterans' facilities) to perform medical tasks during a declared disaster in Maryland.
The Governor's role in defining a 'disaster' triggers the authorization for physician assistants to practice under emergency protocols. The Governor also signed this bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill redefines 'aerial survey' as 'SAV survey' to allow for the use of alternative data and methodologies for mapping submerged aquatic vegetation, provided they meet EPA-approved standards and are approved by the Department of Natural Resources. It also requires the Department to study the feasibility of using these alternative methods to delineate protection zones and report its findings to the General Assembly by December 1, 2025.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Commercial shellfish harvesters using dredges or rakes, aquaculture lease applicants, and the Maryland Department of Natural Resources.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Department of Natural Resources can complete the required study and report using existing budgeted resources. There is no immediate impact on State expenditures unless the department chooses to implement additional survey methods, which would increase general fund expenditures by an indeterminate amount. The Department currently contributes approximately $75,000 annually to existing survey efforts. No material impact on local government finances is anticipated.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DNR is the primary implementing agency for this bill. It is responsible for delineating Submerged Aquatic Vegetation (SAV) protection zones, conducting the mandated study on alternative mapping methods, and approving new survey methodologies in coordination with federal partners.

MDE co-funds the annual SAV survey mentioned in the bill and utilizes SAV data as a key indicator for water quality and clarity attainment. The bill also references methodologies approved by the EPA's Chesapeake Bay Program, with which MDE closely coordinates.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Council on the Chesapeake Bay
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This Council advises the Governor on the management and restoration of the Chesapeake Bay and coordinates interagency work between DNR and MDE. The mapping and protection of SAV are central to the state's Bay restoration strategies overseen by the Council.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the DNR and other state agencies regarding the implementation of new statutory definitions and the enforcement of restrictions on dredging gear in protection zones.

DBM is responsible for the state's fiscal planning and budget execution. The fiscal note indicates that if DNR chooses to implement additional survey methods authorized by the bill, general fund expenditures may increase, requiring DBM oversight.

This multi-agency initiative coordinates the activities of DNR and MDE specifically to protect the Chesapeake Bay and preserve environmental resources, aligning with the bill's focus on improving SAV mapping and protection.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives related to environmental protection and the Chesapeake Bay, as well as the annual state budget which funds these surveys.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a local delivery service permit that allows third-party contractors, independent contractors, or agents to deliver alcoholic beverages from authorized retail license holders to purchasers in jurisdictions where local licensing boards have authorized such deliveries. It sets a $1,000 annual permit fee, requires deliverers to be at least 21 years old and complete an alcohol awareness program, and outlines specific penalties for violations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Local licensing boards and the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Retail alcoholic beverage license holders, third-party delivery companies, independent contractors, and consumers of alcoholic beverages.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, local government revenues will increase by $1,000 for each local delivery service permit issued starting in fiscal year 2025. Local revenues may also increase due to fine revenues for violations, which range from $1,000 to $6,000 depending on the number of offenses. State revenues and expenditures are not anticipated to be materially affected, and local licensing boards are expected to manage enforcement with existing resources.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency category encompasses the local licensing boards (liquor boards) established under the state's Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article. These boards are the primary implementers of the bill, tasked with issuing the new Local Delivery Service Permits, adopting local regulations, collecting the $1,000 annual fees, and enforcing administrative penalties.

The Comptroller is identified as the state's principal regulator for the alcohol industry. Since this bill establishes a new permit type and regulatory framework for the retail delivery of alcoholic beverages, it falls directly within the Comptroller's oversight responsibilities for alcohol regulation and revenue collection.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for state agencies and boards. It would be responsible for providing legal counsel to the Executive Director regarding the adoption of enforcement regulations and advising local licensing boards on the legalities of the new permit system.

The bill specifically authorizes the use of 'independent contractors' for delivery services. The classification and regulation of such workers, as well as general workplace safety for delivery personnel, fall under the jurisdiction of the Department of Labor.

As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing criminal laws, including those related to the sale and distribution of alcohol to minors. The expansion of retail delivery services introduces new enforcement considerations for public safety and compliance.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the individual or entity that appointed a member of a state-level board, committee, commission, task force, or workgroup to remove or suspend that member for cause, such as misconduct, incompetence, or neglect of duties. It establishes procedural requirements for such removals, including consultation with the board chair and providing the member with notice and an opportunity to be heard, while protecting the members' constitutional rights to express views on matters of public concern.
qa_results.responsible_party: The individual or entity that appointed the member (State appointing authorities)
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of boards, committees, commissions, task forces, or workgroups created by Maryland State law, and the appointing authorities of those entities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are not materially affected as the bill is expected to apply in a limited number of cases. There may be a minimal operational impact if the removal of a member disrupts an entity's work. Local government operations and finances are not anticipated to be materially affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor is the primary appointing authority for hundreds of State boards and commissions. This bill directly dictates the legal procedures the Governor must follow to remove or suspend appointees, including notice and opportunity to be heard requirements.

As the office that recruits, vets, and recommends candidates for over 600 boards and commissions, they will be responsible for managing the administrative process of removals and ensuring the procedural requirements of this bill are met for gubernatorial appointees.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly applies to all members of these bodies who are appointed for fixed terms, establishing the criteria and procedures for their removal or suspension.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Governor and state agencies regarding the removal of board members. They will be responsible for ensuring that 'notice and opportunity to be heard' meet constitutional and statutory standards.

As a high-level administrative body with members who are individual appointing authorities (Governor, Comptroller, Treasurer), the BPW and its members are directly impacted in their capacity to manage their respective appointees to various boards.

MDH oversees a significant number of regulatory health boards and commissions. The fiscal note specifically mentions MDH regulatory boards as entities where removal provisions exist but may be supplemented or unified by this bill.
MD Labor oversees numerous professional licensing boards and commissions. The bill provides a uniform standard for the removal of members within these regulatory bodies.

The fiscal note specifically identifies the MDTA as an entity that currently lacks provisions for the removal of appointed members, making this bill particularly relevant to their governance structure.
The fiscal note specifically lists the State Board of Elections as one of the long-standing boards with removal provisions that would be subject to the standards set by this bill.
The fiscal note explicitly mentions the IAC as a board that includes provisions for the removal of appointed members which must align with this bill's new uniform procedures.
The PSC is an independent regulatory commission mentioned in the fiscal note as an entity impacted by the procedural requirements for member removal.

The bill requires providing members an 'opportunity to be heard.' In some contested removal cases, this process may involve the Office of Administrative Hearings to ensure a fair and impartial review.

The bill allows removal for 'misconduct,' which often relates to ethics violations. The State Ethics Commission was consulted on the bill and oversees the standards of conduct for these public officials.
The USM is governed by a Board of Regents whose members are appointed for fixed terms. This bill governs the process for the potential removal of those Regents.

The agency is governed by the Board of Trustees of the State Retirement and Pension System, which is specifically mentioned in the fiscal note as a board with removal provisions impacted by this bill.
The fiscal note mentions the State Board of Education as an affected entity regarding the removal of appointed members.

The Secretary of State is the custodian of official state records, including commissions and appointments. Changes in the membership of boards due to removals under this bill would be recorded by this agency.
The Comptroller is an individual appointing authority for various boards and commissions and would be subject to the bill's procedural requirements when removing their appointees.
The Treasurer is an individual appointing authority for various boards (e.g., Maryland 529) and would be subject to the bill's procedural requirements when removing their appointees.
The Judiciary and the Administrative Office of the Courts were consulted on the bill as it relates to members of boards and commissions within the judicial branch created by State law.
The MIA was consulted as an entity that regulates industries and may have associated boards or commissions whose members are subject to the removal procedures established by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Board of Contract Appeals
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As an independent adjudicatory board with members appointed for fixed terms, the board is directly subject to the removal and suspension provisions of this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames 'rapid fire trigger activator' to 'rapid fire activator' and expands the definition to include 'switch/auto-sears,' which are devices that convert firearms into automatic weapons. It prohibits the transport, manufacture, possession, sale, or transfer of these devices and applies existing criminal penalties for violations, including mandatory minimum sentences when used in the commission of a felony or crime of violence.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of State Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Firearm owners, licensed firearms dealers, law enforcement agencies, and individuals involved in the criminal justice system.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates a potential minimal increase in State and local general fund revenues and expenditures resulting from the expanded application of existing criminal penalties. Implementation by the Judiciary, the Department of State Police, and other agencies is expected to be handled within existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's primary law enforcement agency, it is responsible for enforcing the bill's prohibitions on rapid-fire activators and switch/auto-sears, and it manages the machine gun registration process mentioned in the bill's exemptions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency will be responsible for providing legal representation to indigent defendants charged with the new or expanded misdemeanor firearm offenses established by the bill.
The judiciary will handle the adjudication of criminal cases resulting from violations of the expanded firearm prohibitions, impacting the operations and workload of the state court system.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The department is responsible for the incarceration and community supervision of individuals convicted of the firearm-related crimes defined or expanded by this legislation.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office coordinates state public safety strategies and advises on criminal justice policy, which is directly impacted by the bill's changes to weapons laws.
The Maryland Capitol Police, housed within this department, are law enforcement officers affected by the bill's exemptions and responsible for enforcing the law within their jurisdiction.
The Natural Resources Police are law enforcement officers affected by the bill's exemptions and responsible for enforcing state conservation and criminal laws, including firearm violations.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies regarding the new law and represents the state in appeals related to criminal convictions under these statutes.
The Comptroller is responsible for collecting state revenues, which would include any fines generated from the expanded criminal penalties created by the bill.
The university was a source of information for the fiscal note, and the bill's context includes addressing firearm incidents that have occurred on university campuses.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies implementing the law and holds the authority to sign the bill into law.
DBM oversees the fiscal impact of the bill on state agency budgets and ensures resources are allocated for any resulting law enforcement or correctional costs.
The department was listed as an information source for the fiscal note, likely providing data related to the public health impacts of firearm violence.
The department was involved as an information source during the fiscal analysis of the bill.
The system was consulted as an information source regarding the bill's impact on campus safety and state institutional policy.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Financial Well-Being Pilot Program at the University of Maryland, College Park Campus and Morgan State University to provide comprehensive financial guidance, support, and coaching to students dealing with significant financial burdens, complex student loan debts, high levels of financial anxiety, or a lack of financial literacy.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Higher Education Commission, University of Maryland (College Park Campus), and Morgan State University
qa_results.stakeholders: Students at the University of Maryland, College Park Campus and Morgan State University who face significant personal financial burdens, complex student loan debts, financial anxiety, or lack financial literacy knowledge.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State higher education expenditures increase by an estimated $315,500 in FY 2025 for contractual staff and the development of student identification systems. Future year expenditures are estimated at $208,900 in FY 2026, $218,000 in FY 2027, and $18,200 in FY 2028 before the program's termination on July 31, 2027. The Maryland Higher Education Commission can implement the bill's reporting requirements using existing resources. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is mandated to receive interim and final reports from the Maryland Higher Education Commission regarding the operation and effectiveness of the pilot program.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees the state budget and personnel. The bill results in higher education expenditures of over $300,000 for contractual staff and system development at state institutions.
The commission is responsible for jointly administering the pilot program with the participating institutions and must submit reports on the program's operation to the Governor and General Assembly.
The commission is responsible for jointly administering the pilot program with the participating institutions and must submit reports on the program's operation to the Governor and General Assembly.

Morgan State University is explicitly designated as one of the two participating institutions required to implement the pilot program, hire a financial coach, and develop student identification systems.
As a state-funded public college, Saint Mary's is a potential recipient of the best practices and guidance document produced by the pilot program for use by other institutions.
The University of Maryland, College Park, which is part of the University System of Maryland, is specifically mandated to establish the pilot program and hire dedicated staff.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the mandatory annual appropriation from the Cigarette Restitution Fund to the Tri-County Council for Southern Maryland from $900,000 to $1,000,000 and repeals the sunset provision, making the funding permanent. The funds must be used to support the activities of the Southern Maryland Agricultural Development Commission.
qa_results.responsible_party: The Governor and the Tri-County Council for Southern Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Farmers and agricultural businesses in Calvert, Charles, and St. Mary's counties; the Southern Maryland Agricultural Development Commission; and the Tri-County Council for Southern Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, special fund expenditures for the Tri-County Council increase by up to 1000000 annually beginning in fiscal 2026 due to the extension and enhancement of the mandated appropriation. General fund expenditures may also increase by up to 1000000 annually to backfill other programs supported by the Cigarette Restitution Fund. No direct local fiscal impact is expected as the Council is a quasi-governmental entity.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates that the Governor include a specific annual appropriation of $1,000,000 in the state budget for the Tri-County Council for Southern Maryland and the Southern Maryland Agricultural Development Commission.

As the agency responsible for developing the state budget and supervising its execution, DBM must ensure the mandated $1,000,000 appropriation from the Cigarette Restitution Fund is included and managed appropriately in the annual budget process.

The Southern Maryland Agricultural Development Commission (SMADC) activities funded by this bill are agricultural in nature. Funds from the Cigarette Restitution Fund are historically appropriated to MDA, which then issues grants to the Tri-County Council for SMADC activities.

The bill impacts the Cigarette Restitution Fund (CRF). Since a significant portion of the CRF is statutorily required to fund Medicaid, increasing and making permanent other mandates on the CRF can impact the overall availability of these special funds for public health programs.
The OAG provides legal counsel regarding the Master Settlement Agreement and the Cigarette Restitution Fund, both of which are central to the funding mechanisms altered by this bill.
As the state's chief financial officer and tax collector, the Comptroller is responsible for managing state revenues and funds like the Cigarette Restitution Fund mentioned in the bill.

The bill supports regional economic development and the viability of the agricultural industry in Southern Maryland, aligning with the department's mission to support existing companies and promote Maryland's assets.

The Council is dedicated to addressing challenges in Maryland's rural counties and promoting regional cooperation; the bill's support for the Tri-County Council and Southern Maryland agricultural development directly aligns with these efforts.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill significantly reforms Maryland's juvenile justice system by expanding court jurisdiction to include children ages 10 to 12 for specific crimes involving firearms, aggravated animal cruelty, and third-degree sexual offenses. It shortens the juvenile intake inquiry timeline, extends the maximum duration of juvenile probation, and requires the Department of Juvenile Services to notify the court and legal counsel within 24 hours of electronic monitoring violations. The legislation also mandates Child in Need of Supervision (CINS) petitions for children under 13 involved in motor vehicle theft, restricts juvenile sex offenders from entering school properties, and formally codifies the Governor's Office for Children, the Children's Cabinet, and the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Juvenile Services (DJS), Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy (GOCPP), Governor's Office for Children (GOC), and the Children's Cabinet
qa_results.stakeholders: Juveniles under the jurisdiction of the justice system (particularly those aged 10-12 and repeat offenders), juvenile sex offender registrants, law enforcement agencies, victims of juvenile crime, and personnel within the Department of Juvenile Services and Office of the Public Defender.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures will increase by approximately $6,600,000 in FY 2025, rising to $10,000,000 by FY 2029. The primary drivers are increased staffing needs for the Department of Juvenile Services (at least $4.4 million) and the Office of the Public Defender (up to $1.9 million) to manage expanded court jurisdiction and more intensive supervision requirements. Local governments may see operational cost increases for State's Attorneys' offices, while circuit courts are expected to manage the changes within existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill codifies the Governor's Office for Children and the Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy within the Executive Department. It also grants the Governor authority to appoint the chair and various members of the Commission on Juvenile Justice Reform and Emerging and Best Practices.

The Office of the Attorney General currently houses the Juvenile Justice Monitoring Unit (JJMU), which is subject to expanded reporting requirements under this bill regarding the care and supervision of children in facilities.

The bill has a significant fiscal impact, with general fund expenditures increasing by millions of dollars for agencies like DJS and OPD. As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees the budget execution and personnel management associated with these changes.

The bill regulates the entry of juvenile sex offenders onto school property and mandates that the State Board of Education develop guidelines and model policies for their education. It also places a local school superintendent and principal on the Commission.

The fiscal note anticipates significant capital costs for the Department of Juvenile Services for facility improvements. DGS is responsible for the planning, design, and construction of state-owned facilities.

The bill requires a representative from the Department of Health to serve on the reform commission and involves coordination on reports regarding the length of stay for juveniles in secure facilities undergoing competency evaluations.
The Secretary of Human Services is a designated member of the Commission on Juvenile Justice Reform and Emerging and Best Practices, reflecting the agency's role in child welfare.
DJS is the primary agency affected by the bill, which mandates major reforms to juvenile court jurisdiction, intake procedures, detention, probation, and reporting requirements.
The bill specifically requires the Commission to coordinate with the Maryland Department of Labor to identify job and apprenticeship opportunities for juveniles under DJS supervision.
The bill alters procedures for law enforcement officers taking children into custody and includes a representative of the state's law enforcement community on the reform commission.
The bill requires the Governor to appoint numerous members and a chair to the Commission on Juvenile Justice Reform and Emerging and Best Practices, a process managed by the Office of Appointments.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals the State Advisory Board for Juvenile Services and significantly expands the membership and duties of the Commission on Juvenile Justice Reform and Emerging and Best Practices.
The bill codifies this office in statute, defines its purpose related to child well-being and poverty reduction, and tasks it with administering the Children's Cabinet.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill codifies this office, assigns it responsibility for staffing the Commission, and requires it to produce several new reports on juvenile arrests and diversion programs.
The bill changes the jurisdiction of the juvenile courts and alters judicial procedures for intake, detention hearings, and probation, requiring support and policy implementation from the AOC.
The reform commission is specifically required to review data from the Maryland Longitudinal Data System Center regarding arrests, program completion, and recidivism.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill has a major operational and fiscal impact on the OPD, which must provide legal representation in the expanded number of juvenile cases and has a representative on the reform commission.
The team's mission is to address child poverty and economic mobility; the bill codifies the Governor's Office for Children with a core mission to reduce child poverty.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners to issue an alcoholic beverages license for an establishment located within 500 feet of a place of worship or 1,000 feet of a school building, provided that a license of the same class was previously held by the same person at that establishment and was in effect before the place of worship or school was constructed.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Alcoholic beverages license applicants in Prince George's County, the Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners, and local schools and places of worship.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State finances are not affected. Prince George’s County revenues are projected to increase by approximately $2,305 annually starting in fiscal 2025 from the issuance of one Class B beer, wine, liquor (BWL) license. Local enforcement activities can be managed using existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly expands the licensing authority of the Prince George's County Board of License Commissioners, which is an ongoing administrative body established by the state to address specific regulatory issues.
The Comptroller's Office is the principal state agency responsible for regulating the alcohol industry and enforcing related tax laws, as mentioned in its provided summary.
The bill amends a section of the code that defines geographic exceptions to licensing rules, including districts designated by the Secretary of Commerce.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for signing the bill into law and appointing members to the relevant local licensing board.
The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions, including those regulating alcoholic beverages.
The Office of Appointments vets and recommends candidates for state boards such as the Prince George's County Board of License Commissioners.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the eligibility for financial assistance from the Catastrophic Event Account and the Federal Government Shutdown Employee Assistance Loan Fund to include noncivilian federal employees working in Maryland during a federal government shutdown. These resources were previously limited to civilian federal employees.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor and the Maryland Department of Budget and Management
qa_results.stakeholders: Noncivilian federal employees working at work sites located in Maryland who are unpaid during federal government shutdowns.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill alters the purposes for which existing funds may be used and does not require new state or local appropriations. The Department of Budget and Management and the Maryland Department of Labor can handle administrative changes with existing resources. As of the 2024 session, the Catastrophic Event Account was projected to have a balance of $19,400,000 and the loan fund had an average daily balance of $375,000.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill authorizes the Governor to provide appropriations for the Catastrophic Event Account and to transfer funds via budget amendment to the Loan Fund during a federal shutdown.
The bill explicitly requires the Comptroller to account for both the Catastrophic Event Account and the Federal Government Shutdown Employee Assistance Loan Fund.
The bill explicitly tasks the State Treasurer with holding and investing the money in both the Catastrophic Event Account and the Federal Government Shutdown Employee Assistance Loan Fund.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Labor and Department of Budget and Management as they establish new procedures and eligibility criteria for noncivilian federal employees.
DBM administers the Catastrophic Event Account, which is one of the primary funds altered by this bill to include noncivilian federal employees.
The Maryland Department of Labor is explicitly charged with administering the Federal Government Shutdown Employee Assistance Loan Fund and establishing eligibility criteria for applicants.
The bill expands financial assistance to 'noncivilian' federal employees, which includes active-duty military members and their families, a core constituency of this department.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the establishment of a Student Support Specialist Network in every public high school in Prince George's County. Each network, composed of school staff like counselors and nurses, must develop and implement a Student Support Success Action Plan to improve student outcomes in areas such as mental health access, behavioral supports, and disciplinary measures, based on a template created by the Prince George's County Board of Education.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Students, staff, and administrators of public high schools in Prince George's County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no state fiscal impact. However, local expenditures for Prince George's County are estimated to increase by as much as 3,700,000 annually starting in fiscal year 2025. This cost accounts for the potential need to hire a student support network specialist at each of the county's 27 public high schools at an estimated cost of 138,000 per position for salary and benefits.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill focuses on school safety personnel, including School Resource Officers and security employees, and requires tracking metrics such as school-based arrests and restorative approaches. MCSS is the state's independent unit responsible for school safety coordination, training, and SRO oversight.

The bill adds a new section to the Education Article of the Maryland Code and impacts local school system operations, including behavioral health ratios and community school assessments, which are overseen by MSDE.

The student support networks include school-based nurses and psychologists, and the success plans must track behavioral and mental health supports. MDH jointly develops school health standards with MSDE and oversees behavioral health policy.
The bill explicitly requires the Student Support Success Action Plan to include metrics regarding student referrals to the Department of Juvenile Services.

The bill requires the inclusion of Career Coaches in the specialist networks and tracks student referrals to career counseling services, which falls under the department's workforce development mission.

The student support plans must include metrics for student referrals to food pantries, which aligns with the Department of Human Services' role in managing food assistance and aiding vulnerable populations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The agency advises on juvenile delinquency and public safety. The bill's emphasis on reducing school-based arrests and utilizing restorative justice aligns with the office's core mission to develop criminal justice strategies and reduce recidivism.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the Maryland Medical Assistance Program’s Employed Individuals with Disabilities (EID) program by establishing specific service requirements, prohibiting eligibility limits based on the income or assets of applicants or their spouses (including retirement and independence accounts), and setting a minimum eligibility age of 16. It also mandates that the Maryland Department of Health report to the General Assembly by December 1, 2024, regarding the fiscal and operational impacts of expanding the program to individuals aged 65 and older.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Employed individuals with disabilities, Maryland Medical Assistance Program recipients, and disability advocacy organizations.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no material impact on State Medicaid revenues or expenditures as it largely codifies requirements that have already been implemented by the Maryland Department of Health. The department can complete the required fiscal and operational impact report using existing budgeted resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for administering the Maryland Medical Assistance Program and specifically the Employed Individuals with Disabilities (EID) program. The bill mandates MDH to implement new eligibility criteria, application procedures, and reporting requirements for this program.

The bill focuses on the EID program, which is designed to support employment for individuals with disabilities. This directly aligns with the department's mission to coordinate and improve services and advance employment opportunities for residents with disabilities.

The Maryland Health Benefit Exchange determines eligibility for Medicaid programs. The bill's changes to eligibility criteria, income verification, and the application process for the EID program directly affect their administrative and technological operations.

The bill specifically requires a study and report on the impact of expanding the EID program to individuals aged 65 and older, which falls under the purview of the Department of Aging's mission to support older adults.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives. The Governor signed this bill and directs the Department of Health, and the bill's focus on economic mobility and health access aligns with executive priorities.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors changes to the Medicaid program, which is a major component of the state budget. They also review fiscal notes for potential long-term impacts on state resources.
The OAG provides legal counsel to MDH and would be involved in drafting the new regulations and advising on the federal CMS waiver and state plan amendment process mandated by the bill.

OAH adjudicates contested administrative cases. Changes to EID program eligibility and application processes could lead to an increase in hearings if applicants contest denials or benefit determinations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically mentions the Coalition for Work Incentives Improvement as a consulting body for the program, which acts in an advisory capacity typical of entities in this category.

The EID program's primary goal is to encourage individuals with disabilities to maintain employment, which aligns with the Department of Labor's mission regarding workforce development and adult learning.

The Commission on Civil Rights enforces anti-discrimination laws. Programmatic changes that enhance access to services for individuals with disabilities relate to broader civil rights and accessibility protections.

The Innovation Team identifies strategies to enhance economic mobility. The EID program is a tool for economic mobility for people with disabilities, and the team may be interested in the outcomes of the required report on age caps and premiums.

GOPI tracks the responsiveness and efficiency of state services. The bill's requirements for a simplified application process and reporting on fiscal/operational impact are subjects that align with their oversight of state operations.

qa_results.bill_summary: Known as the Melanie Nicholle Diaz Fire Safety Act, this bill mandates fire safety upgrades for residential rental high-rise buildings, including the installation of smoke detectors in public corridors and emergency escape lighting. It requires landlords to provide specialized smoke alarm notification appliances to deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing tenants at no cost and without requiring medical documentation. Buildings without automatic sprinkler systems must post warning signs and include specific disclosures in lease agreements. Additionally, the bill authorizes local governments to offer property tax credits for fire safety improvements and establishes a temporary workgroup to identify best practices for older high-rise buildings.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Housing and Community Development, State Fire Marshal, and Department of State Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and owners of high-rise apartment and condominium buildings, deaf and hard of hearing individuals, local governments, and state universities
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $35,600 in FY 2025 for the Department of State Police to staff a fire safety workgroup. Higher education expenditures for the University System of Maryland are expected to increase significantly, including an estimated $1,200,000 for the University of Maryland, College Park to comply with smoke detector requirements. Local property tax revenues may decrease significantly starting in FY 2025 if local jurisdictions choose to grant the property tax credits authorized by the bill for fire safety improvements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Fire Marshal, who is housed within the Department of State Police, is a primary authority for implementing and enforcing the fire safety standards established by the bill. Additionally, the Department of State Police is explicitly required to provide staff for the newly created Workgroup to Develop Fire Safety Best Practices for Pre-1974 High-Rise Apartment Buildings.

The bill explicitly tasks the Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) with identifying and publishing information on funding sources available to building owners for installing automatic sprinkler systems. DHCD is also required to coordinate this effort with the State Fire Marshal and the Maryland Department of Emergency Management.

The bill requires the Maryland Department of Emergency Management (MDEM) to coordinate with DHCD and the State Fire Marshal to identify funding sources for automatic sprinkler systems in residential rental high-rise buildings.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill expands fire safety requirements to include individuals who are deafblind and prohibits landlords from charging for notification appliances (visual/vibrating alerts) requested by deaf, hard of hearing, or deafblind tenants. This agency oversees the welfare and equal access for this specific population.

The fiscal note indicates a significant fiscal impact on the University System of Maryland, specifically mentioning over $1 million in estimated costs for the University of Maryland, College Park to upgrade high-rise residential housing facilities with required smoke detectors and lighting.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes a specific 'Workgroup to Develop Fire Safety Best Practices for Pre-1974 High-Rise Apartment Buildings' to study risks and recommend improvements for older high-rise buildings without automatic sprinklers.

The department is responsible for improving service delivery to residents with disabilities. The bill's provisions regarding deafblind individuals and mobility-impaired residents' rights to request first-floor units directly align with the department's mission.

As a state university with residential facilities, Morgan State University may incur fiscal costs similar to the University System of Maryland to ensure its high-rise dormitories comply with the new smoke detector and emergency lighting standards.
As a public honors college with residential housing, the college may be impacted by requirements to upgrade its high-rise residential buildings to meet new fire safety standards.
The Governor is responsible for appointing several members to the Workgroup established by the bill, and the Workgroup must report its findings directly to the Governor.

The fiscal note identifies a need for general fund expenditures to cover the cost of a contractual management associate to staff the Workgroup, which falls under DBM's oversight of state personnel and budget execution.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the implementing agencies (DHCD, MSP/Fire Marshal) and the Workgroup regarding the interpretation and enforcement of the new fire safety regulations and the establishment of local tax credits.

DGS manages state-owned facilities and procurement. To the extent that these fire safety upgrades apply to state-owned residential buildings or involve significant state procurement for equipment, DGS would be involved.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the maximum number of public members on the governing board of each regional library resource center from one to two members from each area served by participating library systems. This change is intended to improve diversity and community representation on these boards.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Library Agency (MSLA) and the governing boards of regional library resource centers
qa_results.stakeholders: Regional library resource centers, participating library systems, and the general public in the Eastern Shore, Southern Maryland, and Western Maryland regions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state or local fiscal impact. According to the fiscal note, altering the appointment process of board members does not affect governmental finances, and the Maryland State Library Agency determined there is minimal or no impact on small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Library Agency (MSLA) is the primary state agency responsible for coordinating Maryland's library systems and the State Library Network. This departmental bill directly impacts the governance and membership of the regional library resource centers that MSLA oversees and supports.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill modifies the composition and membership requirements for the governing boards of regional library resource centers, which are state-authorized bodies responsible for regional library coordination and governance.

The Governor's Office provides administrative support for the Governor, who signed this bill into law. Additionally, as a departmental bill, it reflects the policy priorities of an agency within the executive branch overseen by the Governor's Office.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Insurance Administration (MIA) to conduct a study on professional employer organizations (PEOs) within the State. The study must examine federal and state regulations, the history and evolution of the PEO industry, PEO health plan benefit designs, and the potential impact of proposed statutory changes on Maryland's small group health insurance market. MIA is required to report its findings to the General Assembly by December 31, 2024.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Professional employer organizations (PEOs), small employers, employees in co-employment arrangements, and health insurance providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the Maryland Insurance Administration can conduct the required study using existing budgeted resources. The bill has no impact on state revenues, local government finances, or small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates the Maryland Insurance Administration to lead the study of professional employer organizations (PEOs), their regulation, and their health benefit designs.

The study requires examining the impact of PEO health coverage on the small group market and reviewing plan benefit designs, which aligns with the Commission's role in health system planning and market oversight.
PEOs operate through co-employment agreements to provide human resource and payroll services, which directly intersects with the Department's oversight of labor laws and workforce regulation.
The study addresses how PEO health plans affect the small group market, which is directly relevant to the Exchange's mission of providing a marketplace for small businesses to access health coverage.
As the chief legal officer for state agencies, the OAG would provide legal counsel to the MIA regarding the comparison of federal and state laws required by the study.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies involved and coordinates the policy initiatives that may arise from the study's findings.
The study's focus on health insurance benefit designs and health coverage policy relates to the Department's broader responsibility for statewide health policy.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill defines "damages" and "losses" within Maryland statutory causes of action for civil rights or consumer protection to include monetary compensation for loss or injury resulting from the violation of rights and specifically includes reasonable nominal damages.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals and consumers seeking to enforce civil rights or consumer protection rights in Maryland courts.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect Maryland State or local government finances or operations, as stated in the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The OAG's Consumer Protection Division enforces the Maryland Consumer Protection Act, and the agency is responsible for civil rights enforcement. This bill directly clarifies the definition of damages available in the statutory causes of action the OAG litigates.

The MCCR is the primary state agency responsible for enforcing civil rights and anti-discrimination laws. The bill specifically addresses statutory causes of action created to protect civil rights and clarifies the inclusion of nominal damages in such cases.

The bill provides a statutory definition for 'damages' and 'losses' that courts must apply in specific types of civil litigation, affecting judicial proceedings and the interpretation of Maryland law.

The Treasurer's Office manages the State Insurance Trust Fund. While the fiscal note suggests no material impact, the agency is responsible for state liability and was consulted on the bill's implications for damages awarded against state entities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a legal presumption that placing a child with a parent undergoing residential substance use disorder treatment is in the child's best interest and that the child is receiving proper care. It requires local departments of social services to justify decisions not to place a child with such a parent and empowers the Behavioral Health Administration to require treatment facilities to provide accommodations for children.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health (Behavioral Health Administration) and Department of Human Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Parents receiving substance use disorder treatment, children of parents in treatment, and substance use disorder treatment facilities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures related to substance use disorder treatment programs may increase significantly if the Behavioral Health Administration requires facilities to provide beds and services for children, though the exact impact is indeterminate. The Department of Human Services may see a transfer of costs between out-of-home placement providers. There is potential for matching federal Medicaid revenues. Local government finances are not expected to be materially affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Judicial Branch as circuit courts handle Children in Need of Assistance (CINA) proceedings. Judges must apply the new presumption regarding a child's best interest and proper care when a parent is in substance use disorder treatment, and the court receives the reports required to be filed by local departments of social services.

The fiscal note indicates a potential significant increase in State expenditures related to substance use disorder (SUD) treatment programs if the Behavioral Health Administration requires facilities to provide beds and services for children, necessitating DBM's oversight of fiscal planning and budget execution.

The bill requires the State Superintendent of Schools to jointly adopt regulations with other agencies to ensure children in shelter care are provided appropriate services, including educational services.

The Behavioral Health Administration (BHA), part of the Maryland Department of Health, is authorized by the bill to require substance use disorder treatment facilities to provide beds and services for patients' children and allow children to be present during treatment. MDH is also involved in joint regulations for children in shelter care.

The Department of Human Services oversees local departments of social services, which are responsible for authorizing shelter care, filing CINA petitions, and are now required to file specific reports with the court when a child is not placed with a parent who is in residential substance use disorder treatment.
The Secretary of Juvenile Services is one of the agency heads required to jointly adopt regulations ensuring appropriate services for children in shelter care.

The Special Secretary for Children, Youth, and Families (who leads this office) is required to jointly adopt regulations regarding services for children in shelter care. The bill's focus on child well-being and reducing child poverty aligns with the agency's core mission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of the Public Defender provides legal representation to indigent defendants in juvenile and parental defense proceedings, which include the CINA cases directly affected by the bill's new presumptions and reporting requirements.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the agencies involved in implementing this bill (DHS and MDH) and would likely be involved in drafting the joint regulations required by the legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Calvert County to update its local ethics laws to include judicial relief provisions equivalent to Maryland state law, mandates that the Calvert County Ethics Commission publicize any recommendations for expelling a commissioner, and authorizes the county governing body to remove elected officials for conflict of interest violations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Calvert County governing body and the Calvert County Ethics Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Calvert County elected officials and the Calvert County Ethics Commission
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill does not materially affect State operations or finances and is not expected to affect Calvert County government operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Ethics Commission is responsible for overseeing the approval of local government ethics laws to ensure they meet minimum State standards. This bill mandates specific inclusions in Calvert County's ethics laws concerning conflict of interest and enforcement mechanisms, directly involving the Commission's oversight role.

The bill requires local ethics provisions to include judicial relief measures equivalent to State law, allowing courts to issue cease and desist orders, grant injunctive relief, and impose fines for ethics violations. The Administrative Office of the Courts supports the judicial branch in implementing these enforcement authorities.

The bill specifies that fines imposed by a court for violations of the Calvert County ethics title must be paid to the State Treasurer and deposited into the General Fund. The Treasurer's Office is the agency responsible for receiving and distributing such state funds.
As the state's chief executive, the Governor signed the bill into law. The office also oversees general executive branch policy initiatives related to governance and ethics at the local level.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill authorizes the Board of County Commissioners of Calvert County to enact an ordinance that establishes a local percentage preference for procurement contracts. This allows the county to award a contract to a resident business whose bid is slightly higher than the lowest bid from a nonresident business, provided the difference does not exceed a specified percentage.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of County Commissioners of Calvert County
qa_results.stakeholders: Businesses located in Calvert County, nonresident businesses seeking county contracts, and the Calvert County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no state fiscal impact. The bill is authorizing in nature, so there is no direct local impact until Calvert County chooses to enact a local preference ordinance. If an ordinance is enacted, local procurement costs may increase by an indeterminate amount as the county awards contracts to resident businesses that did not submit the lowest bid. Specific dollar estimates are unavailable because the county did not submit a response for the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the County Commissioners of Calvert County to file any local procurement preference ordinance with the clerk of the court for the county and requires the clerk to certify the ordinance for use as evidence in court proceedings, which directly impacts the administrative and operational responsibilities of the clerks' offices supported by the AOC.

As the head of the Maryland executive branch, the Governor signed this bill into law (Chapter 683). The Governor's Office is responsible for overseen the state's legislative agenda and coordinating policy initiatives related to local government authorities.

The OAG serves as the primary legal representative for the state and provides counsel to the Governor and General Assembly on the legal sufficiency of bills, including the interaction between local procurement laws and broader state and federal legal standards.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office oversees socioeconomic procurement programs in Maryland. While the bill pertains specifically to Calvert County, the creation of local preferences for resident businesses aligns with the office's mission to promote opportunities for small and specialized business entities within the state's economic landscape.

As Maryland's primary economic development agency, the Department of Commerce is concerned with policies that affect business retention and the competitive environment for companies within the state. A local procurement preference in Calvert County directly impacts these economic factors.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Prince George’s County Family Child Care Home Expansion Grant Program. Administered by the Prince George’s County Public School System, the program provides grants to nonprofit organizations to offer training, financial assistance, and technical support to individuals in low-income communities who wish to become registered family child care providers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Prince George’s County Public School System
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonprofit organizations in Prince George's County, prospective family child care providers, and residents of low-income communities in Prince George's County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not directly affect State finances, despite text in the bill requesting a 500,000 annual appropriation starting in fiscal 2026. However, the bill creates a local mandate for the Prince George’s County Public School System (PGCPS). PGCPS expenditures are estimated to increase by approximately 1,600,000 in fiscal 2025 for administrative setup and grant awards, with costs potentially rising to 2,000,000 by fiscal 2029.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The program is established within the Education Article and is administered by a local school system under the department's oversight. The legislation also requires participating providers to navigate the department's official registration process for family child care and achieve defined quality benchmarks.

The program's focus on expanding child care in low-income communities directly supports the office's primary mission of reducing child poverty and enhancing support networks for Maryland's children and families.
The grant program aligns with the team's mandate to identify and pilot novel strategies for enhancing economic mobility and reducing child poverty in historically underinvested communities.

This legislation requires partnerships with nonprofit organizations to address local community needs and economic opportunities, which aligns with the office's mission to facilitate community engagement and service.

The Governor's Office provides general policy coordination and administrative oversight for executive branch initiatives that address the Governor's top priorities, such as the poverty reduction goals established in this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the threshold for public work contracts and equipment purchases in Carroll County that require competitive bidding and public notice from $25,000 to $50,000. It also updates the title of the official responsible for advertising bids to Procurement Officer.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Carroll County and the Procurement Officer
qa_results.stakeholders: Carroll County government departments, nonprofit entities receiving county funds, and contractors or vendors providing supplies and services to the county.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no state impact. Locally, there is no direct effect on Carroll County finances or revenues, though the county likely experiences administrative efficiencies by raising the threshold for formal competitive bidding from $25,000 to $50,000.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for the final review and signing of all state and local legislation passed by the Maryland General Assembly, including this local law for Carroll County.

The Office of the Attorney General reviews all state and local legislation for constitutional and legal sufficiency and serves as legal counsel to the General Assembly during the bill-drafting and review process.

As the state's central procurement authority, the Department of General Services monitors procurement policies and terminology statewide. This bill's changes to local procurement thresholds and titles are of interest to the agency's broader oversight of public contracting standards.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows new vehicle dealers and salespersons of motor homes (Class M) and trailer homes (Class G) to participate in more than two annual vehicle shows and removes the restriction that these shows must be limited exclusively to those vehicle classes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation
qa_results.stakeholders: New vehicle dealers and licensed salespersons of Class M motor homes and Class G trailers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill does not materially affect State or local government finances or operations. The impact on small businesses is expected to be minimal.
