qa_results.stakeholders: Health insurance carriers, pharmacy benefits managers, health care providers, and insured individuals in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures for the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program are estimated to increase by 1,750,000 in fiscal 2025 and 3,500,000 annually thereafter due to revised coverage and authorization requirements. Minimal increases in special fund revenues for the Maryland Insurance Administration are expected from form filing fees. Local governments and small businesses may see increased healthcare expenditures or premiums for fully insured health benefit plans.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly tasks the Commission with establishing a process for health care provider waivers, conducting a study on standards for payor programs, and monitoring the progress of online prior authorization processes.

As the primary regulator of insurance in Maryland, the MIA is responsible for overseeing the implementation of these utilization review revisions, conducting joint studies, and reporting on violations by private review agents.
The fiscal note indicates a significant impact on the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program, which DBM manages, with projected annual cost increases of $3.5 million.

The OAG's Health Education and Advocacy Unit (HEAU) is central to the bill, as it is required to receive grievance information, participate in evaluating the grievance process, and publish annual summary reports.
The bill creates new requirements for health care providers and the Maryland Health Care Commission, both of which fall under the health policy and regulatory umbrella of the Department of Health.
The Governor's Office is a recipient of the mandated annual summary reports regarding health insurance grievances and the outcomes of utilization reviews.
The bill defines designees of the Insurance Commissioner to include administrative law judges, who may be tasked with adjudicating contested cases related to the new insurance mandates.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a 9-8-8 fee of $0.25 per month for each subscriber to switched local exchange access service, commercial mobile radio service, and other 9-8-8-accessible services. It also establishes a $0.25 fee for each retail transaction of prepaid wireless telecommunications service beginning in 2027. The fees are collected by the Comptroller and deposited into the 9-8-8 Trust Fund to support Maryland's behavioral health crisis response services, including suicide prevention hotlines, mobile crisis teams, and crisis stabilization centers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Comptroller of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Telephone and mobile service subscribers, prepaid wireless consumers, telecommunications providers, and individuals requiring behavioral health crisis services.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, special fund revenues for the 9-8-8 Trust Fund are estimated to increase by $20,750,000 in FY 2025, with projected increases reaching $27,170,000 by FY 2029. General fund revenues and expenditures for the Comptroller's administrative costs are estimated to increase by approximately $1,320,000 in FY 2025 to cover system upgrades and personnel. Local governments may experience minor expenditure increases from paying the new fees, though jurisdictions providing behavioral health services may see significant revenue increases through fund distributions.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly tasks the Comptroller with collecting and remitting 9-8-8 fees, auditing telephone companies and CMRS providers, accounting for the 9-8-8 Trust Fund, and adopting implementing regulations.

The Department of Health is the primary administrator of the 9-8-8 Trust Fund and is responsible for managing the behavioral health crisis response services, such as call centers and mobile crisis teams, funded by the new fees.
The State Treasurer is responsible for holding the 9-8-8 Trust Fund separately and investing the money in the same manner as other state funds.

The bill requires the Commission to direct telephone companies to add the 9-8-8 fee to customer bills and provides that providers have the same immunity from liability approved by the Commission for other telecommunications services.

As the agency overseeing the 9-1-1 Board, MDEM is relevant due to the bill's use of the 9-1-1 fee structure as a template for 9-8-8 and the integration of these services within Public Safety Answering Points (PSAPs).
The bill explicitly states that the 9-8-8 Trust Fund is subject to audit by the Office of Legislative Audits.

The agency oversees state budget development and expenditure execution, which includes the mandated appropriations to the 9-8-8 Trust Fund and the administrative personnel costs associated with fee collection.
The Governor's Office provides executive oversight and policy coordination for the state agencies implementing the fee and includes mandated fund appropriations in the annual state budget.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to the primary implementing agencies (Department of Health and Comptroller) and would provide guidance on regulatory compliance and enforcement of the new fee provisions.
MDOT is identified in the fiscal note as a state agency that will incur increased operational costs as a subscriber to telecommunications services subject to the new 9-8-8 fee.
As the state's telecommunications strategy lead, DoIT may be involved in the technical coordination of 9-8-8 accessibility and the management of state-owned communication lines affected by the fee.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The agency manages state emergency communications infrastructure, which links to the behavioral health crisis response network supported by the 9-8-8 Trust Fund.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the requirements and conditions under which unlicensed individuals may perform specific X-ray procedures in a physician's office. It mandates that such individuals must complete at least 115 hours of didactic training, 480 hours of clinical training, pass a board-approved examination, and register with the Maryland Board of Physicians. The bill also defines the anatomical areas permitted for these procedures, sets supervision requirements, and authorizes civil penalties for violations by either the individual or the physician's office.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Board of Physicians
qa_results.stakeholders: Physicians, unlicensed individuals performing X-ray duties, radiologic technologists, and healthcare patients.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Board of Physicians can implement the bill with existing budgeted resources. According to the Fiscal Note, state revenues are not materially affected, although the Board is authorized to impose civil penalties of up to $5,000 on physician offices and $1,000 on unlicensed individuals for each violation of the bill's requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Physicians, which is an agency under the Maryland Department of Health, is the primary body responsible for implementing the bill's registration requirements, adopting necessary regulations, and enforcing penalties for the performance of unlicensed X-ray duties.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the composition and name of the Radiation Therapy, Radiography, Nuclear Medicine Technology, Radiology Assistance, and Limited Licensed Radiologic Technology Advisory Committee, which is an entity that provides expertise and recommendations to the State Board of Physicians.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the State Board of Physicians regarding the development of new regulations and would represent the board in legal matters concerning the enforcement of the bill's new civil penalties.

The bill authorizes the State Board of Physicians to impose civil penalties ($5,000 for offices and $1,000 for individuals), which are remitted to the State's General Fund; the Comptroller is the chief official responsible for the collection and oversight of state revenues.

If individuals or physician offices contest the civil penalties imposed by the Board for unauthorized X-ray duties, the Office of Administrative Hearings would likely preside over the resulting contested cases.

The bill requires individuals to complete a specific 115-hour didactic educational program and 480 hours of clinical training. MHEC regulates and sets standards for postsecondary career schools and programs that may provide this training.

The State Board of Physicians is required to submit a report to the General Assembly by 2029 regarding the registration process, and the Governor's Office maintains general executive oversight of the Department of Health and its constituent boards.

As the custodian of state funds, the Treasurer's Office is responsible for the management of the State's General Fund, which will receive the revenues generated from the civil penalties established by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the state agency overseeing a state-owned nursing home operated by a contractor to report deficiencies, enforcement actions, and correction plans to the Governor, the General Assembly, and local government bodies. It shifts the primary notification responsibility from the private contractor to the state oversight agency, establishes specific timelines for reporting after final survey findings or dispute resolutions, and expands the definition of monitoring agencies to include entities like the Maryland Department of Labor and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and state agencies overseeing state-owned nursing homes (such as the Maryland Department of Veterans Affairs)
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of state-owned nursing homes (such as Charlotte Hall Veterans Home), private contractors operating state-owned nursing homes, the Governor, the Maryland General Assembly, and local governing bodies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's requirements can be handled with existing budgeted resources, resulting in no estimated impact on state expenditures. Revenues are not affected. Local finances and operations are not expected to be materially impacted.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor is a mandated recipient of notifications regarding nursing home deficiencies and enforcement actions within 15 days of findings, as well as the annual comprehensive report on survey activities. The Governor's Office provides the administrative support to manage these executive functions.

The bill amends the Health - General Article, which falls under the department's purview. The Office of Health Care Quality, located within the department, is a monitoring agency. Furthermore, the department is responsible for submitting an annual report to the Governor and General Assembly regarding survey activities and enforcement actions at State-owned nursing homes.

The bill specifically expands the statutory definition of a 'monitoring agency' to include the Maryland Department of Labor, meaning its findings of deficiencies or enforcement actions at State-owned nursing homes now trigger the bill's reporting requirements.

This department is responsible for the oversight of the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home, currently the only State-owned nursing home in Maryland operated by a contractor. The bill shifts the responsibility for reporting deficiencies, citations, and plans of correction from the private contractor to this department.

qa_results.bill_summary: House Bill 945 alters Maryland's initial teacher certification requirements by repealing the mandate that teacher preparation programs require a portfolio-based assessment for graduation. The bill establishes alternative pathways for certification, allowing candidates to either pass a nationally recognized assessment or complete a rigorous local school system induction program. Additionally, it requires the State Superintendent to pursue reciprocity agreements with other states to facilitate the certification of out-of-state teachers and updates literacy and subject-specific examination standards.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Teacher candidates, local school systems, Maryland institutions of higher education with teacher preparation programs, and educators credentialed in other states.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) and the Accountability and Implementation Board (AIB) can receive induction program plans using existing resources, although MSDE may require additional staff if extensive review or technical assistance is required. Public four-year institutions of higher education are not expected to see material changes in revenue or expenditures. Local school systems are expected to experience increased expenditures to develop and implement the rigorous induction programs authorized by the bill. Specific dollar estimates for these local increases are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note indicates that MSDE may require additional staff (Educational Specialist II) to review and approve local induction programs, which would involve the Department of Budget and Management in its role as the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency.

As the primary agency for teacher certification and oversight of teacher preparation programs, MSDE is responsible for implementing the bill's changes to certification requirements, literacy assessments, and reviewing induction program plans.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires local school systems to submit induction program plans to the Accountability and Implementation Board (AIB), an independent body within the executive branch, and requires MSDE to report to the AIB on the diversity impact of new assessments.

The commission oversees policies for the state's teacher preparation programs at public and private colleges and universities, which are directly impacted by the removal of the portfolio-based assessment as a graduation requirement.

The MLDS links student and workforce data; changes to teacher certification pathways and qualification requirements are significant variables for their longitudinal analysis of teacher workforce outcomes.

As a state-operated educational agency that employs teachers, the Maryland School for the Deaf is directly impacted by changes in certification standards and the development of induction programs for new educators.

As a state agency with its own teacher preparation programs, Morgan State is directly affected by the elimination of the portfolio-based assessment as a graduation requirement and changes to certification standards for its graduates.

As Maryland's public honors college with educational programs, St. Mary's is impacted by the modification of graduation requirements for teacher preparation and the new pathways for initial teacher certification.

The fiscal note specifically mentions concerns from USM institutions (such as Towson and UMCP) regarding how the removal of graduation assessments might impact program enrollment and out-of-state reciprocity for their students.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Gun Industry Accountability Act of 2024 prohibits firearm industry members from knowingly creating or contributing to a public nuisance through the sale, manufacture, or marketing of firearm-related products. It requires these members to implement 'reasonable controls' to prevent straw purchases, firearm trafficking, and theft. The bill authorizes the Attorney General, county attorneys, and the Baltimore City Solicitor to bring civil actions against violators and requires the Attorney General to study the feasibility of requiring dealers to report firearm trace requests.
qa_results.responsible_party: Office of the Attorney General
qa_results.stakeholders: Firearm industry members including manufacturers, distributors, and retailers, as well as the general public in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Office of the Attorney General (OAG) estimates general fund expenditures of $207,816 in fiscal 2025 to hire staff for litigation and enforcement, with ongoing annual costs of at least $252,200. State and local revenues may increase if civil actions result in awarded damages or restitution. Additionally, local government expenditures may increase for jurisdictions that choose to pursue civil actions as authorized under the bill.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly authorizes the Attorney General to bring civil actions against firearm industry members for public nuisance. Additionally, the Attorney General is mandated to lead a study on the legality and feasibility of requiring firearm trace request records, in consultation with the Maryland State Police.

The bill requires the Attorney General to consult with the Maryland State Police regarding a study on firearm trace requests. As the state's primary regulator of firearms and licensed dealers, the agency is a central stakeholder in policies affecting firearm industry compliance.

The bill creates a new civil cause of action for public nuisance against firearm industry members. The Administrative Office of the Courts manages the operations and resources of the judicial branch, which will handle these new lawsuits and litigation.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency serves as the Governor's primary coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies. This legislation is a significant policy tool intended to reduce firearm-related harm and crime through civil industry accountability.

The fiscal note identifies a potential need for the Office of the Attorney General to hire additional legal and support staff to litigate these cases. The Department of Budget and Management oversees state personnel and budget allocations necessary for such expansions.

The bill imposes new regulatory requirements and liability risks on 'firearm industry members,' which include manufacturers, distributors, and marketers. This affects the business climate and economic operations of a specific commercial sector in the state.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note highlights a potentially meaningful impact on small businesses within the firearm industry due to increased litigation, liability, and insurance costs. This office monitors procurement and economic conditions for small business entities.

The Governor signs the bill into law and oversees the executive agencies (OAG and MSP) involved in the mandated study. The Attorney General is required to report the findings and recommendations of the firearm trace study to the General Assembly.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Governor to include a mandatory minimum appropriation of at least $80,533,314 in the annual budget bill for the Locally Operated Transit System Grant Program, sourced from the Transportation Trust Fund, beginning in fiscal year 2026.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation
qa_results.stakeholders: Locally Operated Transit Systems (LOTS) and local governments in Maryland
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill establishes a mandated minimum appropriation of $80,533,314 for the Locally Operated Transit System (LOTS) Grant Program beginning in fiscal 2026. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not expected to increase total expenditures because current funding levels ($93.7 million in fiscal 2025) already exceed the mandate; however, it reduces the Maryland Department of Transportation's flexibility to reduce program funding during future budget deficits.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the agency that administers the LOTS Grant Program, the MTA is the primary entity responsible for the distribution of the mandated funds.
The bill mandates funding from the Transportation Trust Fund, which is managed by MDOT, and impacts the department's overall budget flexibility and long-term planning.
DBM is the principal agency for developing the state budget and ensuring that mandated appropriations are included in the Governor’s budget bill for statewide program operations.
The bill explicitly requires the Governor to include a specific minimum appropriation for the LOTS Grant Program in the annual budget bill starting in fiscal year 2026.

The LOTS Grant Program funds transit systems that are critical for providing accessible transportation and paratransit for residents with disabilities, ensuring they remain integrated in their communities.
The locally operated transit systems supported by this bill provide essential transportation for older adults to reach healthcare, nutrition programs, and community services.
This grant program is a vital source of transit funding for Maryland's 18 rural counties, which the Council advocates for to improve quality of life and healthcare access.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Governor and state agencies regarding the implementation of statutory mandates within the executive budget process and transportation laws.
The Comptroller oversees the collection of revenues that fund the Transportation Trust Fund and is responsible for processing state payments and expenditures authorized by the budget.
The Treasurer's Office serves as the custodian of the Transportation Trust Fund and manages the state's financial assets used to support these grants.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill authorizes the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange to carry over and spend unused funds from previous years for the State-Based Young Adult Health Insurance Subsidies Pilot Program. Specifically, for calendar years 2024 and 2025, the Exchange may exceed the $20,000,000 annual subsidy limit by applying any unspent funds from prior calendar years to provide health insurance subsidies to eligible young adults.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Health Benefit Exchange
qa_results.stakeholders: Young adults aged 18 to under 41 with household incomes between 133% and 400% of the federal poverty level who seek health insurance through the State exchange.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Maryland Health Benefit Exchange special fund expenditures may increase in fiscal 2025 and 2026 to the extent that unspent funds from previous years are used to provide subsidies. The Exchange identifies approximately $12,900,000 in unspent funds from calendar 2022 and 2023 that would become available for use in calendar 2024 and 2025 under this bill. While this allows for increased spending in those specific years, there is no net increase in expenditures beyond the total funding initially allocated for the pilot program.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Health Benefit Exchange is the primary agency responsible for administering the State-Based Young Adult Health Insurance Subsidies Pilot Program. The bill directly authorizes the Exchange to manage the program's funds and designate unspent funds from previous years for future subsidies.

The Maryland Health Benefit Exchange is required by law to consult with the Insurance Commissioner (the head of the Maryland Insurance Administration) to establish eligibility and payment parameters for the pilot program. The bill also amends the Insurance Article of the Maryland Code.

The Secretary of Health serves on the Board of Trustees of the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange. Furthermore, the fiscal note highlights that enrollment in this subsidy program is significantly influenced by Medicaid redeterminations, which are managed by the Department of Health.

As the agency responsible for overseeing the state's budget and supervising budget execution, the Department of Budget and Management has an oversight interest in the allocation and expenditure of the special funds used for these health insurance subsidies.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and sets broad policy priorities, including health equity and access. The Governor signed this bill as an emergency measure necessary for the preservation of public health.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Maryland Health Benefit Exchange and the Maryland Insurance Administration, assisting with the interpretation of statutory changes and the drafting of necessary implementing regulations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the continuing education requirements for certified dialysis technicians by requiring them to complete three hours of continuing education approved by the Maryland Board of Nursing, rather than a single three-hour course, for the renewal of their certification.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Board of Nursing
qa_results.stakeholders: Certified dialysis technicians and the Maryland Board of Nursing
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no state or local fiscal impact because the changes are technical in nature and do not directly affect governmental finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Board of Nursing, which is a regulatory body under the Department of Health, is responsible for the certification and renewal process for dialysis technicians. This bill directly changes the continuing education requirements that the Department must enforce and verify for certification renewals.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal advice and representation to the Department of Health and its professional boards, including the Board of Nursing, regarding the implementation of the Health Occupations Article and regulatory changes.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and approved this legislation into law. The office maintains general oversight of the Department of Health's regulatory functions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the legal provisions that allow or require District Court judges in the City of Frederick to sentence vagrants or municipal law offenders to work on public roads in Frederick County or the City of Frederick.
qa_results.responsible_party: District Court of Maryland and the Frederick County Sheriff's Office
qa_results.stakeholders: Offenders of municipal laws in the City of Frederick, the Frederick County Sheriff, and the City of Frederick government
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not have a material impact on State government operations or finances. It also does not have a material impact on the operations or finances of the City of Frederick or Frederick County.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Maryland Judicial Branch by repealing a statutory requirement that District Court judges in the City of Frederick assign certain offenders to work on public roads.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals a section within the Correctional Services Article of the Maryland Code, which is the primary regulatory domain of this department regarding the custody and rehabilitation of offenders.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency coordinates public safety strategies and sentencing policy; the repeal of an offender labor program falls under its purview of monitoring criminal justice reforms.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office is responsible for the oversight of the state's correctional framework and the signing of legislation that alters municipal law enforcement procedures.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the participation goal for the Small Business Reserve (SBR) Program from 15% to 20% of the total dollar value of certain State procurement contracts. It allows all contract awards made to small businesses to count toward this goal, rather than only those specifically designated as SBR contracts. The Maryland Department of Transportation is required to develop an outreach program to increase participation among small businesses owned by economically disadvantaged individuals, and State agencies must update their reporting procedures to reflect these changes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT) and the Governor’s Office of Small, Minority, and Women Business Affairs (GOSBA)
qa_results.stakeholders: Small businesses in Maryland, specifically those owned by economically disadvantaged individuals, and State procurement agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures across all funds may increase if the higher participation goal leads to more contract set-asides, which can limit competition and result in higher contract costs. However, the Department of Legislative Services estimates the impact will be minimal in the near term because actual participation (11.7% in FY 2023) is currently below the existing 15% goal. The Maryland Department of Transportation can implement the required outreach and reporting using existing resources. There is no local government fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for implementing and monitoring the Small Business Reserve (SBR) Program, GOSBA is tasked with establishing standards, collecting data, receiving annual reports from all state units, and consulting on the new outreach program mandated by the bill.

Acting as the designated 'certification agency' for the SBR program (as identified in the fiscal note), MDOT is specifically required by the bill to develop an outreach program for economically disadvantaged individuals and submit annual reports on its status and results.

The Board of Public Works designates the certification agency, oversees significant state procurement contracts, and is a recipient of the mandatory annual reports regarding the outreach program and SBR award status.

DGS houses the Office of State Procurement, which serves as the central procurement authority. It is responsible for assisting in the administration and exemption procedures of the SBR program and ensuring state-level contract compliance with the new 20% goal.
The OAG is required to consult with the Special Secretary of GOSBA to establish program standards and guidelines, and also assists in the study of the Minority Business Enterprise and SBR programs.

GOSBA is a coordinating office within the Executive Department. The bill impacts executive branch procurement policies, and the Governor is a member of the Board of Public Works, which has oversight duties defined in the bill.
The Comptroller is a member of the Board of Public Works, which approves significant state contracts and receives the mandated reports on the SBR outreach program and award data.
The State Treasurer is a member of the Board of Public Works, which oversees state procurement and receives the reports required by the bill regarding SBR participation and outreach.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees budget execution and procurement costs; the bill's change to procurement goals may have a minimal fiscal impact on state contract expenditures which DBM monitors.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System to temporarily suspend the retirement benefit of a retiree of the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System who is elected as a sheriff within one year of their retirement date. The benefit, including accumulated cost-of-living adjustments, is reinstated once the individual ceases serving as sheriff. Survivorship benefits remain protected for the retiree's family during the suspension period.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Retirees of the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System who are elected as sheriffs within one year of retirement.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no discernible effect on State or local pension liabilities, contribution rates, or revenues, as it is expected to apply to a limited number of cases.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Retirement Agency is the primary agency responsible for managing the State Retirement and Pension System (SRPS). The bill directly mandates the Board of Trustees of the SRPS, through the State Retirement Agency, to implement the suspension and reinstatement of retirement benefits for retirees elected as sheriffs.

The Maryland State Police is the state's primary law enforcement agency, and its officers are major participants in the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System (LEOPS). Its retirees are directly affected by these changes if they are elected to the office of sheriff.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services employs various law enforcement personnel who participate in the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System (LEOPS), making their retirees eligible for the reemployment conditions described in the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the State Retirement Agency and the Board of Trustees, and would be responsible for advising on the legal interpretation and enforcement of the new pension suspension requirements.
As the state's principal personnel and fiscal agency, the Department of Budget and Management oversees state employee benefits and the long-term fiscal planning related to state pension systems.
The State Treasurer serves as a key member of the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, which is responsible for overseeing the benefits managed by this legislation.

The Comptroller functions as the state's chief financial officer and serves as a member of the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, which will oversee the implementation of these benefit suspensions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits the parking of certain vehicle classes, including heavy trucks (Class E), tractors (Class F), trailers (Class G), school buses (Class H), and passenger buses (Class P), on streets or highways within the Town of Keedysville in Washington County. The restriction does not apply to passenger vehicles, light trucks under 7,000 pounds, or vehicles parked for essential immediate use.
qa_results.responsible_party: Town of Keedysville
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners and operators of commercial trucks, tractors, trailers, school buses, and passenger buses in the Town of Keedysville, Washington County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's Fiscal Note indicates that there is no material effect on State or local government finances or operations, and enforcement is expected to be handled using existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Maryland Vehicle Law (Transportation Article) to establish specific parking restrictions. As the umbrella agency for the state's transportation systems and laws, MDOT is responsible for the overall policy and legal framework affected by this legislation.

The bill's parking restrictions are defined based on vehicle registration classes (Class E, F, G, H, and P). The MVA is responsible for the registration and classification of these vehicles, making their regulatory framework central to the bill's implementation.

The bill prohibits parking on any 'street or highway' within the town limits. Since the SHA is responsible for the construction and maintenance of state-numbered highways, some of which may pass through Keedysville, the agency is impacted by these localized restrictions.

The Administrative Office of the Courts was identified as an information source in the fiscal note. The judiciary is responsible for processing citations, handling contested parking violations, and collecting fines generated under the amended law.

The Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing the Maryland Vehicle Law throughout the state. While parking enforcement is often handled locally, the MSP retains enforcement authority on state-numbered highways that may be affected by the bill's provisions.

qa_results.bill_summary: Known as the Katherine Morris Death Reclassification Act, this bill requires medical examiners to notify local law enforcement and the State's Attorney when a death certificate is amended to reflect a cause or manner of death as undetermined or homicide. It mandates that the Secretary of Health provide an amended death certificate to a 'person in interest' at no cost, requires law enforcement to reopen or reinvestigate such cases and keep them open for at least 20 years, and requires assistant State's Attorneys to meet with family members upon request to explain why an investigation may or may not be pursued.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health, Office of the Chief Medical Examiner, local law enforcement agencies, and State's Attorney's offices
qa_results.stakeholders: Persons in interest (spouses, adult children, parents, siblings, grandparents, or guardians of the deceased), law enforcement agencies, and State's Attorneys
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill's requirements can be absorbed with existing budgeted resources by the Maryland Department of Health, State's Attorney's offices, and local law enforcement agencies. This assessment is based on the low volume of death certificates historically amended to undetermined or homicide (6 cases statewide across 2022 and 2023).

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health oversees the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner (OCME), which is responsible for death investigations and death certificate corrections. The Secretary of Health is also mandated by the bill to provide amended death certificates to persons in interest at no cost.

The bill modifies the processes following the reclassification of a death, which often results from contested case hearings and findings of fact submitted by administrative law judges within the Office of Administrative Hearings.

As a primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police is directly impacted by the requirement to reopen or reinvestigate deaths when a certificate is amended to homicide or undetermined, and it must comply with the new mandate to keep such cases open for at least 20 years.

The bill references the process of appealing death certificate correction decisions through the circuit court system, which is part of the judicial branch supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner and represents the state in administrative appeals and circuit court cases related to death certificate reclassifications.

The Governor's Office provides administrative support to the Governor, who oversees the executive agencies responsible for implementing this bill (MDH and OAH) and who signed the legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Washington County Board of Education to waive enrollment requirements for high school students to participate in interscholastic athletics at their zoned high school if their current school lacks an athletic program. It also mandates that these students be included in the zoned school's enrollment count for athletic classification purposes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Washington County Board of Education and Washington County Superintendent of Schools
qa_results.stakeholders: Public high school students in Washington County, the Washington County Board of Education, and the Maryland Public Secondary Schools Athletic Association (MPSSAA).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no material impact on State or local finances. Any potential administrative or transportation costs are considered voluntary since the board is authorized but not required to grant waivers.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts school policy regarding interscholastic athletics and student eligibility. The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) oversees the Maryland Public Secondary Schools Athletic Association (MPSSAA), which is the body responsible for the regulations (COMAR) mentioned in the bill and the organization to which Washington County must report enrollment for school classification.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates state policy initiatives. The Governor signed this specific local legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill mandates the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission (MPTSC) to create a uniform system for receiving positive community feedback regarding law enforcement officers. Law enforcement agencies are required to adopt this system, maintain feedback records for all officers, and make unsolicited feedback available for public inspection under the Maryland Public Information Act.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission and Maryland law enforcement agencies
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland law enforcement officers, law enforcement agencies, and the general public
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission can develop and implement the required feedback process using existing resources. There is no expected material impact on State or local expenditures or revenues. Law enforcement agencies are expected to manage the administrative requirements of maintaining records and handling public information requests within their existing budgets.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission (MPTSC), which falls under this category, to develop a uniform citizen positive community feedback process for all law enforcement agencies.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Police Training and Standards Commission is administratively housed within this department, which oversees state correctional and public safety functions.
As the state's primary law enforcement agency, it is directly required to adopt the uniform feedback process, maintain records of positive feedback, and make them available for public inspection.

This department oversees several modal administrations with law enforcement agencies, such as the Maryland Transit Administration Police and the Maryland Port Administration, which must comply with the bill's requirements.
MDTA operates its own nationally accredited police force, which is specifically subject to the requirement of adopting the feedback process and maintaining public records.
DNR operates the Natural Resources Police, which must adopt the feedback process and maintain public records for its officers as mandated by the bill.
DGS oversees the Maryland Capitol Police, which is a law enforcement agency required to adopt the uniform citizen feedback process and maintain related records.
The system's member institutions operate campus police departments that are classified as law enforcement agencies and must comply with the new record-keeping and transparency standards.
As an independent state agency with its own campus police department, the university must adopt the uniform feedback process and manage the required records.
The college operates its own campus police department that falls under the bill's requirements for law enforcement agency compliance.
The OAG investigates police-involved fatalities and provides legal counsel on the Maryland Public Information Act, which this bill amends regarding the definition of personnel records.

The Comptroller's office was identified as an information source in the fiscal note, likely due to its regulatory enforcement agents who may be impacted by the changes to law enforcement record requirements.
Listed as an information source in the fiscal note, the department oversees enforcement units like the Office of Controlled Substances Administration that may be subject to these new standards.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOCPP coordinates public safety policy and advises the Governor on criminal justice strategies, making the implementation of uniform law enforcement feedback processes relevant to its mission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Somerset County Commissioners to enact local laws and adopt measures to manage, direct, and regulate fire, rescue, and emergency medical services in the county. It also permits the county commissioners to create an administrative entity to oversee these services while maintaining volunteer emergency services and requires the establishment of an Emergency Services Advisory Council to provide operational recommendations if such an entity is formed.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Somerset County
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Somerset County, volunteer fire companies, rescue squads, and the Somerset County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no State fiscal impact. According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect Somerset County operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives. As the chief executive, the Governor signed this bill into law, which grants Somerset County specific authority to reorganize its local emergency services administration.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the independent state agency responsible for regulating and coordinating Maryland's statewide EMS network, MIEMSS is directly relevant to local measures that manage or regulate emergency medical services in Somerset County.

The Department of Emergency Management coordinates state preparedness and supports local jurisdictions. A change in how Somerset County manages and directs its fire and rescue services affects the operational coordination between the county and the state during emergencies.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the County Commissioners of Somerset County to increase annual appropriations for eight specific volunteer fire companies from fiscal year 2025 through 2030. The funding levels established for fiscal year 2030 will remain in effect for subsequent years unless modified by future legislation.
qa_results.responsible_party: Somerset County Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Volunteer fire companies in Somerset County and the residents served by those companies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances. Locally, Somerset County expenditures will increase by $7,317 in fiscal 2025, with annual increases leading to a total additional expenditure of $46,154 by fiscal 2030. This represents a 2.0% annual increase for each company. County revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor signed the bill into law and oversees the executive branch's coordination with local jurisdictions as established in the Public Local Laws.

MDEM coordinates Maryland's preparedness and supports local jurisdictions. Volunteer fire companies are essential partners in the state's emergency response and public safety framework, and their funding levels are relevant to state-wide resilience.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS oversees and regulates the statewide EMS network. Since Somerset's volunteer fire companies are primary providers of emergency medical services, this funding mandate directly supports the stability of the EMS system the agency manages.
The OAG reviews all legislation for legal sufficiency and provides counsel to the Governor and the General Assembly regarding the enforcement of state and local laws.

The RMC addresses the unique challenges of Maryland's rural counties, including Somerset. The sustainability and funding of essential services like volunteer fire companies are core to the agency's mission of improving the quality of life in rural areas.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This agency promotes volunteerism and civic engagement across Maryland; volunteer fire companies are a primary component of the state's volunteer service and community support infrastructure.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Somerset County Commissioners to sell a specific county-owned property located on Revells Neck Road (Tax Map 32, Parcel 149) under terms the commissioners consider appropriate, bypassing the standard public bidding requirements for surplus property.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Somerset County
qa_results.stakeholders: The government and residents of Somerset County, and potential purchasers of the designated Revells Neck Road property.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no impact on State finances. For Somerset County, the fiscal note indicates the bill will not materially affect the operations or finances of the county, although it enables the sale of a specific parcel of surplus land.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office is responsible for the final administrative review and signing of House Bill 977 into law, which provides the necessary legal authorization for Somerset County to conduct the specific land sale.

The Office of the Attorney General reviews all legislation passed by the General Assembly for legal sufficiency and constitutionality prior to gubernatorial action, ensuring that local land sale authorizations comply with Maryland law.

The Department of Planning monitors local land use policy and maintains the State's property maps and parcel data records, which are used to identify the specific Revells Neck Road property (Tax Map 32, Parcel 149) authorized for sale by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill, known as the Biodiversity and Agriculture Protection Act, streamlines the regulation of invasive plant species in Maryland by replacing the existing two-tier system with a single category of "prohibited invasive plants" and a "watch list." It requires the Secretary of Agriculture to establish assessment protocols for nonnative plants, maintain a consolidated list of invasive species, and distribute these lists to the horticultural industry. The bill also authorizes the State Highway Administration to manage prohibited plants along state highways and mandates that state agencies provide public access to these lists on their websites.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Agriculture
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed nurseries, plant dealers, plant brokers, landscaping services, state agencies such as the State Highway Administration and Department of Natural Resources, and local governments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's implementation results in a State expenditure increase of approximately $226,626 starting in FY 2025. This includes $196,626 for three new positions within the Maryland Department of Agriculture's Plant Protection and Weed Management Program and an estimated $30,000 annual expenditure for the State Highway Administration. Local governments and small businesses in the horticultural industry may also face indeterminate costs for removing and disposing of an expanded list of prohibited invasive plants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Agriculture is the primary implementing agency for the bill. The Secretary of Agriculture is responsible for adopting regulations, establishing the Consolidated List of Maryland Invasive Plant Species and the watch list, overseeing the Invasive Plants Advisory Committee, and distributing these lists to industry stakeholders.

The Department of Natural Resources is involved in the assessment of nonnative plants, provides advice to the Secretary of Agriculture on regulations, and is required to post the consolidated invasive plant and watch lists on its website.

The bill specifically authorizes the State Highway Administration (SHA), an agency of MDOT, to control and dispose of prohibited invasive plants along state highways without prior approval. The fiscal note estimates an annual expenditure increase of approximately $30,000 for SHA.
The bill mandates that the University of Maryland Extension, which is part of the University System of Maryland, post links to the consolidated invasive plant and watch lists on its website.

The Attorney General is tasked with instituting proceedings to collect expenses incurred by the Secretary of Agriculture for the destruction of prohibited invasive plants when owners fail to dispose of them.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is involved in the allocation of funding for the three new positions within the Department of Agriculture identified in the fiscal note as necessary to implement the bill.

DGS manages many state-owned facilities and may incur costs for the removal and disposal of prohibited invasive plants on state-owned or state-leased properties, as noted in the fiscal note regarding state agencies as landowners.
MES is identified in the fiscal note as an agency that may incur additional costs for removing and disposing of prohibited invasive plants on properties it manages or owns.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a major state landowner, DPSCS may incur costs related to the removal and disposal of prohibited invasive plants from its facility grounds, as referenced in the fiscal note.
DJS is mentioned as an agency that may be impacted by costs associated with plant removal on state-owned property.
Morgan State University, as a state agency and landowner, may incur costs for the removal of prohibited invasive plants on its campus, as identified in the fiscal note.
St. Mary's College, as an independent state agency and landowner, is listed as potentially incurring fiscal impacts for plant management on its grounds.
The Administrative Office of the Courts is listed as an information source in the fiscal note regarding the bill's impact, indicating a minimal but acknowledged administrative relevance.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Secretary of Health to present decisions for the allocation of money from the Opioid Restitution Fund (ORF) to the Opioid Restitution Fund Advisory Council and to publish these decisions and the Council's recommendations on the department's website. It expands the permissible uses of the ORF to include supporting community-based nonprofit recovery organizations providing nonclinical services and requires a report on making opioid settlement expenditures transparent to the public.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and Maryland's Office of Overdose Response
qa_results.stakeholders: Community-based nonprofit recovery organizations, local health departments, political subdivisions, and individuals with substance use disorders.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that State ORF revenues and expenditures are not materially affected, as the Maryland Department of Health can implement the bill using existing budgeted resources. Local government revenues and expenditures may increase to the extent that local health departments qualify as community-based recovery organizations eligible for funding under the bill's expanded criteria.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MDH is the primary agency impacted by the bill. The Secretary of Health is responsible for presenting allocation decisions for the Opioid Restitution Fund, and the department must staff the Opioid Restitution Advisory Council through the Office of Overdose Response. MDH is also tasked with website reporting and preparing a study on settlement expenditures.

The bill mandates the Advisory Council to provide findings and recommendations directly to the Governor. Additionally, the bill renames and restructures roles for special secretaries and offices within the executive branch that report to the Governor.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides legal counsel to the Governor and MDH. As the agency that negotiates global opioid settlements, they are critical to the legal framework governing the use of the Opioid Restitution Fund.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the membership, staffing, and reporting requirements of the Opioid Restitution Advisory Council, which is an advisory body under this classification.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: GOCPP is a key policy coordinating body for public safety. The bill includes the enforcement of opioid prescription laws as a permitted use of the fund, which aligns with GOCPP's mission to reduce crime and manage public safety funds.

DBM oversees the state budget and the execution of funds. As the bill dictates how Opioid Restitution Fund money must be allocated and used (including specific percentages for community organizations), DBM is responsible for the fiscal oversight of these expenditures.
The Treasurer's Office is the custodian of state funds, including the Opioid Restitution Fund, and manages the state's financial accounts.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller is responsible for the accounting and payment of state funds, including those distributed from the Opioid Restitution Fund.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly allows the Opioid Restitution Fund to be used for peer support and treatment services in correctional facilities, directly impacting the operations of DPSCS.
One of the authorized uses of the fund revised by the bill is the organization of primary and secondary school education campaigns to prevent opioid use.
The bill includes enforcement of laws regarding opioid prescriptions and sales as a permitted use for the fund, which falls under the law enforcement mandate of the State Police.
MDEM is identified as a stakeholder and information source in the fiscal note regarding the impact of the opioid crisis and state response coordination.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires all principal departments of the Executive Branch to provide equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency (LEP) by July 1, 2025. Agencies must provide oral language services and translate vital documents for any LEP population constituting at least 3% of the geographic area served. Departments created on or after October 1, 2024, must comply within one year of their creation.
qa_results.responsible_party: Principal departments of the Executive Branch, including the Maryland Department of Disabilities and the Maryland Department of Emergency Management.
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals with limited English proficiency in Maryland and principal departments of the Executive Branch.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by approximately 100000 in FY 2025 and 200000 annually thereafter. These costs are primarily driven by the Maryland Department of Emergency Management's requirement to provide 24/7 translation services. Other departments like the Maryland Department of Planning and the Department of Disabilities expect minimal fiscal impacts that are likely absorbable within existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the Executive Branch, the Governor's Office oversees the principal departments newly mandated to implement language access services and ensures interagency coordination to meet the bill's requirements.
The Comptroller is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

The OAG is already listed in the statute's implementation schedule and is specifically named as a consultant to the Secretary of Human Services in determining which additional independent agencies must comply with language access requirements.
The Department of Aging is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Department of Agriculture is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

As a principal department not previously listed in the implementation schedule, DBM is newly required by this bill to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.
The Department of Commerce is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

The department was the primary subject of the bill as originally drafted and is newly required as a principal department to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.
The Department of Education (MSDE) is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
As a newly included principal department, MDEM must implement LEP access by July 2025; the fiscal note indicates a notable impact of approximately $200,000 annually for expanded translation services.
The Department of the Environment is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

As a principal department not previously listed in the implementation schedule, DGS is newly required by this bill to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.
The Department of Health is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

The Department of Housing and Community Development is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

DHS is already listed in the statute; additionally, the Secretary of Human Services is specifically tasked with determining which independent agencies, boards, or commissions must implement language access requirements.

As a principal department not previously listed in the implementation schedule, DoIT is newly required by this bill to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.
The Department of Juvenile Services is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Maryland Department of Labor is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Department of Natural Resources is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

As a principal department not previously listed in the implementation schedule, MDP is newly required by this bill to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a newly created principal department, DSCI is required by this bill to fully implement equal access to public services for LEP individuals by July 1, 2025.
The Department of Transportation (MDOT) is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

The Department of Veterans and Military Families is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Department of State Police is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Commission on Civil Rights is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Maryland Transit Administration is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.
The Workers' Compensation Commission is already listed in the statute as an entity required to fully implement equal access to public services for individuals with limited English proficiency.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the pay rates for certain Maryland Department of Health (MDH) employees by expanding eligibility for specific salary tiers. It includes teachers and librarians in bargaining unit G and employees working at the Regional Institutes for Children and Adolescents in Baltimore and Rockville, requiring their pay to be set at or calculated at a rate equal to similarly qualified employees at the Clifton T. Perkins Hospital.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland Department of Health employees in bargaining units D, E, F, G (specifically teachers and librarians), and H; specifically those working at Clifton T. Perkins Hospital, the Regional Institutes for Children and Adolescents (RICAs) in Baltimore and Rockville, and facilities within the Behavioral Health Administration or Developmental Disabilities Administration with high forensic admission rates.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by at least $87,900 and reimbursable funds increase by at least $13,600 beginning in fiscal 2025, for a total impact of $101,500. This increase reflects a roughly 10% salary hike for nine eligible employees. Total annual expenditures are projected to rise to $120,500 by fiscal 2029. There is no impact on local government revenue or expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates pay rate increases and specific salary calculations for several classes of employees within the Maryland Department of Health, specifically those working in the Behavioral Health Administration, the Developmental Disabilities Administration, and facilities like Clifton T. Perkins Hospital and the Regional Institutes for Children and Adolescents.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency for the state, DBM is responsible for managing state employee resources, benefits, and salary classifications. It must supervise the execution of these mandated pay increases and integrate the fiscal impact into the state budget.

The bill modifies pay requirements specifically for employees within designated collective bargaining units (Units D, E, F, G, and H). PERB is responsible for the administration of collective bargaining and labor relations for state executive branch employees.

The Comptroller functions as the state's principal financial officer and pays state employees. This bill mandates salary increases that the Comptroller's office must process and administer through the state payroll system.

The Governor oversees the executive branch agencies affected by this bill and is responsible for proposing the annual state budget, which must reflect the increased personnel expenditures mandated by this legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Health to conduct a study on the impact of requiring the Maryland Medical Assistance Program to provide comprehensive coverage for obesity treatment, which includes intensive behavioral therapy, bariatric surgery, and FDA-approved antiobesity medications. The department must identify coverage in other states and estimate potential savings, reporting its findings to the General Assembly by December 31, 2024.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland Medical Assistance Program recipients and the Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, Maryland Department of Health expenditures increase by $250,000 in fiscal 2025 only for an actuarial analysis to support the required study. This expenditure is funded by a 50/50 split between State general funds ($125,000) and federal funds ($125,000), with federal fund revenues increasing by $125,000 accordingly. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates the Maryland Department of Health to conduct a study on the impact of requiring the Maryland Medical Assistance Program to provide comprehensive coverage for obesity treatment and report its findings to the General Assembly.

The fiscal note identifies a $250,000 expenditure increase for actuarial analysis to support the mandated study, which falls under the purview of DBM as the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency.

As the agency responsible for health system planning and providing information on the cost and quality of health services, the study's focus on obesity treatment costs and potential savings directly aligns with their mission.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Health regarding the implementation of legislative mandates and any regulatory changes required for the Medical Assistance Program.

The study must examine comprehensive coverage, which includes bariatric surgery—a hospital-based procedure. The HSCRC regulates hospital rates and revenues, making the financial impact of this coverage relevant to their oversight.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates state policy initiatives, including the Department of Health's study and the reporting of findings to the legislature.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Montgomery County Planning Board to tentatively approve subdivision plats subject to the completion of specific conditions. It streamlines the process by removing the requirement for a post-completion hearing once the specified conditions have been met, allowing the plat to be approved and submitted to land records immediately upon completion of the conditions.
qa_results.responsible_party: Montgomery County Planning Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Land developers, property owners, and the Montgomery County Planning Board
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not directly affect State finances and is not expected to materially affect local government finances. A formal Fiscal Note indicates that the impact on small businesses is minimal and no specific revenue or expenditure changes are projected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Subdivision plats must be recorded in the land records of the county, which are maintained by the Clerks of the Circuit Court. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides operational and technological support for these court functions.

The Maryland Department of Planning provides technical assistance, data analysis, and policy guidance to local governments regarding land use planning and the implementation of the Land Use Article, which this bill amends.
The Maryland State Archives is the central depository for permanent judicial and county records, including land records and subdivision plats, and is responsible for their long-term preservation.

This multi-agency initiative coordinates state and local planning visions related to land use, transportation, and resource conservation, all of which are impacted by changes to subdivision plat regulations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill excludes cement production from the definition of manufacturing for the purposes of greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reduction requirements, allowing the State to regulate the sector's emissions. It mandates that the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) consider specific economic and technological factors and consult with stakeholders when regulating cement production. Additionally, it adjusts GHG regulation limits for other manufacturers active in 2023, ensuring they are not required to reduce emissions below 2023 levels or face significant cost increases compared to that year, and exempts manufacturers of renewable energy components from certain emission reduction requirements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE)
qa_results.stakeholders: Cement producers, manufacturers, state and local government agencies, and small businesses involved in construction.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Department of the Environment can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There may be an indeterminate increase in costs for cement on state and local construction projects, which could result in fewer projects receiving funding within fixed capital budgets; however, specific dollar estimates for these cost increases are unavailable. Revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of the Environment is the primary agency responsible for implementing the Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reduction Act (GGRA). The bill specifically tasks MDE with regulating cement production, consulting with stakeholders, and considering energy intensity and financial incentives for emissions abatement.

The bill directly impacts the manufacturing sector by altering the definition of manufacturing and changing greenhouse gas emission reduction protections. Commerce, as the primary economic development agency, is concerned with business retention and the competitive position of Maryland's industrial sector.

DGS oversees the construction and maintenance of state-owned facilities. The fiscal note indicates that this bill may lead to higher cement costs for state construction projects, directly impacting DGS operations and project budgets.

MDOT is responsible for building and maintaining the state's transportation network, including highways and bridges which require significant amounts of cement. Increased material costs will affect the fiscal planning of the State Highway Administration and other modal units.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for developing the state's budget and long-term fiscal planning. The fiscal note specifies that while total capital spending may not change, higher cement costs could reduce the number of projects funded annually.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives such as greenhouse gas reduction. The Governor is also responsible for proposing the annual state budget, which would be impacted by rising infrastructure material costs.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like MDE. They will likely be involved in the drafting, review, and enforcement of new regulations regarding cement production and manufacturing emissions standards.

The Board of Public Works approves significant state contracts and expenditures. Higher costs for cement on state construction and infrastructure projects will require fiscal oversight by the Board during the procurement process.

The Maryland Stadium Authority plans, finances, and builds large-scale facilities. Increased costs for cement, as noted in the fiscal impact analysis, would affect the budgeting and execution of their construction projects.

This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and reducing the state's carbon footprint. The bill's focus on refining greenhouse gas reduction targets for the manufacturing and cement sectors is directly aligned with the initiative's goals.
The Department of Labor was listed as an information source for the fiscal note and regulates various industrial professions and workplace safety within the manufacturing sector.

MES provides technical environmental services to state and local governments. As MDE develops new regulations for industrial emissions, MES may be involved in operational or technical support for compliance and abatement projects.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a food processing residuals utilization permit program within the Maryland Department of Agriculture (MDA). It prohibits persons from utilizing food processing residuals—organic material generated by processing agricultural products for human or animal consumption—without a permit. The bill sets operational requirements for agricultural sites, including the use of nutrient management plans, creates the Food Processing Residuals Administration Fund to cover regulatory costs, and authorizes the MDA to administer a certification program for commercial haulers.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Agriculture (MDA)
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners and operators of agricultural operations, commercial brokers, commercial haulers of food processing residuals, and food processing plants.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, special fund revenues and expenditures are estimated to increase by $481,700 in FY 2025. MDA is required to set permit and tonnage fees to approximate the costs of administering the program. General fund revenues may increase minimally due to interest earnings from the new special fund and the collection of administrative penalties, which are set at up to $5,000 per day with a $50,000 cap per violation.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Agriculture (MDA) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill.
