It is tasked with establishing the Food Processing Residuals Utilization Permit program, administering the Food Processing Residuals Administration Fund, conducting site inspections, and potentially managing a Commercial Hauler Certification Program.

The bill requires the Department of Agriculture to consult with the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) when adopting regulations for the new permit program and the Commercial Hauler Certification Program. Additionally, the bill references compliance with MDE discharge permits for agricultural operations.

The Office of the Attorney General (OAG) provides legal counsel and enforcement support for the new regulatory program. The fiscal note explicitly accounts for the addition of one Assistant Attorney General position to assist the Department of Agriculture with permit reviews and enforcement actions.

The Department of Budget and Management (DBM) is relevant due to its oversight of state personnel and the fiscal impact of adding new positions (specialist and administrative roles) to the Department of Agriculture to manage the new regulatory program.

The bill specifies that all fees collected under the new permit program must be paid to the Comptroller of Maryland, who then distributes them to the newly established Food Processing Residuals Administration Fund.

The Maryland State Treasurer's Office is responsible for the management and investment of state funds. The fiscal note indicates the Treasurer will hold and invest money in the newly established special fund, with interest earnings accruing to the General Fund.

The bill explicitly requires that the accounts and transactions of the Food Processing Residuals Administration Fund be subject to audit by the Legislative Auditor, which falls under the Office of Legislative Audits (OLA).

The bill mandates that permit suspensions, revocations, or modifications be handled in accordance with the Administrative Procedure Act. The fiscal note also quantifies potential costs for administrative hearings, which are conducted by the Office of Administrative Hearings (OAH).

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (MDA, MDE) responsible for the program, and the Governor signed the bill into law. The office also coordinates policy initiatives that may include agricultural and environmental regulations.

The bill involves administrative and potential criminal penalties for violations of the new permit requirements, which could lead to legal proceedings within the state's judicial system overseen by the Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC).

The bill requires the Department of Agriculture to provide notices to local health officials regarding permit applications and potential violations. The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) oversees local health departments and shares concerns regarding public health risks related to residuals.

The Rural Maryland Council (RMC) addresses challenges facing rural counties and advocates for agricultural interests. The establishment of a new permitting system for agricultural operations significantly impacts the farming community the Council represents.

The Maryland Food Center Authority (MFCA) operates facilities for food distribution and processing. As the bill regulates residuals from food processing plants, tenants or operations within MFCA facilities may be directly impacted by the new permit requirements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires local health departments or county governments that have been delegated authority to manage well and septic programs to submit a schedule of review times for well and septic program permits to the Maryland Department of the Environment for approval. Additionally, these delegated authorities must submit permit data to the department every three months for review.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment
qa_results.stakeholders: Local health departments, county governments, property owners, developers, and licensed well drillers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill's requirements can be handled with existing budgeted resources by both the Maryland Department of the Environment and local governments. No impact on state or local revenues is expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly requires the Department of the Environment (MDE) to receive, review, and approve schedules of review times for well and septic program permits from delegated authorities. It also requires MDE to receive and review periodic permit data from these local entities.

The bill impacts Local Health Departments (LHDs), which often serve as the 'delegated authorities' for well and septic permitting. The Maryland Department of Health oversees these local departments and has been involved in studies regarding the delegation of this permitting authority.

As the executive office overseeing state agencies, the Governor's Office is responsible for the overall administrative coordination of departments like MDE and MDH, and the Governor signed this legislation into law.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies like the Department of the Environment regarding the implementation and enforcement of new regulatory requirements and delegated authority agreements.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes residency requirements for members of the Washington County Board of Education, requiring them to be residents of the county for at least 6 months and the State of Maryland for at least 12 months immediately preceding their term. Members must also maintain their primary residence in Washington County during their term; failure to do so disqualifies them from continuing as a member.
qa_results.responsible_party: Washington County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Current and future members of the Washington County Board of Education, and residents of Washington County.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note states that the bill has no effect on State or local finances, as it is procedural in nature.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly grants the State Board (referring to the State Board of Education, the governing body of MSDE) the authority to remove a local board member for specific causes, such as misconduct or neglect of duty, and requires them to conduct administrative hearings if requested.

Since Washington County Board of Education members are elected at large, the State Board of Elections is responsible for overseeing candidate eligibility, including the residency requirements established by this bill.
The bill provides for a de novo review of a board member's removal by the Circuit Court for Washington County, involving the judicial branch in the adjudication of these disputes.
The OAG would provide legal counsel to the State Board of Education during removal proceedings and represent the state in any subsequent judicial reviews in the Circuit Court.
As the chief executive, the Governor oversees the executive agencies involved in education and elections and signs local board-related legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill renames the Consumer Council within the Division of Consumer Protection of the Office of the Attorney General to the Consumer Protection Commission. It also clarifies the commission's role in advising the division, undertaking studies, and fostering cooperation among agencies.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Office of the Attorney General, Division of Consumer Protection
qa_results.stakeholders: The Consumer Protection Commission (formerly the Consumer Council), the Office of the Attorney General, and the Maryland Division of Consumer Protection.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not materially affect State finances or operations. There is no fiscal impact on local government or small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Consumer Protection Commission is an advisory body within the Division of Consumer Protection, which is a unit of the Office of the Attorney General. The Division Chief of Consumer Protection also serves as a member of the Commission.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly renames and amends the operating structure of the Consumer Council, which is an executive advisory body. This collective designation encompasses such entities within the state government.
The Governor is responsible for appointing nine members to the Consumer Protection Commission with the advice and consent of the Senate.
The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for state boards and commissions, including the nine members of the newly renamed Consumer Protection Commission.
The bill authorizes per diem compensation for commission members in accordance with the state budget, which is managed and executed by the Department of Budget and Management.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Department of Labor to conduct a study on the 'actively seeking work' requirement for unemployment insurance, specifically focusing on the impact of 'ghosting,' which occurs when a claimant fails to attend a scheduled job interview or maintain contact with an employer. The study must evaluate how other states verify work-seeking activities, examine the feasibility of updating the BEACON system to allow employers to report ghosting incidents without requiring Social Security numbers, and provide recommendations for system improvements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Unemployment insurance claimants and employers
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Department of Labor can complete the required study using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Maryland Department of Labor to conduct a study on the unemployment insurance system, specifically focusing on the 'actively seeking work' requirements and the effects of 'ghosting' by claimants.

The study requires an evaluation of the online BEACON system (the State's IT portal for UI claimants and employers) to determine why it cannot currently allow employers to input information about ghosting and whether modifications are feasible.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies, including the Department of Labor, and would likely be involved in reviewing the legal implications of changes to UI eligibility verification and the use of Social Security numbers in reporting systems.

This agency's mission is to enhance the efficiency and responsiveness of state government operations using data-driven strategies, which aligns with the bill's focus on verifying UI eligibility and improving the technical capabilities of state reporting systems.

The board coordinates state workforce solutions and is concerned with policies that impact unemployment and the labor market, including UI eligibility requirements and employer-employee interactions during the job search process.

As the executive authority over the Department of Labor, the Governor's Office oversees major policy studies and the implementation of UI system reforms that may arise from the findings of this report.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Workgroup on Establishing a Science and Technology Best Practices and Innovation Network. The workgroup is tasked with identifying and evaluating options for creating an open-access network or hub for citizen scientists and industry experts to facilitate collaboration, data exchange, and evidence-based decision-making. The workgroup must report its findings and recommendations to the Governor and General Assembly by December 1, 2024.
qa_results.responsible_party: University of Maryland, Baltimore County
qa_results.stakeholders: Citizen scientists, academic researchers, private and nonprofit sector technology experts, and Maryland institutions of higher education including HBCUs and community colleges.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill's requirements can be handled using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on state revenues or local government finances. While workgroup members serve without compensation, they are entitled to reimbursement for travel expenses as provided in the State budget.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly establishes a new Workgroup on Establishing a Science and Technology Best Practices and Innovation Network, which falls under this collective entity of temporary advisory bodies.

The Executive Director of TEDCO (or their designee) is a mandated member of the Workgroup. The workgroup's focus on a science and technology innovation network aligns with TEDCO's core mission of supporting tech-based businesses and innovation.

The University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC), which is part of the USM, is specifically tasked with providing staff for the Workgroup. Additionally, the workgroup requires representatives from institutions of higher education.

The Governor is responsible for appointing ten members to the Workgroup (five community experts and five higher education experts). The Workgroup is also required to submit its final report of findings and recommendations to the Governor.

The bill requires at least one representative from a historically Black college or university (HBCU) to be appointed to the Workgroup. As a prominent state HBCU, Morgan State University is a likely participant or stakeholder in this process.
The Office of Appointments will likely assist the Governor in the recruitment and vetting of the ten specific representatives from the community and higher education sectors required by the bill.

MHEC oversees the state's higher education policies. The bill significantly involves higher education institutions (HBCUs, community colleges, and independent institutions) in the development of the innovation network.

While the Secretary of Commerce was removed as a designated member in the bill's amendments, the agency remains a stakeholder in the state's technology and innovation strategy and was listed as an information source in the fiscal note.

The Workgroup must include representatives from institutions of higher education, including independent or public honors colleges. St. Mary's is a state agency and stakeholder in higher education innovation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Maryland Attorney General, or a designated Deputy or Assistant Attorney General, to issue subpoenas to produce documentary material, answer written interrogatories, and provide sworn oral testimony to further ongoing health care fraud investigations. It also establishes a process for the Attorney General to report a failure to obey such a subpoena to a circuit court for enforcement and appropriate relief.
qa_results.responsible_party: Office of the Attorney General
qa_results.stakeholders: Health care providers, individuals under investigation for health care fraud, and the Office of the Attorney General.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill's changes are generally procedural in nature and have no meaningful impact on State or local governmental finances. There is no anticipated material fiscal or operational impact on the circuit courts.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill is highly relevant as it directly grants the Attorney General, Deputy Attorneys General, and Assistant Attorneys General the specific authority to issue subpoenas for health care fraud investigations, specifically supporting the work of the Medicaid Fraud and Vulnerable Victims Unit.

The bill addresses health care fraud involving Maryland Department of Health funds. The OAG's Medicaid Fraud and Vulnerable Victims Unit coordinates with the Department's Inspector General to investigate fraud, waste, and abuse of departmental resources.
The bill establishes a process where failures to comply with OAG subpoenas are reported to and adjudicated by the circuit courts, which are supported by the Administrative Office of the Courts.
The bill identifies the Office of Legislative Audits Fraud Hotline as a primary contact for individuals when the Health Department's Inspector General is unable to investigate a fraud complaint.
As the office overseeing the executive branch, the Governor's Office manages policy coordination between the primary agencies involved (OAG and MDH) and the Governor signed this legislation into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes subdivision plats in Montgomery County to be drawn, recorded, and maintained in an electronic format approved by the State Archivist, providing an alternative to traditional physical media requirements like transparent mylar or linen.
qa_results.responsible_party: Clerk of the Circuit Court for Montgomery County and the Maryland State Archivist
qa_results.stakeholders: Landowners, land developers, the Montgomery County Circuit Court, and the Maryland State Archives
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect Maryland State or Montgomery County operations or finances. The fiscal note indicates that any impact on small businesses will be minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly tasks the State Archivist with approving the specific methods and formats for drawing, recording, and maintaining electronic plats in Montgomery County. The agency is also responsible for receiving and filing copies of these plats as part of the state's permanent records.

The bill modifies the recordation duties of the Clerk of the Circuit Court for Montgomery County, which is a component of the state judiciary. The Administrative Office of the Courts provides the operational and technological support necessary to manage these court records and implement the transition to electronic forms.

The bill amends a section of the Real Property Article (Section 3-108) that governs the submission of plats by the State Highway Administration, an agency within MDOT. While the primary changes focus on Montgomery County's electronic options, SHA is explicitly mentioned in the statute's standards for plat submission and recordation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill provides that salary increases intended to address compensation inequities or to align pay with market rates for a specific position classification are not considered 'extraordinary salary increases' when calculating a member's average final compensation for retirement benefits within the State Retirement and Pension System. To qualify, a retiring member must submit an employer-certified form to the State Retirement Agency documenting that the increase meets these criteria.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Retirement Agency
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the State Retirement and Pension System, including the Employees', Teachers', Law Enforcement Officers', Correctional Officers', State Police, and Local Fire and Police systems.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is expected to have no discernible effect on State or local pension liabilities or contribution rates. The State Retirement Agency reported very few instances of extraordinary salary increases, noting only one such case in fiscal 2023. Additionally, the Board of Trustees of the State Retirement and Pension System already possesses some statutory authority to determine that certain increases are not extraordinary.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency responsible for administering the State Retirement and Pension System, the SRA is tasked with implementing the bill's changes to average final compensation calculations and processing the required employer certification forms.

DBM is the state's central personnel agency and is responsible for the position classification reviews and market adjustments that would now be exempted from being considered 'extraordinary salary increases' under this bill.

The bill explicitly amends sections of the State Personnel and Pensions Article that govern the State Police Retirement System, directly affecting how the average final compensation is calculated for its members.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Retirement Agency and the Board of Trustees, and would be involved in interpreting these new statutory requirements and drafting any necessary regulatory updates.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill applies to members of the Correctional Officers' Retirement System, which constitutes a significant portion of the department's workforce, requiring the agency to certify salary adjustments at the time of an employee's retirement.
The bill covers the Teachers' Pension and Retirement Systems; as the leader of the state's public education system, MSDE is relevant to policies affecting the benefits of school system personnel.

MDOT is a major state employer with many staff in the Employees' Pension and Retirement Systems. The agency will be responsible for completing certification forms for employees receiving qualifying compensation adjustments.

The University System employs a large number of individuals who participate in the State Retirement and Pension System and will need to certify salary adjustments for employees to ensure their pension calculations are accurate under this bill.

As a state agency and employer whose personnel participate in the State Retirement and Pension System, Morgan State will be required to certify that certain salary increases for its staff were related to classification adjustments.

Staff at St. Mary's College participate in the state pension system, and as an independent state employer, the college will have administrative responsibilities regarding the certification forms required by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands membership in the Correctional Officers’ Retirement System (CORS) to include specific employee classifications within the Maryland Department of Health and the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services. It provides for the transfer of service credits from the Employees' Pension and Retirement Systems to CORS, allows members to receive credit for unused sick leave, and specifies the calculation of disability benefits for those transferred into the system.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System
qa_results.stakeholders: Designated employees of the Maryland Department of Health (at various state hospital centers) and the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services (Correctional Maintenance Regional Managers)
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State pension liabilities will increase by approximately $13,700,000, and the normal cost will increase by $2,500,000. Consequently, State pension contributions are projected to increase by approximately $4,870,000 starting in fiscal 2027, with costs expected to be allocated among general funds (60%), special funds (20%), and federal/other funds (20%). There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Retirement Agency is the primary implementing body for this bill. It is responsible for managing the transition of specified employees into the Correctional Officers' Retirement System (CORS), transferring service credits from the Employees' Pension System, calculating disability and retirement benefits, and administering the member opt-out process.

The bill directly impacts over 800 employees of the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) across multiple facilities (including Spring Grove, Springfield, and Eastern Shore Hospital Centers) by changing their retirement system membership from the Employees' Pension System to the Correctional Officers' Retirement System.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill adds 'Correctional Maintenance Regional Managers' within the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services (DPSCS) to the Correctional Officers' Retirement System, representing a direct shift in benefits for these employees.

As the state's principal personnel and fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for the overall management of state employee benefits and the fiscal impact of increased state pension contributions. The bill is projected to increase state pension liabilities by $13.7 million and annual contributions by nearly $5 million starting in FY 2027.

The State Treasurer's Office oversees the Maryland State Retirement Agency and the Treasurer chairs the Board of Trustees for the State Retirement and Pension System, which is specifically tasked by the bill with conducting the service transfers and benefit calculations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the timeframe during which the State of Maryland can recover grant funds from a project under the Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHC) Grant Program if the property is sold, transferred, or ceases to be an FQHC. Specifically, for properties leased by an FQHC, the recovery period is reduced from 30 years to 15 years after project completion, while the recovery period for owned properties remains at 30 years.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Board of Public Works
qa_results.stakeholders: Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs), nonprofit organizations, and local governments (counties and municipalities) that receive state grants for health center projects.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund revenues may increase by an indeterminate amount sooner than expected due to the acceleration of the State’s right of recovery for leased properties from 30 to 15 years. Any additional workload for the Maryland Department of Health can be handled with existing budgeted resources. There is no direct effect on local government operations or finances, and the impact on small businesses is minimal. Specific dollar estimates for potential revenue increases are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for administering the Federally Qualified Health Centers Grant Program. The Secretary of Health makes recommendations for project funding and the department handles the application process for grants.

The Board of Public Works is explicitly named in the bill as the body that makes the grants on recommendation and approves transferees. The bill alters the conditions under which the Board/State may initiate recovery proceedings for grant funds.

The Office of the Attorney General represents the state in legal matters, including the recovery proceedings and the pursuit of reasonable attorneys' fees explicitly mentioned in Section 24-1306 of the bill.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors state revenues and expenditures. The fiscal note indicates an indeterminate impact on general fund revenues due to the acceleration of recovery rights, which falls under DBM's fiscal oversight.

The Governor is a member of the Board of Public Works, which is the entity responsible for awarding the grants and approving the transfer of properties involved in the Federally Qualified Health Centers Grant Program.
The Comptroller serves as a member of the Board of Public Works and is the state's chief tax collector. Any recovered grant funds would be processed as state revenue through the Comptroller's office.
The State Treasurer is a member of the Board of Public Works and manages the state's financial assets and bond revenues, which are often the source of capital grants for health centers.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows Maryland State officers and employees who are required to have surety bonds to be covered by insurance policies instead. It abolishes the Maryland State Employees Surety Bond Committee and transfers its duties and responsibilities to the State Treasurer, who is now responsible for determining the type and amount of coverage for state personnel.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Treasurer
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland State officers and employees required to be bonded or insured, particularly those entrusted with state funds or property.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is expected to result in operational efficiencies and potential cost savings because insurance policies may be less expensive than traditional surety bonds. The Maryland State Treasurer’s Office can manage the new requirements using existing resources, and the bill does not affect state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill was introduced at the request of the State Treasurer and transfers the duties of the Maryland State Employees Surety Bond Committee to this office. The Treasurer is granted the authority to determine the type and amount of coverage for surety bonds and insurance policies for state officers and employees.

The Comptroller was a member of the Maryland State Employees Surety Bond Committee being abolished. The bill also requires the Comptroller to maintain records of bonds issued for state officers and employees.
The Attorney General was a member of the committee being abolished. The bill retains roles for the Attorney General in approving the form of certain bonds and providing legal consultation.

The bill impacts various boards and commissions housed within the Department of Labor (under Business Occupations and Business Regulation articles). Additionally, the Secretary of Labor must be consulted regarding insurance for the Treasurer as custodian of the Unemployment Insurance Fund.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically amends statutes regarding the surety bond and insurance requirements for the Commissioner of Correction and other officers and employees within the Division of Correction.
The bill explicitly modifies the Agriculture Article to update bonding and insurance requirements for committee employees and soil conservation district supervisors.
The bill updates bonding and insurance requirements for members of various authorities and entities related to natural resources, such as waste disposal authorities.
The bill specifically mentions bonding and insurance policy options for employees of the State Highway Administration.
The bill updates the Insurance Article to specify that the Insurance Commissioner and Deputy Commissioner may be covered by insurance policies in lieu of surety bonds, as determined by the Treasurer.
The bill alters the bonding requirements for fiduciaries of the State Retirement and Pension System, requiring consultation with the Board of Trustees.
The bill updates the Education Article to allow fiduciaries of the Maryland 529 trust (now managed by the Treasurer's Office) to be covered by insurance policies.
The bill specifically names the Legislative Auditor as an officer who must be covered by a surety bond or insurance policy of the type and amount determined by the Treasurer.
The bill modifies the Natural Resources Article specifically regarding the surety bond or insurance requirements for the Treasurer and Deputy Treasurer of the Maryland Environmental Service.
The bill amends the Economic Development Article regarding the bonding and insurance requirements for members, the Executive Director, and employees of the Authority.

The Governor retains authority to establish certain bond amounts and types for key officials like the Comptroller, the State Treasurer, and the Commissioner of Correction, in consultation with the Treasurer.

As the state's principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is impacted by changes to the administration of employee benefits (insurance vs. bonds) and the resulting impact on the state budget for premiums.
The bill repeals provisions that required the Board of Public Works to approve the purchase of bonds by the State Treasurer, streamlining the administrative process.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes licensed clinical professional counselors (LCPCs) to sign the certificates of illness or disability required for Maryland State employees to receive pay when using five or more consecutive workdays of sick leave for their own illness or that of an immediate family member.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Budget and Management
qa_results.stakeholders: State employees within the State Personnel Management System and licensed clinical professional counselors
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state or local fiscal impact as it generally codifies existing practice and is procedural in nature. It does not directly affect governmental finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the central personnel agency for the State Personnel Management System (SPMS), DBM is responsible for managing state employee benefits, resources, and leave policies. This bill directly amends the statutory requirements for sick leave certification that DBM oversees for the majority of the state's executive branch workforce.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides counsel to state agencies on the interpretation and implementation of personnel laws, including the authorized use of sick leave and the validity of medical certifications.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Frederick County School Construction Master Plan Workgroup to evaluate school facility needs, balance fiscal demands for construction priorities, and explore cost-saving or cost-sharing opportunities for new projects and renovations in Frederick County.
qa_results.responsible_party: Frederick County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Frederick County Public Schools, the Frederick County Board of Education, the Frederick County Executive and Council, and Frederick County residents and students.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Interagency Commission on School Construction and Frederick County Public Schools can implement the bill's requirements using existing resources. There is no expected impact on State or local revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Interagency Commission on School Construction is directly involved as it is required to have a member serve on the workgroup and will receive the workgroup's final report and recommendations regarding Frederick County's educational facilities master plan.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill creates a new, temporary workgroup composed of public servants and appointed members to conduct a study and provide recommendations on a specific government issue (school construction in Frederick County).
The workgroup is tasked with evaluating school facility needs and master plans for a local school system (Frederick County Public Schools), which falls under the leadership and oversight of MSDE.
The Governor signed the bill into law and oversees the executive department agencies, such as the IAC, which are involved in the workgroup's reporting and membership.
The bill specifies that workgroup members are entitled to reimbursement for expenses under the Standard State Travel Regulations, which are administered by the Department of Budget and Management.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill consolidates the two existing soil conservation districts in Frederick County—the Catoctin Soil Conservation District and the Frederick Soil Conservation District—into one single Frederick County Soil Conservation District. This alignment makes the district boundaries correspond exactly with the county boundaries and reduces the total number of soil conservation districts in Maryland from 24 to 23.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Agriculture
qa_results.stakeholders: Farmers, landowners, and residents of Frederick County, as well as the supervisors and staff of the consolidated soil conservation districts.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local expenditures or revenues. The Maryland Department of Agriculture provides financial and technical assistance to the districts, and because the two Frederick County districts already share an office, consolidation is not expected to change operational costs. The existing statutory mandate requiring $10,000,000 in annual funding for all state soil conservation districts remains in effect.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Agriculture (MDA) is the primary agency providing leadership, financial assistance, and department staff to soil conservation districts. This bill directly consolidates two districts into one within the MDA's scope of operations.

The Department of Budget and Management oversees the state's budget, which includes a statutory mandate to provide at least $10 million annually to the soil conservation districts. The bill updates the statutory number of districts from 24 to 23 for budget appropriation purposes.

Soil conservation districts are designated as approval authorities for sediment control plans under the Environment Article, which is implemented and enforced by the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE).

The Governor's Office is responsible for proposing the annual state budget, which includes mandated funding for soil conservation districts, and for signing the legislation that alters these administrative boundaries.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the County Commissioners of Washington County to borrow up to $50,000,000 through the issuance and sale of general obligation bonds to finance various public facilities projects, including water and sewer systems, public schools, roads, bridges, and loans to volunteer fire departments for equipment and facilities.
qa_results.responsible_party: County Commissioners of Washington County
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Washington County, Washington County Government, and volunteer fire departments in Washington County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Washington County will receive up to $50,000,000 in bond proceeds for capital improvement projects. The county's debt service expenditures are expected to increase by approximately $3,400,000 annually over a 20-year period, based on an estimated 3.37% interest rate. There is no impact on State finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor signed this local bill into law and oversees the executive department responsible for state laws. While primarily a local authorization, it requires state executive approval through the legislative process.

The bill stipulates that the bonds, interest, and income derived from them are exempt from State and local taxation. As the state's chief tax collector and regulator of tax laws, the Comptroller is responsible for the administration of these tax exemptions.

The bill authorizes the use of bond proceeds for installment purchase obligations to acquire agricultural land preservation easements. This aligns with the Department's mission to protect environmental resources and promote the viability of farming.

A significant portion of the bond proceeds ($17 million) is dedicated to public school capital projects, including maintenance and new elementary school construction. The Department provides oversight and leadership for Maryland's 24 local school systems.

The bill authorizes financing for public facilities including water and sewer infrastructure and landfills. The Department of the Environment regulates these sectors to ensure water quality and proper waste management.
The bill includes the construction and improvement of facilities for public health as a permitted use for the authorized bond proceeds.
The bill explicitly allows bond proceeds to be used for the acquisition or improvement of buildings and facilities for social services.
The bill allows for the acquisition of woodland preservation easements, which falls under the conservation and resource management mission of the Department of Natural Resources.

The largest portion of the bond funding ($28.8 million) is allocated to public works projects, including roads, bridges, culverts, and sidewalks, which are primary focus areas for the Department of Transportation.
The bill authorizes funding for community college buildings and facilities. The Commission is the regulatory and coordinating board for all public colleges and universities in the state.

The agency is responsible for overseeing public school construction and capital improvement projects. Given the bill allocates $17 million for school facilities in Washington County, this commission would be relevant for planning and execution oversight.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill reforms the Washington Suburban Transit Commission (WSTC) by allowing the Secretary of Transportation’s designee to attend WMATA Board meetings with full powers when the Secretary is unavailable, authorizing the reappointment of specific WSTC appointees to the WMATA Board, and removing the requirement for Prince George’s and Montgomery county governments to approve WSTC contracts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Washington Suburban Transit Commission and Maryland Department of Transportation
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents of Montgomery and Prince George's counties
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not materially affect State or local government operations or finances according to the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly pertains to the Secretary of Transportation and their designee on the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority (WMATA) Board of Directors. It specifies that the designee must be an employee of the Department of Transportation, have transit qualifications, and establishes their powers when attending meetings on the Secretary's behalf.

The Governor is responsible for appointing members to the Washington Suburban Transit Commission (WSTC). The bill modifies the rules regarding the reappointment of Governor-appointed commissioners to the WMATA Board of Directors.

As a modal administration within the Department of Transportation, the MTA is responsible for operating transit systems and coordinating mass transit programs with entities like WMATA and the WSTC. Changes to the governance and contracting authority of these transit bodies impact the broader transit landscape the MTA manages.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in vetting and recommending candidates for state boards and commissions. This bill changes the reappointment criteria for a Governor-appointed position on the WMATA Board, which falls under their administrative purview.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Washington Suburban Transit Commission (WSTC) is a state-authorized body that falls under this broad category of executive commissions. The bill specifically reforms the powers and board composition of this commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the Washington Suburban Sanitary Commission (WSSC) Connection Pipe Emergency Replacement Loan Program to include sewer pipes and diagnostic actions. It increases the maximum individual loan amount from $5,000 to $10,000 and doubles the total outstanding loan limit for the program to $2,000,000. Additionally, it raises the mandatory annual budget allocation for the program to $200,000 and extends the program's termination date from 2029 to 2034.
qa_results.responsible_party: Washington Suburban Sanitary Commission (WSSC)
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential customers of the Washington Suburban Sanitary Commission in Montgomery and Prince George's counties, and licensed plumbers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: WSSC expenditures increase by $100,000 annually from fiscal 2025 through 2034 for additional funding to the loan program. Program revenues increase upon repayment of loans via charges on customer water and sewer bills. The bill increases the individual loan cap to $10,000 and stipulates that WSSC may not have more than $2,000,000 in total loans outstanding at any one time.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the Washington Suburban Sanitary Commission (WSSC) to record loan-related liens in the land records of the county where the property is located. These land records are managed and maintained by the Clerks of the Circuit Court, which are supported and overseen by the Administrative Office of the Courts.

The bill expands a loan program that assists residential homeowners in financing the replacement of critical infrastructure (water and sewer pipes), which aligns with the agency's mission to support safe and secure housing and community revitalization.

As the primary state agency responsible for environmental protection and water quality, the expansion of a program to replace malfunctioning water and sewer pipes and identify leaks via diagnostic actions is relevant to the agency's oversight of state water and wastewater infrastructure.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state commissions and agencies. As the bill expands the statutory loan and lien authority of the WSSC ( a state-created entity), the OAG may be involved in advising on the implementation and legal enforcement of these provisions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill adds the U.S. Space Force to various Maryland laws governing the armed forces, ensuring its members receive the same protections and recognitions as other military branches. It expands consumer contract termination rights (such as for internet, gym, or telecommunications services) to military spouses and Maryland residents serving in the National Guard of other states or reserves when relocated under military orders. Furthermore, it updates the Purple Star Schools Program to include dependents of Space Force and out-of-state National Guard members and authorizes the Governor to award posthumous commissions or promotions within the Maryland Defense Force.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Military Department, Governor of Maryland, and the Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the U.S. Space Force, military spouses, Maryland residents serving in the National Guard of other states or reserve components, military-connected students, and consumer service providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. Small business impacts are expected to be minimal, primarily affecting providers of consumer services like telecommunications and gym memberships that may face contract terminations from eligible service members or spouses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Adjutant General is the head of the Maryland Military Department and recommends the appointment and commissioning of officers. The bill amends Public Safety Article provisions regarding the organized militia, incorporating the Space Force and authorizing posthumous commissions which fall under the Adjutant General's oversight.

The bill directly impacts the National Guard by requiring the agency to host a notice of service member rights on its website. It also incorporates the Space Force into the Maryland Code of Military Justice and updates qualifications for militia members and judge advocates.

The bill expands the definition of 'military-connected student' for the Purple Star Schools Program, which is administered by the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE), to include dependents of Space Force members and members of the National Guard from other states.

The bill specifically authorizes the Governor to award commissions, appointments, or promotions posthumously within the organized militia and expands the Governor's authority regarding military appointments to include those with Space Force experience.

The bill amends the Family Law Article concerning custody and visitation orders for deployed parents to include Space Force members. The Judicial Branch must implement these updated criteria in family law proceedings.

The bill creates new consumer protection rights allowing military spouses and certain residents to terminate various service contracts (telecom, internet, etc.) upon receiving relocation orders, which falls under the OAG's enforcement of consumer protection laws.

The bill mandates that providers of telecommunication, internet, and television services allow contract terminations for military relocations. The PSC regulates many of these utility and communication providers in Maryland.

As the principal agency for assisting military families and advocating for their benefits, the bill's focus on supporting military spouses and expanding definitions to include the Space Force aligns with the department's core mission.

The bill expands criminal prohibitions regarding the unauthorized wearing of military uniforms to include the Space Force. As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police is responsible for enforcing these public safety laws.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment Workgroup to develop a framework for reliability and reporting standards for electric vehicle (EV) charging stations. The workgroup is responsible for studying government accountability and making recommendations for regulations regarding EV supply equipment, including payment options, uptime requirements, and consumer transparency.
qa_results.responsible_party: Public Service Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Electric vehicle owners and users, electric vehicle service providers, and private sector businesses in the electric vehicle industry.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Public Service Commission can staff the workgroup with existing budgeted resources. Expense reimbursements for workgroup members are expected to be minimal and absorbable within existing resources. The bill has no effect on State revenues or local government finances according to the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Service Commission is the primary agency responsible for the bill's implementation; it must provide staff for the Workgroup, the Chair designates the Workgroup chair, and the agency is a designated member of the Workgroup.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill's primary action is the establishment of the Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment Workgroup, which is a temporary body tasked with conducting studies and providing recommendations.
The Comptroller is a designated member of the Workgroup and the agency is designated to receive reports on EV supply equipment reliability and status.

The Department of Agriculture is a designated member of the Workgroup. Furthermore, the Workgroup must recommend standards in accordance with NIST Handbook 44, which is typically overseen by the Department's Weights and Measures program.
The Secretary of Transportation designates a member to the Workgroup, and the agency is responsible for the state's transportation network and integration of zero-emission vehicles.
The Maryland Energy Administration is a designated member of the Workgroup and leads state efforts in promoting renewable energy and energy efficiency, including EV infrastructure.

The Workgroup is tasked with making recommendations regarding employee training and certification requirements for individuals who install or maintain EV supply equipment, which aligns with the Department of Labor's oversight of workforce training and professional regulation.
The bill requires the Workgroup to develop recommendations for EV charging payment options specifically for customers with disabilities.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the primary implementing agencies, including the Public Service Commission, regarding the development of the regulatory framework and civil penalties mentioned in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes and codifies the Tourist Area and Corridor (TAC) Signage Program to create a system of supplemental guide signs that direct motorists to groups of eligible attractions. Administered by the State Highway Administration in collaboration with the Department of Commerce, the program requires participating attractions to pay for the design, fabrication, installation, and maintenance of their signs, as well as administrative and review fees.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Highway Administration and the Maryland Department of Commerce
qa_results.stakeholders: Tourist attractions providing recreational, historical, cultural, or leisure activities; motorists; and businesses such as wineries, breweries, distilleries, and marinas.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures for the Department of Commerce increase by $53,000 in FY 2025 and $14,700 in FY 2026 for a contractual position. Transportation Trust Fund (TTF) expenditures increase by $86,400 in FY 2025 (rising to $117,900 by FY 2029) to manage the program. TTF revenues increase correspondingly through sign review and annual administrative fees collected from participating attractions. Local government finances are not materially affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates the State Highway Administration to lead the administration of the Tourist Area and Corridor Signage Program, investigate site feasibility, and oversee the review and approval of signage plans.
The bill requires the Department of Commerce to collaborate with the State Highway Administration in administering the program, providing initial attraction approvals, and coordinating signage plans.

As the state agency responsible for driving the tourism economy and managing the 'Visit Maryland' brand, this office within the Department of Commerce will be directly involved in identifying eligible attractions and coordinating tourism signage.
As the parent department of the State Highway Administration, the Department of Transportation oversees the infrastructure and funding (Transportation Trust Fund) impacted by this program.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates the establishment of an Eligibility Committee to develop standards and review applications for the signage program.
The fiscal note identifies the need for new personnel and general fund/special fund expenditures to implement the program, which falls under DBM's oversight of state personnel and budgeting.
The Department of Natural Resources manages state parks and waterways which constitute significant recreational attractions eligible for signage under the program's criteria.
The Department of Planning oversees historical and cultural programs, which are categories specifically listed as eligible attractions for the signage program.
As an independent agency managing a significant historical and cultural site, it would be a primary applicant for the signage program to direct motorists to its facilities.
The museum managed by this corporation is a key cultural attraction that would meet the eligibility criteria for the newly established signage program.
This agency manages a major heritage tourism area in Cumberland which is likely to utilize the program's signage to increase visibility for its recreational and historical assets.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive agencies (Commerce and MDOT) implementing the program and the Governor signed the bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the required number of assistant sheriffs to be appointed by the Baltimore City Sheriff from one to three. It also authorizes the Sheriff to appoint up to two social workers and additional sworn or civilian personnel, provided that funding is supplied via the ordinance of estimates or other sources.
qa_results.responsible_party: Baltimore City Sheriff
qa_results.stakeholders: The Baltimore City Sheriff's Office, current and prospective law enforcement and civilian staff, and the Baltimore City government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local finances. Baltimore City indicates the bill has no fiscal impact as it provides flexibility for staff appointments without directly altering the budget or the city's budget authorization process. At the time of the note, the Sheriff's Office had 40 vacant positions out of 212 total positions.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill modifies the staffing requirements for the Baltimore City Sheriff's Office, which is a constitutional office primarily responsible for court security and service of process. Changes to sheriff personnel can directly impact the operational environment and security of the judiciary, and the bill amends the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article.

The bill explicitly mentions the Secretary of Budget and Management in Section 2-316(i)(10), stating that collective bargaining agreements for the Sheriff's Office prevail over rules or regulations adopted by the Secretary of Budget and Management regarding merit system or personnel regulations.

The Governor is responsible for signing the legislation into law and oversees the executive branch. The Baltimore City Sheriff, while an elected official, is a constitutional officer whose staffing and organizational structure are governed by state law.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides counsel to all state agencies and boards. As the bill amends state statutes (Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article) regarding a state constitutional office, the OAG may be involved in legal interpretation or advising on the implementation of these staffing changes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Task Force on the Creation of a Division of Returning Citizens and Expanded Reentry Services. The task force is required to identify and assess existing reentry services, identify service gaps, and develop a comprehensive plan to establish a Division of Returning Citizens within the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services to coordinate pre-release and post-release support and reduce recidivism.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Formerly incarcerated individuals, currently incarcerated individuals and their families, state and local reentry and workforce development agencies, and nonprofit reentry organizations.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, state expenditures for task force staffing and member expense reimbursements are expected to be minimal and absorbable within existing budgeted resources. Local government finances are not expected to be materially affected, and there is no impact on state revenues.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS is the primary agency for this bill. The Secretary (or designee) is a required Task Force member, the agency must provide staffing for the Task Force, and the bill proposes the creation of a new Division of Returning Citizens within DPSCS.

The Secretary of Housing and Community Development (or designee) is a required member of the Task Force. Additionally, a major focus of the Task Force is developing plans to expand housing services, halfway houses, and home buyer programs for returning citizens.

The Secretary of Health (or designee) is a required member of the Task Force. The bill tasks the body with expanding health services for released individuals, including mental health, dental care, and substance abuse counseling.

The Secretary of Human Services (or designee) is a required member of the Task Force, which is charged with coordinating services for vulnerable populations returning to the community from incarceration.

The Secretary of Labor (or designee) is a required member of the Task Force. The bill and fiscal note emphasize expanding employment services, entrepreneur training, and vocational education (GED) for returning citizens, programs currently overseen by Labor.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Defender (or designee) is a mandated member of the Task Force. The bill specifically identifies the need for legal assistance in reentry, including help with child support and expungement of criminal records.
The Attorney General (or designee) is a mandated member of the Task Force, which will evaluate legal frameworks and services related to the rehabilitation and reentry of citizens.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly establishes a new Task Force of both public officials and private citizens to conduct a comprehensive study and provide recommendations on reentry services.

The Governor is responsible for appointing twelve specific members to the Task Force, including formerly incarcerated individuals, family members, and representatives from various nonprofit and legal organizations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As noted in the fiscal note, GOCPP manages the Performance Incentive Grant Fund which supports reentry programs. The Task Force's findings on recidivism and reentry success markers directly relate to the office's public safety strategies.
The Office of Appointments will likely assist the Governor in vetting and recommending the numerous private and stakeholder representatives mandated for the Task Force.

The Task Force is charged with developing plans to expand educational services, specifically including GED preparation and connections to further education for individuals released from incarceration.
The bill's mandate to expand 'connection to postsecondary education' for returning citizens directly impacts MHEC's coordination of policies for colleges and universities.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note identifies this office as a collaborator in microloan programs for formerly incarcerated individuals, which aligns with the bill's focus on expanding entrepreneur training and financial literacy for returning citizens.
The team's mission to enhance economic mobility and reduce child poverty aligns with the Task Force's goal of removing barriers to reentry and empowering residents to break cycles of recidivism.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill streamlines the hiring process for Maryland state agencies by repealing administrative delays in the recruitment of skilled and professional service positions. It eliminates the requirement for agencies to notify the Secretary of Budget and Management one week before posting a job announcement and allows hiring authorities to review applications as they are submitted instead of waiting for the posting period to end. Additionally, the bill mandates that the Department of Budget and Management actively recruit for vacant positions using school-to-work programs, apprenticeships, and local workforce board collaborations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Budget and Management
qa_results.stakeholders: State job applicants, current Maryland state employees, and state agency hiring managers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the bill has no direct impact on state or local finances as the changes are procedural. The bill is intended to create operational efficiencies by removing administrative requirements that currently delay the hiring process for skilled and professional service positions within the State Personnel Management System.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the principal personnel agency for the State, DBM is the primary agency affected. The bill requires the Department to actively recruit for vacant positions, develop apprenticeship programs, and collaborate with other entities to improve state hiring efficiency.

The bill requires the Department of Budget and Management to collaborate with local workforce development boards (under Labor's purview) and access labor and employment data to recruit for State positions.
The bill mandates collaboration with career counselors employed under the Blueprint for Maryland's Future and the use of educational data to assist in the recruitment of State employees.
The bill requires DBM to collaborate with local workforce development boards to improve state recruitment; the GWDB sets the statewide vision and coordination for these boards.

The bill requires DBM to actively promote union representation available for State employees during the recruitment process, which aligns with PERB's role in overseeing collective bargaining and union activities.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and workforce policy initiatives; the bill also requires DBM to study and make recommendations to the Governor on reducing hiring time.
As a major state employer with its own hiring authority, MDOT is impacted by the bill's changes to the timing and procedures for reviewing applicants and posting job announcements.
As a large executive agency and appointing authority within the State Personnel Management System, it will be impacted by the procedural changes to recruitment and applicant review.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As a major appointing authority with significant staffing needs, the department is impacted by the removal of hiring delays and the new active recruitment strategies mandated by the bill.
As a principal executive department, it functions as an appointing authority impacted by the changes in state personnel law intended to streamline hiring.
As an executive branch agency and appointing authority, MSP is affected by the changes to the State Personnel Management System procedures for posting and reviewing applications.
DGS is a principal unit of state government that acts as an appointing authority and is affected by the updated state employment regulations and recruitment requirements.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies regarding personnel laws and would be involved in advising on the implementation of updated recruitment and hiring practices.
The Comptroller manages state employee pay and serves as a major employer; the bill's changes to workforce policy and recruitment impact the administrative functions of the executive branch.
The Treasurer's Office is a key fiscal agency and employer impacted by the broader changes to State Personnel Management System policies and recruitment mandates.

While the bill primarily targets the State Personnel Management System, the Administrative Office of the Courts was listed as a source of information in the fiscal note, suggesting potential impacts on judicial branch hiring alignment.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits the sale, distribution, or marketing of self-administered sexual assault evidence collection kits unless issued by the Maryland Department of Health, classifying such activities as unfair or deceptive trade practices. It establishes that information derived from these kits is generally inadmissible in civil or criminal proceedings and voids any agreements that waive legal rights or warranties regarding sexual assault evidence collection products. Furthermore, it creates the Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program and a special fund to provide grants to nonprofits and higher education institutions for training nurses in forensic examination techniques.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy (GOCPP)
qa_results.stakeholders: Victims of sexual assault, forensic nurse examiners, nonprofit organizations providing victim resources, and higher education institutions in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by at least $50,000 annually starting in fiscal 2025 to capitalize the Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program Fund, with special fund revenues and expenditures increasing by a matching amount. Violators of the kit sale prohibition are subject to a civil penalty of up to $1,000 per violation, which is deposited into the grant fund. There is a potential minimal increase in grant revenues for local community colleges and state higher education institutions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates this office to establish and administer the Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program and its associated special fund, including setting application processes and eligibility criteria.

The Attorney General is the only entity authorized to enforce the consumer protection violations established by the bill and is required to adopt regulations for uniform statewide implementation of sexual assault evidence collection policies.

The bill's prohibition on self-administered kits specifically exempts kits issued by the Maryland Department of Health, and the agency is mentioned in the fiscal note as an information source regarding kit regulation.
The bill changes the rules of evidence admissibility in criminal and civil trials, which directly impacts judicial proceedings and the operations of the state court system managed by the AOC.
The bill requires the State Treasurer to hold the newly created Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program Fund separately and to invest the money in the fund.
The bill requires the Comptroller to account for the newly established Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program Fund.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The new evidentiary rules regarding the inadmissibility of information obtained through prohibited kits directly impact the legal representation provided by the Public Defender in criminal proceedings.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive agencies (GOCPP and Health) tasked with implementing the bill and is responsible for the state budget which must accommodate the new grant program.
DBM is responsible for the fiscal oversight of the new program and would manage any personnel or budgetary adjustments required to staff the grant program at GOCPP.
Member institutions of the University System are eligible to apply for grant funding under the Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program.
As a public institution of higher education, Morgan State is an eligible applicant for grants provided through the new Forensic Nurse Examiner Training Grant Program.
As a public honors college, St. Mary's is eligible to apply for grant funding established by the bill for nurse examiner training.
The agency coordinates policies for the higher education institutions that are identified as eligible recipients for the new grant program.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the membership and rules for the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access. It requires the two groups to work together, hold joint meetings, and issue shared reports. It also tasks them with recommending whether Maryland should continue to manage mental health and substance abuse services separately from other medical services in its Medicaid program.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents receiving behavioral health treatment, their families, healthcare providers, and state agencies including the Maryland Department of Health.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Health and the Department of Legislative Services indicate that the bill's requirements can be absorbed within existing budgeted resources. There is no projected impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is relevant because the Governor appoints members to both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access, and both bodies are required to submit their annual reports to the Governor.
The Secretary of Aging (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The Secretary of Budget and Management (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.
The Secretary of Disabilities (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The State Superintendent of Schools and the Division of Rehabilitation Services within the department are represented as statutory members of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.

As the primary implementing agency, MDH has five representatives on the Council, the Secretary serves on the Commission, and the bill requires the department to evaluate and potentially apply for federal behavioral health innovation models and manage Medicaid behavioral health integration.
The Secretary of Housing and Community Development (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.
The Secretary of Human Services (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The Secretary of Juvenile Services (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The Secretary of Labor (or a designee) is added as a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Secretary of Public Safety and Correctional Services (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
This office assists the Governor in vetting and recommending individuals for the various gubernatorial appointments to these boards and commissions as altered by the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly modifies the composition, meeting requirements, and reporting duties of two such permanent advisory bodies: the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Executive Director (or a designee) is a statutory member of both the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Executive Director (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.
The bill requires two representatives of the Maryland Judiciary (one District Court and one Circuit Court judge) to serve as members of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.
The Executive Director (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The Executive Director (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council.
The Executive Director (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.
The Maryland Insurance Commissioner (or a designee) is a statutory member of the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Defender is a statutory member of the Behavioral Health Advisory Council and the Governor must appoint a representative of the office to the Commission on Behavioral Health Care Treatment and Access.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Maryland Maternal Health Act of 2024 establishes comprehensive requirements for prenatal and postpartum assessments, referrals, and reporting. It requires healthcare providers to complete prenatal risk assessment forms for Medicaid patients and submit them to local health departments. Birthing facilities must provide specialized resources and conduct follow-up calls for patients following high-risk pregnancies. Furthermore, the bill mandates the creation of a 'Maryland Report Card for Birthing Facility Maternity Care' to track quality measures and requires hospitals and birthing centers to participate in the Severe Maternal Morbidity Surveillance and Review Program.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Pregnant individuals and birthing parents, newborns, licensed obstetric healthcare providers, hospitals, freestanding birthing centers, and local health departments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Department of Health and local health departments can implement the requirements of the bill using existing budgeted resources. No significant impact on state or local revenues is expected. While the bill codifies certain existing Medicaid assessment practices and introduces new reporting and administrative mandates for birthing facilities, these are expected to be handled within current operational budgets. Specific dollar estimates for potential Medicaid reimbursement denials for non-compliance are unavailable.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary implementer of the Maryland Maternal Health Act of 2024, the Department of Health (MDH) is responsible for developing standardized prenatal risk assessment forms, establishing an electronic submission process, overseeing the Severe Maternal Morbidity Surveillance and Review Program, and enforcing Medicaid reimbursement compliance for birthing facilities.

The bill explicitly mandates that the Secretary of Health collaborate with the Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC) to develop the Maryland Report Card for Birthing Facility Maternity Care and collect the data necessary to complete annual report cards for each facility.
