The Governor's Office is relevant as the Severe Maternal Morbidity Surveillance and Review Program is required to report its findings and recommendations to the Governor by December 1, 2025.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for overseeing the budgetary resources and personnel requirements for MDH to implement the bill's mandates, including the electronic submission system and new reporting requirements.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and the Maryland Health Care Commission regarding the development of the Report Card, data suppression best practices, and the enforcement of statutory requirements for health care facilities.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows the Baltimore City Board of License Commissioners to issue 12 new alcoholic beverage licenses in a specific area of the 46th district and provides exceptions for licenses near schools or churches under certain conditions. It also allows certain restaurants to remain open after hours by waiving room adjacency rules and extends the expiration of several specific licenses for transfer or renewal purposes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City
qa_results.stakeholders: Alcoholic beverage license holders, business owners, community associations, and residents in Baltimore City's 46th alcoholic beverages district.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Baltimore City revenues are expected to increase minimally starting in fiscal 2025. Local revenues will rise from application and annual fees for new Class B-D-7 ($1,920 initial) and Class A-7 ($2,100 initial) licenses, as well as a $15,000 special transfer fee for certain license conversions. There is no state fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the state's principal regulator of the alcoholic beverages industry and tax collector for the sector, the Comptroller oversees the broader regulatory framework within which the Baltimore City Board of License Commissioners operates, particularly regarding industry compliance and revenue collection from newly authorized licenses.

The Governor signed this bill into law and is responsible for appointing the members of the Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City, who are the primary officials tasked with implementing the licensing revisions and geographical exceptions authorized by this legislation.

The bill includes a specific prohibition against the transfer of licenses to persons or locations that have received funding through legislative bond initiatives; the Board of Public Works is the state body responsible for overseeing and approving such bond-funded expenditures and state grants.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel and representation to state agencies and boards, including the Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City, which may require legal guidance on implementing new license categories and enforcing memoranda of understanding with community associations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the qualifications for the Executive Director of the State Board of Nursing by removing the requirement that the individual be a registered nurse with a minimum of a master's degree in nursing or its equivalent in professional education and administrative experience.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Nursing
qa_results.stakeholders: Potential candidates for the Executive Director position, the State Board of Nursing, and the Maryland Department of Health.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no state or local fiscal impact. According to the fiscal note, the change is technical in nature and does not directly affect governmental finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Department of Health and its constituent boards. The Governor also approved this legislation, which modifies the operational and personnel requirements for a state board.

The State Board of Nursing is an agency housed within the Maryland Department of Health. This bill directly changes the statutory qualifications for the Board's Executive Director, a position over which the Secretary of Health currently exercises specific infrastructure and staffing authority.

The OAG serves as the legal counsel for the Maryland Department of Health and its various health occupation boards, including the State Board of Nursing. It would be responsible for providing legal guidance on the implementation of these modified statutory qualifications.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits 340B drug manufacturers from restricting or discriminating against the acquisition or delivery of 340B drugs to pharmacies acting on behalf of covered entities, such as community health centers and certain hospitals. It makes violations of these prohibitions unfair, abusive, or deceptive trade practices under the Consumer Protection Act and mandates a study of the 340B Program by the Maryland Prescription Drug Affordability Board.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Board of Pharmacy, Office of the Attorney General Consumer Protection Division, and the Maryland Prescription Drug Affordability Board
qa_results.stakeholders: 340B drug manufacturers, wholesale drug distributors, pharmacies, covered entities (such as federally qualified health centers and hospitals), and patients receiving discounted outpatient drugs.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State expenditures increase by $47,100 in FY 2025. This includes $39,600 in special fund expenditures for the State Board of Pharmacy to hire a part-time investigator and $7,500 in general fund expenditures for the Prescription Drug Affordability Board to conduct a mandated study (with an additional $7,500 in FY 2026). The Office of the Attorney General can handle the initial workload with existing resources. Violations are subject to a civil fine of up to $5,000 per violation, though the total revenue impact is not expected to be material.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly tasks the Consumer Protection Division of the Office of the Attorney General with investigating alleged violations of the Act. Furthermore, it designates violations of the 340B drug distribution rules as unfair, abusive, or deceptive trade practices under the Consumer Protection Act, which is enforced by this agency.

The Maryland Department of Health is required to consult with the Prescription Drug Affordability Board on a mandatory study regarding the 340B Program. Additionally, the State Board of Pharmacy, which resides within the department's regulatory structure, is authorized to investigate violations and impose disciplinary actions, such as license suspension or revocation, on licensees who violate the Act.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This entity encompasses independent bodies like the Maryland Prescription Drug Affordability Board (PDAB). The bill specifically mandates the PDAB to conduct a comprehensive study on the implementation, scope, and finances of the 340B Program in the state and to report its findings to the General Assembly by 2026.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, the Department of Budget and Management is impacted because the bill necessitates special fund expenditures for the State Board of Pharmacy to hire an investigator and general fund expenditures for the Prescription Drug Affordability Board to secure contractual support for the required study.

The Office of Administrative Hearings provides the impartial hearing process for contested cases. Because the bill allows the State Board of Pharmacy to impose discipline, suspension, or revocation of licenses for violations, any appeals of these regulatory enforcement actions would be adjudicated by Administrative Law Judges within this agency.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases the annual salary of the Sheriff of Carroll County. The salary is set to increase from $140,000 to $191,078.10 beginning in 2026, and then to $212,309 beginning in 2027. These increases will take effect at the start of the next term of office.
qa_results.responsible_party: Carroll County Government
qa_results.stakeholders: The Sheriff of Carroll County and Carroll County taxpayers
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, Carroll County expenditures will increase by approximately $47,000 in fiscal 2027 and by $84,100 annually thereafter. These figures include the salary increase and associated fringe benefits. There is no impact on state revenues or expenditures.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor signed the bill into law and is responsible for overseeing the executive branch, which includes coordinating policy initiatives related to public safety and local government relations.

The bill amends the Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article of the Maryland Code; sheriffs serve as officers of the court, and their compensation is codified within the legal framework overseen by the judicial branch's administrative agency.

The bill explicitly uses the Department of State Police pay plan, specifically the salary of a lieutenant colonel, as a benchmark and potential cap for the Carroll County Sheriff's future salary adjustments.

The agency administers the state pension systems for public officials and law enforcement; a change in the Sheriff's annual salary impacts the calculation of retirement contributions and future benefit distributions.

DBM is the state's central personnel and fiscal agency responsible for maintaining the state pay plan, including the State Police scales that serve as the mandatory reference for this local salary calculation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the legal definition of an 'underlying crime' used to identify a pattern of organized criminal activity within criminal organizations. It adds several offenses to this definition, including the use of a firearm in a crime, felony fraud and related crimes, bribery, false statements, child pornography, and the abuse or neglect of a vulnerable adult. By expanding this definition, the bill extends the reach of existing criminal organization laws to individuals and groups committing these specific acts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, Office of the Attorney General, and the Office of the Public Defender
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals associated with criminal organizations, law enforcement agencies, state and local prosecutors, the state judiciary, and the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is expected to have a minimal fiscal impact. State general fund expenditures may increase slightly due to more individuals being incarcerated in state facilities. The average variable cost of housing a new incarcerated individual is estimated at $1,244 per month. Local revenues may experience a minimal increase from criminal fines imposed in circuit courts.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor has the authority to request that the Attorney General investigate and prosecute violations of the criminal organization laws, as specified in the bill.

The bill explicitly authorizes the Attorney General to aid in the investigation and prosecution of criminal organization violations at the request of the Governor or a State's Attorney, granting them all the powers of a State's Attorney.
As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is concerned with the potential increase in general fund expenditures for incarceration and public defense resulting from the expanded scope of criminal penalties.

The bill involves the Addiction Treatment Divestiture Fund, which is established under the Health - General Article and receives assets divested from criminal organizations involved in specific drug-related offenses.
The bill adds 'abuse or neglect of a vulnerable adult' to the definition of 'underlying crime,' which relates to the protective services managed by DHS.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill expands felony definitions which may lead to an increase in convictions and commitments to State correctional facilities; the fiscal note explicitly identifies a potential increase in department expenditures.
The bill includes 'removal or falsification of vehicle identification number' as an underlying crime, which falls under the regulatory and safety purview of the Motor Vehicle Administration.

As the primary state law enforcement agency, MSP is responsible for investigating the various underlying crimes (such as child pornography, firearms violations, and organized crime) added or affected by the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office coordinates state public safety strategies and manages funds to reduce crime and recidivism, aligning with the bill's focus on prosecuting and dismantling criminal organizations.
The bill impacts the Judiciary through the expansion of crimes that can be prosecuted in circuit courts and the potential for court-ordered divestiture or dissolution of enterprises.
The bill adds felony insurance fraud to the list of underlying crimes that can constitute a pattern of organized crime activity.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The expansion of underlying crimes to establish criminal organization felonies is expected to increase the workload and resource requirements for the OPD in defending indigent clients.
The MVA is specifically relevant due to the inclusion of vehicle identification number (VIN) falsification as a new underlying crime for criminal organization prosecutions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill allows for an income tax subtraction modification for death benefits paid to a surviving spouse or beneficiary of a deceased law enforcement officer or firefighter, provided the death occurred in the line of duty. The subtraction applies specifically to payments made under a collective bargaining agreement by a county or municipality. The bill is retroactive, applying to tax years 2022 and beyond.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Comptroller's Office
qa_results.stakeholders: Surviving spouses and beneficiaries of deceased law enforcement officers and firefighters; Maryland counties and municipalities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund revenues and local income tax revenues are expected to decrease, likely minimally, beginning in fiscal 2024 due to the subtraction modifications. Because the number of eligible beneficiaries and the specific amounts of death benefit payments under collective bargaining agreements are unknown, a precise dollar estimate of the revenue loss is not feasible. The Comptroller's Office can implement the bill with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the primary agency responsible for administering the state's income tax laws. This bill creates a new income tax subtraction modification, which the Comptroller must implement, process, and regulate.

The bill modifies a section of the tax code that currently provides subtractions for payments from pension systems to beneficiaries of deceased law enforcement officers and firefighters. The agency manages these retirement and death benefits for state and participating local employees.
DBM is responsible for state revenue estimation and long-term fiscal planning. The bill is expected to decrease general fund revenues, which impacts the state budget managed by this department.
The bill specifically addresses death benefits provided under collective bargaining agreements. PERB oversees and standardizes collective bargaining for the public sector in Maryland.
As a primary employer of law enforcement officers in the state, the Maryland State Police is affected by changes to tax laws that benefit the surviving families of their personnel.
MDOT employs law enforcement officers through the Maryland Transportation Authority (MDTA) police, whose beneficiaries are eligible for the tax modifications mentioned in the bill.
DNR employs the Natural Resources Police, whose members qualify as law enforcement officers under the bill's definitions.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DPSCS employs a significant number of law enforcement and justice-involved personnel whose families may be impacted by state tax policies regarding line-of-duty death benefits.

The Tax Court adjudicates disputes regarding tax assessments and modifications. Any legal challenges or appeals regarding the qualification for this new subtraction modification would fall under its jurisdiction.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Comptroller and other state agencies on the interpretation and enforcement of tax laws.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and has administrative responsibility for the signing and enactment of this tax legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Maryland Entertainment District Security Grant Program and a dedicated special fund to provide grants for security operations in entertainment districts characterized by high pedestrian traffic and crime calls. The program supports local governments, nonprofits, and community organizations in enhancing safety near establishments serving alcohol, hookah bars, or live entertainment venues.
qa_results.responsible_party: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention, Youth, and Victim Services
qa_results.stakeholders: Community organizations, neighborhood associations, nonprofit entities, local governments, and businesses within designated entertainment districts.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $250,000 in fiscal 2025 due to existing budget allocations and are estimated to increase by $1,000,000 annually starting in fiscal 2026 to capitalize the Maryland Entertainment District Security Grant Program Fund. Special fund revenues and expenditures increase accordingly to facilitate grant awards. Local governments may see a revenue increase if awarded grants, with a corresponding increase in security-related spending.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly tasks the Executive Director of this office with establishing the Maryland Entertainment District Security Grant Program, including the application process, eligibility criteria, and administering the associated special fund.
The bill mandates that the State Treasurer hold the Maryland Entertainment District Security Grant Program Fund separately and invest the money of the fund.
The bill requires the Comptroller to account for the Maryland Entertainment District Security Grant Program Fund.
The Governor's Office oversees the administering agency (GOCPP), and the bill authorizes the Governor to annually appropriate up to $1,000,000 to the program's fund starting in fiscal year 2026.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for the development and execution of the state budget, including the new annual appropriations and the fiscal oversight of the special fund established by the bill.

The fiscal note identifies that this department currently administers the Community Health and Safety Works Program, which has similar objectives regarding security improvements in business districts.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies like GOCPP and would likely be involved in drafting the regulatory criteria and application legalities for the new grant program.

The Grants Office identifies and manages grant funding and provides technical assistance to agencies and non-profits, which is highly relevant to the administration and procurement of this new grant program.

The program is aimed at districts with a high volume of calls for police assistance; as the state's primary law enforcement agency, MSP is involved in the broader public safety context and data reporting that informs such programs.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Comptroller to implement procedures that provide residents filing their individual income tax returns electronically the opportunity to register as organ donors via a hyperlink to the state's anatomical gift donor registry.
qa_results.responsible_party: Comptroller of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland resident individual income tax filers who file electronically and the state's designated organ procurement organization.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Comptroller’s Office can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. There is no impact on State or local revenues, and no impact on small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Comptroller to implement procedures for income tax filers to register as organ donors and mandates that the Comptroller transmit registration data to the state's organ procurement organization.
The bill specifically names the Motor Vehicle Administration as an agency that must cooperate with the Comptroller to transfer relevant donor information to the statewide registry.
The Department of Health is responsible for contracting with the nonprofit entity that maintains the organ donor registry, which will receive new data from tax filers as a result of this bill.
The Governor signed the bill into law and oversees the executive agencies (Comptroller and MVA) responsible for its implementation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Workgroup for the Deaf, Deafblind, and Hard of Hearing to study and make recommendations regarding services, programs, advocacy, and outreach efforts to improve the quality of life for individuals who are deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing. The workgroup, staffed by the Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing, will evaluate communication barriers and civic engagement, submitting its final report by June 1, 2025.
qa_results.responsible_party: Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing (ODHH)
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals who are deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing; nonprofit organizations; and State and local government agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $67,123 in fiscal 2025 for the Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing to staff and support the workgroup, including costs for a graduate assistant, consultant services, and interpretation services. The fiscal 2025 budget includes $10,000 for interpretation services contingent on the bill's enactment. No material fiscal impact on local governments is expected.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: ODHH is the primary agency tasked with staffing the new Workgroup and its Director is a designated member. The agency is also responsible for managing the $67,100 general fund expenditure identified in the fiscal note for interpretation and consulting services.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill establishes the Workgroup for the Deaf, Deafblind, and Hard of Hearing as a temporary body (terminating in 2026) to study and make recommendations on specific government services and policies.
The Governor is responsible for appointing ten community representatives to the Workgroup and receives the Workgroup's final report on findings and recommendations.
The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting and vetting candidates for the ten community representative positions established by this bill.
DBM is responsible for state budget oversight and was identified in the fiscal note as a source of information regarding the bill's general fund impact.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies and makes permanent the reporting requirements for health insurance carriers to demonstrate their compliance with the federal Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act. It requires carriers to submit biennial reports beginning July 1, 2024, focusing on nonquantitative treatment limitations (NQTLs), and establishes that the burden of persuasion lies with the carrier to prove compliance. The bill also enhances the Maryland Insurance Commissioner's enforcement authority, allowing for daily penalties and administrative orders to cease noncompliant practices.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Health insurance carriers (insurers, health maintenance organizations, and nonprofit health service plans) and individuals covered under health benefit plans for mental health and substance use disorders.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's fiscal impact is primarily on the Maryland Insurance Administration (MIA) special funds. Revenues are expected to increase by an indeterminate amount starting in FY 2025 and every two years thereafter due to penalties (at least $1,000 per day for failure to submit information) and charges for reviewing additional reports. MIA special fund expenditures for contractual services are projected to decrease in FY 2024 and 2025 due to a streamlined review process but will increase every two years thereafter because the bill repeals the previous 2026 sunset date, making the reporting requirement permanent. Contractor review costs are approximately $155 per hour.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Insurance Administration is the primary agency responsible for the bill's implementation. The Insurance Commissioner is specifically tasked with setting reporting requirements for carriers, developing data templates, reviewing comparative analyses of mental health parity, and enforcing compliance through administrative orders and penalties.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Maryland Insurance Administration. It would represent the Commissioner in administrative and judicial proceedings resulting from the enforcement of mental health parity laws and the assessment of penalties against carriers.

The bill concerns insurance benefits for mental health and substance use disorders, which are critical components of the Department of Health's mission to ensure equitable access to care. MDH was also consulted in the development of the fiscal note.

As the agency responsible for the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program, DBM monitors changes to health insurance regulations and benefit mandates that could affect the state's health plan and personnel costs.

OAH adjudicates contested cases for state agencies. If an insurance carrier appeals a penalty or administrative order issued by the Insurance Commissioner under this bill, the case would be heard by an Administrative Law Judge at OAH.

MHBE administers the state's health insurance marketplace. Ensuring that plans offered on the marketplace comply with mental health parity laws is essential to the Exchange's goal of providing accessible and affordable health coverage.

MHCC is responsible for health system planning and improving access to care. The reporting and enforcement of mental health parity are directly related to the commission's efforts to increase accountability and transparency in the healthcare system.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates major policy initiatives, including those related to behavioral health and insurance regulation. The Governor signed this legislation as an emergency measure.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires Maryland county boards of education to identify and coordinate the provision of instructional materials in specialized formats (such as braille, large print, audio, or digital text) for blind and visually impaired students by the first day of the school year. Local boards must determine which materials are needed by March 15 annually and coordinate with the Instructional Resources Center. It also mandates that textbook publishers provide electronic files of pupil edition textbooks to the National Instructional Materials Access Center and establishes annual reporting requirements for the Maryland State Department of Education regarding the delivery and equity of these materials.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE), local boards of education, and the Instructional Resources Center (IRC)
qa_results.stakeholders: Blind and visually impaired students, local school systems, textbook publishers, and families of students with visual impairments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $86,400 in fiscal 2025, which reflects the cost for the Maryland State Department of Education to hire one education program specialist to oversee data collection and reporting. Expenditures are projected to increase to $117,900 by fiscal 2029. Local school board costs may increase if they are unable to fulfill material requirements through the Instructional Resources Center and must enter into alternative remediation plans with student families to acquire necessary materials.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Education is the primary implementing agency. It oversees the Instructional Resources Center (IRC), which must coordinate the delivery of specialized instructional materials for blind and visually impaired students. The bill requires the Department to provide support to the IRC, hire an education program specialist to manage compliance and data collection, and submit annual reports to the Governor and General Assembly.

The Governor's Office is responsible for receiving annual reports from the Department of Education regarding textbook equity for blind students. Additionally, the bill mandates that the Governor include a specific $150,000 appropriation for the Instructional Resources Center in the annual budget submission.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, the Department of Budget and Management is impacted by the bill's fiscal note, which identifies a need for a new education program specialist position at the Department of Education and an increase in general fund expenditures to manage the bill's reporting and coordination requirements.

The bill's focus on accessibility standards and specialized formats (such as Braille and large print) for students with visual impairments aligns with the Department of Disabilities' mission to coordinate and improve the delivery of services to residents with disabilities across all state agencies.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill requires the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid) to cover remote ultrasound procedures and remote fetal nonstress tests for eligible pregnant women. These remote services must utilize FDA-approved digital technology for secure data transmission and adhere to the same standard of care as on-site procedures. Additionally, Medicaid must reimburse remote fetal nonstress tests at the same rate as on-site tests.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Pregnant women with family incomes at or below 250% of the federal poverty level who are enrolled in the Maryland Medical Assistance Program
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, Maryland Department of Health expenditures will increase by a minimal amount starting in fiscal 2025, with costs shared between general funds (40.8%) and federal funds (59.2%). This increase is due to potential improvements in service utilization and fewer missed appointments. There is no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the Department of Health and coordinates policy initiatives related to healthcare access and maternal health outcomes.
The Comptroller is responsible for paying state bills and serves on the Board of Public Works, which oversees significant state expenditures, including those for the Medical Assistance Program.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and ensures that implementation of remote medical procedures complies with federal laws such as HIPAA and state health regulations.
DBM is responsible for state budget development and execution. The fiscal note indicates an increase in Medicaid expenditures (general and federal funds) to support the new remote services.

The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency responsible for administering the Maryland Medical Assistance Program (Medicaid). The bill explicitly requires the department to cover remote ultrasound and fetal nonstress tests and to issue guidance for program providers.

As an independent regulatory agency within the Department of Health focused on improving access and planning for health system needs, the Commission may be involved in tracking the effectiveness and quality of these new remote healthcare services.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill regulates the collection, storage, and use of data from automatic license plate reader (LPR) systems. It designates captured data as the property of law enforcement agencies, prohibits the sale of this data by agencies or vendors, and restricts unauthorized access and data transfers. Additionally, it requires the Maryland Department of State Police and other law enforcement agencies to adopt security procedures, including data breach reporting, and extends criminal penalties for data misuse to contracted vendors.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of State Police
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland law enforcement agencies, third-party LPR system vendors, and the general public.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government finances, as indicated by the Department of Legislative Services fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly names the Department of State Police as one of the primary entities responsible for adopting procedures related to the operation, use, and auditing of automatic license plate reader (LPR) systems and reporting data breaches.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the agency responsible for the supervision of justice-involved individuals and criminal records, DPSCS is relevant to the bill's new requirements for managing 'criminal justice information' and coordinating with law enforcement databases.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office serves as the primary coordinating body for public safety and criminal justice strategies. It oversees policy development for law enforcement tools and manages funds that may support LPR infrastructure.

The MDTA operates a nationally accredited police force that utilizes LPR systems for law enforcement. The bill's requirements for data ownership, vendor restrictions, and usage procedures apply to its police operations.

MDOT is the parent department for the MDTA and MVA. The bill impacts how modal administrations within MDOT handle vehicle-related data and law enforcement activities involving license plate identification.

The bill updates definitions to include cloud computing storage and mandates reporting procedures for data breaches and unauthorized releases, which aligns with DoIT's role in overseeing state cybersecurity and data governance.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the State Police and other state law enforcement agencies regarding the implementation of new privacy regulations and the enforcement of criminal penalties for data misuse.

The MVA is responsible for vehicle registration and regulating vehicle laws. The bill defines the 'legitimate law enforcement purpose' for ALPR use as including the investigation of Maryland vehicle laws, which falls under MVA's scope.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires each county board of education to provide $150,000 for the salary and benefits of a Blueprint for Maryland’s Future implementation coordinator. The funding for these positions is shared between the State and the county in the same proportion as the foundation program. Additionally, the bill extends the requirement for counties to have an implementation coordinator indefinitely, whereas it was previously scheduled to expire after fiscal year 2026.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland county boards of education, the Accountability and Implementation Board, and local school systems
qa_results.stakeholders: Local school systems, county governments, and Blueprint implementation coordinators
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State expenditures increase by approximately 1,837,333 annually beginning in fiscal 2025. Local school systems receive these funds as additional state aid but are responsible for a total statewide expenditure of 3,600,000 annually (150,000 per jurisdiction) to cover coordinator salaries and benefits. Because the coordinator position was already required through fiscal 2026, local board expenditures are noted to increase by the full 3,600,000 starting in fiscal 2027 as the requirement is made permanent.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency overseeing the state's public education system and 24 local school systems, MSDE is directly involved in the Blueprint's implementation. This bill mandates that local boards of education provide funding and joint appointments for Blueprint implementation coordinators.

DBM is responsible for state budget development and fiscal planning. This bill creates a mandated State appropriation starting in FY 2026 to cover the State's share of the salary and benefits for implementation coordinators.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Accountability and Implementation Board (AIB), which is the primary entity overseeing the Blueprint for Maryland's Future, is identified in the fiscal note as the body that will administer the salary grants for implementation coordinators.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives like the Blueprint for Maryland's Future. The Governor also proposes the annual budget which will now include mandated funding for implementation coordinators.

GOPI utilizes data-driven strategies to oversee the implementation of the State Comprehensive Plan and align agency activities with the Governor's priorities, which includes the successful rollout and coordination of the Blueprint.

The OLA is responsible for conducting fiscal compliance audits of state agencies and local school systems; it will be tasked with ensuring that the mandated funds for implementation coordinator salaries are correctly allocated and expended.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Governor, MSDE, and other state entities regarding the interpretation and implementation of the Education Article and the statutory requirements of the Blueprint.

The Comptroller functions as the state's chief financial officer and is responsible for the distribution of state revenues and payment of state obligations, including education aid formulas affected by this bill.

The Treasurer's Office manages and distributes state funds and anticipates monetary needs; it oversees the protection of the funds that will be allocated for the state's share of the coordinator salaries.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the requirements for Class 4 limited winery licenses in Maryland by changing the cultivation standards from 'grapes or other fruit' to 'Maryland-grown agricultural products' (excluding cannabis and THC products). It also requires that any finished wine purchased by a license holder be made entirely of Maryland-grown agricultural products fermented by a licensed manufacturer.
qa_results.responsible_party: Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission and the Maryland Department of Agriculture
qa_results.stakeholders: Class 4 limited winery license holders, Maryland farmers and vineyard owners, and the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Cannabis Commission
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local revenues or expenditures. However, it is expected to have a meaningful impact on small businesses, as the updated requirements for Maryland-grown agricultural products may affect the ability of some wineries to obtain or renew their licenses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly tasks the Secretary of Agriculture with granting annual exemptions from Maryland-grown product percentage requirements and adopting regulations for those exemptions, making them a primary implementing agency for these agricultural standards.

The Comptroller is the state's principal tax collector and regulator of the alcohol industry. The bill requires license holders to maintain records and file reports that the Comptroller requires for winemaking and packaging activities.

The bill specifies that its provisions do not limit the application of Title 21 of the Health - General Article and associated regulations, which the Maryland Department of Health oversees regarding food safety and service allowed at limited wineries.

The bill provides a specific definition of 'Maryland-grown agricultural product' that explicitly excludes cannabis and products containing THC, directly referencing statutes administered by the Cannabis Administration to define these exclusions.

The Department of Commerce is responsible for stimulating the economy and promoting Maryland's assets. This bill impacts the economic viability of agribusiness and the winery industry, which are components of the state's commerce and tourism sectors.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and policy initiatives. The Governor signed this bill into law, and the agency coordinates the cabinet-level departments (Agriculture, Health, Commerce) impacted by this legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the compensation and benefits framework for individuals erroneously convicted, sentenced, and confined under State law. It expands eligibility for compensation to individuals convicted of conspiracy to commit a felony and cases where a conviction is reversed or vacated without the possibility of a retrial. The bill also extends the duration of higher education benefits from five to eight years, adjusts the eligibility period for housing and healthcare benefits, and requires the State to provide specific written notice before attempting to reduce or prevent a compensation award due to concurrent sentences for other crimes.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of Public Works and the Office of Administrative Hearings
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals erroneously convicted of felonies or conspiracy to commit a felony, their estates, the Office of Administrative Hearings, the Board of Public Works, and Maryland public higher education institutions.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures are expected to increase as the bill expands eligibility for compensation to individuals erroneously convicted of conspiracy to commit a felony. While the Office of the Attorney General identifies two individuals who may retroactively qualify, the total fiscal impact is indeterminate because it depends on the number of successful petitions and the duration of wrongful confinement for each claimant. Other benefit modifications are not expected to have a material financial impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Board of Public Works is the primary agency responsible for paying compensation to erroneously convicted individuals and is required to report annually to the General Assembly on awarded compensation and services.

The Office of Administrative Hearings is explicitly designated to receive petitions, conduct hearings through administrative law judges, and issue formal orders of eligibility for compensation and benefits.
The Office of the Attorney General serves as the mandatory legal representative for the State in all compensation proceedings and originally requested this legislation.
The Governor's issuance of a full pardon remains a primary statutory path for an individual to establish conclusive eligibility for compensation.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller oversees the fiscal integrity and approval of the multi-year compensation payments mandated by the bill.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Treasurer is involved in the management and distribution of state funds for these compensation awards.
The bill mandates that administrative law judges may direct the department to provide free health and dental care to eligible individuals for a period of five years.
The department is responsible for providing housing accommodations to eligible individuals for up to five years as part of the mandated benefit package.
The department is tasked with providing vocational training, job training, and financial literacy education to individuals erroneously convicted.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The department manages confinement records and concurrent sentence data which the State uses to determine valid compensation amounts and verify eligibility timelines.
The MVA is responsible for providing State identification cards and other essential documents to individuals upon their release as part of the court-ordered benefits.
The commission coordinates state policies for public senior higher education institutions which are required to provide tuition-free enrollment for up to eight years under the bill.
As a public senior higher education institution provider, the system must provide access and tuition/fee waivers for eligible individuals for the expanded duration of eight years.
As a public senior higher education institution, Morgan State is required to facilitate the expanded tuition and fee benefits for eligible erroneously convicted individuals.
The judiciary provides the official records regarding reversed or vacated convictions that serve as the evidentiary basis for compensation petitions.
The department is responsible for managing the general fund impact resulting from the bill's expanded eligibility and altered benefit durations.
As the parent agency of the MVA, it oversees the administration of providing identification documents to individuals awarded benefits.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Baltimore City Board of License Commissioners to issue Class B beer, wine, and liquor licenses to restaurants located in Ward 4, precincts 1 and 2, provided they meet specific requirements. Qualifying restaurants must derive at least 51% of daily receipts from food sales, be classified as high or moderate priority food service facilities, and maintain a memorandum of understanding with the Downtown Partnership of Baltimore. The bill prohibits off-premises sales (except for refillable container permits) and limits the issuance of new licenses under these provisions to the period ending June 30, 2028.
qa_results.responsible_party: Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City
qa_results.stakeholders: Restaurant owners in Ward 4 (precincts 1 and 2) of Baltimore City, the Baltimore City government, and the Downtown Partnership of Baltimore.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, Baltimore City revenues may increase minimally beginning in FY 2025. For each license issued, the city will collect an initial application fee of $600 plus an annual license fee of either $1,320 (for seating capacity up to 200) or $1,800 (for capacity over 200). In the first year, total revenue per license is estimated to be between $1,920 and $2,400. Subsequent annual revenue for renewals is estimated between $1,370 and $1,850 per license, including a $50 renewal fee.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the state's principal tax collector and regulates the alcohol industry. Since the bill authorizes the issuance of new Class B beer, wine, and liquor licenses and establishes revenue-related requirements (such as food sales percentages), it falls under the Comptroller's broad regulatory and tax enforcement mandate for alcoholic beverages.

The bill requires that restaurants seeking this license be classified as high or moderate priority food service facilities by the Baltimore City Health Department. The Maryland Department of Health oversees local health departments, making the state agency relevant to the health and safety classifications utilized as criteria in the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the chief legal officer for the state, providing counsel to all state agencies and boards, including the Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City. The OAG would be involved in interpreting the legal requirements and ensuring compliance with the state's Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and the enforcement of state laws. The Governor signed this specific bill into law, and the office is responsible for coordinating policy initiatives related to economic development and community management, such as those in the Downtown Management District.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the purposes of the 9-1-1 Trust Fund to include funding for telecommunications cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) training for public safety answering point (PSAP) personnel. It also specifies that training standards for PSAP personnel must include instruction in high-quality telecommunications CPR following current national guidelines.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Emergency Management (MDEM) and the Maryland 9-1-1 Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Public safety answering point (PSAP) personnel, 9-1-1 specialists, and local government agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of Emergency Management (MDEM) indicates that the bill is clarifying in nature and does not affect State special fund expenditures because CPR training is already an allowable expense from the 9-1-1 Trust Fund. Local government expenditures for this training are reimbursable by the fund, resulting in no material impact on local finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MDEM oversees the Maryland 9-1-1 Board, which is responsible for administering the 9-1-1 Trust Fund and establishing training standards for public safety answering point (PSAP) personnel. The bill directly impacts the authorized uses of the 9-1-1 Trust Fund for telecommunications CPR training.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS oversees and coordinates the state's emergency medical services network. As the agency responsible for the EMS system and prehospital care standards, it has a significant interest in the quality of telecommunications CPR instruction provided by 9-1-1 dispatchers to callers during cardiac arrests.

The Comptroller is responsible for collecting the State and county 9-1-1 fees from service providers and depositing them into the 9-1-1 Trust Fund, which is the funding mechanism addressed by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Anne Arundel County Human Relations Commission to seek enforcement of its subpoenas by applying to a circuit court for an order requiring the attendance and testimony of witnesses or the production of documents and other property. If the court issues an order, failure to obey it may be punished as contempt of court.
qa_results.responsible_party: Anne Arundel County Human Relations Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Residents and entities in Anne Arundel County involved in discrimination investigations, witnesses, and the judicial system.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill's fiscal note states that the legislation does not materially affect State or local government operations or finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill authorizes the Anne Arundel County Human Relations Commission to apply to a circuit court for orders to compel testimony and document production. As the central support agency for the Maryland Judicial Branch, the Administrative Office of the Courts is responsible for supporting the circuit courts that will handle these applications and any subsequent contempt proceedings.

The Maryland Commission on Civil Rights is the state agency responsible for enforcing anti-discrimination laws. While the bill specifically empowers a local county commission, the expansion of enforcement mechanisms for human relations and housing discrimination cases is directly relevant to the Commission's mission and the state's civil rights framework.

The Governor's Office is responsible for overseeing the executive branch and signing state legislation. The Governor approved and signed this bill, which amends the Local Government Article of the Annotated Code of Maryland.

The Office of the Attorney General serves as the primary legal representative for the state and provides legal counsel to the General Assembly. They review legislation for legal and constitutional sufficiency, including the granting of subpoena enforcement powers to local entities via the state's Local Government Article.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill defines the roles and scope of practice for veterinary technicians and veterinary assistants in Maryland. It authorizes veterinary assistants to perform specific non-invasive tasks under direct or immediate supervision and allows veterinary technicians to perform more complex medical procedures, including anesthesia induction, simple dental extractions, and euthanasia, under varying levels of supervision (direct, indirect, or immediate) by a licensed veterinarian. The bill also establishes specific definitions for different levels of supervision and qualifications for veterinary technicians.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed veterinarians, veterinary technicians, veterinary assistants, animal treatment facilities, animal shelters, and pet owners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Special fund revenues for the State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners are estimated to increase by $2,700 annually from FY 2025 through FY 2027, and by $5,400 annually thereafter. This increase is driven by an anticipated rise in the number of registered veterinary technicians paying application and triennial registration fees due to the expanded and clarified scope of practice. There is no projected impact on State expenditures or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners (SBVME) is a regulatory body within the Department of Agriculture. The bill directly modifies the definitions, duties, and supervisory requirements for veterinary technicians and assistants regulated by the SBVME, and the fiscal note indicates a revenue increase for the Department's special funds.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions, including the State Board of Veterinary Medical Examiners. The bill requires the Board to develop regulations for alternative pathways for veterinary technicians and define noninvasive skills, which will require legal review and guidance.

The bill authorizes veterinary technicians to access and fill prescriptions for Schedule II drugs. The Department of Health's Office of Controlled Substances Administration (OCSA) is responsible for enforcing the state's Controlled Dangerous Substance Act and regulating the prescribing and dispensing of drugs.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments, including the Department of Agriculture, and the Governor signed this bill into law.
DBM is responsible for supervising budget execution and estimating revenues. The bill has a notable fiscal impact on special fund revenues derived from veterinary technician registration fees.

The OAH adjudicates contested administrative cases for state agencies. Changes to the scope of practice and registration requirements for veterinary professionals may lead to disputes or disciplinary hearings heard by OAH judges.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Clean Water Justice Act of 2024 grants legal standing to persons and associations who meet federal threshold requirements to bring civil actions regarding violations of state standards for nontidal wetlands, ephemeral streams, and intermittent streams. The bill establishes procedures for filing suits, including notice requirements, and authorizes courts to order enforcement, grant equitable relief, and award litigation costs and civil penalties.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment
qa_results.stakeholders: Regulated businesses, local governments, environmental advocacy organizations, and individuals affected by water pollution or wetland degradation.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund expenditures for the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) increase by $86,400 in FY 2025 to hire an assistant Attorney General to manage legal actions and notices, with costs rising to $117,900 by FY 2029. Special fund revenues may increase through civil penalties collected from lawsuits. Local governments may face higher litigation and compliance costs if they are defendants in civil actions brought under the bill.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the 'Clean Water Justice Act' within the Environment Article, specifically targeting standards related to nontidal wetlands, ephemeral streams, and intermittent streams regulated by MDE. The agency is the primary recipient of mandatory legal notices and is responsible for enforcing the standards cited in the bill.

The OAG is a required recipient of 60-day notices and proposed consent judgments for all civil actions brought under this act. Additionally, the OAG provides legal counsel to MDE and will manage the state's involvement or intervention in these environmental proceedings.

The bill explicitly expands standing for civil claims arising under several subtitles of the Natural Resources Article, including those related to the Chesapeake Bay Critical Area Protection Program and forest conservation, which are managed by DNR.

The bill creates new civil causes of action in circuit courts, authorizes the awarding of litigation costs, and empowers courts to grant equitable relief and impose civil penalties. The AOC provides operational and legal support to the courts implementing these new procedures.

As a principal agency involved in large-scale infrastructure construction (State Highway Administration), MDOT is a potential defendant in civil actions regarding compliance with standards for nontidal wetlands and stream protection during project development.

The bill's definition of political subdivisions includes soil conservation districts, which are overseen by the MDA. Additionally, the MDA's mission includes protecting environmental resources and soil conservation, which are directly related to the water quality standards addressed by the bill.
The fiscal note identifies a need for increased general fund expenditures for personnel (an Assistant Attorney General) to handle the legal workload generated by this bill, a process managed by DBM.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies (MDE, DNR, MDA) impacted by the bill and is responsible for the annual state budget, which must accommodate the fiscal impacts noted by the Department of Legislative Services.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Miriam Kelty Aging and Senior Social Connection Hub and Spoke Pilot Program within the Maryland Department of Aging to support volunteer-led organizations known as 'villages' that help seniors age in place. The program aims to develop and test a 'hub and spoke' administrative model to centralize tasks, coordinate resources, and combat social isolation among seniors. The Montgomery County Village Consortium is designated to implement the three-year program and must provide strategic plans and progress reports.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Aging
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland seniors aging in place, local volunteer-led 'village' organizations, and the Montgomery County Village Consortium.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Maryland Department of Aging general fund expenditures increase by $80,000 annually for fiscal years 2026, 2027, and 2028 to satisfy a mandated appropriation for the pilot program. The total estimated state fiscal impact is $240,000. There is no direct impact on local government finances or operations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Miriam Kelty Aging and Senior Social Connection Hub and Spoke Pilot Program within the Department of Aging, designating it as the primary agency responsible for administering the program, selecting the implementation consortium, and overseeing reporting.

As the principal fiscal agency for the state, DBM is responsible for budget development and execution. The bill creates a mandated appropriation of $80,000 annually for fiscal years 2026 through 2028, requiring DBM's involvement in the fiscal oversight and budget inclusion process.
The bill mandates that the Governor include a specific appropriation of $80,000 in the annual budget bill for the pilot program for three consecutive fiscal years.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for state agencies and would provide legal counsel to the Department of Aging regarding the implementation of the new pilot program, contractual agreements with the Montgomery County Village Consortium, and the processing of annual and final reports.

As the state's chief financial officer responsible for paying state bills and distributing funds, the Comptroller's office will oversee the actual transfer of the mandated $80,000 appropriation from the Department to the Montgomery County Village Consortium.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Service and Civic Innovation
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The pilot program focuses on 'villages,' which are defined as volunteer-led organizations using a 'neighbor-helping-neighbor' model to support community members. This volunteer-centric approach aligns with the agency's mission to promote service, volunteerism, and civic engagement.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires an individual to be certified as an advanced practice registered nurse (APRN) before practicing advanced practice registered nursing in Maryland, with specific exceptions for students in graduate-level accredited programs and individuals permitted to practice under Maryland Board of Nursing regulations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Board of Nursing
qa_results.stakeholders: Advanced Practice Registered Nurses (APRNs), graduate-level nursing students, and the Maryland Board of Nursing
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The fiscal note indicates that the bill has no state or local fiscal impact, as the changes are technical in nature and do not directly affect governmental finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Board of Nursing (MBON), which is an unit within the Department of Health, is the primary body responsible for the certification and regulation of Advanced Practice Registered Nurses (APRNs). The bill directly modifies certification requirements and exceptions administered by this board.

The OAG serves as the chief legal counsel to state agencies and boards, including the Maryland Board of Nursing. They would be responsible for providing legal advice on the implementation and enforcement of the new certification requirements.

The bill includes specific exceptions for students enrolled in graduate-level accredited APRN programs. The commission oversees policies for these public and private colleges and universities offering such programs.

USM institutions (such as the University of Maryland, Baltimore) host graduate-level nursing programs for APRNs. The bill's certification exceptions specifically apply to students in these programs during their training.

Morgan State University provides various academic programs, including potential nursing pathways. As a state agency providing higher education, it is relevant to the bill's exceptions for graduate nursing students.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Health. The bill was signed by the Governor, who also has the authority to declare States of Emergency mentioned in the bill's nursing practice exceptions for disaster situations.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill adds the City of Frederick as a participating jurisdiction in the Maryland Intrastate Emergency Management Assistance Compact (MIEMAC). MIEMAC is a statewide program that facilitates mutual aid, cooperation, and resource sharing between member jurisdictions during emergencies and for training exercises.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Emergency Management
qa_results.stakeholders: City of Frederick, emergency responders, and current member jurisdictions of the Maryland Intrastate Emergency Management Assistance Compact.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill does not materially affect State operations or finances. While the City of Frederick and other member jurisdictions may benefit from the inclusion of an additional compact member for mutual aid, the bill is not anticipated to significantly affect local government finances according to the fiscal note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Emergency Management (MDEM) is the primary state agency responsible for coordinating the Maryland Intrastate Emergency Management Assistance Compact (MIEMAC). The bill explicitly requires MDEM to consult with participating jurisdictions and receive notifications of assistance requests.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS oversees the state's emergency medical services (EMS) network. Since EMS is a core functional area for mutual aid under the compact, Frederick's participation involves MIEMSS in the broader coordination and oversight of these services.

Law enforcement is a core functional area of the MIEMAC. As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police provides specialized support and coordinates with the local jurisdictions that are members of this compact.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, including the Department of Emergency Management. The Governor also approves the participation of jurisdictions in state compacts and coordinates related policy initiatives.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to the Department of Emergency Management and the state regarding the legal frameworks of intrastate compacts and liability provisions.
The Department of Budget and Management is the principal fiscal agency and reviewed the bill for its fiscal impact on state operations and finances, as noted in the fiscal note.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Transparency in Education Spending Act requires the State Board of Education to establish a uniform process for county boards of education to report budget information to local governing bodies. It mandates that county board budgets include specific details on reserve funds, federal funds, and long-term retiree health care obligations. Additionally, boards must post their proposed and approved budgets on their websites and provide annual audit results directly to their respective county councils or commissioners.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Education and County Boards of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland public school systems, county governing bodies, and the general public.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, there is no State fiscal impact as the Maryland State Department of Education can implement the uniform reporting process with existing resources. Local school systems may see a minimal increase in administrative costs to meet new reporting and website disclosure requirements, though specific dollar estimates are not provided.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the State Board of Education (which is part of MSDE) to adopt a uniform reporting process for county school board budgets. It also requires the State Superintendent to approve auditors for annual school system audits and receive approved budget copies.

The bill amends Section 5-109 of the Education Article regarding the annual audit of financial transactions for county boards of education. OLA is the state agency responsible for conducting fiscal and performance audits of local school systems.

The bill renumbers and amends budget reporting requirements for the 'School Construction Fund' (Part II of the budget), which the Commission oversees to coordinate planning and funding for capital projects across the state's school systems.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires a licensed restaurant to obtain a trader's license if the restaurant generates more than 10% of its annual sales from nonfood items, narrowing an existing broad exemption from trader's licensing for all licensed restaurants.
qa_results.responsible_party: Clerks of the Circuit Court
qa_results.stakeholders: Owners of licensed restaurants in Maryland that sell nonfood items or merchandise, and small business owners in the food service sector.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not anticipated to materially affect State finances or operations. Local government revenues may see a minimal increase from the issuance of additional trader's licenses, with 92% of fees retained by the counties and 8% by the State general fund. The circuit courts are expected to manage the administrative workload of increased licensing with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note indicates circuit courts (administered by the Administrative Office of the Courts) handle the licensing activity, specifically the issuance of trader's licenses through the Clerks of the Circuit Court.
The Comptroller's office oversees business license regulations and is responsible for collecting and distributing the state's share of revenue from trader's licenses.
The bill directly amends the Business Regulation Article, which is the primary statutory area overseen by the Department of Labor for many categories of business licensing and industry oversight.
The bill affects the regulatory requirements and costs for restaurants, which impacts the economic landscape and small business environment managed by the Department of Commerce.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The fiscal note highlights a potential meaningful impact on small businesses, as restaurants may be required to obtain additional licenses and manage the associated costs.
The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state agencies on regulatory changes and was a consulted entity regarding the implications of the revised licensing requirements.
The Department of Budget and Management oversees revenue estimation and the budget, which includes the split of licensing fee revenue between the state General Fund and local jurisdictions.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Tenant Safety Act of 2024 establishes that landlords who offer residential dwellings for rent are deemed to warrant that the property is fit for human habitation. The bill expands tenant remedies for serious and dangerous defects, including mold, by allowing multiple tenants to join as plaintiffs in a single civil action against a landlord. It establishes rebuttable presumptions regarding rent abatement and the conditions for rent escrow, and authorizes the court to award attorney's fees, litigation costs, and expenses (such as mold assessments) to prevailing tenants, while allowing landlords to recover fees if a complaint is filed in bad faith.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Judiciary
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential tenants, landlords, and property management companies in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not anticipated to materially affect State or local government operations or finances. However, it is expected to have a meaningful impact on small business landlords and property management companies, who may face more complex litigation, awards of attorney's fees, and additional litigation-related expenses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly amends legal procedures for landlord-tenant disputes, including authorizing the joinder of multiple plaintiffs, establishing rebuttable presumptions for rent abatement and escrow, and requiring specific findings of fact for relief. These changes directly impact the adjudication and administrative operations of the Maryland Judiciary.

As the state's lead agency for ensuring safe and secure housing, this bill's establishment of a warranty of habitability and enhanced tenant remedies for serious defects directly aligns with the department's mission to manage and support community living standards.

The OAG's Consumer Protection Division frequently handles landlord-tenant issues and provides legal counsel regarding state statutes and consumer rights; the bill clarifies the legal framework for 'fit for human habitation' and attorney's fee awards which the OAG may enforce or advise upon.

DHS serves residents in economic need and those facing homelessness. Enhancing tenant protections and legal remedies for unsafe housing conditions directly affects the stability of the vulnerable populations the agency serves through its local departments.

The bill addresses serious health-related defects such as mold, sewage disposal, and rodent infestations. The department and local health units may be involved in identifying these conditions or providing the 'actual notice' from a government agency required for tenant relief.

The bill explicitly includes mold hazards as conditions threatening the health of occupants. The department is responsible for environmental protection and restoration, including managing environmental health risks like indoor air quality issues.

The department advocates for the rights and safety of older adults. This bill provides a legal mechanism for seniors—who may be more susceptible to health risks from unsafe housing—to secure repairs for dangerous defects.

The department focuses on accessible and integrated community living. Improving habitability standards and remedies for dangerous defects supports the agency's mission to ensure safe housing for residents with disabilities.

The office leads efforts to reduce child poverty and improve access to services. Safe housing (habitability) is a critical factor in child well-being and health, making the bill relevant to the office's advocacy for comprehensive support networks.

As the bill applies to residential units that are 'publicly or privately owned,' state-operated residential facilities such as university-managed housing may be subject to the habitability standards and legal actions established by this legislation.

The university provides on-campus residential housing and is a state agency. It would be subject to the bill's provisions regarding the warranty of habitability for publicly owned residential dwelling units.
The college operates residential housing for students. As a public institution, its housing units fall under the bill's expanded habitability requirements and tenant remedies.

The team focuses on addressing child poverty and enhancing economic mobility. Safe housing conditions and the prevention of displacement are foundational components of economic stability for the families the team targets.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the performing arts theater alcoholic beverages license in Harford County by allowing for-profit organizations to obtain the license, which was previously restricted to nonprofits. It expands the scope of the license to include the sale of liquor for on-premises consumption alongside beer and wine, removes the showing of movies from the definition of qualifying activities, and increases the annual license fee from $500 to $1,500.
qa_results.responsible_party: Harford County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: For-profit performing arts theaters in Harford County and theater patrons.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no state fiscal impact. Local revenues for Harford County are estimated to increase by approximately $1,000 annually starting in fiscal 2025, as the bill increases the license fee from $500 to $1,500 and the county expects to issue one such license. Administrative monitoring can be handled with existing resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller regulates the state's alcohol industry and collects alcoholic beverage taxes. This bill adds liquor to performing arts theater licenses and changes eligibility to include for-profit entities, directly affecting the regulatory and tax framework overseen by this agency.

The Department of Commerce promotes the arts and economic development. By expanding the licensing and revenue potential for performing arts theaters, this bill supports the economic health of cultural venues, which is a core part of the agency's mission.

The Maryland Office of Tourism Development markets the state's cultural and recreational assets. Enhancing the amenities available at performing arts theaters through expanded beverage service can increase their appeal as tourism destinations.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch, signs state laws, and handles the appointment of officials to the local boards of license commissioners that administer these licenses.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions. Since local boards of license commissioners are state entities, the OAG may be involved in advising the Harford County board on the interpretation and implementation of these new licensing criteria.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires individuals or entities to submit a formal request to the Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC) and provide notice to residents and staff before acquiring a nursing home. It establishes a review and approval process for acquisitions based on the State Health Plan and public interest, mandates annual reporting for three years following an acquisition, and requires regulations to reduce the number of resident rooms containing more than two beds.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Health Care Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Nursing home owners and prospective purchasers, nursing home residents and their representatives, nursing home employees, and the Maryland Department of Health.
