qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund expenditures for the Maryland Health Care Commission increase by $107,100 in FY 2025 to hire a program manager to oversee the new acquisition review process. Costs are projected to increase annually, reaching $148,100 by FY 2029. The bill has no impact on State revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC) is the primary agency responsible for implementing this bill. It is tasked with reviewing nursing home acquisition requests, adopting regulations for the process, determining if acquisitions are in the public interest, and managing post-acquisition reporting.

The Secretary of Health must be consulted on acquisition approvals. Additionally, the Office of Health Care Quality (a division of MDH) is responsible for nursing home licensure and must consider the MHCC's final decisions before granting or revoking licenses.

The Attorney General is specifically required to be consulted by the MHCC to determine if a nursing home acquisition raises public interest concerns. The OAG also receives final decisions and annual reports from the Commission.

The Secretary of Aging and the State Long-Term Care Ombudsman (housed within the Department of Aging) are required recipients of the Commission's final decisions regarding acquisitions and subsequent annual reports.

The bill provides for judicial review and allows parties to a nursing home acquisition to take a direct judicial appeal of the Maryland Health Care Commission's final decisions, involving the court system in the oversight process.

The bill has a notable fiscal impact on the MHCC, requiring the addition of staff (program manager) to handle the new acquisition review process. DBM oversees the state's budget and personnel management associated with these changes.
The bill includes a provision authorizing the Governor to direct any State officer or agency to cooperate in carrying out the functions of the Commission related to these acquisitions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill updates the licensing and designation process for nursing assistants in Maryland by replacing the 'Geriatric Nursing Assistant' title with 'Certified Nursing Assistant-I' (CNA-I) for individuals qualified to practice in any setting and creating a 'Certified Nursing Assistant-II' (CNA-II) title for those with restricted practice authority. The legislation establishes training and competency evaluation requirements for these roles, provides for a temporary practice exemption of up to four months under federal regulations, and allows acute care training programs to request waivers for certain instructor experience requirements.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Board of Nursing
qa_results.stakeholders: Certified Nursing Assistants (CNAs), Geriatric Nursing Assistants (GNAs), nursing students, nursing homes, acute care training programs, and the Maryland Board of Nursing.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State Board of Nursing special fund expenditures will increase by $209,900 in FY 2025, which includes $150,000 for a one-time licensing system update and staff costs for a nurse program administrator. Ongoing annual expenditures for staff are estimated to range between $103,600 and $117,900 through FY 2029. Revenues are not expected to be affected as no new certification fees are established for the redesignation process. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health, specifically through the State Board of Nursing and the Office of Health Care Quality, is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill. It must adopt regulations, update licensing systems, notify certificate holders, and oversee the new CNA-I and CNA-II designations.

The Commission is explicitly required by the bill to work in conjunction with the State Board of Nursing to approve nursing assistant training programs and provide periodic surveys of these programs in the State.

As the state's regulatory board for postsecondary education, this agency is mandated to co-approve nursing assistant training programs and curricula alongside the Board of Nursing as specified in the bill text.

The bill's changes to the Geriatric Nursing Assistant (GNA) designation and requirements for nursing home quality assurance committees directly impact the Department's mission to protect the rights and care quality of older adults in long-term care facilities.
The fiscal note indicates a significant impact on special fund expenditures for new personnel and one-time licensing system updates, which falls under the fiscal and personnel oversight of DBM.
The OAG serves as the legal counsel to the State Board of Nursing and the Department of Health, providing advice on the regulatory updates and administrative changes required by the bill.

The bill impacts workforce development and professional licensing requirements for a critical segment of the healthcare industry, which is relevant to the Department's oversight of labor laws and workforce training programs.

The MHCC is responsible for health system planning and monitoring the quality of health services; changes to the certification and training of nursing support staff are relevant to their healthcare workforce data and planning initiatives.
The bill modifies the certification requirements for nursing assistants who provide essential care in state facilities, including the Charlotte Hall Veterans Home managed by the Department.
USM institutions that offer nursing education are affected by the bill's provisions regarding nursing students performing assistant tasks and the standards for approved nursing education programs.

The MLDS Center tracks student and workforce outcomes; the creation of new CNA-I and CNA-II categories and the repeal of the GNA designation will require updates to how workforce data for these professionals is collected and analyzed.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the reimbursement of sexual assault forensic examinations conducted through peer-to-peer telehealth and requires the Maryland Sexual Assault Evidence Kit Policy and Funding Committee to study the feasibility of establishing a telehealth program, including a 'TeleSAFE' pilot program, for these examinations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Sexual Assault Evidence Kit Policy and Funding Committee and the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board
qa_results.stakeholders: Victims of alleged sexual assault, forensic nurse examiners, and healthcare providers
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures may increase starting in fiscal 2025 to process additional claims and provide reimbursements for telehealth exams, though the total impact is indeterminate. The Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy identifies a potential need for an administrator costing approximately $91,800, and the fiscal 2025 budget includes $1,300,000 for the Sexual Assault Reimbursement Unit.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly impacts the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board (CICB) and the Sexual Assault Reimbursement Unit (SARU), both of which are units within GOCPP. The agency is responsible for processing and paying the reimbursement claims for sexual assault forensic examinations, including those conducted via telehealth as authorized by the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General staffs the Maryland Sexual Assault Evidence Kit Policy and Funding Committee, which is the entity mandated by the bill to conduct a study and report on the feasibility of a telehealth program and a TeleSAFE Pilot Program.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Sexual Assault Evidence Kit Policy and Funding Committee, which falls under this category, is specifically tasked with conducting a feasibility study on the TeleSAFE Pilot Program and reporting its recommendations to the General Assembly.

The bill involves 'qualified health care providers' licensed under the Health Occupations Article and forensic nurse examiners. The Department of Health regulates these health professionals and oversees public health services that include forensic examination standards and telehealth policy.

The fiscal note indicates that general fund expenditures will increase to support the new reimbursements. Additionally, GOCPP may require additional administrative staff to handle the increased volume of claims, which falls under DBM's personnel and fiscal oversight.

The bill establishes civil immunity for physicians and healthcare providers who perform these exams via telehealth. This provision affects the judicial branch's adjudication of potential liability claims, and the AOC was consulted as an information source for the bill.

As the primary administrative support for the Governor, who oversees the executive branch and units like GOCPP and executive commissions, the office is responsible for coordinating the policy initiatives and appointments related to the mandated study and implementation of the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Talent Innovation Program and the Talent Innovation Fund within the Maryland Department of Labor. The program's purpose is to increase access to high-quality job training in key sectors like cybersecurity, healthcare, biotechnology, manufacturing, and artificial intelligence using innovative funding models such as pay-it-forward revolving funds and pay-for-performance programs. It specifically requires an initial pilot program focused on expanding regional cyber ranges for the cybersecurity industry.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents seeking job training, employers in emerging industry sectors, higher education institutions, local workforce development boards, and registered apprenticeship sponsors.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, general fund expenditures increase by $2,000,000 annually beginning in FY 2025 to capitalize the Talent Innovation Fund; the FY 2025 budget already includes this $2,000,000 appropriation. Special fund revenues and expenditures increase correspondingly as funds are received and spent. Local governments and small businesses may see a positive fiscal impact through the receipt of grants or improved talent recruitment and training support.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of Labor is the primary agency responsible for establishing and administering the Talent Innovation Program and the Talent Innovation Fund. Its Division of Workforce Development and Adult Learning jointly manages the program and the Secretary oversees the fund.

The Governor's Workforce Development Board is explicitly named as a joint administrator of the Talent Innovation Program, responsible for approving talent finance mechanisms and collaborating with the Department of Labor on implementation.
The State Treasurer is required by the bill to hold the Talent Innovation Fund separately and invest its money in the same manner as other state funds.
The Department of Budget and Management oversees the state's budget process, including the $2 million general fund appropriation required annually to capitalize the new Talent Innovation Fund.
The Comptroller is responsible for accounting for the Talent Innovation Fund as established by the bill.
The Department of Labor is required to report annually to the Governor on the activities and expenditures of the Talent Innovation Program and Fund.
The bill identifies institutions of higher education as eligible entities to receive grants from the Talent Innovation Fund for job training and supportive services.
Institutions within the University System of Maryland are eligible grant recipients under the program for providing high-quality job training in priority sectors like cybersecurity and biotechnology.
As an institution of higher education, Morgan State University is eligible to apply for grants from the Talent Innovation Fund to support its training and workforce development programs.
As a state-supported institution of higher education, the college is an eligible entity to receive grants for training costs and support services under the new program.

The program focuses on industry sectors central to the Department of Commerce's mission, such as biotechnology, cybersecurity, and manufacturing, and impacts the state's overall economic development strategy.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands the definition of 'accredited residential treatment center' for Maryland health laws to include facilities accredited by the Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities (CARF) or the Council on Accreditation (COA), in addition to those accredited by the Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organizations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Residential treatment centers, children and adolescents with severe and chronic emotional disturbances, and the Maryland Department of Health.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland Department of Health can absorb the bill's changes within existing budget resources, and State revenues are not affected. There is no direct impact on local government finances. Small business residential treatment centers may experience a meaningful impact as accreditation options are expanded, potentially making the cost of obtaining accreditation more competitive.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary agency responsible for licensing residential treatment centers and regulating hospitals and related institutions. The bill directly impacts MDH's procedures for granting 'deemed status' to facilities based on reports from approved accreditation organizations.

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is the state's primary social service provider and frequently places foster youth and other vulnerable children in residential treatment centers. Changes to the accreditation standards and the pool of accredited facilities directly affect DHS's placement options and quality oversight.

The Department of Juvenile Services (DJS) manages youth in the juvenile justice system who often require placements in residential treatment centers for psychiatric evaluation and treatment. Expansion of recognized accreditation bodies affects the providers DJS utilizes for these services.

As an independent regulatory agency within MDH, the MHCC plans for health system needs and improves access to health care. Changes to the definitions of accredited residential treatment centers impact their planning and accountability efforts regarding the quality and availability of health services.

Residential treatment centers are required to provide educational programs for the children they serve. The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) oversees the educational components and special education services provided within these facilities.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and the General Assembly regarding the enforcement of state laws for hospitals and health care facilities, including the implementation of new accreditation standards.

The GOC coordinates services for well-being of Maryland's children. Since residential treatment centers specifically treat children and adolescents with severe emotional disturbances, the standards for these facilities are relevant to the GOC's mission.

The Maryland Department of Disabilities coordinates services for residents with disabilities, including children with severe emotional and chronic disturbances who are treated in residential treatment centers.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill permanently establishes the Growing Family Child Care Opportunities Program, which was previously a pilot program. The program provides grants to local jurisdictions to increase the supply and sustainability of family child care providers, specifically targeting areas with high poverty, high unemployment, or a lack of available child care.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Family child care providers, local county governments, the Maryland Child Care Resource Network, and families in underserved communities.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill mandates an annual appropriation of $450,000. For fiscal 2025, a contingent appropriation of $450,000 is effectuated by the bill. For fiscal 2026, the bill establishes a mandated appropriation of $450,000, and annual general fund expenditures of $450,000 are assumed to continue thereafter. Local governments benefit from continued grant funding but are required to provide a 1:1 match in funds or in-kind resources.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Department of Education is the primary implementing agency for the Growing Family Child Care Opportunities Program. The bill establishes the program as a permanent entity within the Department, tasking it with grant administration, developing guidelines, and reporting annual progress to the General Assembly.

The bill creates a permanent mandated annual appropriation of $450,000 starting in fiscal year 2026. As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM is responsible for managing this mandate and ensuring its inclusion in the state's long-term fiscal planning.

The program's goals specifically target areas with high rates of poverty and unemployment to increase child care supply, directly aligning with the Governor's Office for Children's mission to reduce child poverty and improve access to health and education services.

The bill requires the Governor to include a specific appropriation for the program in the annual budget bill. Additionally, the program supports the Governor's policy priorities regarding economic mobility and child well-being.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires grant recipients to establish partnerships with organizations that support women and minority-owned businesses to help child care providers implement best business practices and achieve financial sustainability.

The program defines 'family child care' to include the care of developmentally disabled persons under the age of 21, making the department's mission of improving service delivery and community living for residents with disabilities relevant to the program's scope.
The Innovation Team is specifically charged with identifying and testing initiatives to reduce child poverty and enhance economic mobility, which are the core objectives of this child care program.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Maryland Emergency Department Wait Time Reduction Commission to address factors across the health care system that contribute to increased wait times in emergency departments. The commission is tasked with developing strategies to improve hospital efficiency, increasing inpatient throughput, improving post-discharge resources, and facilitating the sharing of best practices and data between hospitals and state agencies.
qa_results.responsible_party: Health Services Cost Review Commission (HSCRC) and the Maryland Emergency Department Wait Time Reduction Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland hospitals, emergency department patients, emergency medical services (EMS) providers, behavioral health providers, and state health agencies including the Maryland Department of Health and the Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems (MIEMSS).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, Health Services Cost Review Commission (HSCRC) special fund expenditures increase by $100,000 annually from FY 2025 through FY 2027 for contractual services to staff the commission and complete required reports. There is no projected impact on state revenues or local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the Executive Director of HSCRC to co-chair the newly established Maryland Emergency Department Wait Time Reduction Commission. Additionally, HSCRC is responsible for providing staff for the commission and managing data sharing agreements, resulting in a fiscal impact of $100,000 annually.

The Secretary of Health is a co-chair of the new commission and is responsible for appointing several of its members. The commission is established within the Health - General Article and is empowered to request data from the Department to analyze emergency department wait times.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Executive Director of MIEMSS is a statutory member of the commission. The bill's core focus on emergency medical services and hospital throughput directly impacts the agency's mission to coordinate and regulate the state's EMS network.
The Executive Director of MHCC is a statutory member of the commission, and the agency is listed as a primary source of data for the commission's research into healthcare system efficiencies.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill creates a new executive commission tasked with studying a specific government issue (emergency department wait times), providing recommendations, and reporting to the Governor and General Assembly.

The commission must submit an annual report of its activities, findings, and recommendations to the Governor. The office also oversees the various executive agencies (MDH, HSCRC, MIEMSS) tasked with implementing the bill.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to all state agencies and boards. It will likely be involved in reviewing and approving the required data sharing and use agreements for personally identifiable information as mandated by the bill.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees budget execution. The fiscal note indicates an increase in special fund expenditures for HSCRC to staff the commission, which requires DBM's administrative coordination.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits the installation, sale, or solicitation of playground surfacing materials that contain more than 90 parts per million of lead or intentionally added PFAS chemicals starting October 1, 2024. It requires manufacturers to maintain and provide certificates of compliance to the Maryland Department of the Environment and establishes civil and administrative penalties for violations.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment
qa_results.stakeholders: Manufacturers and suppliers of playground materials, state and local government agencies responsible for public parks and schools, and children who use playgrounds.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $118,900 in FY 2025 for the Maryland Department of the Environment to hire a compliance specialist and perform laboratory testing. Local government and state agency expenditures may increase if compliant playground materials are more expensive than those currently used, though the impact is estimated to be minimal for many jurisdictions. Revenue from penalties is not expected to be significant.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Department of the Environment is the primary agency responsible for implementing and enforcing the bill, including establishing regulations, requesting and verifying manufacturer certificates of compliance, and managing civil and administrative penalties for violations.

As a major operator of state parks and public lands with outdoor recreation areas, the Department of Natural Resources is identified in the fiscal note as a primary state agency purchaser and user of playground surfacing materials that must meet the new safety standards.

The Department of Education oversees the state's 24 local school systems, which manage numerous playgrounds; the bill's prohibitions will impact future school construction and renovation projects requiring compliant surfacing materials.
The Office of the Attorney General is tasked with prosecuting violations of the bill and representing the state in legal proceedings related to the enforcement of the new environmental standards.

The Department of General Services is responsible for the planning, design, and construction of state-owned facilities and serves as the central procurement authority, meaning it will manage the acquisition of compliant materials for state playgrounds.

The fiscal note indicates a need for a new environmental compliance specialist at the Department of the Environment to enforce the bill, which involves personnel and budget management overseen by the Department of Budget and Management.
The Department of Juvenile Services operates residential facilities that include playgrounds for youth, making them an impacted state agency user and purchaser of surfacing materials.
The Administrative Office of the Courts is involved because the judicial branch may handle appeals of administrative decisions and the litigation of civil penalties established under the bill.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch departments responsible for implementation and enforcement, and the Governor signed the bill into law as an executive priority.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill exempts certain accounts under the Maryland 529 Program—including the Maryland Prepaid College Trust, the Maryland College Investment Plan, and the Maryland ABLE program—from the provisions of the Maryland Uniform Disposition of Abandoned Property Act. It also clarifies that while these accounts are exempt, any uncashed checks drawn against them remain subject to the state's abandoned property laws.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Treasurer
qa_results.stakeholders: Account holders and beneficiaries of the Maryland 529 Program and Maryland ABLE Program, the Maryland State Treasurer's Office, and the Maryland Comptroller's Office.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill codifies an existing interpretation of current law and is not anticipated to materially affect State operations or finances. The State Treasurer's Office will continue to manage inactive accounts internally while coordinating with the Comptroller for unclaimed checks. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The State Treasurer's Office administers the Maryland 529 Program, including the Prepaid College Trust, College Investment Plan, and ABLE program, and was the primary proponent of this legislation to clarify abandoned property procedures.

The bill specifically exempts Maryland 529 accounts from certain abandoned property laws while clarifying that checks drawn from these accounts remain subject to the Maryland Uniform Disposition of Abandoned Property Act.

As the administrator of the Maryland Uniform Disposition of Abandoned Property Act (MUDAPA), the Comptroller is responsible for receiving and managing unclaimed property, which the bill clarifies for 529 program funds.
The Office of the Attorney General provided the formal legal opinion in 2023 that interpreted the application of MUDAPA to 529 accounts, an interpretation that this bill codifies into statute.
The Department of Disabilities is a key stakeholder and consultant for the Maryland ABLE program, which is one of the specific account types exempted from certain MUDAPA provisions by this bill.

The Office of Legislative Audits conducted the 2019 audit that first identified the issues regarding uncashed checks and MUDAPA compliance within the Maryland 529 program, leading to this legislative remedy.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies involved in implementing these programs and was responsible for signing the bill into law.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Protecting State Waters From PFAS Pollution Act requires the Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE) to identify significant industrial users that intentionally use PFAS chemicals, develop monitoring protocols, and create mitigation plans for industrial discharges. These users must measure PFAS levels, implement reduction measures, and follow strict storage and disposal requirements for PFAS chemicals. The bill excludes federal, state, and local governments from the definition of significant industrial users and requires an update to the State's PFAS Action Plan.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of the Environment (MDE)
qa_results.stakeholders: Significant industrial users in the private sector, publicly owned treatment works (POTWs), and water quality testing laboratories.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Maryland Department of the Environment can implement the bill with existing budgeted resources, and local finances are not expected to be materially affected. Significant industrial users, including small businesses, may incur costs for PFAS measurement, reduction, and storage, while water quality testing laboratories may see increased demand for their services.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for water pollution control, MDE is responsible for implementing the entire bill, including identifying industrial users, developing monitoring protocols, setting action levels, creating mitigation plans, and updating the state's PFAS Action Plan.

The bill explicitly authorizes the Attorney General to request documentation regarding PFAS storage or reuse from significant industrial users. Additionally, the OAG would handle legal enforcement related to the new regulations.

MES functions as a state-owned corporation that operates many water and wastewater treatment systems (POTWs) throughout the state. Since the bill requires MDE to collaborate with POTWs to identify users and develop action levels, MES is directly impacted as an operator and technical partner.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and MDE. The bill requires updates to the PFAS Action Plan, which aligns with the Governor's policy initiatives, and the office provides administrative support for the implementation of such high-priority environmental legislation.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Council on the Chesapeake Bay
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Council is tasked with advising the Governor on the management and restoration of the Chesapeake Bay. PFAS is a significant pollutant affecting the watershed, and the bill's focus on protecting state waters directly intersects with the Council's advisory mission.

The bill establishes new administrative penalties for violations related to PFAS discharge and pretreatment permits. Contested cases regarding these penalties would likely be adjudicated by the OAH's Administrative Law Judges.

The bill creates new civil and criminal enforcement provisions. The judiciary, supported by the AOC, would be responsible for processing cases related to violations of the pretreatment permits and PFAS discharge limits.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires hospitals to establish and maintain protocols and capacity for treating patients who present with opioid-related overdoses or emergency medical conditions. It mandates that hospitals provide evidence-based interventions, maintain a supply of medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD), and offer these medications to patients when appropriate and agreed upon. Furthermore, hospitals must refer patients to community providers for continued treatment upon discharge and coordinate with peer support professionals to assist patients in accessing treatment services.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Patients with opioid use disorder, hospital emergency departments, peer support professionals, and community-based treatment providers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Fiscal Note indicates that the bill has no state or local fiscal impact, as it primarily pertains to private-sector hospital activities. Although a previous version of the bill included a mandated appropriation of $500,000 from the Opioid Restitution Fund for fiscal year 2026, this provision was stricken from the final legislation.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Department of Health (MDH) is the primary regulatory body for state hospitals and behavioral health services. The bill requires hospitals to establish clinical protocols for opioid-related emergencies and medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD), which falls under MDH's oversight of public health policy and hospital licensing.

The HSCRC regulates hospital rates and revenues. The bill mandates that hospitals maintain specific capacities and possess various formulations of MOUD, which may impact hospital operating costs and rate-setting structures.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS oversees and regulates the statewide emergency medical services (EMS) network and designates specialty referral centers. The bill's focus on hospital emergency department protocols for overdose patients is directly related to the continuity of care between EMS providers and hospitals.

The bill explicitly tasks the Attorney General with identifying and designating the controlling versions of opioid settlement agreements related to the Opioid Restitution Fund. Additionally, the OAG provides legal counsel to MDH and state hospitals regarding regulatory compliance.

The MHCC is responsible for planning for state health system needs and improving access to care. The bill's requirements for hospital capacity and community referrals for opioid treatment align with the commission's planning and access mission.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and MDH. While the fiscal note indicates the final version of the bill may have removed specific budget mandates, the Governor maintains ultimate oversight of the agencies implementing these public health protocols.

DBM is the principal fiscal agency and was consulted for the bill's fiscal note. They monitor legislation for potential impact on the state budget, particularly regarding the use of the Opioid Restitution Fund mentioned in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Grow Your Own Educators Grant Program, administered by the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE), to support noncertified education support professionals and provisional teachers in becoming certified teachers. The program provides grants to local school systems that are part of 'collaboratives' with employee unions and higher education institutions to cover costs like tuition, books, and fees for candidates, as well as stipends for mentor teachers. Participants must commit to a two-year service obligation in a high-needs school or shortage area.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Noncertified education support professionals, provisional teachers, local school systems, teacher unions, Maryland institutions of higher education, and students in high-needs schools or subjects.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures are projected to increase by approximately $1,500,000 in fiscal 2025, growing to at least $5,400,000 annually by fiscal 2028 once four cohorts are fully phased in. These expenditures cover grant awards and MSDE's administrative staff costs. Local school systems may experience increased costs for stipends and administration, though these may be offset by grant revenues. Public and private higher education institutions will likely see increased revenue from tuition and fees paid via the grants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill designates the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) as the primary administrator of the Grow Your Own Educators Grant Program, requiring it to award grants, provide technical assistance, and report program data annually.

The program requires partnerships with institutions of higher education (IHEs) that have teacher preparation programs approved by the Department. The Commission oversees these institutions and the academic standards for their programs.

The bill explicitly requires the Maryland Department of Labor to collaborate with MSDE to provide technical assistance to school collaboratives in obtaining state, federal, or other funding for the program.

The Department of Budget and Management will oversee the fiscal implementation of the program, including the establishment of new personnel positions at MSDE and the administration of the program's annual budget appropriations.
The bill authorizes the Governor to include an appropriation for the program in the annual budget bill and involves the implementation of a major executive educational initiative.
Public universities within the University System of Maryland that offer teacher preparation programs will likely serve as essential partners in the collaboratives established by local school systems.
As a public university with a teacher preparation program, Morgan State University is a potential partner for school systems forming the collaboratives required by the grant program.
As a public honors college that offers a Master of Arts in Teaching, St. Mary's College of Maryland is a potential higher education partner for the program's required collaboratives.
The bill requires local school systems to negotiate with union employee organizations regarding the program's implementation, pay, and benefits, areas over which PERB has jurisdiction.

The bill requires the collection of teacher candidate retention and status data; MLDSC links student and workforce data and may be utilized to analyze the long-term outcomes of the program's graduates.
The OAG will provide legal counsel to MSDE in developing the regulations, grant agreements, and service obligation contracts required to implement the new program.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the reporting requirements for the Maryland Attorney General by shifting the publication of annual reports and legal opinions from physical bound volumes to the official website of the Attorney General. It also repeals a specific reporting requirement concerning actions taken under the Maryland Defense Act, removes the requirement to include itemized receipts and disbursements in the annual report, and adds the General Assembly as a recipient of the annual report.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Office of the Attorney General
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland Office of the Attorney General, the Governor, the Maryland General Assembly, and residents of the State of Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill is not expected to have a material effect on State finances or operations. The Office of the Attorney General may experience minor administrative efficiencies by publishing reports online rather than in bound volumes and by reducing specific reporting requirements. There is no impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly alters the reporting and publication requirements of the Office of the Attorney General (OAG), requiring them to publish annual reports and legal opinions on their website instead of in bound volumes and streamlining certain reporting mandates.

The Governor's Office is a primary recipient of the Attorney General's annual reports and mandated notices regarding legal actions taken against the federal government; the bill modifies the frequency and format of these communications.

As the central depository for government records of permanent value, the Maryland State Archives is affected by the transition from bound volumes to digital website publication for the Attorney General's official records and opinions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the 9-1-1 Specialist Recruitment and Retention Workgroup to identify and examine recruitment and retention challenges for 9-1-1 specialists in Maryland. The workgroup is required to review previous reports, study salary ranges and benefits, analyze training requirements, and make recommendations regarding uniform incentives and the feasibility of including these specialists in the Law Enforcement Officers' Pension System.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Legislative Services and the Maryland Department of Emergency Management
qa_results.stakeholders: 9-1-1 specialists, Public Safety Answering Point (PSAP) Directors, and local government emergency response agencies.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State agencies (DLS and MDEM) are expected to staff the workgroup using existing resources, though general fund expenditures may increase if subject-specific contractual support is required. Expense reimbursements for workgroup members are assumed to be minimal and absorbable within existing budgets. Local governments are expected to provide necessary data and information using existing budgeted resources. There is no projected impact on revenues.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill designates the Secretary of Emergency Management as a member of the Workgroup and requires the Department of Emergency Management to provide staff for the Workgroup. Additionally, the department oversees the Maryland 9-1-1 Board, which is directly affected by the bill's focus on 9-1-1 specialists.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly creates a new temporary entity (the 9–1–1 Specialist Recruitment and Retention Workgroup) that fits the description of this collective entity, tasked with conducting a study and making recommendations to the General Assembly and the Governor.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Executive Director of the Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems (or their designee) is a designated member of the Workgroup.
The Secretary of State Police (or their designee) is a designated member of the Workgroup.
The Governor is responsible for appointing eight members of the Workgroup and the Workgroup is required to submit both interim and final reports to the Governor.

The Workgroup is tasked with making recommendations regarding the feasibility of and barriers to incorporating 9-1-1 specialists into the Law Enforcement Officers’ Pension System (LEOPS), which is administered by the Maryland State Retirement Agency.
The Workgroup is required to examine salary ranges and benefit offerings for 9-1-1 specialists, which falls under the purview of DBM as the state's principal personnel and fiscal agency.

As the chief legal officer for the State, the OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, including those tasked with staffing and participating in this Workgroup, regarding the implementation of findings and legal barriers to policy changes.
The bill requires the Workgroup to analyze the amount and types of workers' compensation claims made by 9-1-1 specialists, data which is managed by the State Workers' Compensation Commission.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes the Governor to include an increase in the general fund appropriation in the annual budget bill to ensure the State's share of funding for the Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS) within the Maryland State Department of Education is met in accordance with federal acts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Individuals with disabilities receiving vocational rehabilitation or independent living services, transitioning students with disabilities, and the Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS).
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: General fund expenditures increase by $700,000 in FY 2025, reflecting funds in the FY 2025 budget passed by the General Assembly contingent on this legislation. Otherwise, the bill is authorizing in nature, consistent with the Governor's current budgetary authority. It aims to ensure the State meets its 21.3% match requirement to secure the full 78.7% federal share of vocational rehabilitation grants.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically concerns the Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS), which is a division within the Maryland State Department of Education. The State Board of Education is also tasked with cooperating with federal agencies to secure benefits under the federal rehabilitation acts.
The bill authorizes the Governor to include specific increased appropriations in the annual budget bill to meet federal matching requirements for vocational rehabilitation services.

As the principal fiscal and personnel agency responsible for developing the state's budget and supervising its execution, DBM will be involved in managing the increased general fund appropriation and fiscal planning related to the federal match.
The bill text explicitly designates the State Treasurer as the custodian of any money received under the federal acts and requires the Treasurer to disburse the money in accordance with those acts.

The Division of Rehabilitation Services (DORS) is responsible for rehabilitating and placing individuals with disabilities into gainful employment. This aligns directly with the Department of Disabilities' mission to coordinate and improve services for Maryland residents with disabilities.

DORS focuses on placing individuals with disabilities in gainful employment and providing vocational training, which intersects with the Department of Labor's responsibilities for workforce development and adult learning programs.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Whole Watershed Act establishes the Whole Watershed Restoration Partnership and the Whole Watershed Fund to accelerate restoration efforts for the Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bays. The bill creates a State management team to oversee up to five comprehensive restoration projects every five years, focusing on cost-effectiveness and ecological impact. Additionally, it sets new regulatory requirements for stream and floodplain restoration projects, including mandatory public notices, community meetings, and at least five years of post-project monitoring to ensure project stability and effectiveness.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Natural Resources and the Maryland Department of the Environment
qa_results.stakeholders: Local governments, environmental restoration contractors, agricultural landowners, and residents of overburdened or underserved communities near project sites.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are primarily affected by the reallocation of existing special funds—including the 2010 Trust Fund, Bay Restoration Fund, and Clean Water Commerce Account—into the newly created Whole Watershed Fund; these transfers are estimated to exceed 5,000,000 dollars annually. The bill mandates a 100,000 dollar annual distribution from the 2010 Trust Fund between FY 2026 and FY 2030 for operations grants. The Department of Natural Resources requires at least 200,000 dollars annually for administration, and the Maryland Department of the Environment expects annual costs of 285,000 to 325,000 dollars for new staffing starting in FY 2026. Local governments may see significant cost increases for stream restoration project compliance and monitoring.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: DNR is the primary administrative agency for the Whole Watershed Restoration Partnership. The Secretary of Natural Resources chairs the State Management Team, and the department administers the newly established Whole Watershed Fund and coordinates grant awards for restoration projects.

MDE is a primary implementing and regulatory agency. It designates a member to the State Management Team and is responsible for authorizing stream and floodplain restoration projects, conducting technical assessments, and overseeing mandatory five-year monitoring requirements for all approved projects.

MDA is represented on the State Management Team. Furthermore, major agricultural programs including the Agricultural Land Preservation Fund and the Agricultural Water Quality Cost-Share Program serve as mandatory funding sources for the Whole Watershed Fund.

MDP is a designated member of the State Management Team and is involved in coordinating land use policies and restoration protocols at the local level to ensure the sustainability of watershed projects.

As an agency that frequently performs stream restoration projects via the State Highway Administration to meet environmental permits, MDOT is subject to the bill's new public notice, meeting, and technical assessment requirements, which will increase project costs and operational timelines.

The Governor's Chief Resilience Officer is a mandated member of the State Management Team. Additionally, the office oversees the executive branch departments responsible for the inter-agency coordination required by the Whole Watershed Act.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Whole Watershed Restoration Partnership as a new inter-agency body and specifically requires a representative from the Critical Area Commission for the Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bays to serve on the State Management Team.

DBM is responsible for the state personnel and budget allocations identified in the fiscal note, including the creation of new permanent positions at DNR and MDE required to implement the bill's mandates.
DoIT is responsible for maintaining the statewide digital watershed file which serves as the statutory standard for identifying project locations within specific Maryland 8-digit watersheds.
The Comptroller's Office is responsible for the financial accounting and disbursement of funds for the newly established Whole Watershed Fund.
The State Treasurer is responsible for holding the Whole Watershed Fund and managing its investments, ensuring that interest earnings are properly credited back to the fund.

The Board of Public Works oversees the use of state bond proceeds for cost-sharing projects and has the authority to approve wetlands licenses and significant state contracts related to the restoration projects funded under the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Secure Schools Emergency Response Grant Program within the Maryland Center for School Safety. The program provides grants to local school systems to implement emergency notification systems and enhance communications between public elementary and secondary school personnel, public safety answering points, and public safety agencies during emergency events.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Center for School Safety
qa_results.stakeholders: Local school systems, public school faculty and staff, students, first responders, public safety agencies, and public safety answering points.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures increase by 3000000 annually beginning in FY 2025 to provide grants to local school systems. Local school system revenues and expenditures increase by an equivalent amount to the extent they receive grant funding. While the bill text mentions a potential appropriation of 4500000 for fiscal year 2026, the Fiscal Note prioritizes an estimate of 3000000 annually for a viable program.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly establishes the Secure Schools Emergency Response Grant Program and designates the Maryland Center for School Safety (MCSS) as the primary agency responsible for its administration.

As the parent agency of the Maryland Center for School Safety and the overseer of the state's public elementary and secondary schools, the Department of Education is central to the implementation of the grant program for local school systems.

The Interagency Commission on School Construction (IAC) manages the finances for existing school safety grants under a memorandum of understanding with MCSS. This bill's new program likely involves similar financial management of capital/equipment funds for schools.

The department oversees the Maryland 9-1-1 Board and Public Safety Answering Points (PSAPs), which are the critical communication hubs required to integrate with the new school emergency notification systems established by the bill.
The Governor is authorized to include the necessary funding for the program in the annual budget and will receive required reports on life-threatening incidents from MCSS.
The department will be responsible for managing the estimated $3 million annual fiscal impact and incorporating the grant program's funding into the state's fiscal planning.

As a primary public safety agency and first responder, the Maryland State Police are key stakeholders in the emergency communications network and are mentioned as an information source in the bill's fiscal note.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill's emergency notification systems cover medical emergencies; MIEMSS regulates the statewide emergency medical services network that would receive and respond to these alerts.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office advises the Governor on public safety strategies and manages funds for law enforcement and youth programs, making it relevant to the bill's goals of improving school safety and emergency response.

The bill requires that the communication platform be certified under the federal SAFETY Act, a process that typically involves coordination with the state's homeland security office regarding disaster prevention and response readiness.
The Board may be required to review and approve significant state procurement contracts related to the acquisition and implementation of the statewide emergency communication platform.

The bill involves the procurement and implementation of an emergency communication platform, which may fall under the department's oversight of statewide information technology and telecommunications strategy.

qa_results.bill_summary: The Green Death Care Options Act legalizes and regulates two alternative methods for disposing of human remains: alkaline hydrolysis (a chemical and heat-based decomposition process) and natural organic reduction (human composting). The bill establishes a regulatory framework for 'reduction facilities' and 'reduction operators,' requiring them to be licensed or permitted. It mandates specific safety and health regulations, including minimum temperature requirements during reduction and testing for heavy metals and pathogens in the resulting soil remains. Additionally, the bill prohibits using the soil remains from natural organic reduction to grow food for human or livestock consumption or selling the soil to third parties.
qa_results.responsible_party: The Maryland Office of Cemetery Oversight and the State Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors
qa_results.stakeholders: Funeral establishments, crematory and cemetery operators, individuals seeking alternative burial options, and veterans and their eligible dependents.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues for the Office of Cemetery Oversight and the State Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors are expected to increase minimally starting in FY 2025 due to new permit and registration fees for reduction facilities and operators. The Office of Cemetery Oversight (OCO) provides an illustrative estimate that its revenues could increase by approximately 20,000 dollars over a five-year period. State expenditures are not expected to change significantly, as the agencies can manage the additional workload and regulatory adoption with existing resources. Local health departments may experience a minor impact if required to monitor facility compliance.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Office of Cemetery Oversight (OCO) is a unit within the Department of Labor. The bill explicitly requires the Director of OCO to adopt regulations, establish registration processes for reduction operators, and oversee the licensing and inspection of reduction facilities.

The State Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors, housed within the Department of Health, is required to jointly establish regulations and oversight processes for alkaline hydrolysis and natural organic reduction. Additionally, local health departments may be involved in monitoring compliance and receiving testing results.

The bill establishes protocols for identifying and handling the remains of veterans or their dependents. It mandates that reduction facilities notify the department regarding unclaimed soil remains and provides for the transfer of those remains to the department for appropriate disposition in veteran cemeteries.

The bill requires the Division of Consumer Protection within the Office of the Attorney General to collaborate with other agencies to publish a consumer information pamphlet describing rights and services related to these new death care options.

As noted in the fiscal note, the new methodologies of alkaline hydrolysis and natural organic reduction may require facilities to obtain environmental permits from MDE related to air quality, water discharge, or soil management.

The bill regulates soil remains and prohibits their use in growing food for humans or livestock, which overlaps with the department's mandate over soil conservation and food quality assurance. The department was also a consulted information source for the fiscal note.

The bill references the Forest Conservation Act and allows for the transfer of soil remains to owners of protected woodlands. DNR is responsible for managing the state's natural resources and forests and was consulted as an information source.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes registered nurses in local health departments to provide nonprescription drugs and devices, including over-the-counter contraceptive drugs and devices approved by the FDA, without a prescription. It also modifies the regulations regarding the sale of condoms through vending machines by prohibiting the sale of natural membrane condoms while repealing the previous prohibition on the sale of nonlatex condoms.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Committee on Registered Nurses Personally Preparing and Dispensing Drugs and Devices in Local Health Departments
qa_results.stakeholders: Registered nurses at local health departments, patients seeking reproductive and family planning services, and vending machine operators.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill is not expected to have a material impact on state or local finances. The Maryland Department of Health can update the required formulary and training programs using existing budgeted resources. The repeal of certain criminal penalties related to vending machine sales is not anticipated to significantly affect the workload or revenue of the District Court or local law enforcement.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill repeals certain criminal penalties and exempts specific violations from existing criminal and civil penalties within the District Court's jurisdiction. The fiscal note explicitly mentions the work of the District Court in its analysis.

As the state's principal fiscal agency, DBM monitors the budgetary impact of new legislation. The fiscal note indicates that the bill's requirements can be met with existing resources, which falls under DBM's oversight.

The bill's fiscal note lists the Maryland State Department of Education as an information source. Additionally, the legislation involves the repeal of certain provisions that formerly regulated the sale of contraceptives in school environments.

The Maryland Department of Health is the primary agency impacted. It administers the Maryland Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, oversees the local health departments where registered nurses will now be authorized to provide nonprescription drugs without a prescription, and houses the committee responsible for the drug formulary.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly mandates action from the Committee on Registered Nurses Personally Preparing and Dispensing Drugs and Devices in Local Health Departments, requiring it to include all FDA-approved over-the-counter contraceptives in its formulary.
As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of new public health policies and the administration of the executive committee mentioned in the bill.

The Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing the state's criminal laws. The bill modifies the Criminal Law Article by repealing and adding specific prohibitions and penalties related to the sale of products through automatic devices.
Morgan State University is listed as an information source in the fiscal note, reflecting potential interest in student health services or the training of nursing professionals affected by the bill.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Health and the Committee on Registered Nurses, advising on the implementation of the new authorization for registered nurses and the enforcement of the Maryland Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.
St. Mary's College of Maryland is listed as an information source in the fiscal note regarding the bill's potential impact.

The University System of Maryland is listed as an information source in the fiscal note, as the bill impacts health service provision and nursing practice standards relevant to its clinical and educational missions.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill relaxes the disqualification criteria for video lottery employee licenses, allowing individuals with past nonviolent misdemeanor convictions for crimes involving moral turpitude to be licensed under certain conditions: they must not work in gaming or security roles, and the casino operator must specifically request their licensure. Additionally, the bill establishes that for the purposes of theft-related offenses, the value of a table game chip is determined by its face value or its color-coded value as designated by the State Lottery and Gaming Control Commission.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Lottery and Gaming Control Commission
qa_results.stakeholders: Applicants for video lottery employee licenses with certain criminal records, video lottery operation licensees (casinos), and individuals involved in criminal cases regarding the theft of table game chips.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill has no significant impact on State or local finances. Altering licensing qualifications is not expected to materially change the number of license applications or associated fee revenues. Furthermore, the change to the definition of 'value' for theft of gaming chips does not affect State operations or finances.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Lottery & Gaming Control Agency
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The agency is the primary regulator for the gaming industry and is responsible for implementing the licensing changes and determining the value of table game chips as prescribed by the bill.

The bill modifies the definition of property value for theft crimes and references the Office of Problem-Solving Courts, both of which fall under the administrative and operational purview of the judicial branch.
The OAH adjudicates contested cases and appeals from state agencies, including potential disputes regarding the denial or disqualification of video lottery employee licenses by the Commission.
The OAG provides essential legal guidance to the State Lottery and Gaming Control Commission on the interpretation of licensing standards and changes to the Criminal Law Article.
As the primary state law enforcement agency, the Maryland State Police are responsible for enforcing the theft laws that are modified by this bill's new valuation criteria for gaming chips.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The department manages individuals on parole and probation whose ability to obtain employment in the gaming industry is directly affected by the bill's changes to licensing disqualification criteria.
The Governor's Office provides executive oversight of the state's gaming commissions and is responsible for the broader policy coordination regarding the state's workforce and criminal justice laws.

qa_results.bill_summary: Known as Elijah's Law, this bill requires the Maryland State Department of Education and the Maryland Department of Health to jointly develop guidelines and a model policy to reduce the risk of anaphylactic food allergen exposure in child care settings. Registered large family child care homes and licensed child care centers with at least one enrolled child with a food allergy must adopt and implement a policy based on these guidelines. Furthermore, the bill establishes civil immunity for child care providers and employees who respond in good faith to a child's anaphylactic reaction, provided their actions are not willfully or grossly negligent.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education and Maryland Department of Health
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed child care centers, registered large family child care homes, child care providers and employees, and children with anaphylactic food allergies and their parents or guardians.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the Maryland State Department of Education and the Maryland Department of Health can develop the required guidelines and model policy using existing budgeted resources. No impact is expected on state revenues or local government finances. The financial impact on small business child care providers is considered minimal.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MSDE is the primary agency responsible for child care oversight and is explicitly mandated by the bill to jointly develop guidelines, create a model policy for child care providers, and host resources on its website.
MDH is explicitly required by the bill to work jointly with the Department of Education to develop the guidelines for reducing exposure to anaphylactic food allergens in child care settings.

This office coordinates state efforts regarding the well-being of children and access to health services; the bill's focus on protecting children from life-threatening food allergies in child care settings aligns directly with its mission.

The bill establishes statutory civil immunity for certain child care providers. The OAG would provide legal counsel to MSDE and MDH regarding the interpretation and implementation of these indemnity provisions.

As the bill establishes new civil immunity standards for child care providers and employees responding to anaphylactic reactions, it impacts the adjudication of future civil litigation within the state court system.
The Governor signed this legislation into law and oversees the executive branch departments (MSDE and MDH) responsible for its implementation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes a Class M-F (Municipal Family Fun Center) alcoholic beverages license for municipal bowling alleys and skating rinks in Baltimore City's 40th district, specifically for locations on the 1600 block of Pennsylvania Avenue. It also permits the issuance of a Class B beer, wine, and liquor license for a restaurant on the 400 block of West 29th Street and extends the validity of a Class A license on West Biddle Street for renewal purposes until July 2025.
qa_results.responsible_party: Baltimore City Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Baltimore City government, operators of municipal bowling alleys and skating rinks in the 40th district, restaurant owners in the 40th district, and community associations including Upton West, Penn North, and Greater Remington Improvement Association.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Baltimore City revenues are estimated to increase by at least $3,300 in fiscal 2025 from the issuance of three Class M-F licenses. Additional revenue between $1,920 and $2,400 per license may be generated if Class B beer, wine, and liquor licenses are issued. The state has no fiscal impact, and local enforcement costs are expected to be managed within existing resources.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes a new Class M-F license and authorizes the Board of License Commissioners for Baltimore City to issue specific Class B licenses and adopt regulations. Local boards of license commissioners are the primary regulatory bodies for alcoholic beverages in their jurisdictions and fall under the umbrella of state-authorized executive boards and commissions.

The Comptroller's Office is identified in its summary as the state agency that regulates the alcohol industry. This bill significantly revises alcoholic beverage licensing laws and fees within the 40th alcoholic beverages district of Baltimore City.

The Baltimore City Board of License Commissioners is a state-appointed board. The Office of Appointments is responsible for recruiting and recommending candidates for state boards and commissions, including those with regulatory authority over alcoholic beverages.

The Governor oversees the executive branch, including state-authorized boards and commissions, and signed this bill into law. The office is responsible for the overall execution of state laws regarding industry regulation.

The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies, boards, and commissions. They may be required to provide guidance to the Board of License Commissioners regarding the legal implementation of the new license classes and the temporary extension of the West Biddle Street license.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Transportation Institute at the University of Maryland to conduct a comprehensive study to identify methods for improving the Maryland Transit Administration's ADA MobilityLink paratransit service. The study involves comparing the service to national models, analyzing fiscal costs and performance metrics, and developing recommendations for service improvements and interjurisdictional coordination.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Transportation Institute at the University of Maryland
qa_results.stakeholders: MobilityLink paratransit service riders, individuals with disabilities, and the Maryland Transit Administration.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Higher education expenditures for the University of Maryland increase by $449,000 in FY 2025 to conduct the study. General fund expenditures and higher education revenues are expected to increase by $449,000 in FY 2026 as the university is reimbursed for its costs. The bill has no direct impact on local government finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill requires the Maryland Transportation Institute (MTI) at the University of Maryland, which is part of the University System of Maryland, to conduct the mandated study. UMD is also the primary recipient of the funding ($449,000 in higher education revenues/expenditures).

The bill specifically targets the Maryland Transit Administration (MTA), a modal unit of MDOT, to improve its ADA MobilityLink paratransit service. MDOT is required to provide data for the study and receive the final report.

The bill requires MTI to consult with the Maryland Commission on Disabilities, which advises the Department of Disabilities. The study's focus on ADA paratransit services directly aligns with the department's mission to coordinate and improve services for residents with disabilities.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates consultation with the State Coordinating Committee for Human Services Transportation, which is an interagency advisory body.
The bill authorizes an appropriation of at least $150,000 in the fiscal year 2026 budget bill, which is managed by DBM. The fiscal note also indicates a general fund expenditure impact in FY 2026.
The Governor receives the final study report and is authorized to include a specific appropriation in the FY 2026 budget for the study.
The Maryland State Archives is listed as a source of information in the fiscal note, indicating they may provide historical or record-keeping data for the study.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Strategic Infrastructure Revolving Loan Program and Fund within the Maryland Economic Development Corporation (MEDCO) to provide loans for real estate and infrastructure projects in sustainable communities, priority funding areas, and transit-oriented development areas. It also expands tax increment financing (TIF) authority to allow MEDCO to pledge alternative local revenues for debt service or to support the loan fund.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Economic Development Corporation (MEDCO)
qa_results.stakeholders: Real estate and infrastructure developers, local political subdivisions, and residents or businesses located in priority funding areas, sustainable communities, or transit-oriented development areas.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, State general fund expenditures increase by $15,000,000 in FY 2025 and $20,000,000 annually from FY 2026 through FY 2029 for discretionary program funding. Special fund revenues and expenditures for MEDCO increase as funds are received and used for loans. Local government revenues and expenditures may also increase if local alternative revenues are pledged to TIF special accounts to support projects or the Strategic Infrastructure Revolving Loan Fund.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes the Strategic Infrastructure Revolving Loan Program and Fund directly within MEDCO, making it the primary entity responsible for administering the program, reviewing applications via an advisory committee, and setting loan terms.
The bill explicitly mandates the State Treasurer to hold the Strategic Infrastructure Revolving Loan Fund separately and invest its money in the same manner as other State funds.
The bill explicitly requires the Comptroller to account for the newly established Strategic Infrastructure Revolving Loan Fund.
The program provides loans for projects in 'Sustainable Communities,' a designation managed by DHCD, and aligns with the agency's mission to enhance community development and economic vitality.

The bill targets 'Transit-Oriented Development' (TOD) areas for loan eligibility and infrastructure investment; MDOT is responsible for defining, managing, and operating the state's transportation network and TOD initiatives.
The bill utilizes 'Priority Funding Areas' as a core eligibility criterion for loans, which is a fundamental component of the state's sustainable growth and land use planning overseen by this agency.

The fiscal note assumes substantial discretionary general fund expenditures (estimated at $15 million to $20 million annually) to capitalize the fund, which falls under DBM's oversight of state budget development.

The bill prioritizes projects that activate underutilized property owned by the government; DGS is the primary agency responsible for the management, leasing, and real estate operations of state-owned facilities.

As the state's lead economic development agency, Commerce's mission to attract business and create jobs is directly supported by infrastructure loans and tax increment financing for transformative projects.

The program aligns with the Governor's policy priorities regarding infrastructure and inclusive economic growth; the office also coordinates policy initiatives and budget proposals related to these efforts.
The BPW oversees significant state expenditures and the transfer of state property; the bill's focus on capitalized infrastructure funds and government-owned assets may involve their oversight roles.
The OAG provides legal counsel to state agencies and MEDCO, and would be involved in drafting loan documents, managing security interests in property, and adopting program regulations.

This multi-agency initiative focuses on sustainable development and environmental protection, goals that are directly mirrored in the bill's focus on Sustainable Communities and Priority Funding Areas.
The program's mandate for 'inclusive and equitable economic growth' aligns with this team's charge to pilot novel initiatives addressing economic mobility and child poverty.
The bill's focus on job growth, retention, and economic development falls within the Department's mission to protect workers and foster economic growth in the state.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The program's objective of 'inclusive and equitable economic growth' potentially involves this office's mission to connect small and minority businesses to economic opportunities generated by state-funded projects.
The bill prioritizes the activation of underutilized property owned by 'institutions'; as a major holder of such properties, USM may be impacted by or a participant in these redevelopment projects.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill creates a Class BWLT beer, wine, and liquor tasting permit in Allegany County. The permit allows holders of an existing beer, wine, and liquor license to conduct on-premises tastings, provided they notify the local Board of License Commissioners at least seven days before each event.
qa_results.responsible_party: Allegany County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Existing beer, wine, and liquor license holders in Allegany County and consumers attending tasting events.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State finances are not affected. Allegany County revenues are projected to increase by at least 1200 dollars in fiscal 2025 and at least 600 dollars annually thereafter. This is based on the expectation that four to six licensees will apply for the permit, which carries a 150 dollar annual fee and a one-time 150 dollar issuing fee.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill grants new regulatory and administrative authority to the Board of License Commissioners for Allegany County, which is a state-authorized ongoing regulatory body. The board is responsible for issuing the Class BWLT permits, collecting fees, and adopting the regulations necessary to implement the program.
