The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state and provides counsel to all state agencies. This includes interpreting and providing guidance on changes to the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article of the Annotated Code of Maryland.

As the head of the executive branch, the Governor's Office oversees the implementation of state laws and signed this legislation. The change in employment law for Allegany County falls under the administrative oversight of the executive branch.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland Commission on Health Equity to develop and monitor a statewide health equity plan in accordance with federal requirements from the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation. It alters the commission's membership, designates the Secretary of Health and the Executive Director of the Health Services Cost Review Commission as co-chairs, and expands its advisory roles. Additionally, the bill extends the termination date and modifies reporting requirements for the Commission on Public Health.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Health and the Maryland Commission on Health Equity
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents, particularly those in underserved communities, healthcare providers, hospitals, federally qualified health centers, managed care organizations, and commercial health insurers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the fiscal note, the state fiscal impact is minimal. Any additional workload for the Maryland Department of Health and the Health Services Cost Review Commission, as well as expense reimbursements for new commission members, can be handled within existing budgeted resources. The extension of the Commission on Public Health's termination date to June 30, 2026, is also expected to be absorbed within existing resources. No impact on state revenues or local government finances is expected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MDH is the primary agency responsible for staffing the Maryland Commission on Health Equity. The Secretary of Health co-chairs the commission, and several of its deputy secretaries are designated members. The agency is also involved in the Commission on Public Health whose timeline is extended by this bill.

The Executive Director of HSCRC co-chairs the Maryland Commission on Health Equity. The agency is also required to advise on commission appointments and coordinate on the establishment of a data advisory committee for the health equity plan.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill directly revises the structure, membership, duties, and reporting requirements for two entities fitting this summary: the Maryland Commission on Health Equity and the Commission on Public Health.
The Executive Director of MHCC is added as a member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity. The agency also contributes data to the commission's health equity data set.
The bill removes the Governor's authority to designate the chair of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity, replacing that authority with statutory co-chairs from MDH and HSCRC.
The Secretary of Aging remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity, contributing to the development of the statewide health equity plan.
The Secretary of Budget and Management remains a member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity, and the bill references expense reimbursements managed through the state budget.
The Secretary of Disabilities remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The State Superintendent of Schools remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Secretary of Housing and Community Development remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Secretary of Human Services remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Secretary of Planning remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Maryland Insurance Commissioner remains a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Secretary of Agriculture is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Commerce is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of the Environment is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of General Services is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Information Technology is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Juvenile Services is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Labor is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Natural Resources is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Commissioner of Correction, a position within DPSCS, is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity.
The Secretary of State Police is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Transportation is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.
The Secretary of Veterans Affairs is removed as a statutory member of the Maryland Commission on Health Equity by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the sunset provision for the Continuing the CORE Partnership Fund, effectively making the fund permanent, and establishes a mandated annual appropriation of $50,000,000 starting in fiscal year 2026. The fund is dedicated to assisting the Department of Housing and Community Development, the Maryland Stadium Authority, and Baltimore City in the expedited removal of blighted properties within Baltimore City.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.stakeholders: Baltimore City government and residents, the Maryland Stadium Authority, and the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill creates a mandated annual appropriation of $50,000,000 starting in fiscal 2026, increasing State general fund expenditures by that amount annually. No fiscal impact is expected in fiscal 2024 or 2025. Because Project CORE has previously been funded through general obligation bonds, this shift to general fund pay-as-you-go (PAYGO) expenditures may free up bond capacity for other capital projects.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly names the Secretary of Housing and Community Development as the administrator of the Continuing the CORE Partnership Fund and tasks the Department with removing blighted property in Baltimore City.
The bill identifies the Maryland Stadium Authority as a primary partner alongside DHCD in the expeditious removal of blighted property within Baltimore City through the CORE Partnership Fund.
The bill mandates that the Governor include a $50 million appropriation in the annual budget bill starting in fiscal year 2026.
As the principal agency for budget development and fiscal planning, DBM is responsible for implementing the bill's $50 million annual mandated appropriation and managing the resulting fiscal impact.
The bill requires the State Treasurer to hold the Continuing the CORE Partnership Fund separately and invest its money in the same manner as other State money.
The bill mandates that the Comptroller account for the Continuing the CORE Partnership Fund.

The fiscal note highlights the Office of the Attorney General's legal interpretation regarding the Governor's inability to satisfy the funding mandate with GO bonds, necessitating general fund expenditures.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires health insurance carriers to include additional data in their quarterly reports to the Maryland Insurance Commissioner, specifically the total number of members entitled to health care benefits in Maryland and the number of clean claims for reimbursement processed by the carrier.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Commissioner
qa_results.stakeholders: Health insurance carriers and individuals entitled to health care benefits under policies issued or delivered in Maryland.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, the bill does not substantively change State activities or operations and has no local fiscal impact.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Insurance Administration is the primary agency responsible for the bill's implementation. The Insurance Commissioner, who leads the agency, is tasked with receiving quarterly reports from health insurance carriers containing expanded member and claim data and must compile an annual summary report based on this information.

The Office of the Attorney General's Health Education and Advocacy Unit (HEAU) is a key participant in the state's health insurance appeals and grievances process. Although a workgroup involving the HEAU was removed from the bill's final version, the reporting changes relate directly to the regulatory framework in which the HEAU operates to protect consumers.

The Secretary of Health is required by statute to provide data (specifically regarding health maintenance organizations) to the Insurance Commissioner for the compilation of the annual summary report on health insurance appeals and grievances.
The Governor's Office is the designated recipient of the annual summary report on the health care appeals and grievances law compiled by the Insurance Commissioner.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill reduces the term length for members of the Howard County Agricultural Preservation Advisory Board from five years to three years. It also allows current members of the board to complete their existing five-year terms before the new term length applies to their successors.
qa_results.responsible_party: Howard County Governing Body
qa_results.stakeholders: Members of the Howard County Agricultural Preservation Advisory Board and Howard County agricultural landowners
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no State fiscal impact. According to the fiscal note, the bill does not materially affect Howard County operations or finances, and the impact on small businesses is minimal or none.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Agriculture Article of the Maryland Code concerning county agricultural preservation advisory boards. These boards are statutorily required to advise the Maryland Agricultural Land Preservation Foundation (MALPF), which is a program housed within the Department of Agriculture, on the purchase of agricultural easements.

County agricultural preservation advisory boards are responsible for approving or disapproving applications for the certification or recertification of county agricultural land preservation programs, a process conducted in coordination with the Maryland Department of Planning.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires health insurance carriers, including insurers, nonprofit health service plans, and health maintenance organizations, to provide coverage for all medically appropriate and necessary hearing aids for adults when prescribed, fitted, and dispensed by a licensed audiologist. It allows carriers to limit the benefit to $1,400 per hearing aid for each ear every 36 months, while permitting insured individuals to choose more expensive devices and pay the difference without penalty.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Insurance Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Adults covered under health insurance policies or contracts in Maryland, health insurance carriers, and licensed audiologists.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in a minimal special fund revenue increase for the Maryland Insurance Administration (MIA) in fiscal 2025 due to $125 rate and form filing fees. There is no fiscal impact on the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program as it already complies with the bill's requirements. Local government finances and small businesses are not anticipated to be materially affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland Insurance Administration is the primary regulatory body responsible for overseeing insurance carriers, reviewing rate and form filings, and ensuring compliance with state insurance mandates like the hearing aid coverage requirement for adults established by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office advocates for the welfare of Maryland's 1.2 million deaf and hard of hearing residents. The bill directly impacts this population by mandating insurance coverage for essential assistive technology (hearing aids) for adults.

The bill affects the coverage requirements for health plans offered through the state marketplace. The fiscal note specifically mentions the exchange in the context of the state's benchmark plan for essential health benefits.

The department was a primary source for the fiscal analysis. It oversees public health policy and regulates health care professionals, including the licensed audiologists required by the bill to prescribe and fit the hearing aids.

As the agency that manages the State Employee and Retiree Health and Welfare Benefits Program, DBM is responsible for ensuring that the state's own health plans comply with legislative mandates, though the fiscal note indicates the current state plan already meets these requirements.

A significant portion of the adult population requiring hearing aids includes older adults. The Department of Aging is relevant due to its role in advocating for the rights and quality of life for seniors and coordinating long-term care advocacy.

The department focuses on improving services and community living for residents with disabilities. Hearing loss is a major area of concern for disability advocacy, and this mandate facilitates access to necessary assistive technology.

The OAG provides legal counsel to the Maryland Insurance Administration and other state agencies, and enforces consumer protection laws that may be relevant to the implementation and enforcement of this insurance mandate.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill repeals the monopoly of the Wicomico County Liquor Control Board and its dispensaries regarding the wholesale sale and distribution of liquor and wine containing more than 15.5% alcohol by volume. It permits Class A, B, C, and D license holders to purchase alcoholic beverages from either a dispensary or a wholesaler and allows wholesalers to deliver liquor directly to retailers in the county.
qa_results.responsible_party: Liquor Control Board for Wicomico County
qa_results.stakeholders: Alcoholic beverages retail license holders in Wicomico County, alcohol wholesalers, and the Wicomico County Liquor Control Board.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State operations and finances are not materially affected. Wicomico County revenues are expected to decrease as retailers may shift their purchases from the county dispensary system to private wholesalers, though the exact fiscal impact is currently indeterminate. Small businesses such as wholesalers may see increased sales, while retailers may benefit from lower prices.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller is the state's principal regulator of the alcoholic beverages industry and is responsible for issuing the wholesaler licenses referenced in the bill. The repeal of the county monopoly and the changes to wholesale distribution laws directly impact the state-level regulatory and licensing framework managed by this agency.

The bill and its fiscal note indicate a 'meaningful' impact on small businesses, including wholesalers and retailers, by allowing for more competition and potential cost savings. As the state's primary economic development agency, this change to the local business environment in Wicomico County is relevant to its mission.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates state policy initiatives. As the chief executive who signed the bill into law, this office is responsible for the overarching policy change that repeals a long-standing local government monopoly.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the state, providing counsel to agencies like the Comptroller on the enforcement of alcoholic beverage laws and representing the state in legal matters related to regulatory changes.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Class B beer, wine, and liquor license holders in Wicomico County to purchase liquor for retail sale from licensed wholesalers in addition to the county dispensary. It also allows restaurant licensees who hold a Class 9 limited distillery license to sell products manufactured under that license for on- and off-premises consumption at the licensed premises.
qa_results.responsible_party: Wicomico County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Class B beer, wine, and liquor license holders (hotels and restaurants) in Wicomico County, Class 9 limited distillery license holders, and the Wicomico County Liquor Control Board.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no impact on State finances. Local revenues for Wicomico County will decrease by an indeterminate amount to the extent that Class B license holders choose to purchase liquor from licensed wholesalers instead of the county dispensary. Small businesses holding these licenses may experience cost savings or increased sales, but specific dollar estimates are not provided in the Fiscal Note.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller's summary specifies responsibility for regulating the alcohol industry and collecting state revenues. This bill directly modifies regulations regarding the purchase of liquor by retail license holders and the sale of manufactured products from limited distilleries.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and is responsible for signing or vetoing legislation, including this bill which modifies alcoholic beverage laws in Wicomico County.

The OAG serves as legal counsel to state agencies and the General Assembly, providing guidance on the enforcement and interpretation of the Alcoholic Beverages and Cannabis Article affected by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This collective category encompasses the Wicomico County Board of License Commissioners, which is the principal entity responsible for issuing the Class B licenses and wine permits discussed in the legislation.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in appointing members to various state and local boards, including the Wicomico County Board of License Commissioners that administers the license types amended by this bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes Howard County to enter into payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreements with property owners who provide more moderate-income housing units than required by local zoning laws. It requires the Howard County Executive to publish and periodically reevaluate the methodology used to calculate these payments on the county's website.
qa_results.responsible_party: Howard County Governing Body and the Howard County Executive
qa_results.stakeholders: Property owners and developers in Howard County, and moderate-income households.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, Howard County property tax revenues may decrease beginning in fiscal 2025 to the extent that certain moderate-income housing developments are exempt from real property taxes. The total revenue decrease is indeterminate as it depends on the number of participating developments and their assessed values, but the loss will be partially mitigated by the negotiated PILOT payments.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office serves as the executive authority that approved this legislation, which grants Howard County specific authority to enter into PILOT agreements to support moderate-income housing.

As the state agency responsible for affordable and moderate-income housing policy, this department has a strong policy interest in local tax incentives (PILOTs) designed to increase housing availability for moderate-income residents.

The board hears appeals regarding property tax assessments and exemptions; this bill creates a new exemption framework in Howard County for certain housing developments, potentially leading to future adjudication of qualifying properties.

The Tax Court acts as the highest administrative body for tax disputes and would likely handle final appeals regarding the application of Howard County's PILOT methodology or the tax-exempt status of affected properties.

As the state's chief financial officer and tax collector, the Comptroller monitors state and local tax policies, including payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreements that impact the state's broader fiscal landscape.
The OAG provides legal oversight and counsel regarding the constitutionality and legality of state laws, including those that modify local government taxing authority and property tax exemptions.
DBM monitors local fiscal impacts and the execution of state-authorized tax exemptions, as these factors can influence broader state revenue estimations and long-term fiscal planning.

This department provides guidance on sustainable growth and land use; the bill's focus on moderate-income housing units and zoning requirements aligns with the agency's mission to promote effective community development.

This multi-agency initiative focuses on community revitalization and sustainable growth, which includes supporting local government tools like PILOTs to ensure affordable and diverse housing options in growing counties.

The office is tasked with enhancing economic mobility and reducing poverty for families; increasing access to moderate-income housing units is a direct contributor to these objectives in historically underinvested or high-growth areas.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Department of Natural Resources and Baltimore City to convene focus group meetings, establish a stakeholder advisory committee, and develop a list of priority needs and cost assessments for improvements to the proposed Gwynns Falls State Park. The Department must submit a report to the General Assembly by December 1, 2025, detailing suitable properties for the park and the funding required for its establishment.
qa_results.responsible_party: Department of Natural Resources
qa_results.stakeholders: Baltimore City residents, local neighborhood organizations bordering the proposed park, environmental and historic preservation organizations, and the Friends of Gwynns Falls/Leakin Park.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general and special fund expenditures increase by an estimated $70,000 in FY 2025 and $30,000 in FY 2026 to hire a consultant to assist with developing priority needs and cost assessments. Baltimore City is expected to implement the bill using existing budgeted resources. There are no ongoing requirements once the report is submitted in 2025, and revenues are not directly affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary implementing agency, DNR is responsible for convening focus group meetings, establishing a stakeholder advisory committee, and developing a report on the priority needs, costs, and property suitability for the proposed Gwynns Falls State Park.

The bill requires a list of priority needs including historic site restoration and an assessment of property suitable for inclusion in the park, which aligns with the Department of Planning's role in land use planning and historical preservation through the Maryland Historical Trust.

The fiscal note indicates a notable expenditure of up to $100,000 for consultant services to assist with the cost assessments and reporting required by the bill, which falls under DBM's oversight of state budget and personnel resources.
The bill mandates the development of priority needs for capital improvements and critical maintenance, areas where DGS provides primary operational support and facility management for the state.
The OAG provides legal counsel to DNR regarding the establishment of advisory committees and the development of potential partnership agreements or memoranda of understanding mentioned in the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the composition of the seven-member State Board of Cosmetologists by requiring one member to be a licensed esthetician and reducing the number of consumer members from two to one.
qa_results.responsible_party: State Board of Cosmetologists (Maryland Department of Labor)
qa_results.stakeholders: Licensed estheticians, consumer members of the State Board of Cosmetologists, and the Maryland Department of Labor.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, altering the membership of the board does not affect State operations or finances. There is no impact on local governments or small businesses.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor's Office is responsible for appointing members to various boards and commissions, including the State Board of Cosmetologists, as specified in the bill.

The State Board of Cosmetologists is a unit within the Department of Labor, and the Secretary of Labor advises the Governor on board appointments. The department also oversees the licensing and regulation of the professions governed by this board.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in recruiting, vetting, and recommending candidates for state boards and commissions, and will be responsible for managing the changes in board membership required by this bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This bill directly alters the composition of the State Board of Cosmetologists, which is a permanent regulatory body categorized under this collective entity.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Class DBR brewery license and the Class DDS distillery permit in Frederick County, which serve as the required on-premises consumption permits for Class 5 breweries and Class 1 distilleries. These new licenses allow holders to sell food, nonalcoholic beverages, and their manufactured alcoholic products for on-premises and off-premises consumption as permitted by their primary license.
qa_results.responsible_party: Frederick County Board of License Commissioners
qa_results.stakeholders: Class 5 brewery license holders, Class 1 distillery license holders, and the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: Frederick County revenues may increase as the Board of License Commissioners adopts regulations to set and collect annual fees for the new Class DBR license and Class DDS permit. There is no impact on Maryland State finances. Local enforcement activities are expected to be managed with existing resources. Small businesses in the brewing and distilling sectors may see a positive impact from expanded sales capabilities.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Comptroller of Maryland is the primary state-level regulator of the alcohol industry. The bill creates local Frederick County permits and licenses that are contingent upon businesses holding a state-issued Class 1 distillery license or Class 5 brewery license, both of which are under the Comptroller's jurisdiction and regulatory oversight.

The Governor's Office is responsible for appointing members to the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners. This board is the entity empowered by the bill to issue the newly created Class DBR brewery licenses and Class DDS distillery permits and to adopt implementing regulations.

The Office of Appointments assists the Governor in the recruitment, vetting, and recommendation of candidates for state boards and commissions, including the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners, which is the primary implementing body for this legislation.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to state boards and commissions. They may be consulted regarding the legal requirements and drafting of regulations by the Frederick County Board of License Commissioners to carry out the provisions of the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill requires the Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) to develop guidelines for an antibias training program for school employees, covering topics such as implicit bias, cultural awareness, and discrimination against various racial, ethnic, and religious groups, specifically including antisemitism and Islamophobia. Each local board of education must develop or update a training program based on these guidelines and provide it biennially to school employees who frequently interact with students. Additionally, public institutions of higher education must include training on preventing antisemitism and Islamophobia in their cultural diversity plans.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) and local boards of education
qa_results.stakeholders: Public school employees, students, local school boards, and public institutions of higher education
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State general fund expenditures will increase by $100,000, likely in fiscal 2025, for MSDE to hire a consultant to develop the required antibias training guidelines. Local school systems will experience minimal expenditure increases to develop and provide the training to staff every other year. While many school systems may absorb these costs into existing professional development schedules, some may incur additional costs to ensure the training is paid professional development as required by the bill.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) is the primary agency responsible for implementing the bill. It is tasked with developing guidelines for the school employee antibias training program, periodically updating these guidelines, and providing oversight for local boards of education.

As an independent unit within MSDE, the Center for School Safety develops resources and training related to safe learning environments. The bill specifically requires that the new antibias guidelines and training incorporate lessons learned from bullying, harassment, and intimidation incidents, which fall under the Center's data and safety mandate.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly requires the Department of Education to develop the antibias training guidelines in consultation with the Professional Standards and Teacher Education Board, which is a permanent body within the scope of this collective entity.

According to the fiscal note, MSDE will require an increase in general fund expenditures (approximately $100,000) to hire a consultant to develop the training guidelines. DBM oversees the state budget and the allocation of these funds.

The bill focuses on preventing discrimination and reducing implicit bias regarding various protected classes (race, religion, etc.). The Maryland Commission on Civil Rights provides education and outreach to prevent discrimination and is a key stakeholder in the state's efforts to combat the biases listed in the bill.

The Office of the Attorney General provides legal counsel to MSDE and the General Assembly. They would likely be involved in reviewing the new guidelines for legal sufficiency and compliance with existing anti-discrimination laws.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates state policy initiatives. The Governor signed this bill into law and oversees MSDE as an executive department.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill prohibits or limits the use of noncompete and conflict of interest provisions in employment contracts for veterinary and health care professionals. It declares such provisions null and void for veterinary practitioners, veterinary technicians, and health care professionals providing direct patient care who earn $350,000 or less annually. For health care professionals earning more than $350,000, noncompete clauses are limited to a duration of one year and a geographic distance of 10 miles. Additionally, the Maryland Health Care Commission is required to conduct a study on the impact of private equity and hospital consolidations on the state's health care market.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Health Care Commission and the Maryland Department of Labor
qa_results.stakeholders: Veterinary practitioners, veterinary technicians, licensed health care professionals providing direct patient care, and health care employers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State special fund expenditures increase by $150,000 in fiscal 2025 for the Maryland Health Care Commission to contract with a private consultant for the required health care market study. There is no projected impact on State revenues or local government finances. Small businesses in the veterinary and health care sectors may experience meaningful impacts as the bill encourages job mobility and potentially higher wages by restricting noncompete agreements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill explicitly mandates the Commission to contract with a private consultant to study private equity firms, hospital consolidations, and physician practice acquisitions in the health care market. The Commission must report its findings to the General Assembly by January 1, 2025, and the fiscal note accounts for a $150,000 special fund expenditure for this purpose.

The bill amends Section 3–716 of the Labor and Employment Article, which falls under the regulatory and enforcement jurisdiction of the Department of Labor. This department oversees state laws regarding employment contracts and workplace protections, including the expanded bans on noncompete clauses.

The bill extends noncompete and conflict of interest prohibitions to veterinary practitioners and veterinary technicians who are licensed under the Agriculture Article. These professionals are regulated by the Department of Agriculture.

The bill impacts health care professionals licensed under the Health Occupations Article. Additionally, the Maryland Health Care Commission, which is tasked with the primary study required by the bill, is an independent agency located within the Maryland Department of Health.

The bill requires a study on how private equity and hospital consolidations impact the State's ability to meet the goals of the Total Cost of Care Model. The HSCRC is the primary agency responsible for administering this model and regulating hospital revenues, making it a critical stakeholder in the study's findings.

As the chief legal officer for the State, the OAG provides legal counsel to the Departments of Labor, Health, and Agriculture. It would be responsible for interpreting the new statutory language and potentially defending the State's public policy position in litigation involving nullified contract provisions.

DBM is responsible for fiscal planning and supervising budget execution. The bill requires a $150,000 special fund expenditure in FY 2025 for the health care market study, which falls under DBM's oversight of agency spending.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies responsible for implementing the bill. The Governor signed the bill into law and is responsible for appointing members to the Maryland Health Care Commission and other relevant regulatory boards.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the administration and funding of public school construction in Maryland. Key provisions include repealing the scheduled sunset of the School Safety Grant Program (SSGP), establishing a $10,000,000 mandated annual appropriation for the SSGP starting in FY 2027, and reducing the mandated appropriation for the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund by $10,000,000 to offset that cost. Additionally, the bill accelerates State payments for the Prince George’s County public-private partnership (P3) to FY 2025, establishes a Workgroup on the Assessment and Funding of School Facilities, and provides for a 100% State share of eligible costs for specific major high school renovation projects in counties meeting certain size and wealth criteria.
qa_results.responsible_party: Interagency Commission on School Construction
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland local school systems, students, the Prince George's County Board of Education, and residents of Howard, Kent, Queen Anne’s, Talbot, and Worcester counties.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill results in a $54,000,000 increase in State special fund expenditures in FY 2025 due to a mandated distribution to the Prince George’s County Public-Private Partnership Fund. Beginning in FY 2027, the bill establishes a $10,000,000 annual mandated appropriation for the School Safety Grant Program (general funds) which is offset by a $10,000,000 annual reduction in the mandated appropriation for the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund (special funds). Local revenues for Prince George's County increase by $54,000,000 in FY 2025, while other provisions may affect local matching requirements and the distribution of school construction funds across jurisdictions.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor oversees the executive branch and proposes the state budget, which is impacted by the bill's mandated appropriations for the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund and the School Safety Grant Program. Additionally, the Governor serves on the Board of Public Works, which oversees significant state contracts and expenditures related to school construction.

The Comptroller is responsible for accounting for the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund and serves as a member of the Board of Public Works, which approves state contracts and expenditures for public school construction.

The State Treasurer (or designee) is a designated member of the Workgroup on the Assessment and Funding of School Facilities. The Treasurer also holds and invests the money for the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund and serves on the Board of Public Works.

The Secretary of Budget and Management (or designee) is a member of the Workgroup on the Assessment and Funding of School Facilities. The department is also responsible for state budget development, which includes the mandated appropriations for school construction funds specified in the bill.

The State Superintendent of Schools (or designee) is a member of the Workgroup on the Assessment and Funding of School Facilities. The bill directly impacts the funding and administration of the public education system's facilities, which the department oversees.

The Secretary of General Services (or designee) is a member of the Workgroup on the Assessment and Funding of School Facilities. DGS provides operational support for facility planning and construction, making their expertise relevant to the workgroup's mission.

The Board of Public Works is responsible for overseeing significant state expenditures and contracts. The bill involves substantial funding mechanisms for school construction, including public-private partnerships and state share allocations that fall under the Board's fiscal oversight.

The Interagency Commission on School Construction is the primary agency responsible for administering the programs mentioned in the bill, including the Nancy K. Kopp Public School Facilities Priority Fund, the School Safety Grant Program, and the Prince George's County P3 fund. The IAC chair is also a member of the new workgroup, and the IAC is required to report on AI weapon detection systems.

The Maryland Center for School Safety is required to consult with the Interagency Commission on School Construction in the implementation and administration of the School Safety Grant Program, which is extended by the bill.

The Maryland Stadium Authority is a key partner in the Prince George's County public-private partnership (P3) agreement, responsible for reviewing agreements, depositing funds, and managing the Supplemental Public School Construction Financing Fund used for school construction bonds.

qa_results.bill_summary: HB 1393 expands the scope of Maryland's electric system planning from focusing solely on distribution to encompassing the entire electric system. The bill requires the Public Service Commission (PSC) to report on projects promoting greenhouse gas reduction and system resiliency, mandates that electric companies diligently pursue federal funding (such as from the Inflation Reduction Act), and requires the PSC to adopt regulations by December 31, 2025, for system planning and investment in demand-side reliability technology like virtual power plants. It also applies prevailing wage and labor standards to electric infrastructure projects funded by federal sources.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Public Service Commission and Maryland Energy Administration
qa_results.stakeholders: Investor-owned electric companies, electric cooperatives, municipal electric utilities, utility contractors and subcontractors, Maryland residents, and energy consumers.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The Public Service Commission and the Maryland Energy Administration can implement the bill's requirements using existing budgeted resources. The bill does not otherwise materially affect Maryland State or local government finances, as it focuses on regulatory mandates and the pursuit of federal funds. Small businesses involved in utility projects may experience a meaningful impact due to changes in project funding or labor requirements.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill designates the Public Service Commission as the primary regulatory body responsible for electric system planning, adopting regulations for federal fund pursuit, requiring investment in demand-side reliability methods, and reporting to the General Assembly.

The bill explicitly tasks the Maryland Energy Administration with identifying federal funding sources and providing assistance and support to electric companies to obtain access to these funds to meet state energy goals.

The bill amends the Labor and Employment Article to apply prevailing wage, apprenticeship, and recruitment standards to electric infrastructure projects funded by federal grants, which falls under the department's enforcement of labor laws.

As the advocate for residential utility consumers, this agency is directly impacted by the bill's focus on electric system planning, reliability investments (like virtual power plants), and the mandate for least-cost debt in utility projects.

The bill is designed to promote state policy goals for greenhouse gas reduction and renewable energy, and current law requires MDE to coordinate with PSC and MEA regarding federal funds for the electric distribution grid.

The Governor's Office oversees executive branch policy initiatives and coordinates intergovernmental relations regarding federal funding like the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the PSC and MEA, assisting in the development and enforcement of the new regulations and orders required by the bill.
This office assists state agencies in identifying and managing federal grant funding, which is the primary mechanism for funding the electric system projects described in the bill.
The bill's requirements for apprenticeship programs and resident recruitment for utility projects align with the board's mission to coordinate workforce solutions and talent pipelines.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill establishes aspirational goals for the recruitment and retention of minority and women workers on federally funded electric system projects.
As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees the budget execution and personnel resources for the agencies (PSC and MEA) implementing the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill expands and standardizes civil rights protections by prohibiting discrimination based on sexual orientation, gender identity, race, religious beliefs, marital status, and disability across various sectors including employment, credit, education, healthcare, and housing. It also clarifies that these protected characteristics cannot be used as legally adequate provocation or a defense in certain criminal cases, and it repeals a redundant 2023 act related to reproductive freedom.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Department of Labor, Maryland Commission on Civil Rights, and the Commissioner of Financial Regulation
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland residents, employees, employers, students, healthcare patients, lenders, and small business owners
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures may increase minimally for the Maryland Department of Labor to handle an increased volume of complaints and provide training for investigators. The bill is not anticipated to materially affect local government finances. Small businesses may experience a meaningful impact as they must comply with expanded anti-discrimination laws and could face penalties for violations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill impacts the Judiciary by prohibiting discrimination in jury service and altering the statute of limitations for certain wage discrimination prosecutions.
The bill amends the State Finance and Procurement Article regarding nondiscrimination clauses in State contracts, which the Board of Public Works oversees and approves.

As the primary agency for enforcing anti-discrimination laws, the Commission is responsible for investigating complaints and enforcing the expanded categories of protected classes established by this bill.
As a member of the Board of Public Works, the Comptroller oversees the implementation of nondiscrimination clauses in state contracts and the fiscal impact of tax-related discrimination provisions.
As the central personnel agency, DBM is responsible for managing state employee policies, which must comply with the expanded anti-discrimination protections in employment and pay.

The bill prohibits discrimination against public school employees and students by the State Superintendent and county boards, adding gender identity and other protected classes to the Education Article.
As a primary procurement authority, DGS must ensure that state contracts and subcontracts include the updated nondiscrimination clauses required by the bill.
The bill prohibits discrimination by home health agencies and health maintenance organizations, and restricts discriminatory practices by health professional boards under its jurisdiction.
The bill amends provisions related to employee organizations and resident rights within the housing sector, ensuring they comply with the expanded list of protected characteristics.
The bill updates the Residents' Bill of Rights for facilities contracted with the agency, prohibiting discrimination based on gender identity and other characteristics.

The fiscal note indicates that the Department of Labor (MDL) will experience increased general fund expenditures to handle a higher volume of complaints related to expanded protections in the Equal Pay for Equal Work Act and financial lending.
The bill amends the Land Use Article concerning employee organizations, for which the Department provides data and policy guidance.
As a major modal administration and procurement authority, MDOT must implement the bill's nondiscrimination requirements in its contracts and employment practices.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch agencies implementing these civil rights expansions and coordinated the policy initiatives reflected in the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Crime Prevention and Policy
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill alters criminal law regarding provocation and defenses related to protected characteristics, which falls under the office's mandate to coordinate public safety and criminal justice strategies.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: This office monitors socioeconomic procurement programs that are directly affected by the changes to nondiscrimination clauses in state contracts.
As an independent unit responsible for health insurance marketplaces, the Exchange must ensure its programs and carrier contracts comply with the expanded non-discrimination categories.
The bill prohibits carriers and health networks from discriminating based on the expanded protected classes, falling under the MIA's regulatory and enforcement authority.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Office of the Public Defender
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The office represents indigent defendants in criminal cases affected by the bill's changes to provocation and defense laws regarding race, religion, and other characteristics.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland State Board of Contract Appeals
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The board adjudicates contract disputes that may arise from the enforcement or interpretation of the updated nondiscrimination clauses in state contracts.
MSP is responsible for investigating crimes, including those where the bill restricts certain defenses or provocation claims based on the victim's protected characteristics.
The Treasurer sits on the Board of Public Works and oversees state insurance and finance matters impacted by the procurement and contract amendments.
As a state agency and employer, Morgan State University must comply with the expanded anti-discrimination and labor provisions of the bill.
OAH is responsible for conducting administrative hearings for contested discrimination cases referred by agencies like the Commission on Civil Rights.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the state on these civil rights changes and is authorized to bring actions for violations of the State's Equal Pay for Equal Work law as expanded by the bill.
The bill prohibits country clubs and golf courses that practice discrimination from entering or continuing certain property tax agreements, which may result in appeals handled by this board.
The bill specifically prohibits electricity suppliers from discriminating against customers based on the expanded list of protected characteristics, which the PSC regulates.
As a state agency and educational institution, the college must adhere to the bill's expanded protections in employment and educational services.
The University System and its constituent institutions are subject to the labor and educational anti-discrimination requirements updated by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the right for nonprobationary employees of the Montgomery County State's Attorney's Office, including nonsupervisory assistant State's Attorneys, to organize and bargain collectively. It subjects these employees to the county's merit system and personnel regulations, and it defines the County Executive and the State's Attorney as the employers for different aspects of collective bargaining.
qa_results.responsible_party: Montgomery County State's Attorney and Montgomery County Executive
qa_results.stakeholders: Nonprobationary employees of the Montgomery County State's Attorney's Office and the Montgomery County government.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State effect is none. Montgomery County expenditures will increase to facilitate collective bargaining and may increase significantly depending on negotiated personnel costs. The fiscal note indicates that a 1% increase in personnel costs for nonsenior positions would cost $183,000 annually, and providing traditional overtime could cost more than $2,000,000 annually.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the head of the executive branch, the Governor oversees policy initiatives and administrative support for state-level offices. This bill, which alters the personnel and collective bargaining structure of the Montgomery County State's Attorney's Office (a state-authorized constitutional office), is relevant to the Governor's policy oversight and the legislative approval process.

The OAG serves as the chief legal officer for the State of Maryland and provides counsel to state agencies and officials. Since State's Attorneys are state constitutional officers, the OAG is relevant for providing legal guidance on the intersection of state constitutional authority and the application of local county personnel and collective bargaining laws.

The Public Employee Relations Board is responsible for standardizing the administration of collective bargaining for public sector employees in Maryland. Although the bill specifies that the Montgomery County State's Attorney's Office will utilize a local bargaining framework and administrator, the determination of which public employees fall under state versus local labor regimes is relevant to the Board's jurisdiction and state labor policy oversight.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill alters the definition of 'public school employee' in Dorchester County to include supervisory noncertificated employees, thereby allowing these individuals to participate in collective bargaining.
qa_results.responsible_party: Dorchester County Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Supervisory noncertificated employees in Dorchester County
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: The bill has no State fiscal impact and is not expected to materially affect Dorchester County finances.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Public Employee Relations Board (PERB) is the primary state agency responsible for administering and standardizing collective bargaining for local public school system personnel. Since this bill alters the definition of 'public school employee' to include supervisory noncertificated employees in Dorchester County for collective bargaining purposes, PERB is the primary regulatory body responsible for overseeing the resulting representation elections and labor practices.

The Maryland State Department of Education (MSDE) provides leadership and oversight for the state's 24 local school systems, including Dorchester County. Changes to the classification and bargaining rights of local school employees fall within the scope of the state's educational policy and personnel framework.

The Governor's Office is responsible for overseeing the executive branch and signing legislation into law. As an enacted chapter of the 2024 Laws of Maryland, this bill was signed by the Governor and affects the administrative policies of a local subdivision within the state.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill makes several technical and substantive alterations to the Blueprint for Maryland’s Future. Key changes include expanding National Board Certification (NBC) fee reimbursements to include nonclassroom teachers such as principals and administrators, extending the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant Program through fiscal year 2029, and authorizing the State Board of Education and the Accountability and Implementation Board to limit dual enrollment courses for fiscal years 2025 through 2027 to align with funding increases. It also transfers authority for certain teacher career ladder specifications from the Accountability and Implementation Board to the State Board of Education and adjusts various reporting deadlines for career and technical education.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education and the State Board of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Public school teachers, school administrators (principals, assistant principals), dually enrolled high school students, and prekindergarten providers and students.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund expenditures are estimated to increase by less than $300,000 annually to cover National Board Certification fee reimbursements for nonclassroom teachers. Local school systems will see an expenditure increase of approximately $140,000 annually for their required one-third match of these fees. Additionally, the bill extends the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant program—funded at roughly $26,600,000 per year—through fiscal 2029, which is expected to be offset by reductions in the full-day prekindergarten funding program but may result in indeterminate changes to local effort requirements and state aid calculations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary agency for oversight of Maryland's public education system, MSDE is responsible for implementing the majority of the bill's provisions, including managing Judy Centers, the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant Program, National Board Certification payments for teachers, and the Kindergarten Readiness Assessment.

The bill alters mandated appropriations for the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant Program and National Board Certification fees, impacting the state budget development and long-term fiscal planning managed by DBM.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically tasks the Career and Technical Education (CTE) Committee with establishing and reporting on statewide goals for apprenticeships and industry credentials, and shifts certain approval powers from the Accountability and Implementation Board (AIB) to the State Board of Education.

The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and policy initiatives like the Blueprint for Maryland's Future. The Governor must also ensure proper funding is included in the annual budget for the centers and grants expanded by this bill.
The bill's Career and Technical Education goals are directly linked to registered apprenticeship programs approved by the Department of Labor's Division of Workforce Development and Adult Learning.

Local departments of social services are required to provide local school systems with contact information for parents eligible for publicly funded prekindergarten, supporting the expansion of the program.
Local health departments are mandated by the bill to provide local school systems with contact information for parents eligible for prekindergarten programs.

The commission oversees the dually enrolled student policies and the student financial assistance programs (like the Janet L. Hoffman Loan Assistance Repayment Program) which are expanded by the bill to include early childhood educators.

The focus on expanding access to prekindergarten, Judy Centers, and family support services aligns with this office's mission to reduce child poverty and improve access to health and education services.
As the state's chief financial officer, the Comptroller is involved in the distribution and oversight of state revenues and education aid formulas modified by this bill.
The Treasurer manages state funds and serves on the Board of Public Works, which oversees significant state contracts and expenditures related to educational infrastructure and programs.
The MLDS Center tracks student performance and workforce outcomes; changes to CTE goals and student eligibility for dual enrollment will affect the data they collect and analyze.
Public universities within the system host dually enrolled students who may be impacted by the new limits on course types and numbers established by the State Board and AIB.
As a public university, Morgan State may host dually enrolled students whose enrollment patterns could be affected by the limits established under the bill.
As a public honors college, St. Mary's may host dually enrolled students subject to the course limitations authorized by the bill.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill increases funding for Maryland's emergency medical and trauma services by raising the annual motor vehicle registration surcharge from $17 to $40 and increasing fines for impaired driving. It expands the types of healthcare practitioners eligible for reimbursement from the Maryland Trauma Physician Services Fund, modifies reimbursement methodologies for trauma centers, and mandates annual appropriations for the Senator William H. Amoss Fire, Rescue, and Ambulance Fund starting in fiscal year 2026.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC), Health Services Cost Review Commission (HSCRC), and the Maryland Department of Transportation (MDOT)
qa_results.stakeholders: Maryland vehicle owners, trauma patients, trauma physicians and healthcare practitioners, designated trauma centers, and local fire, rescue, and ambulance departments.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues increase by $104,781,905 annually starting in FY 2025 due to the increased vehicle registration surcharge. These funds are distributed to the Maryland Emergency Medical System Operations Fund (MEMSOF), the R Adams Cowley Shock Trauma Center, and the Maryland Trauma Physician Services Fund. Special fund expenditures increase by $3,410,000 in FY 2025 and $4,910,000 annually thereafter for credit card processing fees, mandated pediatric trauma grants, and mandated appropriations to the Senator William H. Amoss Fire, Rescue, and Ambulance Fund (increasing that fund's total mandate to $16.5 million starting in FY 2026).

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates that the Governor include specific appropriations in the annual budget bill for the Senator William H. Amoss Fire, Rescue, and Ambulance Fund and specifies the allocation of surcharges to the R Adams Cowley Shock Trauma Center.

The Comptroller is specifically tasked with administering the Maryland Emergency Medical System Operations Fund (MEMSOF), including the accounting and reconciliation of the increased revenues from motor vehicle registration surcharges.

As a member of the Board of Public Works and the state's chief financial agency, the Treasurer's Office is involved in the high-level management and protection of the state funds being redistributed and increased by this bill.

DBM is responsible for the state's budget execution and must ensure that the bill's mandated annual appropriation of at least $16.5 million for the Amoss Fund and other specified distributions are correctly implemented in the annual budget.

The Maryland Department of Emergency Management (MDEM) administers the Senator William H. Amoss Fire, Rescue, and Ambulance Fund, which receives a significant increase in mandated annual funding under this bill.

The bill significantly modifies the Maryland Trauma Physician Services Fund (MTPSF), which is administered by the Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC) and the Health Services Cost Review Commission (HSCRC), both of which are units within or affiliated with the Department of Health.

The Motor Vehicle Administration (MVA), an agency within MDOT, is responsible for collecting the increased motor vehicle registration surcharges that provide the primary revenue stream for the emergency services funds mentioned in the bill.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MIEMSS is the primary regulatory and coordinating agency for Maryland's emergency medical services. The bill directly impacts the funding for trauma centers and the primary adult resource center, which are under MIEMSS's oversight and designation.

The bill alters the authorized uses and distribution of the Maryland Emergency Medical System Operations Fund (MEMSOF), which has historically provided critical funding for the Maryland State Police Aviation Command.

The bill increases criminal fines for DUI and DWI offenses, which are adjudicated by the Maryland Judiciary. This requires the Administrative Office of the Courts to update financial and case management systems to collect and distribute the increased revenue.

The R Adams Cowley Shock Trauma Center, a major beneficiary of the redirected and increased surcharge funding, is a key component of the clinical and research mission affiliated with the University System of Maryland.

qa_results.bill_summary: The bill requires the Howard County Board of Education and the Howard County Superintendent of Schools to publish a report and notify the General Assembly if there are differences between the Superintendent's proposed capital budget and the Board's approved version, or between the current proposed Capital Improvement Program and the one from the previous year. These reports must include a detailed rationale for each difference.
qa_results.responsible_party: Howard County Board of Education and the Howard County Superintendent of Schools
qa_results.stakeholders: Howard County Public School System, Howard County Board of Education, Howard County Superintendent, and Howard County residents
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no State fiscal impact. Locally, the Howard County Board of Education and Superintendent can implement the reporting requirements using existing resources, and local revenues are not affected.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill mandates reporting on differences in the capital budget and capital improvement programs (CIP) of Howard County Public Schools. The IAC is the primary state agency responsible for managing the state's school construction funding and reviewing local CIP submissions, which are directly based on the budget documents addressed in this bill.

As the state agency providing oversight and leadership to all 24 local school systems in Maryland, the Department of Education has a general regulatory interest in the fiscal transparency and governance requirements imposed on the Howard County Board of Education.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill modifies the requirements and delivery of publicly funded prekindergarten programs in Maryland. It establishes a career ladder for educators at private prekindergarten providers, adjusts staff qualification standards to allow for experience-based pathways, and sets a state goal that at least 50% of prekindergarten slots be provided by private providers beginning in the 2027-2028 school year. Additionally, it requires the Maryland State Department of Education to establish prekindergarten provider hubs to support a mixed delivery system, allows local boards of education to apply for state construction funding for leased facilities, and extends mandated funding for grant programs including the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant and the Maryland Child Care Credential Program.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Department of Education
qa_results.stakeholders: Public and private prekindergarten providers, early childhood educators and teaching assistants, and families with children aged 3 to 4 years old.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State general fund expenditures increase by $1,000,000 in FY 2025 to implement the early childhood educator career ladder. Special fund expenditures increase by $5,700,000 in FY 2026 to meet a mandated appropriation for the Maryland Child Care Credential Program. The extension of the Prekindergarten Expansion Grant through FY 2029 is anticipated to be offset by reductions in the full-day prekindergarten funding formula, although this shift may have significant indeterminate secondary effects on local education effort and State education aid. Local school systems may see minimal impact from the authorization to use capital improvement funds for leased facilities.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: As the primary implementing agency, MSDE is responsible for establishing the Early Childhood Educator Career Ladder, creating prekindergarten provider hubs, developing a model memorandum of understanding for county boards, and administering the Child Care Career and Professional Development Fund.

The bill explicitly requires the Commission to prioritize funding requests for high-quality prekindergarten classrooms and authorizes county boards to apply for funding from the Capital Improvement Program for renovations to leased public facilities.

The bill identifies local workforce development boards as key entities to receive and distribute lists of eligible prekindergarten providers and coordinate business support services, aligning with the Board's role in statewide workforce strategy.

The bill mandates that the Governor include specific appropriations in the state budget for the Maryland Child Care Credential Program and other prekindergarten initiatives across multiple fiscal years.
DBM is responsible for oversight of the mandated appropriations and the fiscal impact of the new career ladder and grant program extensions identified in the fiscal note.

The Department oversees the workforce development system and adult learning programs that correlate with the bill's professional development requirements and the utilization of local workforce boards.
The bill directs information to Local Management Boards (LMBs), which are coordinated by this office, and supports the agency's mission to reduce child poverty through expanded educational access.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: Many private prekindergarten providers are small, minority, or women-owned businesses; the bill requires outreach and business plan assistance through local offices supporting these entities.
The OAG provides legal counsel for the development of new regulations, the model MOU, and ensures the implementation of new staff qualifications complies with state and federal laws.

The bill's career ladder significantly increases the demand for associate and bachelor's degrees in early childhood education, impacting the academic standards and coordination of Maryland's higher education institutions.
The Board oversees significant state expenditures and contracts, including capital improvements for school construction projects that are prioritized or altered by this legislation.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Howard County Task Force to Study Revenue Options for School Capital Needs. The task force is charged with identifying capital needs for Howard County Public Schools through 2035, analyzing projected funding and gaps, and exploring revenue sources to address those gaps, such as transfer tax adjustments or new commercial excise taxes. A report with findings and recommendations is due by October 31, 2024.
qa_results.responsible_party: Howard County Executive
qa_results.stakeholders: Howard County Public Schools (HCPS), Howard County residents, local government agencies (Finance, Budget, Recreation and Parks, etc.), and community organizations such as the Howard County PTA Council and Farm Bureau.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: There is no state fiscal impact. Howard County government may incur indeterminate local expenditures associated with staffing the task force and completing the complex analysis and report within the required timeframe. Local revenues are not directly affected by this bill, although task force recommendations may lead to future revenue-generating legislation.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The IAC is the primary state agency responsible for overseeing public school construction and capital improvement projects, directly aligning with the task force's mandate to identify school capital needs and funding options.

The MSA provides significant funding for school construction in Howard County through the Built to Learn Act, as noted in the fiscal note, making its financial activities highly relevant to the task force's study of revenue gaps.

The MSDE provides leadership for the state's public education system and coordinates educational policies, making it relevant to any long-term planning regarding the infrastructure of Howard County's schools.
As the state's chief financial officer and tax collector, the Comptroller is relevant because the task force is studying new revenue options, including transfer taxes and commercial excise taxes.

The OAG provides legal counsel on state law and would be relevant in determining whether Howard County has the legal authority to enact the revenue-generating recommendations proposed by the task force.
The department promotes sustainable growth and provides land use data analysis, which is essential for determining long-term school capital needs and infrastructure requirements through 2035.
The Governor's Office oversees the executive branch and coordinates policy initiatives, including the final approval of the legislation and receiving reports from the local delegation.
As the principal fiscal and personnel agency, DBM is responsible for estimating state revenues and supervising budget execution, which includes the state's contributions to school capital projects.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill establishes the Arts Incubator Workgroup to study and make recommendations on how arts and cultural institutions can support local artists. The workgroup is required to submit a preliminary report of its findings by January 1, 2025, and a final report including budget and facility recommendations by October 1, 2025.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland State Arts Council
qa_results.stakeholders: Local Maryland artists, arts and cultural institutions, local arts councils, and arts districts.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: According to the Fiscal Note, State expenditures for member expense reimbursements are expected to be minimal and absorbable within existing budgeted resources. The Maryland State Arts Council can provide staffing for the workgroup using existing resources. There is no material impact on local government finances or operations.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Governor is responsible for appointing the majority of the workgroup's members (13 individuals), selecting the workgroup's Chair, and receiving both the preliminary and final reports on the group's findings and recommendations.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill creates a new temporary body (the Arts Incubator Workgroup) tasked with conducting a specific study and providing recommendations, which falls directly into this category of executive commissions and task forces.

The Department of Commerce is responsible for overseeing the arts in Maryland. The bill specifically mandates that the Maryland State Arts Council, a unit within this department, provides staff support for the workgroup.

The Governor's Office of Appointments will be responsible for recruiting and vetting the 13 specific appointees required by the bill, including artists, educators, and representatives from various arts entities.

As the principal fiscal agency, DBM oversees budget execution; the bill allows workgroup members to be reimbursed for travel expenses through the state budget according to standard regulations managed by DBM.

qa_results.bill_summary: This bill authorizes soil conservation districts to recommend a fee system to local governing bodies to cover the costs of reviewing plans for 'small ponds' that are exempt from certain state permit requirements. It establishes procedures for determining these costs annually, submitting fee recommendations to local governments, and ensures that collected fees are used only to supplement, not replace, existing state and county funding for the districts.
qa_results.responsible_party: Maryland soil conservation districts and local governing bodies
qa_results.stakeholders: Landowners, small businesses, soil conservation districts, local governments, the Maryland Department of Agriculture, and the Maryland Department of the Environment.
qa_results.fiscal_impact_summary: State special fund revenues may decrease minimally beginning in FY 2025 if more small pond plan reviews are conducted by soil conservation districts instead of the Maryland Department of the Environment (which currently charges a $750 fee). Local soil conservation district revenues may increase as they implement new fee systems to cover review costs; for example, Charles County estimated a potential annual revenue increase of approximately $10,000.

agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill amends the Agriculture Article of the Maryland Code and directly affects soil conservation districts, which are coordinated through the state agricultural framework and involve the Department in data usage for soil conservation and water quality plans.

The bill defines small ponds based on exemptions from MDE permit requirements and directly impacts MDE's workload and special fund revenues, as the agency currently reviews these plans when districts do not.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Executive Commissions, Committees, Task Forces, and Advisory Boards
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The bill specifically references the State Soil Conservation Committee, which is defined in the Agriculture Article as the coordinating body for soil conservation efforts and districts.

The Council addresses the unique challenges of rural counties, where agricultural small ponds are common, and focuses on natural resource stewardship and regional economic development impacted by new fees.
DBM is responsible for estimating revenues and managing the state budget; the fiscal note indicates a decrease in MDE special fund revenues due to the bill.
The OAG provides legal counsel to the Department of Agriculture and the Department of the Environment regarding the implementation and enforcement of new fee systems and regulatory changes.
DNR is responsible for managing the state's natural resources and waterways, which are affected by the construction, maintenance, and regulation of small ponds.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Maryland Agricultural & Resource-Based Industry Development Corp.
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: MARBIDCO supports agricultural profitability; the establishment of new fees for pond plan reviews could impact the capital costs for farm infrastructure projects.
MES provides technical environmental services for water and infrastructure projects and may be involved in the design or management of facilities subject to these reviews.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Council on the Chesapeake Bay
agency_relevance.relevance_explanation: The Council coordinates interagency efforts between Agriculture and Environment for bay restoration, where soil conservation and pond management are key strategies.
This multi-agency initiative involves the Departments of Planning, Environment, and Agriculture to promote sustainable development and resource management related to soil and water.

agency_relevance.agency_name: Governor's Office of Small, Minority & Women Business Affairs
